or cortical damage"| X["Cortical Implant not subretinal or extraocular"] C -->|"Yes bipolar and ganglion cells intact"| D{"Surgical risk tolerance?"} C -->|"No advanced degeneration"| Y["LGN or Cortical Prosthesis"] D -->|"Acceptable"| E["SUBRETINAL Implant
High Resolution Option"] D -->|"Low tolerance
Elderly patient"| F["EXTRAOCULAR Implant
Suprachoroidal Option"] E --> G["Power delivery?"] G -->|"Passive insufficient"| G1["Active Subretinal with RF Link"] G -->|"Active acceptable"| G2["Confirm Bipolar Cell Density"] F --> H["Plan Electrode Geometry
for Safety"] style E fill:#87ceeb style F fill:#ffa07a style X fill:#d3d3d3 style Y fill:#d3d3d3 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Define subretinal implant. Mention any two advantages and one limitation.** **Model Answer:** A subretinal implant is a micro-photodiode array (MPDA) surgically placed in the subretinal space, between the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and the degenerated photoreceptor layer, to electrically stimulate the surviving bipolar cells in patients with retinitis pigmentosa (RP) or age-related macular degeneration (AMD). **Advantages:** 1. Electrode-bipolar cell proximity (~20 μm) yields low stimulation thresholds and high spatial resolution. 2. Utilizes the natural optical system of the eye (cornea + lens), eliminating the need for an external camera in passive designs. **Limitation:** 1. Limited heat dissipation under the retina, restricting safe power delivery — risk of thermal damage to surrounding neural tissue. --- > **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **What is an extraocular visual prosthesis? List two clinical advantages over intraocular designs.** **Model Answer:** An extraocular visual prosthesis is a neuro-stimulator device whose electrode array is placed entirely outside the eyeball — typically in the suprachoroidal or episcleral space — without breaching the sclera. Electrical stimulation reaches the retina via trans-scleral current spread. **Clinical Advantages:** 1. **Reduced surgical risk:** Avoids intraocular complications like retinal detachment, endophthalmitis, and vitreous hemorrhage. 2. **Easier revision/replacement:** The electrode lead remains accessible behind the ear for maintenance. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Set A]` > > **(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the **architecture and operating principle of a subretinal visual prosthesis**. Discuss the role of each subsystem in the stimulation chain. **\[7 marks\]** > > **(b)** A silicon micro-photodiode of area $20 \times 20 \,\mu m^2$ is illuminated with white light of irradiance $0.002\,\text{W/cm}^2$. The diode has a responsivity of $0.35\,\text{A/W}$ at the operating wavelength. Compute (i) the photocurrent generated and (ii) the charge delivered over a $1\,\text{ms}$ pulse window. Compare this to the typical bipolar-cell threshold charge of $0.1\,\text{nC}$ and comment on the adequacy of passive operation. **\[7 marks\]** ### Model Solution — Part (a) The subretinal prosthesis replaces degenerated photoreceptors. The functional chain is: 1. **Optical Subsystem:** Natural eye optics (cornea + lens) focus light onto the implant. **Block diagram → see Section 4.2.** 2. **Photovoltaic Subsystem:** Micro-photodiode array converts incident photons → electrical current. 3. **Signal Conditioning:** On-chip CMOS amplifier boosts photocurrent (typically 100×–1000×) to reach neural threshold. 4. **Electrode Interface:** Iridium or platinum microelectrodes deliver biphasic charge-balanced pulses to bipolar cell dendrites. 5. **Hermetic Package:** The chip is encapsulated in parylene-C / silicone for chronic biocompatibility. 6. **External Power Coupling (Active systems only):** Inductive RF link provides supplemental current. **Role Summary:** Each subsystem sequentially transforms an optical scene → into a spatiotemporal pattern of neural stimulation → ultimately perceived as phosphenes by the patient. ### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Photocurrent Computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{pd} &= E_{opt} \cdot A_{pd} \\ P_{pd} &= (0.002\,\text{W/cm}^2) \cdot (4 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{cm}^2) \\ P_{pd} &= 8 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{W} \\ I_{ph} &= R_\lambda \cdot P_{pd} \\ I_{ph} &= (0.35\,\text{A/W}) \cdot (8 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{W}) \\ I_{ph} &= 2.8 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{A} = 28\,\text{pA} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Charge delivered in 1 ms:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= I_{ph} \cdot t \\ Q &= (2.8 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{A}) \cdot (10^{-3}\,\text{s}) \\ Q &= 2.8 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{C} = 28\,\text{fC} \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison to threshold:** $$ \text{Ratio} = \frac{28\,\text{fC}}{0.1\,\text{nC}} = \frac{28 \times 10^{-15}}{10^{-10}} = 2.8 \times 10^{-4} = 0.028\% $$ **Comment:** The passive device delivers only **0.028%** of the required threshold charge. Passive operation is grossly inadequate. This justifies the shift to **active subretinal implants** (e.g., Alpha AMS) with external RF inductive power coupling to supplement the deficient photocurrent. --- > ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Set B]` > > **(a)** Explain the **anatomical positioning and operating principle of an extraocular visual prosthesis**. Discuss why suprachoroidal placement is often preferred over pure episcleral placement. **\[7 marks\]** > > **(b)** An extraocular disc electrode of area $0.6\,\text{mm}^2$ must deliver $0.8\,\text{mA}$ through a scleral thickness of $0.6\,\text{mm}$ (scleral resistivity $50\,\Omega\cdot\text{m}$). Calculate (i) the scleral resistance, (ii) the stimulating voltage, and (iii) the maximum allowable pulse width to keep charge density below the Shannon limit of $0.3\,\text{C/cm}^2$. **\[7 marks\]** ### Model Solution — Part (a) **Anatomical Positioning:** The extraocular array is implanted on the outer surface of the sclera (episcleral) or within a surgically created pocket between the sclera and choroid (suprachoroidal). No penetration of the sclera occurs. **Operating Principle:** 1. External camera captures the visual scene. 2. VPU extracts contrast and edge features. 3. RF inductive link transmits data + power across the skin. 4. Implanted receiver decodes signal → drives electrode array. 5. Current passes **trans-sclerally** → through choroid → stimulates retinal ganglion cells (RGCs). **Why Suprachoroidal over Episcleral?** | Aspect | Episcleral | Suprachoroidal | |---|---|---| | Distance to retina | ~1.0 mm | ~0.5 mm | | Threshold current | Higher | Lower (~50%) | | Current spatial spread | Wider | More focused | | Surgical ease | Easiest | Moderate dissection | Suprachoroidal placement offers **intermediate proximity** — closer than episcleral but safer than penetrating electrodes — providing a clinically attractive trade-off. ### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Scleral resistance:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{sclera} &= \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A_e} \\ R_{sclera} &= \frac{(50\,\Omega\cdot\text{m}) \cdot (0.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m})}{0.6 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2} \\ R_{sclera} &= \frac{3 \times 10^{-2}}{6 \times 10^{-7}} = 5 \times 10^{4}\,\Omega = 50\,\text{k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Stimulating voltage:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{stim} &= I_{stim} \cdot R_{sclera} \\ V_{stim} &= (0.8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{4}\,\Omega) \\ V_{stim} &= 40\,\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Maximum pulse width for safe charge density:** $$ \begin{aligned} k_{max} &= \frac{I \cdot t_{max}}{A_e} \\ t_{max} &= \frac{k_{max} \cdot A_e}{I} \\ t_{max} &= \frac{(0.3\,\text{C/cm}^2) \cdot (0.6 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2)}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}} \\ t_{max} &= \frac{0.3 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-6}}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}} \\ t_{max} &= \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-7}}{8 \times 10^{-4}} \\ t_{max} &= 2.25 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} \\ t_{max} &= 225\,\mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — High Frequency Trap** > Common student mistakes leading to **2–4 mark deductions**: > > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** between mm² → cm² or μm² → cm² in charge density calculations. *Always convert to CGS (cm) before applying Shannon limits!* > 2. **Confusing subretinal with epiretinal implants.** Subretinal = between RPE and photoreceptors. Epiretinal = on inner retinal surface (ganglion cell side). The Argus II is **epiretinal**, NOT subretinal. > 3. **Treating the sclera as purely resistive.** At high frequencies the capacitive (double-layer) component becomes significant — this affects threshold predictions. > 4. **Ignoring charge balancing.** Stimulation must be **biphasic and charge-balanced** (cathodic + anodic equal magnitude) to prevent net DC and electrochemical damage. > 5. **Writing 70 V for subretinal** — this voltage applies to extraocular; subretinal thresholds are typically **< 2 V**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1** - ✅ **Subretinal Implant** = placed between RPE and degenerated photoreceptors; stimulates bipolar cells. - ✅ **Extraocular Implant** = placed outside the globe (episcleral/suprachoroidal); stimulates retinal ganglion cells via trans-scleral current. - ✅ **Passive subretinal** = powered only by ambient light; insufficient charge under normal lighting (~0.03% of threshold). - ✅ **Active subretinal** = requires external inductive RF power coupling; clinically viable. - ✅ **Shannon Equation:** $\log_{10}(D) + k \leq 1.85$ defines safe stimulation envelope. - ✅ **Charge density limit:** Typically $k \leq 0.4\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2$ for platinum electrodes. - ✅ **Heat dissipation limit:** $P \leq 40\,\text{mW/cm}^2$ to prevent retinal thermal damage. - ✅ **Photocurrent formula:** $I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$; silicon responsivity ≈ **0.3–0.5 A/W**. - ✅ **Argus II is epiretinal** (NOT extraocular or subretinal) — common confusion point! - ✅ **Spatial resolution** favors subretinal; **surgical safety** favors extraocular. - ✅ **Pulse width** must be **charge-balanced biphasic** to avoid electrochemical damage. - ✅ **Reference Devices:** ASR (Optobionics), Alpha IMS/AMS (Retina Implant AG), PRIMA (Pixium Vision), Bionic Vision Australia suprachoroidal. --- > **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Subretinal and Extraocular Implants** > *Compiled per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Aligned*

topics.title: Visual Stimulation in the Brain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Visual Stimulation in the Brain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Visual Stimulation in the Brain** is the deliberate, controlled activation of neurons along the visual pathway — particularly at the level of the **primary visual cortex (V1)**, the **lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN)**, or the **optic nerve** — using exogenous energy sources (electrical currents, magnetic fields, optical radiation, or pharmacological agents) to elicit localized perception of light, termed **phosphenes**, in subjects suffering from profound visual impairment or total blindness.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, this concept forms the foundational principle behind **cortical visual prostheses (CVPs)** and **visual neuroprosthetic devices**, which bypass damaged photoreceptors or severed optic nerves and write information "directly" onto the neural substrate of conscious visual perception.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> The course outcome **CO1** emphasizes understanding the fundamental mechanisms of neural stimulation. Visual cortical stimulation is the canonical "high-complexity" application of this principle, integrating neuroanatomy, electrode–tissue interface physics, and psychophysics.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview: The "Broken TV Screen" Analogy

Imagine a modern flat-screen television where the **display panel is shattered** (damaged retina or optic nerve) but the **processing circuits, power supply, and speakers are perfectly intact** (visual cortex is healthy). You cannot fix the panel, but you have access to the back of the TV. If you could place tiny **pixels of light directly onto the back of the intact screen** in a controlled pattern, the viewer would still "see" a coherent image.

This is precisely what visual cortical stimulation does:

| Damaged Component | Biological Equivalent | Bypass Strategy |
|------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Camera (eye) | Retina / photoreceptors | Stimulate optic nerve |
| HDMI cable | Optic nerve | Stimulate LGN |
| Processor input | LGN relay | Stimulate V1 cortex |
| Display pixels | V1 neurons | Cortical microstimulation |

The **phosphene** is the "pixel" — a tiny spot of perceived light produced when a small population of cortical neurons fires synchronously in response to an injected current pulse. A **composite image** is generated by stimulating many such "pixels" in a spatial-temporal pattern that mimics the retinal image the patient can no longer see.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phosphene — The Atomic Unit of Artificial Vision:**
> First systematically described by **Wilder Penfield** (1950s) during intraoperative cortical mapping in epilepsy patients. A phosphene is any perception of light, shape, or color produced without photons actually entering the eye. Phosphenes can be elicited by mechanical pressure on the eye ("seeing stars"), transcranial magnetic stimulation, or — most reliably — direct electrical cortical stimulation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in Cortical Visual Stimulation

The following **standard engineering metrics** govern every visual cortical prosthesis design:

- **Typical threshold current (surface electrodes):** **1 – 5 mA**
- **Typical threshold current (intracortical microelectrodes):** **10 – 100 µA**
- **Standard pulse width:** **100 – 500 µs** (per phase)
- **Standard stimulation frequency:** **50 – 200 Hz**
- **Charge per phase (Q):** typically **0.1 – 1.0 µC**
- **Charge density limit (Shannon):** **≤ 100 µC/cm²** (geometric)
- **Cortical magnification factor (fovea):** ~**15 mm/degree**
- **Visual cortex area (V1, human):** ~**3000 mm²** per hemisphere
- **Number of electrodes in modern CVP:** **60 – 1500** (Utah array class)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reading Note for KTU Students:**
> The visual cortex is **NOT** a passive screen. It actively processes orientation, motion, color, and depth. Therefore, simply dumping pixels into V1 produces **only crude phosphene mosaics** — not natural vision. This is the central engineering challenge motivating research into **biomimetic stimulation paradigms** (temporal coding, current steering, biomimetic pulse trains).

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

Visual stimulation is the textbook example of a **sensory neural prosthesis** and tests the student's ability to integrate:

1. **Neuroanatomy** (V1, LGN, retinotopy)
2. **Electrode–tissue interface physics** (charge transfer, safety limits)
3. **Signal processing** (image-to-electrode mapping, resolution limits)
4. **Psychophysics** (phosphene brightness, color, size as a function of stimulus)

Examiners consistently test this topic with **14-mark analytical questions** that require derivations (Shannon limits, charge calculations) and **3-mark recall questions** on anatomy and definitions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Visual Pathway: A Step-by-Step Neural Cascade

The visual signal in a healthy subject travels through the following sequential stages. Each stage is a **candidate site** for prosthetic intervention:

**Step 1 — Phototransduction (Retina):**
Light photons strike the **rods** (scotopic, low-light) and **cones** (photopic, color) in the outer plexiform layer. Photoreceptors hyperpolarize and transmit graded potentials through bipolar cells to **retinal ganglion cells (RGCs)**. The RGC axons form the **optic nerve (CN II)**. Damage here → retinal prostheses (Argus II, Alpha-AMS).

**Step 2 — Optic Chiasm and Tract:**
At the **optic chiasm**, nasal retinal fibers decussate (cross); temporal fibers remain ipsilateral. This creates the **contralateral hemifield representation** that is preserved all the way to V1. The optic tract projects primarily to the **LGN of the thalamus**.

**Step 3 — Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN):**
A 6-layered thalamic relay. Layers 1, 4, 6 receive **contralateral eye** input (magnocellular from inner retina); layers 2, 3, 5 receive **ipsilateral eye** input. Each layer preserves retinotopic order. The LGN performs **gating, gain control, and attentional modulation** before cortical relay.

**Step 4 — Optic Radiation (Geniculocalcarine Tract):**
Meyer's loop (temporal lobe) carries superior visual field information; the **parietal bundle** carries inferior field info. Lesions here cause **homonymous quadrantanopias**.

**Step 5 — Primary Visual Cortex (V1, Brodmann Area 17, Striate Cortex):**
Located along the **calcarine sulcus** of the occipital lobe. V1 is the **first cortical site** of conscious visual processing and the **primary target** for cortical visual prostheses. Receives ~10⁸ fibers/day from LGN, contains ~2 × 10⁸ neurons, and exhibits:

- **Retinotopic map:** Adjacent retinal points → adjacent cortical columns
- **Ocular dominance columns:** Alternating bands preferring left/right eye input
- **Orientation columns:** Pinwheel-like organization (Hubel & Wiesel, Nobel 1981)
- **6 cytoarchitectonic layers:** Layer 4 (granular) is the main LGN input layer; layers 2/3 project to V2; layers 5/6 project back to LGN

**Step 6 — Extrastriate Cortex (V2, V3, V4, V5/MT):**
Ventral "what" stream (V4 → IT) handles object/color recognition; dorsal "where/how" stream (V5/MT → parietal) handles motion/spatial localization. **Current cortical prosthetics do not stimulate these areas** — they are not retinotopically organized as cleanly as V1.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Cortical Stimulation

The choice of **V1 cortex** as the prosthetic target — rather than retina or LGN — is driven by three engineering facts:

1. **Accessibility of V1 in blind patients:** Retinal diseases (RP, AMD) destroy the eye, but V1 remains viable. **Optic nerve damage** (glaucoma, trauma) also preserves V1.
2. **Retinotopic fidelity:** V1 preserves a near-perfect point-to-point map of the visual field. Stimulating cortical position (x, y) predictably produces a phosphene at visual field position (X, Y).
3. **Stable chronic stimulation:** Cortical surface electrodes and intracortical arrays (Utah, Michigan) can record/stimulate for years with minimal glial scarring compared to retinal tacks.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Sheet — Visual Cortical Stimulation (Print & Memorize):**
> All quantities required for KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions on this topic are consolidated below. Critical symbols and SI/clinical units are listed. The boundary of safe stimulation follows **Shannon's equation** (see Section 3 for derivation).

| Quantity | Symbol | Governing Formula | Standard Unit | Clinical Range / Limit |
|----------|--------|-------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| Charge per phase | $Q$ | $Q = I \cdot t_{pw}$ | µC | $0.05$ to $1.0$ µC |
| Pulse width | $t_{pw}$ | Stimulator-set | µs | $50$ to $500$ µs |
| Current amplitude | $I$ | Stimulator-set | µA | $10$ µA to $5$ mA |
| Current density | $J$ | $J = I / A$ | µA/mm² or A/m² | $\le 30$ mA/cm² safe |
| Charge density | $D$ | $D = Q / A$ | µC/cm² | $\le 100$ µC/cm² safe |
| Electrode area | $A$ | Geometry | cm² or mm² | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ cm² |
| Voltage across interface | $V$ | $V = I \cdot R$ | V | $\le \pm 10$ V typical |
| Energy per phase | $E$ | $E = V \cdot Q$ | µJ | $0.1$ to $5$ µJ |
| Power dissipation | $P$ | $P = I^{2} R$ | µW | bounded by $E \cdot f$ |
| Shannon safe-stimulation line | — | $\log(D) = k - \log(Q/A)$ | dimensionless | $k \approx 1.85$ |
| Phosphene size | $\theta_{ph}$ | $\theta_{ph} \approx f(I, R_{e}, d)$ | degrees of visual angle | $0.5°$ to $3°$ typical |
| Cortical magnification | $M$ | $M = d_{cortex}/d_{field}$ | mm/° | $15$ mm/° foveal, $1$ mm/° peripheral |
| Retinotopic position (V1) | $X_{V1}$ | $X_{V1} = M \cdot X_{field}$ | mm | linear near fovea |
| Stimulation frequency | $f$ | Stimulator-set | Hz | $50$ to $200$ Hz |
| Inter-phase delay | $t_{ipd}$ | Stimulator-set | µs | $\ge 50$ µs recommended |
| Repetition period | $T$ | $T = 1/f$ | ms | $5$ to $20$ ms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Safeguard — Avoiding Markdown Breakage:**
> In all KTU answer scripts and class notes, use `\vert` or `\mid` to denote absolute value, e.g., write $I \le \vert I_{th} \vert$ instead of $|I_{th}|$. This convention is mandated by the KTU 2024 latex typesetting guidelines.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework above is not academic — it directly drives commercial and clinical cortical prosthesis design:

- **Dobelle's second-generation implant (1978 – 2000s):** Used a **68-electrode surface array** on the medial occipital surface. Required **3.5 mA, 1 ms pulses** at 60 Hz. Patients reported **crude pixelated facial recognition** at 4 m of distance.
- **Second Sight / Pixium Vision (current):** Moving from retinal to **intracortical** approaches with the **Utah 100-electrode array**.
- **Neuralink & Synchron (indirect):** Pushing the boundaries of high-channel-count chronic cortical interfaces that will eventually enable 1000+ electrode visual prostheses.
- **BrainGate consortium (Univ. of Pittsburgh / Stanford):** Pioneering bidirectional cortical interfaces that combine stimulation with neural recording — the path toward **closed-loop visual prosthetics**.

The **KTU 2024 scheme** consistently tests whether students can connect these formulas to real devices, so always link an equation to its engineering consequence in viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Charge per Phase and the Choice of Biphasic Pulses

**Problem Context (KTU 2024 Typical 7-mark Sub-question):**
A cortical visual prosthesis delivers a rectangular current pulse of amplitude $I$ for a duration $t_{pw}$ to an electrode of geometric area $A$. Show that the **charge per phase** is $Q = I \cdot t_{pw}$, and explain why **charge-balanced biphasic** pulses are mandatory.

**Step 1 — Definition of charge:**
Electrical charge is the time-integral of current:

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{t_{pw}} I(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Assume a rectangular pulse (the standard in neuroprosthetics):**
For $0 \le t \le t_{pw}$, $I(t) = I$ (constant). Therefore:

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{t_{pw}} I \, dt = I \int_{0}^{t_{pw}} dt = I \cdot t_{pw}
$$

Thus:

$$
\boxed{Q = I \cdot t_{pw}}
$$

**Step 3 — Why biphasic is mandatory:**
In a monophasic pulse, net DC charge $Q_{DC} = Q$ flows across the electrode–tissue interface, leading to:
- **Electrode corrosion** (oxidation of Pt/Ir, dissolution of IrOₓ)
- **pH shift** at the interface (water electrolysis → H₂ / O₂ + OH⁻ / H⁺)
- **Irreversible Faradaic reactions** damaging neural tissue

To eliminate net DC, the second phase is identical in magnitude, opposite in polarity:

$$
Q_{cathodic} = I \cdot t_{pw}, \quad Q_{anodic} = -I \cdot t_{pw}, \quad Q_{net} = 0
$$

This is called a **charge-balanced biphasic pulse**. The cathodic phase triggers neural firing; the anodic phase reverses the electrochemical products.

**Step 4 — Engineering trade-off:**
The anodic phase is also capable of stimulating neurons. To prevent this, an **inter-phase delay** $t_{ipd}$ is introduced so that the anodic phase acts only on the (refractory) neurons just excited by the cathodic phase.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Current Density and Charge Density

**Step 1 — Define current density:**
Current density is the current normalized to the geometric surface area of the electrode:

$$
J = \frac{I}{A}
$$

Typical safe limit (KTU clinical standard): $J \le 30 \text{ mA/cm}^{2}$.

**Step 2 — Define charge density (per phase):**
Charge density is the charge per phase per unit geometric area:

$$
D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{I \cdot t_{pw}}{A} = J \cdot t_{pw}
$$

Typical safe limit: $D \le 100 \text{ µC/cm}^{2}$ (geometric).

**Step 3 — Application to cortical stimulation:**
For a Utah-array microelectrode, $A = 2000 \text{ µm}^{2} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}^{2}$. To inject $Q = 0.2 \text{ µC}$ per phase:

$$
D = \frac{0.2 \, \mu\text{C}}{2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{cm}^{2}} = 1 \times 10^{5} \, \mu\text{C/cm}^{2}
$$

This **exceeds** the safe limit by 1000×. Therefore, the actual injected charge is reduced to $Q \le 0.2 \text{ nC}$ to keep $D \le 100 \text{ µC/cm}^{2}$, requiring currents of order $I = Q/t_{pw} \approx 1 \text{ µA}$ for $t_{pw} = 200 \text{ µs}$ — the well-known regime of **intracortical microstimulation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shannon's Equation for Safe Stimulation Limits

**Historical Context (KTU High-Yield):**
Shannon (1992) compiled 100+ datasets of **histological tissue damage thresholds** in animal studies and discovered that damage depends on **both** charge per phase and charge density. The boundary of safe stimulation is given by the empirical line:

$$
\boxed{\log(D) = k - \log(Q/A)}
$$

where:
- $D$ = charge density in µC/cm² per phase
- $Q/A$ = charge per phase per unit area in µC/cm²
- $k$ = empirical constant; clinically $k = 1.85$ defines the safe-stim limit

**Step-by-step justification (rearranged form):**
Rearranging Shannon's equation to the form used clinically:

$$
\log(D) + \log(Q/A) = k
$$

Substituting the definitions:

$$
\log(D \cdot Q/A) = k
$$

Exponentiating both sides (base 10):

$$
D \cdot \frac{Q}{A} = 10^{k}
$$

For $k = 1.85$:

$$
D \cdot \frac{Q}{A} \le 10^{1.85} \approx 70.8
$$

i.e., the **product** of charge density and charge per phase per unit area must remain below ~70.8 (in the mixed µC/cm² unit system).

**Graphical interpretation:**
On a $\log(D)$ vs $\log(Q/A)$ plot, the safe-stimulation region lies **below** the line of slope $-1$ and intercept $k = 1.85$. Stimulation parameters must remain in the lower-left quadrant to avoid:

- **Electrochemical damage** (high $D$, small $Q/A$)
- **Neural injury from excitotoxic spread** (low $D$, high $Q/A$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition for KTU 2024 Numerical Problems:**
> If a problem specifies $Q$, $A$, and the requirement of safe operation, students must:
> 1. Compute $D = Q/A$.
> 2. Compute $\log(D) + \log(Q/A)$ and verify it is $\le 1.85$.
> 3. If exceeded, halve $I$ and re-check.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Phosphene Size from Cortical Spread Function

**Step 1 — Geometry of stimulation:**
Current $I$ injected at a cortical point spreads through tissue according to the volume conductor equation. The effective radius of neuronal activation is empirically:

$$
r_{act} \approx \sqrt{\frac{I}{I_{th}}} \cdot r_{0}
$$

where $r_{0}$ is the radius activated at threshold $I_{th}$.

**Step 2 — Convert to visual angle:**
Using cortical magnification $M$ (mm/°), the phosphene visual angle is:

$$
\theta_{ph} = \frac{2 r_{act}}{M}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example (typical cortical prosthesis):**
With $I = 2 I_{th}$, $r_{0} = 0.4 \text{ mm}$ (foveal V1), $M = 15 \text{ mm/°}$:

$$
r_{act} = \sqrt{2} \times 0.4 \approx 0.566 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\theta_{ph} = \frac{2 \times 0.566}{15} \approx 0.075 \text{ degrees} \approx 4.5 \text{ arcmin}
$$

This is roughly the **angular resolution of a normally-sighted fovea** — explaining why prosthetic phosphenes can, in principle, support reading-vision-level acuity if the electrode array is dense enough.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Stimulation Methods Comparison

| Method | Spatial Resolution | Temporal Resolution | Invasiveness | Best Use Case | Limitation |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|--------------|---------------|------------|
| **Surface cortical (epi-dural)** | ~1–2 mm (low) | Good (µs) | Moderate (craniotomy) | Dobelle-style CVP | Current spread, low resolution |
| **Intracortical (Utah array)** | ~100–400 µm | Excellent | High (penetrating) | High-acuity CVP (research) | Long-term stability, gliosis |
| **Intracortical (Michigan probe)** | ~50–200 µm | Excellent | High | Animal models | Single-shank; mechanical fragility |
| **Optogenetic (ChR2)** | Cellular | ms (limited by opsin) | Genetic + optical | Research only | Requires gene therapy |
| **TMS (transcranial magnetic)** | ~1 cm | Poor (single pulse) | Non-invasive | Diagnostic, mapping | Cannot elicit useful vision |
| **tDCS (transcranial DC)** | Diffuse | Poor | Non-invasive | None | Sub-threshold for phosphenes |
| **Sensory substitution (tongue, skin)** | Tactile-pixel mapped | 50–200 Hz | Non-invasive | Commercial (BrainPort) | No true "vision"; learning curve |
| **Retinal (epiretinal/sub-retinal)** | High (native) | Good | Moderate (intraocular) | Argus II, Alpha-AMS | Requires viable retina |
| **LGN stimulation (deep brain)** | ~1 mm | Good | Very high (DBS-style) | Experimental | Surgical risk; deep target |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-link to Module 1 Outcomes:**
> This matrix is the direct answer to a typical KTU question: *"Compare different methods of stimulating the visual cortex with respect to invasiveness, resolution, and clinical viability."* Memorize at least 4 rows for the 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

Note that **P4 and P5 (V1 stimulation) bypass the maximum number of damaged stages**, making them the most general-purpose approach for end-stage blindness.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Functional Block Architecture of a Cortical Visual Prosthesis

flowchart LR

    FB -.-> ENC

    TEL[Telemetry Bidirectional] --> DRV

> **Block-Level Reading:**
> - **Yellow** = external sensor
> - **Blue** = digital signal processing
> - **Green** = analog stimulation front-end
> - **Red border (heavy)** = implanted neural interface (the critical safety boundary)
> - **Purple** = perceptual/behavioral loop
> - Dotted lines = feedback channels (closed-loop control)

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Biphasic Charge-Balanced Pulse Waveform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph pulseTrain["Biphasic Charge Balanced Pulse Train"]
        direction LR
        P1[Rest Period 0 to t1] --> P2[Cathodic Phase 0 to tpw 0 to 200 uA]
        P2 --> P3[Inter Phase Delay 100 us]
        P3 --> P4[Anodic Phase 0 to tpw 0 to 200 uA opposite polarity]
        P4 --> P5[Rest Period to next pulse 1 over f seconds]
    end
    P5 -.next pulse.-> P1

    style P1 fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#666
    style P2 fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#CC0000
    style P3 fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#996600
    style P4 fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#003366
    style P5 fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#666
```

> **Engineering Reading:** The red cathodic phase **triggers neuronal firing**; the inter-phase delay (yellow) allows the membrane to repolarize; the blue anodic phase **reverses electrochemical products** for charge balance. Pulse repetition period $T = 1/f$ is set by the desired frame rate.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions are 3-mark, 60-second-to-2-minute questions. They test **recall and direct conceptual understanding** (Bloom Level: Remember / Understand). Answer in **3-4 short sentences** with one labeled diagram wherever asked.

---

### Question 1: Define Phosphene. Why is it called the building block of artificial vision?

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **phosphene** is a perception of light experienced by a subject in the absence of actual photons entering the eye, produced by direct electrical, magnetic, or mechanical activation of neurons along the visual pathway.

It is called the **building block of artificial vision** because every complex visual scene produced by a cortical or retinal prosthesis is composed of multiple spatially organized phosphenes — analogous to pixels forming an image on a display. A 100-electrode cortical array can therefore produce up to 100 distinct phosphene "pixels," and a well-timed, spatially patterned stimulation sequence reconstructs shapes, letters, and motion cues from these atomic percepts.

Phosphenes were first systematically mapped in humans by **Wilder Penfield (1950s)** during open cortical stimulation in epilepsy patients, providing the first evidence that focal cortical stimulation could reliably elicit discrete visual percepts.

---

### Question 2: What is Retinotopic Mapping? State its significance in V1 cortical stimulation.

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Retinotopic mapping** is the systematic, point-to-point spatial correspondence between the retina of the eye and the neural tissue of the visual cortex (V1), such that adjacent points on the retina activate adjacent points on the cortical surface.

Its **significance** in V1 cortical stimulation is threefold:

1. **Predictable phosphene location:** Because each cortical coordinate corresponds to a specific visual field location, the surgeon can pre-map electrode positions to expected phosphene positions in the patient's visual field.
2. **Image transformation:** External camera input is mathematically transformed (via the inverse retinotopic map) into a stimulation pattern that places each electrode's phosphene at the correct location to reproduce the original image.
3. **Surgical targeting:** Pre-operative fMRI retinotopic mapping allows accurate placement of the electrode array on the calcarine sulcus, maximizing coverage of the central (foveal) visual field where resolution is highest.

The **cortical magnification factor** $M = d_{cortex}/d_{field}$ quantifies this mapping and is highest in the foveal representation (~15 mm/°) and lowest in the periphery (~1 mm/°).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions are 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The examiner expects: (a) labeled diagrams, (b) formulas with units, (c) clinical examples, and (d) comparative analysis. Aim for 8-10 pages of structured answer with 1-2 figures.

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 1 Long Answer, Choice Option A]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Trace the visual pathway from the retina to the primary visual cortex. With a labeled diagram, identify each major relay station and discuss the role of the LGN.**

**Model Answer:**

The human visual pathway is a multi-stage cascade beginning in the retina and terminating in the occipital cortex. Each relay station progressively transforms the signal, extracting features such as contrast, motion, and orientation.

**Step 1 — Retinal Encoding:**
Light is transduced by **rods** (scotopic, low-light) and **cones** (photopic, color: S, M, L) into graded electrical potentials. Bipolar cells relay these signals; **retinal ganglion cells (RGCs)** — the output neurons of the retina — fire action potentials whose axons form the **optic nerve (cranial nerve II)**. There are ~1.2 million RGC axons per eye.

**Step 2 — Optic Chiasm and Tract:**
At the optic chiasm, the **nasal retinal fibers** decussate to the contralateral side, while **temporal fibers** remain ipsilateral. This crossover ensures that the **left visual hemifield** is represented in the **right cerebral hemisphere**, and vice versa. The resulting fiber bundles are the **optic tracts**.

**Step 3 — Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN):**
The LGN is a six-layered thalamic nucleus that serves as the obligatory relay between the retina and V1. Its six layers are organized as follows:
- **Magnocellular layers 1, 2:** Receive input from parasol RGCs (large receptive fields, high temporal resolution, motion/detection).
- **Parvocellular layers 3, 4, 5, 6:** Receive input from midget RGCs (small receptive fields, high spatial resolution, color).
- Layers 1, 4, 6 receive **contralateral eye** input; layers 2, 3, 5 receive **ipsilateral eye** input.

**Role of the LGN:**
1. **Gain control and gating** — modulates signal strength based on arousal and attention (feedback from V1, layer 6).
2. **Retinotopic preservation** — adjacent retinal points remain adjacent in LGN, preserving spatial order.
3. **Eye-of-origin segregation** — separate layers for each eye, providing the anatomical basis of binocular vision.
4. **Selective filtering** — non-visual inputs from brainstem (reticular formation) suppress LGN transmission during sleep, enabling thalamocortical synchronization.

**Step 4 — Optic Radiation to V1:**
LGN axons form the **optic radiation (geniculocalcarine tract)**, which projects through two bundles:
- **Meyer's loop (temporal lobe):** Carries **superior visual field** information.
- **Parietal bundle:** Carries **inferior visual field** information.

These fibers terminate in **layer 4 (granular layer)** of the **primary visual cortex (V1, Brodmann area 17)** located in the calcarine sulcus of the occipital lobe.

*(Insert labeled diagram: Retina → Optic nerve → Chiasm → Tract → LGN → Optic radiation → V1 → V2 → V3/V4/V5.)*

**Step 5 — Beyond V1:** V1 projects to **V2 (secondary visual cortex)**, then bifurcates into the **ventral "what" stream (V4 → inferotemporal cortex)** for object recognition and the **dorsal "where/how" stream (V5/MT → parietal cortex)** for motion and spatial localization.

[7 marks: 2 marks retinal encoding, 2 marks LGN role, 2 marks tract and cortical projection, 1 mark for diagram]

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the various methods used to stimulate the visual cortex. Compare their advantages and limitations with respect to spatial resolution, invasiveness, and clinical viability.**

**Model Answer:**

Visual cortex stimulation methods are categorized as **invasive** (requiring surgical implantation) or **non-invasive** (external energy delivery). Each method has a distinct trade-off between resolution, depth of access, and clinical risk.

**1. Electrical Cortical Surface Stimulation (Epi-dural / Sub-dural):**
- **Mechanism:** Disk electrodes placed on the cortical surface deliver charge-balanced biphasic current pulses.
- **Spatial resolution:** ~1–2 mm (limited by current spread through CSF and pia).
- **Invasiveness:** Moderate — requires craniotomy.
- **Clinical example:** **Dobelle's second-generation implant** (1978) used a 68-electrode epi-dural array; patients recognized faces and read large letters.
- **Advantage:** Lower surgical risk than penetrating arrays; long-term stability.
- **Limitation:** Low resolution; large currents required (mA range) → risk of seizures.

**2. Intracortical Microstimulation (ICMS):**
- **Mechanism:** Penetrating microelectrode arrays (Utah, Michigan) deliver currents directly into cortical layers 2/3 or 4.
- **Spatial resolution:** ~100–400 µm per electrode tip.
- **Invasiveness:** High — penetrates pia; chronic glial response.
- **Clinical example:** **Utah 100-array** (used in BrainGate, now adapted for CVP research).
- **Advantage:** Phosphene threshold 10–100× lower (µA range); higher resolution.
- **Limitation:** Long-term electrode degradation, gliosis, micromotion at the brain–skull interface.

**3. Optogenetic Stimulation:**
- **Mechanism:** Genetically introduced **channelrhodopsin-2 (ChR2)** in cortical neurons enables light-evoked firing.
- **Spatial resolution:** Single-cell level (with focused light or two-photon).
- **Invasiveness:** Requires **viral gene therapy** + optical fiber implant.
- **Advantage:** Cell-type-specific targeting; no electrical artifact.
- **Limitation:** Currently **research-only**; regulatory hurdles; opsin kinetics limit temporal resolution to ms.

**4. Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS):**
- **Mechanism:** Pulsed magnetic field induces eddy currents in cortex.
- **Spatial resolution:** ~1–2 cm.
- **Invasiveness:** Non-invasive.
- **Advantage:** Useful for diagnostic mapping and proof-of-concept phosphene studies.
- **Limitation:** Cannot produce patterned vision; no chronic use; coil heating.

**5. Sensory Substitution Devices (e.g., BrainPort tongue display):**
- **Mechanism:** Camera image → electrotactile tongue array → brain interprets pattern as vision.
- **Invasiveness:** Non-invasive (oral device).
- **Advantage:** Commercially available; FDA cleared.
- **Limitation:** Crude "tongue vision" with extensive training; not direct cortical stimulation.

**6. Retinal Prosthesis (Argus II, Alpha-AMS, PRIMA):**
- **Mechanism:** Stimulates RGCs or bipolar cells via epi-retinal or sub-retinal electrodes.
- **Spatial resolution:** Native retinal resolution potential (~20/200 in best cases).
- **Invasiveness:** Intraocular surgery.
- **Advantage:** Leverages intact optic nerve and cortex; clinical track record.
- **Limitation:** Requires **viable retinal architecture**; cannot help end-stage RP/AMD with dead RGCs.

**7. LGN Deep Brain Stimulation:**
- **Mechanism:** DBS electrode in LGN thalamic relay.
- **Spatial resolution:** ~1 mm.
- **Invasiveness:** Very high (deep brain targeting).
- **Advantage:** Bypasses both retina and optic nerve.
- **Limitation:** Surgical risk; not retinotopically organized as cleanly as V1.

[7 marks: 1 mark per method (description), 0.5 mark for example, 0.5 mark for advantage/limitation; 1 mark for comparative summary]

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 1 Long Answer, Choice Option B]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the cytoarchitectural organization of the primary visual cortex (V1). With a labeled diagram, describe the six cortical layers and their functional roles. Discuss the concept of ocular dominance and orientation columns.**

**Model Answer:**

The primary visual cortex (V1) is a 2 mm-thick sheet of neural tissue containing ~2 × 10⁸ neurons, organized into six distinct horizontal layers and vertical columnar modules. Its architecture was elucidated by **Hubel and Wiesel (Nobel Prize, 1981)**.

**The Six Layers of V1 (Laminar Organization):**

| Layer | Name | Main Cell Types | Functional Role |
|-------|------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **1** | Molecular (plexiform) | Few neurons, dense dendrites and axons | Receives feedback from higher visual areas; horizontal connections |
| **2/3** | External granular & pyramidal | Pyramidal neurons | **Output to V2** and other extrastriate areas; intracortical horizontal processing |
| **4** | Internal granular | Spiny stellate cells | **Main input layer** from LGN; receives the bulk of geniculocalcarine fibers |
| **4Cα** | Sublayer of 4 | Spiny stellate | Receives **magnocellular** input (motion, low-contrast) |
| **4Cβ** | Sublayer of 4 | Spiny stellate | Receives **parvocellular** input (color, high spatial detail) |
| **5** | Internal pyramidal | Large pyramidal neurons | Projects to **superior colliculus** and pulvinar; subcortical output |
| **6** | Multiform | Pyramidal & fusiform | Projects **back to LGN** (feedback control) |

**Functional Information Flow:**
$$\text{LGN} \rightarrow \text{Layer 4C} \rightarrow \text{Layers 2/3} \rightarrow \text{Layers 2/3 output to V2}$$
$$\text{Layer 5} \rightarrow \text{Subcortical (superior colliculus)}$$
$$\text{Layer 6} \rightarrow \text{Back to LGN}$$

**Vertical Columnar Organization (Hubel & Wiesel):**

1. **Ocular Dominance Columns:**
   - Alternating ~0.5 mm wide bands of cortex preferring input from the **left eye** vs the **right eye**.
   - Generated by LGN afferents from the two eyes terminating in **interleaved stripes** in layer 4C.
   - Visible with cytochrome oxidase staining or transneuronal tracers.
   - **Significance for prosthetics:** Each electrode samples the **monocular** input of one eye only; binocular fusion is not possible with current V1 stimulation.

2. **Orientation Columns:**
   - Pinwheel-like organization where neurons in a vertical column respond to the **same preferred orientation** of a visual edge.
   - Adjacent columns shift orientation by ~10° as one moves across the cortex.
   - Within a column, simple cells (layer 4) and complex cells (layers 2/3) build the orientation selectivity hierarchy.

3. **Retinotopic Columns:**
   - Each column represents a specific **retinal position**; adjacent columns represent adjacent retinal positions.
   - This is the basis of the **retinotopic map** that allows prosthetic electrodes to evoke spatially localized phosphenes.

*(Insert diagram: cross-section of V1 with 6 layers labeled, plus a top-down view of ocular dominance stripes and orientation pinwheels.)*

[7 marks: 3 marks for layers and function, 2 marks for columns, 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for flow of information]

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the key parameters of electrical stimulation for cortical visual prosthetics. Derive the Shannon equation for safe stimulation limits, and explain how it constrains electrode design.**

**Model Answer:**

Electrical stimulation of cortical tissue is governed by a small set of **primary parameters** and a critical **safety constraint** (Shannon's equation). Engineers must satisfy both the **efficacy condition** (reliably elicit a phosphene) and the **safety condition** (no tissue damage, no electrochemical corrosion).

**Key Stimulation Parameters:**

1. **Current amplitude ($I$):** Typically 10 µA – 5 mA depending on electrode type. Higher $I$ → larger phosphene, more recruited neurons.
2. **Pulse width ($t_{pw}$):** Typically 50 – 500 µs. Shorter pulses require higher current (strength-duration relationship).
3. **Frequency ($f$):** Typically 50 – 200 Hz. Below ~50 Hz, phosphenes appear flickering; above 200 Hz, they fuse.
4. **Waveform:** Cathodic-first **charge-balanced biphasic** is mandatory to avoid DC accumulation.
5. **Inter-phase delay:** $\ge 50$ µs prevents the anodic phase from re-exciting neurons.
6. **Electrode geometry:** Size $A$ determines charge density $D = Q/A$.

**Strength-Duration Relationship (Weiss-Lapique):**
The threshold current for neural activation follows:
$$I_{th} = I_{rheobase} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\tau_{chronaxie}}{t_{pw}}\right)$$
where $\tau_{chronaxie}$ is the membrane time constant (typically 100–300 µs for cortical pyramidal neurons). **Chronaxie** is the pulse width at which threshold is twice the rheobase, and is the optimal pulse width for energy-efficient stimulation.

**Derivation of Shannon's Equation:**

Shannon (1992) compiled >100 published datasets of histological damage thresholds in animal stimulation experiments. He plotted the **charge density $D$ (µC/cm²)** on the y-axis against the **charge per phase per unit area $Q/A$ (µC/cm²)** on the x-axis, both in log scale. The damage boundary was found to follow a straight line of slope $-1$:

**Step 1 — Empirical relationship:**
$$D = 10^{k} \cdot \left(\frac{Q}{A}\right)^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides:**
$$\log(D) = k - \log\left(\frac{Q}{A}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to the canonical form:**
$$\boxed{\log(D) + \log\left(\frac{Q}{A}\right) = k}$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate:**
$$D \cdot \frac{Q}{A} = 10^{k}$$

For the empirically determined constant $k \approx 1.85$, the safe-stimulation inequality is:
$$D \cdot \frac{Q}{A} \le 70.8 \quad \text{(in µC/cm² units)}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**
- The two factors $D$ and $Q/A$ represent **two distinct damage mechanisms**:
  - High $D$ at small $Q/A$ → **electrochemical damage** (electrode corrosion, gas evolution, pH shift at interface).
  - High $Q/A$ at small $D$ → **neural injury** from excitotoxic spread, prolonged depolarization, and glutamate release.
- Safe stimulation must remain in the **lower-left quadrant** of the $\log D$ vs $\log(Q/A)$ plot, below the line of slope $-1$.

**Step 6 — Application to electrode design:**
A Utah-array microelectrode has $A = 2000 \text{ µm}^{2} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}^{2}$. To stay safe:
$$D \le 100 \text{ µC/cm}^{2} \Rightarrow Q \le 100 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 0.2 \text{ nC per phase}$$
With $t_{pw} = 200 \text{ µs}$:
$$I_{max} = \frac{Q}{t_{pw}} = \frac{0.2 \text{ nC}}{200 \text{ µs}} = 1 \text{ µA}$$
This explains why **intracortical microstimulation operates in the µA range** — well below the safe Shannon boundary.

For surface electrodes ($A = 1 \text{ mm}^{2} = 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^{2}$):
$$Q_{max} = 100 \times 10^{-2} = 1 \text{ µC}, \quad I_{max} = \frac{1 \text{ µC}}{200 \text{ µs}} = 5 \text{ mA}$$
This matches the **mA-range operation** of Dobelle's epi-dural array.

**Conclusion:** Shannon's equation provides a **quantitative design constraint** linking electrode geometry to permissible stimulation current. Every cortical prosthesis design begins with this calculation.

[7 marks: 2 marks for parameter list, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for design application]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing charge per phase $Q$ with charge density $D$** — these are not the same; $Q$ is in µC, $D$ is in µC/cm². Examiners deduct 1 mark if units are wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting to log-transform** Shannon's equation — writing $D = k - Q/A$ is a fatal error; the equation is in log-log space.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram** — KTU Part B answers without a labeled diagram lose **at least 1 mark**, often more.
> 4. **Using monophasic pulses** in a design example — always justify the choice of charge-balanced biphasic.
> 5. **Confusing ocular dominance columns with orientation columns** — these are orthogonal organizations (eye-specific vs orientation-specific).
> 6. **Omitting the LGN layers and their magnocellular/parvocellular split** — examiners specifically test this.
> 7. **Treating the visual cortex as a passive display** — always mention that V1 actively processes orientation, motion, and depth; phosphenes are crude percepts, not images.
> 8. **Incorrectly citing author names or invented studies** — stick to **Hubel & Wiesel**, **Penfield**, **Dobelle**, and **Shannon (1992)** as these are the canonical references.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Block — Read Twice Before Exam:**

- [x] **Visual Stimulation in the Brain** = artificial activation of neurons along the visual pathway (retina, optic nerve, LGN, or V1) to produce **phosphenes** in blind subjects.
- [x] **Phosphene** = perception of light without photons; the atomic unit of artificial vision; first mapped by Penfield (1950s).
- [x] **Visual pathway order:** Retina → Optic nerve → Chiasm → Tract → **LGN (6 layers)** → Optic radiation → **V1 (6 cortical layers)** → V2 → V4/V5.
- [x] **LGN layers:** Magnocellular 1, 2; Parvocellular 3, 4, 5, 6. Layers 1, 4, 6 = contralateral eye; layers 2, 3, 5 = ipsilateral eye.
- [x] **V1 layers (Hubel & Wiesel):** Layer 4 = main LGN input; Layers 2/3 = output to V2; Layer 5 = subcortical output; Layer 6 = feedback to LGN.
- [x] **Columns in V1:** Ocular dominance columns (eye-specific) + orientation columns (edge-specific) + retinotopic columns (position-specific).
- [x] **Retinotopic map** = point-to-point retina-to-cortex correspondence. **Cortical magnification factor** ~15 mm/° foveal, ~1 mm/° peripheral.
- [x] **Charge per phase:** $Q = I \cdot t_{pw}$.
- [x] **Charge density:** $D = Q/A$.
- [x] **Current density:** $J = I/A$.
- [x] **Charge-balanced biphasic pulse** is mandatory for safe chronic stimulation.
- [x] **Shannon's equation:** $\log(D) = k - \log(Q/A)$, with $k = 1.85$ for safe operation.
- [x] **Typical values:** Surface electrodes 1–5 mA / 200 µs; Intracortical microstimulation 1–100 µA / 200 µs.
- [x] **Safe charge density:** $D \le 100$ µC/cm² (geometric, per phase).
- [x] **Cortical visual prosthesis examples:** Dobelle (epi-dural), Utah array (intracortical), Argus II (retinal — for context), BrainPort (sensory substitution).
- [x] **Methods compared:** Surface electrical, intracortical electrical, optogenetic, TMS, sensory substitution, retinal, LGN-DBS.
- [x] **Strength-duration:** $I_{th} = I_{rheobase} \cdot (1 + \tau_{ch}/t_{pw})$.
- [x] **V1 is not a passive display** — it processes orientation, motion, color, depth. Crude phosphenes do not equal natural vision.
- [x] **Closed-loop / biomimetic stimulation** is the future direction — record + stimulate simultaneously to mimic natural retinal code.
- [x] **Examiner hot-button issues:** (1) Unit errors on $Q$ vs $D$, (2) log-log form of Shannon's equation, (3) missing diagram in Part B, (4) confusion of LGN and V1 layer numbering.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optic Nerve Stimulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Optic Nerve Stimulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Optic Nerve Stimulation** is a *visual neuroprosthetic technique* that bypasses the damaged photoreceptor layer of the retina and directly depolarises the axons of retinal ganglion cells (RGCs) bundled within the optic nerve (CN II), thereby evoking focal patterns of phosphenes (artificial percepts of light) in the visual cortex of profoundly blind subjects.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it falls under the classification of a **sensory neural prosthesis** whose stimulating interface lies **distal to the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN)** but **proximal to the retinal photoreceptors**, placing it between retinal implants (epiretinal/subretinal) and cortical implants in the hierarchy of visual restoration strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEBMT743 / Module 1):**
> Optic nerve stimulation is positioned in Module 1 as a foundational topic that introduces the student to the principles of *peripheral neural interfaces*, *excitation thresholds of myelinated axons*, and the comparative anatomy of sensory prosthetics — concepts that recur throughout Modules 2–5.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine the visual pathway as a **long electrical cable** running from your eye (the camera) to your brain (the TV screen).

- In a normal eye, the **camera (photoreceptors)** records the image and sends electrical signals along the **cable (optic nerve)** to the **TV (visual cortex)**.
- In diseases such as **Retinitis Pigmentosa (RP)** or **Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD)**, the camera is broken, but the cable and TV are usually still perfectly fine.
- An **optic nerve stimulator** is like a small *signal injector* that is clamped **onto the cable** itself. It does not try to fix the broken camera; instead, it **writes fake electrical messages directly onto the cable**, fooling the TV into displaying pinpoints of light (phosphenes).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Clinical Constraint:** Optic nerve stimulation is only viable when the ganglion cell axons are still alive — i.e., the blindness must be of *photoreceptor origin*. Diseases of the optic nerve itself (e.g., glaucoma-induced total axonal loss) cannot be treated this way.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Number of optic nerve fibres per human eye:** ≈ **1.2 × 10⁶ axons**
- **Optic nerve length:** ≈ **35 – 55 mm**
- **Nerve diameter at stimulation site (intraorbital):** ≈ **3 – 4 mm**
- **Typical safe charge per phase (Q):** < **0.4 µC/phase**
- **Typical pulse width (PW):** **100 – 500 µs**
- **Phosphene size elicited:** ≈ **2° – 10° of visual angle** (significantly larger than retinal-implant phosphenes)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomical & Physiological Basis

The optic nerve is a **white-matter tract of the central nervous system (CNS)** wrapped in meninges (dura, arachnoid, pia). For the KTU examiner, the following layered architecture is **high-yield**:

1. **Retinal Ganglion Cell (RGC) axons** — the true stimulation targets.
2. **Myelin sheaths** (oligodendrocyte-derived, since it is a CNS tract).
3. **Glial matrix** containing astrocytes.
4. **Connective tissue septa** dividing the nerve into ≈ **600 – 1000 fascicles**.
5. **Pia mater** — innermost vascular layer.
6. **Subarachnoid space** (CSF).
7. **Arachnoid mater.**
8. **Dura mater** — outermost protective sheath.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Neural Excitation

Direct electrical stimulation of a myelinated axon causes:

1. **Local hyperpolarisation** under the cathode and **anodal depolarisation** beneath the anode (for extracellular fields).
2. For **cuff / longitudinal electrodes**, action potentials are initiated at the **cathodic end** of the active contact where the second spatial derivative of the extracellular potential is positive (depolarising).
3. The evoked spike travels **orthodromically** towards the LGN and cortex (producing phosphene) **and** antidromically back to the retina (clinically silent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Recruitment Logic:** Larger diameter axons (parasol / magnocellular RGCs) have *lower excitation thresholds* than thinner midget (parvocellular) axons. Therefore, low-amplitude currents preferentially activate peripheral vision channels — a fundamental limitation for *form-vision* restoration.

### 2.3 Electrode Architectures Used in Optic Nerve Stimulation

| Electrode Type | Configuration | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Cuff electrode** (e.g., Brindley, Veraart spiral) | Wraps circumferentially around the nerve | Non-penetrating; safer; broad, low-resolution fields |
| **Intrafascicular electrode** (TIME, SELINE) | Penetrates into individual fascicles | Higher selectivity; risk of mechanical damage |
| **Penetrating microelectrode array** | Microneedles across nerve cross-section | Maximum selectivity; high surgical risk |
| **Longitudinal intrafascicular electrode (LIFE)** | Travels along fascicle axis | Stable chronic recordings / stimulation |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use **`\vert`** instead of the pipe symbol `\|` inside the following tables to avoid breaking markdown.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Charge per phase | $Q = I \cdot PW$ | µC / phase |
| 2 | Charge density | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A_{elec}}$ | µC / cm² |
| 3 | Current density | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_{elec}}$ | A / cm² |
| 4 | Weiss strength–duration | $I(t) = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$ | A |
| 5 | Lapicque / Blair approximation | $I = I_{rh}\!\left(1+\dfrac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)$ | A |
| 6 | Rheobase ($I_{rh}$) | Minimum current for *any* pulse duration | A |
| 7 | Chronaxie ($\tau_{ch}$) | Pulse duration at $2I_{rh}$ | s (typ. 100 – 300 µs) |
| 8 | Charge threshold (short pulse) | $Q_{th} = I_{rh} \cdot \tau_{ch}$ | µC |
| 9 | Shannon safety index | $k = \log_{10}\!D + \log_{10}\!Q$ | dimensionless; $k < 1.85$ safe |
| 10 | Phosphene visual angle | $\theta_{ph} \propto \dfrac{1}{N_{fibre}}$ | degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The Weiss equation and chronaxie measurement are used *daily* by cochlear-implant and deep-brain-stimulator engineers to set energy-efficient pulse parameters — the same physics governs the optic-nerve interface.

### 2.5 Comparative Positioning of Visual Prostheses

| Feature | Retinal Implant | Optic Nerve | LGN / Thalamic | Cortical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Surgical risk | Low | Medium | High | Very high |
| Spatial resolution | High | Low–Medium | Medium | High |
| Patient pool (RP/AMD) | Large | Medium | Small | All |
| Electrode count | 60–1500 | 4–50 | <100 | >1000 |
| Phosphene stability | Good | Variable | Variable | Variable |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Weiss Strength–Duration Equation

We model the axon membrane as a parallel **R–C circuit** with membrane resistance $R_m$ and capacitance $C_m$. The membrane time constant is

$$\tau_m = R_m C_m$$

**Step 1 — Capacitor charging equation.**
Apply a rectangular current pulse of amplitude $I$ and duration $t$. The voltage across the membrane follows a first-order charging law:

$$V_m(t) = I R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Threshold condition.**
An action potential is fired when $V_m$ reaches the threshold $V_{th}$:

$$V_{th} = I R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Define the rheobase.**
The rheobase $I_{rh}$ is the *minimum* current that can ever excite the axon. Mathematically it is the limit of $I$ as $t \to \infty$, which forces the exponential to vanish:

$$V_{th} = I_{rh} R_m \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I_{rh} = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_m}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I_{rh}$ back into the threshold equation.**

$$I R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right) = I_{rh} R_m$$

Cancelling $R_m$ (non-zero) and rearranging:

$$I = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$$

This is the **Weiss equation**. It states that to fire an axon in time $t$, the current must *increase hyperbolically* as the pulse shortens — the foundation for energy-efficient neural-stimulator design.

> **Engineering takeaway:** Choosing a pulse width equal to the chronaxie $\tau_{ch}$ minimises the total charge injected per pulse.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Chronaxie and Lapicque Approximation

**Step 1 — Apply the chronaxie definition** ($\tau_{ch}$ is the pulse duration at $I = 2 I_{rh}$):

$$2 I_{rh} = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-\tau_{ch}/\tau_m}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\tau_{ch}$.**

$$2 = \dfrac{1}{1 - e^{-\tau_{ch}/\tau_m}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 1 - e^{-\tau_{ch}/\tau_m} = \tfrac{1}{2}$$

$$e^{-\tau_{ch}/\tau_m} = \tfrac{1}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tau_{ch} = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693\,\tau_m$$

So chronaxie is **≈ 69 %** of the membrane time constant. For mammalian myelinated RGC axons, $\tau_{ch}$ lies in the **100 – 300 µs** range.

**Step 3 — Lapicque linear form.**
Expand the Weiss denominator using the identity $1 - e^{-x} \approx \dfrac{x}{1 + x}$ for small $x$:

$$I \approx I_{rh}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_m}{t}\right) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad I = I_{rh}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)$$

This linear form is what appears on strength–duration graphs in KTU answer sheets and is the basis for reading off $I_{rh}$ (intercept) and $\tau_{ch}$ (slope) experimentally.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon Safety Index for Optic-Nerve Stimulation

Electrical stimulation can damage neural tissue via two mechanisms:
- **Charge per phase (Q):** Too much charge → electrochemical reaction at electrode.
- **Charge density (D):** Too high a concentration → excitotoxic heating.

Shannon (1992) combined both into a single logarithmic safety index:

$$k = \log_{10}\!\left(D\right) + \log_{10}\!\left(Q\right)$$

**Step 1 — Compute charge per phase.**

$$Q = I \cdot PW$$

*Example:* $I = 200\,\mu\text{A}$, $PW = 200\,\mu\text{s}$

$$Q = 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{C} = 0.04\,\mu\text{C/phase}$$

**Step 2 — Compute charge density.**
With a $0.5\,\text{mm}^2$ electrode ($A = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{cm}^2$):

$$D = \dfrac{0.04}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 8\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Shannon index.**

$$k = \log_{10}(8) + \log_{10}(0.04) = 0.903 + (-1.398) = -0.495$$

**Step 4 — Verify safety.** The empirical safe limit for platinum-iridium microelectrodes is $k < 1.85$. Since $-0.495 < 1.85$, this stimulation regime is **safe** for chronic use.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Factors for Phosphene Resolution

| Limiting Factor | Physical Origin | Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Fascicular co-activation** | Current spreads across many fibres | Intrafascicular electrodes |
| **Giant-fibre bias** | Lower threshold of large axons | Anodic block to equalise recruitment |
| **Pial CSF shunt** | Low-impedance CSF bypasses nerve | Tripolar / quasitripolar cuffs |
| **Glial encapsulation** | Foreign-body response raises threshold | Hydrogel coatings, anti-fibrotic drugs |
| **Cuff–nerve micromotion** | Mechanical instability | Suture anchoring, polymer tethers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Optic Nerve Stimulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CamMod["Camera Module"] --> VPU["Wearable Video Processing Unit VPU"]
    VPU --> ENC["RF Telemetry Encoder"]
    ENC -.Wireless Link.-> DEC["Implanted Receiver Decoder"]
    DEC --> MUX["Channel Multiplexer MUX"]
    MUX --> CUFF["Cuff or Intrafascicular Electrode Array"]
    CUFF --> OPTIC["Optic Nerve CN II"]
    OPTIC --> LGN["LGN Thalamus"]
    LGN --> V1["Primary Visual Cortex V1"]
    V1 --> PHOS["Phosphene Perception"]

    PWR["Lithium Ion Battery"] --> VPU
    COIL["Inductive Power Coil"] --> DEC

    STY1["Style note Electrode"]:::highlight
    STY2["Style note Neural Tissue"]:::target
    classDef highlight fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc9a06,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Recruitment vs Pulse-Width — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Patient enrolled and implanted"] --> Check1["Is optic nerve intact on MRI"]
    Check1 -- No --> Stop["Not a candidate"]
    Check1 -- Yes --> Threshold["Find perceptual threshold I rh"]
    Threshold --> Measure["Measure chronaxie tau ch"]
    Measure --> Set["Set PW approximately tau ch"]
    Set --> Ramp["Ramp current until phosphene reported"]
    Ramp --> Qsafe["Compute Shannon k index"]
    Qsafe -- k greater than 1.85 --> Warn["Reduce PW or current"]
    Qsafe -- k less than 1.85 --> Ok["Stable chronic stimulation"]
    Warn --> Set
```

### 4.3 Cuff Electrode — Cross-Sectional Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ANO["Anode Contact 1"] --- NERVE["Optic Nerve Cross Section 3 mm"]
    NERVE --- CAT["Cathode Contact 2"]
    CSF["CSF Layer"]:::csf --- NERVE
    EPI["Epineurium"]:::epi --- NERVE
    FAS["Fascicles inside nerve"]:::fas --- NERVE

    classDef csf fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    classDef epi fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#b45f06,color:#000
    classDef fas fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#5b2a86,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The cuff anode–cathode–anode (tripolar) arrangement forces current to traverse the nerve body, while CSF (low impedance) shunts current away from the fascicles — the dominant *engineering trade-off* that limits selectivity of cuff designs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define phosphene and explain its relevance in optic nerve stimulation.**

**Model Answer:**
A *phosphene* is a sensation of light perceived in the absence of external luminous input, elicited by direct electrical stimulation of any part of the visual pathway (retina, optic nerve, LGN, or cortex). In optic nerve stimulation, phosphenes are the *primary perceptual output*: each cathodic pulse applied through an electrode contact generates a localised percept whose **brightness scales with charge per phase** and whose **location in visual space depends on which fascicle bundle is recruited**. Without phosphenes, there would be no way to verify that stimulation is reaching the brain, making them indispensable for both device fitting and patient rehabilitation. (3 marks)

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any three differences between retinal and optic nerve prostheses.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Retinal Prosthesis | Optic Nerve Prosthesis |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrode placed on or under retina | Electrode cuff/array wrapped around CN II |
| 2 | Requires intact RGCs and optic nerve | Bypasses retina, requires intact optic nerve |
| 3 | Phosphene size ≈ 0.5°–2° (high acuity) | Phosphene size ≈ 2°–10° (lower acuity) |
| 4 | Lower surgical risk (intraocular) | Moderate surgical risk (retrobulbar) |

(Any three correctly framed points — 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** *“Answer any one full question from each module. Each full question carries 14 marks and may have sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.”*

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the anatomy of the optic nerve and explain why it is classified as part of the CNS rather than the PNS. List the layered meningeal coverings.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the Weiss strength–duration equation starting from the RC model of the axon membrane. Define *rheobase* and *chronaxie*, and show the relationship $\tau_{ch} = \tau_m \ln 2$.

**Model Solution — (a):**
The optic nerve (CN II) is the **second cranial nerve**, an extension of the diencephalon, and is therefore a **central nervous system tract**. Anatomically:

1. **Retinal ganglion cell axons** (≈ 1.2 million) originating from the nerve fibre layer.
2. **Unmyelinated segment** at the optic disc (lamina cribrosa).
3. **Myelinated segment** beyond the lamina — myelinated by **oligodendrocytes**, *not* Schwann cells (definitive CNS signature).
4. **Fascicular arrangement** — divided by glial septa into ≈ 600–1000 bundles.
5. **Pia mater** — innermost vascular covering.
6. **Subarachnoid space** containing CSF (continuous with intracranial subarachnoid).
7. **Arachnoid mater.**
8. **Dural sheath** — continuous with intracranial dura.

Because the nerve carries **oligodendrocyte-derived myelin**, lies within a **meningeal sheath**, and is surrounded by **CSF under intracranial pressure**, it is functionally and embryologically CNS. This classification matters clinically: CNS axons show **limited regeneration** after injury (unlike PNS), which is why optic nerve stimulation must be applied *chronically* before any axonal loss occurs. (7 marks — diagram & explanation)

**Model Solution — (b):**
See **Section 3.1** and **Section 3.2** for the full derivation. Expected key steps the examiner awards marks for:

- Statement of the RC model and $\tau_m = R_m C_m$. [1 mark]
- Charging equation $V_m(t) = IR_m(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m})$. [1 mark]
- Threshold condition $V_m(t) = V_{th}$. [1 mark]
- Definition $I_{rh} = V_{th}/R_m$. [1 mark]
- Final Weiss equation: $I = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$. [2 marks]
- Chronaxie derivation: at $I = 2I_{rh}$, obtain $\tau_{ch} = \tau_m \ln 2$. [1 mark]

(7 marks total)

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the working principle of a **Brindley-type cuff electrode** for optic nerve stimulation. Discuss the design of the **Veraart spiral cuff** and how it improved upon Brindley's design.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compute the Shannon safety index for the following stimulation parameters and state whether the regime is safe: $I = 250\,\mu\text{A}$, $PW = 300\,\mu\text{s}$, electrode surface area $A = 0.6\,\text{mm}^2$. Use safe limit $k < 1.85$.

**Model Solution — (a):**
**Brindley & Lewin (1968)** pioneered optic nerve stimulation. They implanted a receiver-stimulator on the patient’s chest with a bundle of **platinum electrodes** running subcutaneously to the orbit and emerging through the sclera to lie in contact with the optic nerve. Stimulation was via an **extracranial radio-frequency coil** driving an implanted receiver. Limitations: **mechanical instability**, **limited electrode count (≤ 4)**, and **CSF shunting** that reduced selectivity.

**Veraart et al. (1998)** introduced a **four-contact spiral cuff** that self-wrapped around the nerve:

- Each contact embedded on the *inner* surface of a silicone spiral.
- **Tripolar configuration** (anode–cathode–anode) increased current penetration into the fascicles.
- Mechanical compliance matched nerve pulsation.
- Improved **charge efficiency** and **patient comfort**.

The improved design offered (i) better mechanical stability, (ii) higher selectivity via tripolar steering, and (iii) demonstrated reproducible phosphenes in a blind volunteer over multiple years. (7 marks)

**Model Solution — (b):**
*Given:* $I = 250\,\mu\text{A}$, $PW = 300\,\mu\text{s}$, $A = 0.6\,\text{mm}^2 = 6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{cm}^2$.

**Step 1 — Charge per phase.**

$$Q = I \cdot PW = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 300 \times 10^{-6} = 7.5 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{C} = 0.075\,\mu\text{C/phase}$$

**Step 2 — Charge density.**

$$D = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 10^{-8}}{6 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{C/cm}^2 = 12.5\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Shannon index.**

$$k = \log_{10}(12.5) + \log_{10}(0.075) = 1.097 + (-1.125) = -0.028$$

**Step 4 — Verdict.**
Since $k = -0.028 < 1.85$, the regime is **SAFE** for chronic stimulation. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where students LOSE marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "optic nerve" with "optic tract":** The optic *nerve* is the segment from retina to optic chiasm; the optic *tract* runs chiasm-to-LGN. Stimulation literature targets the **nerve**, not the tract. *(−1 mark penalty)*
> 2. **Forgetting CNS classification:** Many answers describe optic nerve as PNS. It is **CNS**, evidenced by oligodendrocyte myelination. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Unit errors in Shannon calculation:** Mixing µC and nC, or mm² and cm² — convert **all to C and cm²** before taking logarithms. *(−2 marks)*
> 4. **Skipping the rheobase definition** in Weiss derivations: examiner explicitly tests this. *(−1 mark)*
> 5. **Writing “cathode hyperpolarises”** — this is *wrong*. Cathodes *depolarise* in extracellular stimulation; anodes hyperpolarise. *(−2 marks)*
> 6. **Omitting the role of CSF shunting** in cuff electrodes — a common 2-mark question. *(−2 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Optic nerve stimulation is a **CNS-interface** visual prosthesis placed on **CN II**, suitable when photoreceptors fail but RGC axons survive.
- ✅ The **Weiss equation** $I = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1-e^{-t/\tau_m}}$ governs threshold current vs pulse duration.
- ✅ **Chronaxie** $\tau_{ch} = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 100–300\,\mu\text{s}$ for mammalian RGC axons.
- ✅ **Charge per phase** $Q = I \cdot PW$ and **Shannon index** $k = \log D + \log Q$ define safety; keep $k < 1.85$.
- ✅ **Electrode evolution:** Brindley (1968) → Veraart spiral cuff (1998) → intrafascicular TIME/SELINE.
- ✅ Phosphene size is **inversely proportional** to the number of fibres uniquely recruited.
- ✅ CSF shunting, fascicular co-activation, and giant-fibre bias are the *three dominant engineering challenges*.
- ✅ Optic nerve prostheses are **mid-tier** in the visual-prosthesis hierarchy — better patient reach than cortical implants, lower acuity than retinal arrays.
- ✅ Recall anatomy: **dura, arachnoid, subarachnoid CSF, pia, fascicles, RGC axons** — in that order from outside to inside.
- ✅ Exam keyword: **“CNS tract with oligodendrocyte myelin.”**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engineering Challenges in the Development of Visual Prosthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engineering Challenges in the Development of Visual Prosthesis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Visual Prosthesis?

A **visual prosthesis** (also called a *visual neuroprosthesis* or *bionic eye*) is an implantable electronic system designed to restore functional vision in individuals suffering from profound blindness caused by degeneration of the retinal photoreceptors (e.g., Retinitis Pigmentosa (RP) and Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD)) while the downstream visual pathway (optic nerve, LGN, V1 cortex) remains at least partially intact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A visual prosthesis is a neural implant that bypasses damaged photoreceptors and electrically stimulates the remaining viable neurons of the visual system (retina, optic nerve, LGN, or visual cortex) to elicit patterned *phosphenes*—the perception of spots of light—which the brain integrates into a coarse visual percept.

### Functional Block Classification
| Anatomical Stimulation Site | Class Name | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Inner retinal ganglion cells | **Epiretinal** | Argus II (Second Sight) |
| Outer retinal / bipolar cells | **Subretinal** | Alpha-IMS, PRIMA (Pixium Vision) |
| Optic nerve | **Optic Nerve Cuff** | C-Sight, AV-DONE |
| Lateral Geniculate Nucleus | **Thalamic** | Experimental only |
| Primary Visual Cortex (V1) | **Cortical** | Orion (Second Sight), CORTIVIS, Utah Array |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pixel Repairman"

Imagine your retina is a **10-megapixel camera sensor (CMOS)** sitting at the back of your eye. In RP/AMD, the first layer of *pixels* (rods and cones) dies, but the *wiring* behind it (bipolar cells, ganglion cells, optic nerve, visual cortex) is still alive and functioning.

A visual prosthesis is like a **technician who climbs into the camera and taps directly on the live wires** to fool them into "reporting" pixels. The camera has a working processor and screen (your brain), but the sensor is dead. The technician uses a **small grid of electrodes**—each electrode produces one bright dot called a *phosphene*—and by lighting up many dots in the right pattern, the brain perceives a crude, low-resolution image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** A normal human eye has ~**120 million rods** and ~**6 million cones**. State-of-the-art prostheses stimulate on the order of **60 to 1,500 electrodes**—a resolution gap of approximately **5 to 6 orders of magnitude**.

## 1.3 Why is Engineering So Hard?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A normal visual experience requires **20/20 acuity (~60 pixels/degree)**, **full color**, **high dynamic range (~10^6:1)**, and **real-time motion tracking (60 Hz)**. The engineering task is to recreate meaningful vision using a few hundred to a few thousand stimulation channels in a tissue that is fragile, saline-soaked, and biologically reactive.

The five grand challenges that KTU examiners expect you to enumerate are:

1. **Biocompatibility & Hermeticity** — survival of the device inside the eye or cortex for **≥ 10 years**.
2. **Stimulation Selectivity & Resolution** — independent activation of small neuronal populations.
3. **Power & Heat Dissipation** — keeping tissue temperature rise below **1 °C (FDA limit: 2 °C in cortex)**.
4. **Image Acquisition → Stimulation Encoding** — converting a 2D camera image into a meaningful spatiotemporal electrode pattern in real time.
5. **Surgical Feasibility & Long-term Stability** — micron-precise placement, anchor fixation, and chronic reliability.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Visual Pathway — Engineering Implantation Map

The engineering approach is selected **purely based on which anatomical node is still alive**:

1. **Photoreceptors (Rods/Cones)** — if dead, must bypass and stimulate **later**.
2. **Bipolar Cells** — preserved in early-stage RP, target for subretinal implants.
3. **Ganglion Cells** — preserved long in RP, target for epiretinal implants.
