4. **Optic Nerve** — preserved if retinal ganglion cells are alive; requires cuff electrodes.
5. **LGN (Thalamus)** — preserved in nearly all retinal blindness; experimental.
6. **V1 Cortex** — preserved unless cortex itself is damaged (cortical blindness); only option when optic nerve is severed.

## 2.2 The 10 Engineering Challenges — A Logical Hierarchy

### Challenge 1 — Biocompatibility & The Foreign Body Response
- Any implant evokes **gliosis** (astrocytes, microglia) that walls off the device, increasing impedance over time.
- **Materials used:** Platinum-Iridium (Pt-Ir 90/10), Iridium Oxide (IrOx), Titanium nitride (TiN), PEDOT:PSS conducting polymers, diamond-like carbon (DLC).
- **Hermetic packaging:** Glass, ceramic (Al₂O₃), parylene-C, titanium cans with laser-welded feedthroughs.
- **Critical requirement:** Must survive **37 °C, pH 7.4, ionic saline** for **>10 years**.

### Challenge 2 — Electrode–Tissue Interface Impedance
- Impedance $Z$ at 1 kHz must be low (≤ **50 kΩ** for retinal electrodes) to allow low-voltage stimulation.
- Charge transfer occurs through two mechanisms:
  - **Capacitive (non-faradaic)** — via double-layer capacitance, no electron transfer.
  - **Faradaic** — via reversible redox reactions (e.g., IrOx / PEDOT).
- **Goal:** Maximize charge storage capacity (CSC) while minimizing impedance.

### Challenge 3 — Safe Stimulation Limits (Shannon's Equation)
- Stimulus must remain in the **k = log(D) + log(Q) ≤ 1.85 to 2.0** safety window (Shannon, 1992), where:
  - $D$ = charge density (μC/cm²)
  - $Q$ = charge per phase (μC)
- Exceeding this causes **electrochemical tissue damage** (gas evolution, pH shift, metal dissolution).

### Challenge 4 — Spatial Resolution & Current Spread
- A single electrode activates a **roughly spherical volume** of tissue depending on current amplitude and tissue resistivity.
- Higher electrode count → higher resolution, **but** more feedthroughs, more power, more heat, and more data bandwidth.
- Typical electrode diameter: **50–200 μm**; spacing: **100–400 μm**.

### Challenge 5 — Power & Heat Dissipation
- FDA limit: **2 °C** maximum tissue temperature rise (1 °C recommended).
- Heat generated by: stimulator IC, coil telemetry losses, electrode current.
- Power budget for a retinal prosthesis: **≤ 40 mW** average.

### Challenge 6 — Image Acquisition & Encoding
- External **miniature CMOS camera** (mounted on glasses) captures scene.
- **Video Processing Unit (VPU)** performs: contrast enhancement, edge detection, downsampling, and pixel-to-electrode mapping.
- Latency budget: **< 100 ms** for natural head movement.

### Challenge 7 — Wireless Telemetry & Data Transmission
- Forward link (camera → implant): **RF coil @ 10–50 MHz** or **inductive link @ 1–10 MHz**.
- Reverse link (implant → external): often **backscattered modulation**.
- Data rate required: ~**1–10 Mbps** for video-rate stimulation.

### Challenge 8 — Mechanical Flexibility & Surgical Insertion
- Retinal implants must be **thin (< 100 μm)** and flexible to be tucked into the subretinal space via a **sclerotomy** < 1.5 mm.
- Cortical arrays must conform to cortical curvature.

### Challenge 9 — Long-term Anchoring
- Retinal tacks (titanium), magnetic fixation, or **subretinal fluid pressure** itself anchors subretinal devices.
- Cortical: dural suture or pedicle screws.

### Challenge 10 — Phosphene Perception & Visual Field Mapping
- Patients see **phosphenes** (light spots) — but their **size, brightness, color, and position** depend non-linearly on stimulation parameters.
- Brain must **learn to interpret** distorted patterns — requires **training (rehabilitation)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Charge per phase | $Q = I \times t_p$ | $\mu C$ |
| 2 | Charge density | $D = Q / A$ | $\mu C / cm^2$ |
| 3 | Current density | $J = I / A$ | $A / cm^2$ |
| 4 | Shannon safety index | $k = \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q)$ | Must be $\leq 1.85$ |
| 5 | Tissue heating (Pennes' bio-heat eqn.) | $\rho c \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + Q_m + Q_p - W_b C_b (T - T_a)$ | $Q_p = I^2 R$ |
| 6 | Electrode double-layer impedance | $Z = 1 / (j \omega C_{dl})$ | $\Omega$ at freq. $\omega$ |
| 7 | Visual angle per phosphene | $\theta = \arctan(d / f)$ | degrees |
| 8 | Field of view (FOV) | $FOV = 2 \arctan(H / (2f))$ | degrees (typical: 10°–40°) |
| 9 | Nyquist electrode spacing | $d_{Nyq} = 2 \cdot \Delta x_{min}$ | $\mu m$ |
| 10 | Power budget (retinal) | $P_{avg} \leq 40$ mW | mW |
| 11 | FDA thermal limit | $\Delta T_{max} \leq 1-2$ °C | °C |
| 12 | Coil coupling (inductive link) | $k = M / \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | $0 < k < 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** Avoid using the `|` character inside any cell. Use `\vert` (e.g., $k \leq 1.85$ above uses $\leq$ instead of $\vert$ for safe stimulation) to keep the markdown table from breaking.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Visual prosthesis engineering directly feeds into:
- **Deep Brain Stimulators (DBS)** — shares neural-interface know-how.
- **Cochlear implants** — the only commercially successful sensory prosthesis (precedent).
- **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — Utah Array, Neuralink, Synchron.
- **BioMEMS / Lab-on-chip** — flexible electrode fabrication.
- **Neural signal processing** — always-on, low-power, bio-real-time encoding.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Shannon Safety Limit and Its Application

The Shannon equation defines the **safe stimulation envelope** that prevents electrochemical damage at the electrode–tissue interface:

$$k = \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q)$$

where:
- $D$ = charge density per phase ($\mu C / cm^2$, geometric electrode area)
- $Q$ = charge per phase ($\mu C$)
- $k$ must remain $\leq 1.85$ (conservative limit)

### Worked Example — Argus II Electrode Safety Check

**Given:**
- Current amplitude $I = 200 \ \mu A$
- Phase duration $t_p = 0.45 \ ms = 450 \ \mu s$
- Electrode geometric diameter $d = 200 \ \mu m$, so area $A = \pi (d/2)^2$

**Step 1 — Compute charge per phase:**

$$Q = I \times t_p = (200 \times 10^{-6} \ A) \times (450 \times 10^{-6} \ s)$$

$$Q = 9.0 \times 10^{-8} \ C = 0.090 \ \mu C$$

*Logic:* We multiply current by time, then convert to microcoulombs by noting $1 \ \mu C = 10^{-6} \ C$.

**Step 2 — Compute electrode geometric area:**

$$A = \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 = \pi (100 \ \mu m)^2$$

$$A = 3.1416 \times 10^{4} \ \mu m^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \ cm^2$$

*Logic:* Convert $\mu m^2 \rightarrow cm^2$ by dividing by $(10^4)^2 = 10^8$.

**Step 3 — Compute charge density:**

$$D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.090 \ \mu C}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \ cm^2}$$

$$D = 286.5 \ \mu C / cm^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute Shannon k:**

$$k = \log_{10}(286.5) + \log_{10}(0.090)$$

$$k = 2.457 + (-1.046)$$

$$k = 1.411$$

**Step 5 — Compare against limit:**

Since $k = 1.411 \leq 1.85$, the stimulation is **SAFE** (within conservative Shannon window). $\checkmark$

## 3.2 Derivation — Field of View (FOV) of a Visual Prosthesis

The FOV of a camera-driven visual prosthesis is limited by the camera's optics, **not** the electrode count. For a camera of focal length $f$ and sensor height $H$:

$$FOV = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{H}{2f}\right)$$

### Worked Example — Argus II Specs

**Given:** $H = 5.76 \ mm$ (1/3″ CMOS), $f = 3.6 \ mm$.

**Step 1 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{H}{2f} = \frac{5.76}{2 \times 3.6} = \frac{5.76}{7.2} = 0.80$$

**Step 2 — Compute the arctangent:**

$$\arctan(0.80) = 38.66°$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 2:**

$$FOV = 2 \times 38.66° = 77.3° \text{ (diagonal)}$$

*Engineering Insight:* Argus II's *camera* FOV is wide (~77°), but the *phosphene* field perceived by the patient is much smaller (~**20°–30°**) because only a 60-electrode 6×10 array stimulates the macula, which projects to a limited cortical region.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Prosthesis for Which Pathology?

| Pathology | Dead Tissue | Alive Tissue | Best Prosthesis Type | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Retinitis Pigmentosa (RP) | Rods & Cones | Bipolar, Ganglion, Optic Nerve, Cortex | **Epiretinal / Subretinal** | Inner retina intact |
| Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD) | Macular cones | Bipolar, Ganglion, Cortex | **Subretinal** (PRIMA) | Targets central macula |
| Severe optic nerve damage | Photoreceptors + RGC axons | Cortex | **Cortical (V1)** | Bypasses optic nerve |
| Total cortical blindness | — | — | **None** (no viable target) | Pathway destroyed |
| Glaucoma (late stage) | RGCs | Cortex | **Cortical** | Optic nerve + RGC both dead |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Most examination questions will ask you to **justify the choice of stimulation site** for a given disease. Use the table above as your mental model.

## 3.4 Derivation — Temperature Rise from Stimulation Power

The power dissipated at the electrode–tissue interface is:

$$P = I^2 \cdot R$$

In saline tissue, $R_{tissue} \approx 1 \ k\Omega$ for a microelectrode. The steady-state temperature rise is approximated by:

$$\Delta T \approx \frac{P}{4 \pi k_t r}$$

where $k_t \approx 0.6 \ W/(m \cdot K)$ is tissue thermal conductivity and $r$ is effective radius.

**Example:** $I = 200 \ \mu A$, $R = 1 \ k\Omega$, $r = 0.5 \ mm$.

$$P = (200 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 10^3 = 4 \times 10^{-5} \ W = 40 \ \mu W$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi (0.6) (0.5 \times 10^{-3})} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{3.77 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\Delta T \approx 0.0106 \ °C$$

This is far below the FDA's 1–2 °C limit — confirming that **electrode heating is not the main thermal concern**; coil telemetry and IC dissipation are.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Visual Pathway & Stimulation Sites

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light enters eye] --> B[Cornea and Lens focus]
    B --> C[Photoreceptors - Rods and Cones]
    C --> D[Bipolar Cells]
    D --> E[Ganglion Cells]
    E --> F[Optic Nerve]
    F --> G[LGN Thalamus]
    G --> H[V1 Visual Cortex]
    H --> I[Visual Perception]

    C -.Dead in RP AMD.-> C1((Epiretinal or Subretinal Implant))
    D -.Often intact.-> D1((Subretinal Stim))
    E -.Often intact.-> E1((Epiretinal Stim))
    F -.Cuff electrode.-> F1((Optic Nerve Stim))
    H -.Direct cortical.-> H1((Cortical Implant Orion))
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Visual Prosthesis System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXTERNAL["External Unit - Worn on Glasses"]
        CAM[CMOS Miniature Camera]
        VPU[Video Processing Unit - VPU]
        COIL_TX[External RF Coil Transmitter]
        BATT[Rechargeable Battery]
    end

    subgraph IMPLANT["Implantable Unit - Inside Body"]
        COIL_RX[Internal RF Coil Receiver]
        ASIC[Stimulator ASIC]
        ELEC[Electrode Array]
        TISSUE[Target Neural Tissue]

        COIL_RX --> ASIC
        ASIC --> ELEC
        ELEC --> TISSUE
    end

    CAM --> VPU
    VPU --> COIL_TX
    BATT --> COIL_TX
    COIL_TX -.Inductive Link.-> COIL_RX
    TISSUE -.Phosphene Feedback.-> BRAIN[(Patient Visual Cortex)]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Decision Tree for Prosthesis Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Patient Presents with Blindness] --> Q1{Photoreceptors Dead?}
    Q1 -- No --> OBS[Observation - Not a candidate]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Inner Retina Intact?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> RET[Retinal Implant - Argus or Alpha or PRIMA]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Optic Nerve Intact?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ON[Optic Nerve Cuff Stimulator]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{V1 Cortex Intact?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> CORT[Cortical Implant - Orion or Utah Array]
    Q4 -- No --> NA[No viable prosthesis - Rehabilitation only]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Electrode–Tissue Interface Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MET[Metal Electrode Pt-Ir or IrOx] --> CDL[Double Layer Capacitance Cdl]
    MET --> FAR[Faradaic Charge Transfer Rct]
    CDL --> TISSUE[Saline Tissue Zspread]
    FAR --> TISSUE
    TISSUE --> NEURON[Target Neuron - RGC or Cortex]

    CDL -.Non-faradaic - safe.-> S1[Reversible]
    FAR -.Faradaic - must be reversible redox.-> S2[Bounded by water window]
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three major engineering challenges faced in the development of a visual prosthesis.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Biocompatibility and hermetic packaging** – the device must survive in saline, 37 °C body environment for 10+ years without provoking gliosis.
2. **Spatial resolution limitation** – current devices have 60–1,500 electrodes versus 120 million photoreceptors in a healthy eye, yielding very coarse phosphene images.
3. **Power and heat dissipation** – total power budget must remain below 40 mW for retinal prostheses and tissue temperature rise must stay under 1–2 °C to prevent thermal damage.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is a phosphene, and why is it important to visual prosthesis design?**

**Model Answer:**
A **phosphene** is a perceived spot of light elicited by direct electrical stimulation of neurons in the visual pathway (retinal ganglion cells, optic nerve, or visual cortex), in the absence of actual photons. It is the **fundamental perceptual unit** of a visual prosthesis—each electrode ideally produces one phosphene, and a patterned array of phosphenes is integrated by the brain to form a coarse image. The size, brightness, location, and colour of phosphenes depend non-linearly on stimulation parameters (amplitude, pulse width, frequency).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**Q.A) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
*(a)* Describe the **anatomical classification** of visual prostheses with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Discuss in detail the **engineering challenges related to biocompatibility, stimulation selectivity, and power/heat dissipation**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution (a) — Anatomical Classification (7 marks)

Visual prostheses are classified based on the **anatomical site of stimulation** along the visual pathway:

**1. Retinal Prostheses** (most clinically advanced)
- **Epiretinal** – placed on the inner retinal surface (vitreous side); stimulates ganglion cells directly. Example: **Argus II (Second Sight Medical Products)** – 60 Pt-Ir electrodes in a 6×10 grid, FDA-approved in 2013.
- **Subretinal** – implanted between the RPE and the bipolar cell layer; replaces dead photoreceptors. Example: **Alpha-IMS / Alpha-AMS (Retina Implant AG)** – 1,500 electrode photodiode array; **PRIMA (Pixium Vision)** – 378 photovoltaic pixels.

**2. Optic Nerve Prostheses** – cuff electrodes wrap around the optic nerve. Example: **AV-DONE / C-Sight** (experimental).

**3. LGN (Thalamic) Prostheses** – stimulation at the lateral geniculate nucleus (experimental, very limited human data).

**4. Cortical Prostheses** – electrodes placed on or in **V1 (striate cortex)**. Examples: **Orion (Second Sight)** – 60 electrodes on cortical surface; **Intracortical (Utah Array)** – 100 microelectrodes penetrating ~1.5 mm into cortex (used in neural prosthetics research).

**Selection Logic:** Choice is dictated by which neurons are still viable. RP/AMD → retinal; optic nerve damage → cortical.

---

#### Solution (b) — Engineering Challenges in Biocompatibility, Selectivity, Power (7 marks)

**1. Biocompatibility (3 marks)**
- Implant must remain functional in **ionic saline at 37 °C** for ≥10 years.
- Foreign Body Response: encapsulation by **glial cells (astrocytes, microglia)** raises impedance and reduces stimulation efficacy.
- Material solutions: Pt-Ir 90/10, IrOx coatings, PEDOT:PSS conducting polymers, titanium hermetic cans, parylene-C encapsulation, Al₂O₃ ceramic packages.
- Must be **hermetic** (water-vapor transmission < 10⁻⁶ cm³·atm/cm²·day).

**2. Stimulation Selectivity (2 marks)**
- A single electrode activates a spherical volume of neurons; current spread limits independent channel count.
- Higher density → higher resolution, but more feedthroughs and more cross-talk.
- Approaches: smaller electrodes (≤ 50 μm), return electrodes (bipolar configurations), **pseudomonophasic** waveforms, IrOx coatings for safer charge injection.

**3. Power and Heat Dissipation (2 marks)**
- FDA limits tissue heating to **≤ 2 °C (1 °C recommended)**.
- Power budget for retinal prosthesis: **≤ 40 mW**.
- Wireless power delivered via inductive coil link at 1–10 MHz; data at 10–50 MHz.
- Heat sources: stimulator IC, coil losses, electrode current. Mitigation: low-power ASIC design, duty-cycling, efficient coil geometry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write *biocompatibility* and *biostability* as the same concept. **Biocompatibility = no toxic reaction from device to tissue.** **Biostability = device itself does not corrode/degrade.** Both must be addressed. Marks are deducted if the distinction is missing. Also, do not confuse Shannon's safety index k with Boltzmann's constant in derivation steps.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q.B) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**
*(a)* Explain the **role of image acquisition, encoding, and the video processing unit (VPU)** in a visual prosthesis system. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* With the help of a **Shannon safety limit calculation**, demonstrate how safe stimulation parameters are derived. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution (a) — Image Acquisition, Encoding & VPU (7 marks)

**1. Image Acquisition (2 marks)**
- A **miniature CMOS camera** is mounted on the patient's spectacles.
- Typical resolution: 640×480 (VGA) at 30–60 fps; wide FOV (~60–80°).
- Optionally: **bi-ocular / stereo** cameras for depth perception.

**2. Video Processing Unit (3 marks)**
- The VPU (worn externally, e.g., on a belt clip) performs real-time processing:
  - **Pre-processing** – contrast enhancement, white balance, denoising.
  - **Feature extraction** – edge detection, motion estimation.
  - **Downsampling** – the camera's 0.3 MP must be reduced to fit the electrode count (60 for Argus II, 1,500 for Alpha-IMS).
  - **Pixel-to-electrode mapping** – assigns each electrode a luminance value (pulse amplitude or width).
- **Latency budget:** < 100 ms.

**3. Encoding and Transmission (2 marks)**
- Processed image is encoded into a stream of **stimulation commands** (frame-by-frame).
- Commands are sent via **inductive RF link** to the implant.
- Implant ASIC decodes commands and delivers biphasic current pulses to selected electrodes.
- Patient **learns to interpret** the resulting phosphene pattern through rehabilitation.

---

#### Solution (b) — Shannon Safety Limit Demonstration (7 marks)

**Statement of Shannon Equation (2 marks):**

$$k = \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \leq 1.85$$

where $D$ is the **charge density per phase** in $\mu C / cm^2$ and $Q$ is the **charge per phase** in $\mu C$. Exceeding $k$ leads to electrochemical damage (gas evolution, pH shift, metal-ion release, tissue necrosis).

**Worked Example (3 marks):**
- Given: $I = 250 \ \mu A$, $t_p = 0.5 \ ms = 500 \ \mu s$, electrode diameter $d = 100 \ \mu m$.
- $Q = I \times t_p = 250 \ \mu A \times 500 \ \mu s = 0.125 \ \mu C$
- $A = \pi (50 \ \mu m)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \ cm^2$
- $D = 0.125 / 7.854 \times 10^{-5} = 1591.5 \ \mu C / cm^2$
- $k = \log_{10}(1591.5) + \log_{10}(0.125) = 3.202 + (-0.903) = 2.299$
- Since $k = 2.299 > 1.85$, the parameters are **UNSAFE** — must reduce $I$, $t_p$, or increase electrode area.

**Engineering Mitigation (2 marks):**
- Use **larger electrodes** (200 μm diameter) to halve $D$.
- Use **IrOx coating** to allow higher charge injection within safe $k$.
- Use **biphasic symmetric pulses** to ensure no DC residual.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (10-second scan before exam):**

- **Visual prosthesis =** electrical stimulation of viable visual neurons to elicit phosphenes.
- **Five stimulation sites** (in anatomical order): photoreceptor → bipolar → ganglion (retinal) → optic nerve → LGN → V1 cortex.
- **Most clinically mature:** Retinal (Argus II, Alpha-IMS, PRIMA).
- **Only option when optic nerve is dead:** Cortical (Orion, Utah Array).
- **Resolution limit:** Current devices use **60–1,500 electrodes** vs. **~120 million** photoreceptors in a healthy eye.
- **FOV:** Camera-limited (~60–80°), phosphene-perceived FOV is much smaller (~20–30°).
- **Biocompatibility materials:** Pt-Ir, IrOx, PEDOT:PSS, parylene-C, Ti, Al₂O₃.
- **Shannon safety limit:** $k = \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \leq 1.85$.
- **Power budget:** ≤ 40 mW for retinal; FDA thermal limit 1–2 °C.
- **Biostability ≠ Biocompatibility** (a frequent KTU pitfall).
- **10 major challenges** (must memorize): Biocompatibility, hermeticity, electrode impedance, stimulation safety (Shannon), spatial resolution, current spread, power/heat, image acquisition, VPU encoding, surgical anchoring, long-term stability, rehabilitation/learning.
- **Phosphene** = the perceptual unit; depends on stimulation amplitude, pulse width, frequency.
- **Argus II** = 60 Pt-Ir electrodes, epiretinal, FDA-approved 2013.
- **Alpha-IMS** = 1,500 electrodes, subretinal, photovoltaic.
- **PRIMA** = subretinal, photovoltaic, IR (880 nm) illumination.
- **Orion** = cortical, 60 electrodes (Argus-II on cortex).
- **Inductive link frequency:** 1–10 MHz (power), 10–50 MHz (data).
- **Electrode diameter:** 50–200 μm typical; spacing 100–400 μm.
- **Biphasic pulses** are mandatory — no DC residual to avoid tissue damage.
- **Order-of-magnitude fact** examiners love: a healthy eye has ~10⁸ photoreceptors; a prosthesis has ~10²–10³ electrodes → resolution gap of **5–6 orders of magnitude**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Valuation Warning:** Examiners frequently test the difference between **epiretinal** and **subretinal** placement. Memorize: *Epi = on top of retina (vitreous side, ganglion cells).* *Sub = beneath retina (RPE side, bipolar cells).* Confusing these is a guaranteed 2–3 mark loss. Also, remember that **cortical prosthesis is the only option when the optic nerve is severed**, not the first choice for RP/AMD.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neural Electrical Stimulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Neural Electrical Stimulator # ⚡ Module 1 — Neural Electrical Stimulator ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition A **Neural Electrical Stimulator** is a bioelectronic device that delivers precisely controlled, low-energy electrical pulses through electrodes to excitable neural tissue (peripheral nerve, spinal cord, cochlea, retina, cortex, or muscle) in order to artificially elicit or modulate a physiological action potential. It forms the **active signal-generating core** of every neural implant — from cochlear implants and deep brain stimulators (DBS) to functional electrical stimulation (FES) systems for restoring movement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1** > The *Neural Electrical Stimulator* is the *primary building block* of any neural prosthesis. The examiner expects you to know: (1) stimulus waveform shapes, (2) key parameters (amplitude, pulse width, frequency, charge per phase), (3) current-controlled vs voltage-controlled modes, (4) charge balancing, and (5) the basic block-level architecture. ### 🌱 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap on the Shoulder" Imagine you are standing in a dark room and you want a friend to walk forward. Instead of pushing them (which would be crude and could injure them), you give them a **gentle, well-timed tap on the shoulder** — *just enough* to trigger them to take one step. The **tap** is the electrical stimulus, the **shoulder** is the excitable neural membrane, and the **step** is the action potential. A neural stimulator is essentially a sophisticated electronic device that does exactly this: it gives a *carefully engineered "tap"* of charge to a nerve, telling it to fire. The brilliance of the design lies in: - **How big** the tap is → *amplitude (current or voltage)* - **How long** the tap lasts → *pulse width* - **How often** the tap comes → *frequency (or pulse repetition rate)* - **What shape** the tap has → *waveform (monophasic / biphasic)* - **How we clean up afterward** → *charge balancing* > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric — Stimulation Charge per Phase (Q):** The most clinically meaningful parameter in neural stimulation is **charge per phase**, not voltage or current alone. It is measured in **Coulombs (C)** or **nanocoulombs (nC)** and is calculated as **Q = I × PW**, where *I* is the stimulating current amplitude and *PW* is the pulse width. --- ### 🔑 The Five Key Stimulus Parameters (MUST memorise) 1. **Amplitude (I or V)** — Strength of the pulse 2. **Pulse Width (PW)** — Duration of one phase 3. **Frequency (f)** — Pulses per second (Hz) 4. **Waveform Shape** — Monophasic, biphasic, symmetric/asymmetric 5. **Inter-pulse / Inter-phase Interval** — Gap between phases (charge balancing) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🧠 Why and How a Neural Stimulator Works The mechanism of action can be broken into a logical chain: - **Step 1 — Charge Injection:** The stimulator injects a controlled amount of electrical charge through an electrode into the extracellular fluid surrounding a neuron. - **Step 2 — Local Field Effect:** This charge creates a local electric field. If the field is strong enough at the **trigger zone** (e.g., the axon hillock or a node of Ranvier), it causes the local transmembrane potential to *depolarize*. - **Step 3 — Threshold Crossing:** Once the membrane potential reaches the **threshold voltage (typically ~−55 mV)**, voltage-gated sodium channels open en masse, generating a propagating **action potential**. - **Step 4 — Physiological Response:** The action potential travels along the nerve, eventually causing muscle contraction, sensory perception, or inhibition of abnormal signals (e.g., in Parkinson's disease with DBS). - **Step 5 — Charge Balance Restoration:** After each stimulating phase, a *second phase of equal but opposite charge* is delivered (passive or active) to prevent net DC accumulation, which would otherwise cause **electrode corrosion**, **tissue damage**, and **pH shifts**. ### 🔬 Current-Controlled vs Voltage-Controlled Stimulation | Feature | Current-Controlled (CC) | Voltage-Controlled (VC) | |---|---|---| | Output parameter | Constant current I | Constant voltage V | | Charge delivered | Predictable, independent of load | Depends on electrode impedance | | Safety against impedance change | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Poor (impedance ↑ → current ↓) | | Tissue damage risk | Low (if charge ≤ limits) | Variable | | Preferred for implants | ✅ **Yes (standard for clinical implants)** | Sometimes in research | | Circuit complexity | Higher (Howland, op-amp) | Lower (simple MOSFET switch) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why current-mode is preferred clinically:** Tissue-electrode impedance changes over time due to fibrotic encapsulation. A current source guarantees the *intended charge* is delivered regardless of impedance; a voltage source does not. ### 📐 The Strength-Duration Curve (Rheobase & Chronaxie) Every excitable tissue follows the classical **Weiss-Lapicque strength-duration relationship**: $$ I(t) = I_{rh} \left(1 + \frac{C}{t}\right) $$ Where: - $I_{rh}$ = **Rheobase** — the minimum current amplitude that can excite the tissue when pulse width is very long (theoretically infinite) - $C$ = **Chronaxie** — the pulse width at which the threshold current is exactly **2 × rheobase** - $t$ = pulse width (PW) - $I(t)$ = threshold current at pulse width *t* > [!NOTE] > **Chronaxie is a key clinical index.** A *shorter chronaxie* means the tissue is *more excitable* and *easier to stimulate*. Typical values: large myelinated axons ≈ 30–200 µs; small unmyelinated fibers ≈ 200–700 µs; cardiac muscle ≈ 1–3 ms. ### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Charge per phase | $Q = I \times PW$ | Coulombs (C) | Most clinically meaningful | | 2 | Charge density | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A_{electrode}}$ | $\mu C / cm^^{2}$ | Safety metric | | 3 | Current density | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_{electrode}}$ | $A / cm^{2}$ | Safety metric | | 4 | Energy per pulse | $E = V \times I \times PW$ | Joules (J) | Battery life estimation | | 5 | Average power | $P_{avg} = V \times I \times PW \times f$ | Watts (W) | For heat/tissue-damage analysis | | 6 | Threshold current (Weiss) | $I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \dfrac{C_{ch}}{t}\right)$ | Amperes | Strength-duration curve | | 7 | Shannon safe-stimulation limit | $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \le k$ | $\log$ values | $D$ in $\mu C/cm^2$/phase, $Q$ in $\mu C$/phase | | 8 | Duty cycle | $D_{cycle} = PW \times f$ | Dimensionless | Fraction of time active | | 9 | Pulse repetition rate | $f = 1/T$ | Hz | Common: 20–200 Hz for sensory/motor | | 10 | Interphase gap | $IPG$ | seconds | 0–100 µs typical | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Shannon's Safety Limit:** Developed in 1992 from animal studies. The constant $k$ depends on the stimulation paradigm (often chosen between **1.5 and 2.0** for conservative, safe design). The equation states: $\log D + \log Q \le k$. This single equation is a **favourite KTU question** — *memorize it verbatim.* ### 🌍 Real-World Utility Neural stimulators are used in: - **Cochlear implants** (auditory prosthesis — restores hearing) - **Deep brain stimulators** (Parkinson's, dystonia, OCD) - **Spinal cord stimulators** (chronic pain) - **Sacral nerve stimulators** (incontinence) - **Retinal implants** (vision restoration — e.g., Argus II) - **Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES)** for foot drop, grasp - **Vagus Nerve Stimulation (VNS)** for epilepsy & depression - **Cardiac pacemakers** (essentially cardiac-muscle stimulators) - **Diaphragmatic pacing** (phrenic nerve — for ventilator-dependent patients) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 🔢 Derivation 1: Charge per Phase (Q = I × PW) Starting from the fundamental definition of **electric current**: $$ I = \frac{dQ}{dt} $$ **Step 1:** For a constant current pulse of amplitude $I$ delivered over a duration $PW$, the differential can be replaced by a finite change: $$ I = \frac{\Delta Q}{PW} $$ **Step 2:** Rearranging to solve for the charge delivered in one phase: $$ \Delta Q = I \times PW $$ **Step 3:** By convention, we call this delivered charge per phase $Q$: $$ \boxed{Q = I \times PW} $$ > **Engineering interpretation:** If the device delivers $I = 1\ \text{mA}$ for $PW = 100\ \mu s$, then $Q = 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 100\ \text{nC}$ per phase — a typical value for safe neural stimulation. --- ### 🔢 Derivation 2: Strength-Duration Relationship (Weiss-Lapicque) The empirically observed threshold current as a function of pulse width follows a hyperbolic curve. The derivation assumes the nerve membrane behaves as a **parallel RC circuit** and the threshold corresponds to delivering a fixed *threshold charge* $Q_{th}$. **Step 1:** The membrane model is a leaky integrator. For a rectangular current pulse of amplitude $I$ and duration $t$, the voltage across the membrane capacitor rises according to: $$ V_m(t) = I \cdot R \left(1 - e^{-t / \tau}\right) $$ where $\tau = RC$ is the membrane time constant. **Step 2:** Threshold is reached when $V_m(t) = V_{th}$. Solve for the threshold current $I_{th}$: $$ V_{th} = I_{th} \cdot R \left(1 - e^{-t / \tau}\right) $$ **Step 3:** Define **rheobase** $I_{rh} = V_{th}/R$ (the threshold current as $t \to \infty$): $$ I_{th} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}} $$ **Step 4:** Using the identity $\dfrac{1}{1 - e^{-x}} \approx 1 + \dfrac{1}{x}$ for small *x*, and for $t$ much less than $\tau$, the long-pulse limit $I_{rh}$ is recovered and the chronaxie $C_{ch} = \tau$: $$ I_{th}(t) = I_{rh} \left(1 + \frac{\tau}{t}\right) = I_{rh} \left(1 + \frac{C_{ch}}{t}\right) $$ $$ \boxed{I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \frac{C_{ch}}{t}\right)} $$ **Step 5 — Chronaxie derivation:** By definition, chronaxie $C_{ch}$ is the pulse width at which $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$. Substitute: $$ 2 I_{rh} = I_{rh}\left(1 + \frac{C_{ch}}{C_{ch}}\right) = I_{rh}(1+1) = 2 I_{rh} \ \checkmark $$ Hence chronaxie is the pulse width **at which threshold current is exactly double the rheobase**. --- ### 🔢 Derivation 3: Charge Density and Shannon's Safety Plot **Step 1:** Charge density is charge per unit electrode area: $$ D = \frac{Q}{A} \quad [\mu C / cm^{2}\ \text{per phase}] $$ **Step 2:** Take the base-10 logarithm of both: $$ \log_{10}(D) = \log_{10}(Q) - \log_{10}(A) $$ **Step 3:** Shannon's empirical safety criterion for **k = 1.85** (typical conservative value) is: $$ \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \le k $$ where $D$ is in $\mu C/cm^{2}$ per phase and $Q$ in $\mu C$ per phase. **Step 4 — Worked example:** Suppose a stimulator delivers $Q = 0.1\ \mu C$/phase through a $1\ \text{mm}^{2}$ electrode. $$ A = 1 \times 10^{-2}\ cm^{2} \Rightarrow D = \frac{0.1}{0.01} = 10\ \mu C/cm^{2} $$ $$ \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) = 1 + (-1) = 0 $$ Since $0 \le 1.85$, the stimulus is **within the safe region** of Shannon's plot. --- ### 🔢 Derivation 4: Average Power & Energy per Pulse **Step 1:** Energy per single pulse (one phase): $$ E_{pulse} = \int_{0}^{PW} V(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt = V \cdot I \cdot PW $$ **Step 2:** For a train of pulses at repetition rate $f$, the **average power** delivered to the tissue is: $$ P_{avg} = E_{pulse} \times f = V \cdot I \cdot PW \cdot f $$ **Step 3 — Numerical example:** $V = 1\ \text{V}$, $I = 1\ \text{mA}$, $PW = 100\ \mu s$, $f = 100\ \text{Hz}$: $$ P_{avg} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 10\ \mu W $$ This is the *order of magnitude* of power consumption of a typical implantable neural stimulator — small enough to allow multi-year battery life. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Note:** Always state units explicitly in derivation answers. Writing $PW \times f$ as **duty cycle** is highly valued. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 🔧 Block Diagram of a Typical Implantable Neural Stimulator ```mermaid flowchart TD PWR[Power Source
Battery or Inductive Link] --> REG[Voltage Regulator
and DC-DC Converter] REG --> CPU[Microcontroller / DSP
Stimulus Pattern Generator] CPU --> DAC[DAC or PWM Module
Sets Amplitude] DAC --> ISO[Stimulus Isolation Unit
SIU or Optocoupler] ISO --> CC[Current Source / Sink
Howland or Op-Amp Mirror] CC --> MUX[Channel Mux and
Electrode Selector Switch] MUX --> E1[Electrode 1] MUX --> E2[Electrode 2] MUX --> E3[Electrode N] CPU --> MON[Impedance and
Charge Monitor] MON --> CPU TEL[Telemetry Link
RF or IR] --> CPU CPU --> TEL ``` **Explanation of the block diagram (mapping to physical function):** - The **Power Source** supplies energy (lithium-iodine battery or transcutaneous inductive link). - The **Regulator** produces stable rails. - The **Microcontroller / DSP** generates the precise timing pattern of pulses (controls PW, frequency, channel). - The **DAC** sets the analog amplitude. - The **Stimulus Isolation Unit (SIU)** is critical for **patient safety** — it provides galvanic isolation between the digital/clinical side and the electrode/tissue side, preventing mains leakage currents from reaching the patient. - The **Current Source** delivers the precisely controlled current to the tissue. - The **Channel MUX** routes the stimulus to one of many electrode sites. - **Impedance / Charge Monitor** measures electrode-tissue impedance and ensures charge per phase stays within safe limits (Shannon's bound). - **Telemetry** allows external programming (amplitude, frequency, PW, on-time, off-time). --- ### 🔄 Stimulus Waveform Shapes ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Monophasic
Single phase only
DC unsafe] --> B[Biphasic
Two opposite phases
Charge balanced] B --> C[Symmetric Biphasic
Equal phase charges] B --> D[Asymmetric Biphasic
Different amplitudes and widths] C --> E[With Interphase Gap
Brief delay between phases] C --> F[Charge Balanced via
Active or Passive Discharge] ``` **Description of waveform taxonomy:** - **Monophasic:** Only one phase. Injects net DC → *unsafe* for chronic use. Used only briefly in research/diagnostic stimulation. - **Biphasic:** Two opposite phases — the *gold standard* for safety. Phase 1 is the cathodic (depolarizing) stimulating phase; Phase 2 is the anodic (recovery) phase. - **Symmetric Biphasic:** Both phases have equal amplitude × width product. - **Asymmetric Biphasic:** Phase 2 is *longer and smaller* in amplitude — sometimes more charge-efficient. - **Interphase Gap (IPG):** A small (0–100 µs) pause between phases that *increases stimulation efficacy* and reduces the anodic phase's interference with excitation. - **Charge-Balanced:** Critical safety requirement — no net DC over one full cycle. --- ### 📈 Strength-Duration Curve (Rheobase & Chronaxie Graph) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph X[Stimulus Pulse Width axis / mu s] direction LR X1[0 mu s] --> X2[Chronaxie point] --> X3[Infinity] end subgraph Y[Threshold Current axis / mA] direction TB Y1[Minimum] --> Y2[Rheobase level] --> Y3[Infinity Current] end R[Rheobase IRh]:::node C[Chronaxie Cch at I = 2IRh]:::node H[Hyperbolic Strength Duration Curve]:::curve H --- R H --- C classDef node fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 classDef curve fill:#ffe699,color:#000000,stroke:#cc7a00 ``` **Reading the curve:** As pulse width increases, threshold current decreases asymptotically toward $I_{rh}$. The chronaxie $C_{ch}$ sits at the "knee" of the curve and is the most efficient pulse width for stimulation. --- ### 🛡️ Shannon's Safety Window ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SAFE[Safe Stimulation Region] direction LR SA[log D vs log Q below k]:::safe end subgraph UNSAFE[Unsafe Region] direction LR UN[log D vs log Q above k line]:::unsafe end SA --> UN classDef safe fill:#c6efce,color:#006100,stroke:#006100 classDef unsafe fill:#ffc7ce,color:#9c0006,stroke:#9c0006 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define a neural electrical stimulator. List any four key stimulus parameters.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A neural electrical stimulator is an electronic device that delivers precisely controlled, low-energy electrical pulses through electrodes to excitable neural tissue (nerve, muscle, or sensory organ) to elicit or modulate an action potential, thereby restoring or replacing lost neural function. (1½ marks) **Four key stimulus parameters** (1½ marks): 1. **Amplitude** (current or voltage) 2. **Pulse width** (PW) 3. **Frequency / pulse repetition rate** 4. **Waveform shape** (monophasic / biphasic / charge-balanced) *(Optional 5th: Interphase gap or duty cycle.)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Why is biphasic charge-balanced stimulation preferred over monophasic stimulation in clinical neural implants?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Monophasic stimulation delivers a net **DC current** to the tissue, which causes: - **Electrode corrosion / dissolution** (1 mark) - **Electrochemical generation of toxic species** (e.g., chlorine, metal ions) and pH shifts that damage tissue (1 mark) - **Tissue damage** at the electrode-tissue interface Biphasic charge-balanced stimulation delivers a **second phase of equal and opposite charge** that *exactly cancels* the DC component, ensuring no net charge accumulates per cycle. This makes the stimulation **safe for chronic use** (1 mark). --- ### 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical **implantable neural electrical stimulator**. Discuss the role of the **stimulus isolation unit (SIU)** and the **current source**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Weiss-Lapicque strength-duration equation** and define **rheobase** and **chronaxie**. Explain their clinical significance. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution to Q3A:** **(a) Block diagram and explanation (7 marks):** *(Draw the block diagram given in Section 4 above — 2 marks for diagram, 5 marks for explanation.)* The architecture consists of the following functional blocks: - **Power source** — Lithium-iodine battery or inductively coupled transcutaneous link provides safe, long-life energy. - **Regulator** — Provides clean, stable DC rails for analog and digital blocks. - **Microcontroller / DSP** — Generates the digital timing pattern of stimulus pulses; controls pulse width, frequency, channel selection, and ramp-up/ramp-down profiles. - **DAC / PWM block** — Converts the digital amplitude setting into an analog control voltage. - **Stimulus Isolation Unit (SIU)** — Provides **galvanic isolation** (typically optical or transformer-based) between the digital/clinical side and the patient-side electrode. This is mandated by **IEC 60601** medical safety standards. Without it, a fault in the device could deliver **mains-frequency leakage currents** (≥ 10 µA can cause ventricular fibrillation in direct contact with the heart). - **Current source** — Converts the control voltage into a **constant, load-independent current** (typically a Howland current pump or a current-mirror topology). Current-mode is preferred because tissue-electrode impedance drifts with time, and the actual *charge* delivered must remain predictable. - **Channel multiplexer** — Routes the current to the desired electrode pair out of N available sites. - **Impedance and charge monitor** — Real-time measurement of electrode-tissue impedance and charge-per-phase to enforce Shannon's safety limit. - **Telemetry** — Bidirectional RF or infrared link for clinician programming and device status reporting. **(b) Strength-duration derivation (7 marks):** *(Present the derivation from Section 3 above. Marks distribution:)* - Membrane model as leaky RC integrator (1 mark) - Voltage build-up equation $V_m(t) = IR(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ (1 mark) - Threshold condition $V_m = V_{th}$ (1 mark) - Solving for $I_{th}$ and defining $I_{rh} = V_{th}/R$ (1 mark) - Final Weiss-Lapicque equation: $I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}(1 + C_{ch}/t)$ (1 mark) - Definition of **rheobase** (minimum current for very long pulse) and **chronaxie** (pulse width at $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$) (1 mark) - Clinical significance: chronaxie is the most efficient operating point; shorter chronaxie → more excitable tissue; determines whether stimulation is achievable within safe charge limits (1 mark) --- #### **Q3B. [Alternative Choice — KTU Format]** **(a)** Define the following terms with proper units: (i) Charge per phase, (ii) Charge density, (iii) Current density, (iv) Pulse width, (v) Frequency, (vi) Duty cycle. **(7 marks)** **(b)** State and explain **Shannon's safety equation** for neural stimulation. A stimulator delivers $0.2\ \mu C$/phase through a $0.5\ \text{mm}^{2}$ electrode. Using **Shannon's limit k = 1.85**, determine whether the stimulation is safe. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution to Q3B:** **(a) Definitions (7 marks — 1 mark each + 1 mark for units table):** | Term | Definition | Unit | |---|---|---| | Charge per phase (Q) | Total electrical charge delivered in one phase of the pulse | Coulombs (C) or nC | | Charge density (D) | Charge per phase divided by electrode area | $\mu C/cm^{2}$ | | Current density (J) | Stimulating current amplitude divided by electrode area | $A/cm^{2}$ | | Pulse width (PW) | Duration of one phase of the pulse | seconds (s) or µs | | Frequency (f) | Number of complete stimulus cycles per second | Hz | | Duty cycle | Fraction of time the stimulus is active, $PW \times f$ | dimensionless (0–1) | **(b) Shannon's safety analysis (7 marks):** **Shannon's equation (1992):** $$ \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \le k $$ where $D$ is charge density in $\mu C/cm^{2}$ per phase and $Q$ is charge per phase in $\mu C$. **Reasoning (3 marks):** Shannon developed this from animal experiments to delineate the **safe stimulation regime** from the **unsafe regime** in the *log Q – log D* plane. It captures the trade-off: if you use **low charge** you can tolerate **high charge density**, and vice versa. The constant *k* is set conservatively (typically 1.5–2.0) to provide a wide safety margin against neuronal damage. **Numerical evaluation (4 marks):** Given: $Q = 0.2\ \mu C$/phase, $A = 0.5\ \text{mm}^{2} = 0.5 \times 10^{-2}\ cm^{2} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ cm^{2}$. $$ D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.2\ \mu C}{5 \times 10^{-3}\ cm^{2}} = 40\ \mu C / cm^{2} $$ $$ \log_{10}(D) = \log_{10}(40) \approx 1.602 $$ $$ \log_{10}(Q) = \log_{10}(0.2) \approx -0.699 $$ $$ \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) = 1.602 - 0.699 = 0.903 $$ Since $0.903 \le 1.85$, the stimulation **lies inside the safe region** of Shannon's plot. ✅ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers** > 1. **Do NOT** mix up the unit of $D$ in Shannon's plot. It must be **$\mu C/cm^{2}$ per phase** — not $C/cm^{2}$ or $mC/cm^{2}$. > 2. **Do NOT** confuse *rheobase* (current) with *chronaxie* (time). Many students swap them. > 3. **Do NOT** state that monophasic stimulation is "more efficient" — it is **unsafe** for chronic use; the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks. > 4. In derivations, **always show the assumption** (e.g., rectangular pulse, constant current, parallel RC membrane) before substituting. > 5. For current-controlled vs voltage-controlled, the *expected answer* is: current mode is preferred because of **load-impedance independence** — not just because of "accuracy." --- ### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] A **neural electrical stimulator** delivers controlled electrical pulses to excitable tissue to elicit action potentials. - [ ] **Five core parameters:** Amplitude, Pulse Width, Frequency, Waveform, Interphase Gap. - [ ] **Charge per phase** $Q = I \times PW$ is the most clinically meaningful parameter. - [ ] **Charge-balanced biphasic waveforms** are the *gold standard* — they prevent net DC and protect tissue/electrode. - [ ] **Current-controlled stimulation is preferred** over voltage-controlled for implants due to impedance-independent charge delivery. - [ ] **Rheobase** = minimum current at infinite PW; **Chronaxie** = PW at $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$. - [ ] **Weiss-Lapicque equation:** $I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \dfrac{C_{ch}}{t}\right)$. - [ ] **Shannon's safety limit:** $\log D + \log Q \le k$ (with $k \approx 1.5$–$2.0$, $D$ in $\mu C/cm^{2}$, $Q$ in $\mu C$). - [ ] A typical **Stimulus Isolation Unit (SIU)** provides galvanic isolation — a mandatory safety feature per IEC 60601. - [ ] A typical **implantable stimulator** draws only **µW to low mW** average power, enabling multi-year battery life. - [ ] Common **clinical applications:** cochlear implant, DBS, spinal cord stimulator, retinal implant, FES, VNS, cardiac pacemaker. - [ ] **Units to memorize:** $Q$ in nC, $D$ in $\mu C/cm^{2}$, $PW$ in µs, $f$ in Hz, $E$ in µJ, $P_{avg}$ in µW. - [ ] **Key safety numbers:** Max safe $D \approx 30\ \mu C/cm^{2}$ for k = 1.5; Max safe charge per phase ≈ 0.1–1 µC. - [ ] **Interphase gap (0–100 µs)** between phases improves stimulation efficacy and reduces anodic interference. - [ ] **Duty cycle** = $PW \times f$ → directly controls average power and battery life.

topics.title: DSP-Based Image Processing System for Visual Prostheses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DSP-Based Image Processing System for Visual Prostheses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **DSP-based image processing system for visual prostheses** is a real-time, embedded computational architecture that acquires external visual information through an image sensor, applies a sequence of computationally optimized digital signal processing algorithms (filtering, edge detection, contrast normalization, motion extraction, object segmentation, and phosphene mapping), and translates the processed image into charge-balanced, biphasic electrical stimulation parameters delivered to an electrode array interfacing with the visual pathway (retina, optic nerve, lateral geniculate nucleus, or visual cortex).

The system is constrained by strict engineering envelopes: **low power dissipation (< 100 mW)**, **real-time latency (< 50–100 ms)**, **miniaturized form factor**, and **biocompatible packaging**, while meeting the **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome (CO1)** benchmark for design-level understanding of sensory neural prostheses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic directly maps to **CO1** – "Describe the principles, design considerations, and signal processing strategies of neural prostheses and implantable devices." It forms the analytical foundation for cortical visual implants (Dobelle, Second Sight Argus II, Monash Phoenix99, Illinois Intracortical Visual Prosthesis).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **foreign language interpreter sitting in a soundproof booth** at the United Nations. On one side, a diplomat speaks French (the real-world scene). On the other side, a deaf listener can only understand a particular pattern of taps on the table (electrical pulses to neurons).

The interpreter's job is exactly what the **DSP-based image processing system** does:

1. **The Camera (eye substitute)** listens to the "French" light scene.
2. **The DSP chip** understands the meaning of the scene — it strips out noise (low-pass filtering), highlights outlines (edge detection), and converts shapes into simple tap patterns (phosphene mapping).
3. **The Electrode array** delivers the tap pattern to the brain's visual cortex, which has learned to interpret the taps as crude "pixels of light" called **phosphenes**.

The result: a **blind person perceives synthesized visual patterns**, not natural sight, but enough to navigate, detect faces, and read large print.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phosphene**: The fundamental unit of artificial vision — a perceived point of light elicited by direct electrical stimulation of neurons in the visual pathway. The collection of phosphenes forms the prosthetic "image."

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following metrics govern the design envelope and must be memorized for the KTU board exam:

- **Phosphene size** = approximately **0.5°–1.5°** of visual angle
- **Phosphene persistence** ≈ **100–300 ms** in human cortex
- **Safe charge density limit** = **30 μC/cm²** (Shannon limit, modified by Shannon 1992)
- **Safe charge per phase** ≤ **0.4 μC/phase** (typical cortical implant)
- **Normal human visual acuity** = **60 pixels/degree** (≈ 20/20 vision)
- **Practical prosthetic acuity** = **3–20 pixels/degree** (current state of art)
- **Operating supply voltage** for implantable DSP: **±1.8 V to ±3.3 V**
- **Maximum frame refresh rate** = **60 Hz** (limited by phosphene persistence and power budget)
- **Stimulation frequency** per electrode: **20–300 Hz**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System-Level Operational Block Decomposition

A DSP-based image processing pipeline for a visual prosthesis consists of **six canonical stages**. Each stage must be understood in terms of *what* it does, *why* it is necessary, and *how* it is implemented.

### Stage 1 — Image Acquisition
- **Function:** Capture the external scene using a **CMOS/CCD image sensor** mounted externally (typically on eyeglass frames) or, in fully implanted designs, a miniature intraocular camera.
- **Why:** The native eye is non-functional in candidates for visual prostheses (e.g., retinitis pigmentosa, age-related macular degeneration). An artificial transducer replaces the photoreceptor layer.
- **How:** Light is focused onto a pixel array, each pixel is read as an 8-/10-bit intensity value, and a full **M × N frame** is delivered to the DSP at the **frame rate $f_s$**.

### Stage 2 — Pre-processing (Pixel Domain Operations)
- **Function:** Remove sensor noise, normalize illumination, and compress dynamic range.
- **Key operations:** **Histogram equalization**, **Gaussian low-pass filtering**, **gamma correction**, and **luminance–chrominance separation (Y Cb Cr)** to reduce bandwidth.
- **Why:** Phosphene brightness is non-linearly related to stimulation amplitude. Dynamic range compression improves perceptual mapping fidelity.

### Stage 3 — Feature Extraction
- **Function:** Extract perceptually meaningful information — **edges, contours, motion vectors, and object centroids**.
- **Key algorithms:**
  * **Sobel / Prewitt / Canny edge detection** for contour extraction
  * **Optical flow (Lucas–Kanade)** for motion
  * **Saliency-based object detection** to flag regions of interest
- **Why:** Phosphene density is sparse (10²–10³ phosphenes), so only **high-information regions** should be rendered.

### Stage 4 — Phosphene Mapping / Image-to-Stimulus Transformation
- **Function:** Map each selected image region to a **2D phosphene grid** corresponding to the electrode array geometry.
- **Strategy options:**
  * **Direct pixel-to-electrode mapping** (1:1 for high-density arrays)
  * **Log-polar / foveated mapping** (high resolution centrally, sparse peripherally — mirrors the human retina)
  * **Score-based mapping** (only top-K salient features delivered)
- **Why:** The electrode count $N_e$ is far below the natural photoreceptor count (~130 million). Intelligent mapping maximizes perceptual utility.

### Stage 5 — Stimulation Parameter Generation
- **Function:** Convert mapped intensity values into biphasic, charge-balanced pulse trains.
- **Parameters per electrode $(i, j)$:**
  * Pulse amplitude $A_{ij}$ (μA)
  * Pulse width $PW_{ij}$ (μs)
  * Inter-phase gap (μs)
  * Pulse train frequency $f_{ij}$ (Hz)
- **Why:** Phosphene brightness perception follows the **strength–duration curve** of neural excitation, governed by the **Weiss–Lapicque equation**.

### Stage 6 — Telemetry and Electrode Driving
- **Function:** Deliver pulse parameters to the implanted electrode array via **wireless inductive telemetry** (RF or IR) or **percutaneous hardwired link**.
- **Why:** Hermetic sealing of the implant mandates wireless power and data; a **transcutaneous link** is mandatory for chronic use.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist sampling (image sensor) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Sets minimum camera frame rate |
| 2 | Phosphene count (theoretical max) | $N_{ph} = N_e$ | Upper bound on artificial visual resolution |
| 3 | Effective visual acuity (prosthetic) | $VA_{prost} = \dfrac{N_{ph}}{FOV_{deg}}$ | Pixels per degree delivered to the patient |
| 4 | Visual field of view | $FOV = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right)$ | Optics design constraint |
| 5 | Weiss–Lapicque strength–duration | $I_{th} = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}}$ | Pulse amplitude vs. width for neural threshold |
| 6 | Rheobase current | $I_{rh} = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_m}$ | Minimum DC current to fire neuron |
| 7 | Chronaxie time | $t_c = \tau \cdot \ln 2$ | Optimal pulse width (lowest threshold charge) |
| 8 | Charge per phase | $Q = I \cdot PW$ | Must be below tissue safety limit |
| 9 | Charge density | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A_{electrode}}$ | Must be < **30 μC/cm²** (Shannon limit) |
| 10 | Stimulation pulse power | $P_{stim} = V \cdot I \cdot DF$ | Power budget for implant |
| 11 | Phosphene refresh rate (min) | $f_{refresh} \geq \dfrac{1}{t_{persist}}$ | Avoid flicker; typical ≥ 30 Hz |
| 12 | Total system latency budget | $T_{lat} = t_{acq} + t_{DSP} + t_{tx} + t_{stim}$ | Must be < **100 ms** for real-time perception |
| 13 | Log-polar mapping radius | $\rho = \ln(r/r_0)$ | Foveation model |
| 14 | Log-polar mapping angle | $\theta = q \cdot \phi$ | Foveation model |
| 15 | Gaussian spatial filter | $G(x,y) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} e^{-(x^2+y^2)/(2\sigma^2)}$ | Pre-processing smoothing kernel |
| 16 | Sobel edge gradient (x) | $G_x = S_x \ast I(x,y)$ | Edge detection |
| 17 | Optical flow (Lucas–Kanade) | $\vec{v} = (A^TA)^{-1} A^T \vec{b}$ | Motion vector estimation |
| 18 | Biphasic charge balance | $Q_{anodic} = Q_{cathodic}$ | Prevents electrode corrosion |
| 19 | Data rate (telemetry) | $R = N_e \cdot f_{stim} \cdot b$ | Bits per second over wireless link |
| 20 | Inductive link efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{k^2 Q_L Q_S}{(1+k^2 Q_L Q_S)}$ | Power transfer efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units to memorize for KTU exams:** $\mu$C for charge, $\mu$C/cm² for charge density, $\mu$A for current, $\mu$s for pulse width, Hz for frequency, mW for power dissipation, ms for latency.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same DSP-image-processing architecture is adapted in:

- **Second Sight Argus II Retinal Prosthesis** (epiretinal, 60 electrodes, 20° FOV)
- **Monash Phoenix99 Cortical Visual Prosthesis** (intracortical, 16-channel research version)
- **Illinois Intracortical Visual Prosthesis (ICVP)** — uses flexible polymer electrode arrays with 1024 channels
- **Hannover retinal implant** (subretinal, light-sensitive photodiodes)
- **Dobelle's cortical visual prosthesis** (early 2000s, 64 electrodes on the visual cortex)
- **Bioelectronic retinal research platforms** for optogenetic prosthetic vision

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Prosthetic Visual Acuity from Electrode Count and Field of View

**Problem statement:** A visual prosthesis uses an electrode array of $N_e = 100$ electrodes uniformly distributed across a visual field of view of $FOV = 20°$. Determine the **prosthetic visual acuity in pixels per degree** and compare it with the standard 20/20 vision benchmark.

### Step 1 — Define the relationship

We assume a uniform rectangular phosphene grid. The number of phosphenes per linear degree is:

$$\text{phosphenes/deg} = \dfrac{\sqrt{N_e}}{FOV}$$

**Explanation:** With $N_e$ total electrodes, the linear density of electrodes along one spatial dimension is $\sqrt{N_e}$ (square array assumption).

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

$$\text{phosphenes/deg} = \dfrac{\sqrt{100}}{20}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the square root

$$\sqrt{100} = 10$$

### Step 4 — Compute the ratio

$$\text{phosphenes/deg} = \dfrac{10}{20} = 0.5 \;\text{phosphenes/deg}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to common acuity units

Since **1 pixel per degree ≈ 20/20 vision**, the patient's effective acuity is:

$$VA_{prost} = 0.5 \times 20/20 \;\text{equivalent} = 20/800$$

### Step 6 — Interpret

The patient perceives a coarse **10 × 10 grayscale grid** over a 20° field — sufficient for navigation and large-object detection, but inadequate for reading normal print (which requires ≥ 10 pixels/degree).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Stimulation Pulse Width from Chronaxie

**Problem statement:** A retinal ganglion cell has a membrane time constant $\tau_m = 100\;\mu s$. Determine the **chronaxie time** (the pulse width requiring twice the rheobase current) and the **rheobase current** given a threshold voltage $V_{th} = 15\;\text{mV}$ and membrane resistance $R_m = 50\;\text{k}\Omega$.

### Step 1 — Recall the Weiss–Lapicque equation

The threshold current to elicit an action potential for a rectangular pulse of duration $t$ is:

$$I_{th}(t) = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$$

**Explanation:** As $t \to \infty$, $I_{th} \to I_{rh}$ (rheobase). As $t \to 0$, $I_{th} \to \infty$ (capacitive short).

### Step 2 — Apply the chronaxie definition

Chronaxie is the pulse width at which $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$:

$$2 I_{rh} = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t_c/\tau_m}}$$

### Step 3 — Cancel $I_{rh}$ and invert

$$2 = \dfrac{1}{1 - e^{-t_c/\tau_m}}$$

$$1 - e^{-t_c/\tau_m} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the exponent

$$e^{-t_c/\tau_m} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm

$$-\dfrac{t_c}{\tau_m} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln 2$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $t_c$

$$t_c = \tau_m \cdot \ln 2$$

### Step 7 — Substitute $\tau_m = 100\;\mu s$

$$t_c = 100 \times \ln 2 \;\mu s$$

### Step 8 — Evaluate

$$t_c = 100 \times 0.6931 \;\mu s \approx 69.3 \;\mu s$$

### Step 9 — Compute the rheobase

$$I_{rh} = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_m} = \dfrac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$I_{rh} = 3 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{A} = 0.3\;\mu\text{A}$$

### Step 10 — Interpret

The **optimal pulse width** for energy-efficient neural stimulation is approximately **70 μs**, requiring a current of **0.6 μA** (twice the rheobase). This minimizes charge injection and prevents tissue damage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Phosphene Refresh Rate and Frame Period

**Problem statement:** Cortical phosphenes persist for approximately $t_{persist} = 200\;\text{ms}$ before the patient perceives flicker. Determine the **minimum refresh rate** of the visual prosthetic display.

### Step 1 — Apply the flicker-fusion principle

To prevent the patient from perceiving discrete frames, the refresh frequency must satisfy:

$$f_{refresh} \geq \dfrac{1}{t_{persist}}$$

**Explanation:** If the time between successive stimulations of the same phosphene exceeds the persistence time, the patient perceives "flicker" — a discontinuous on/off pattern that is uncomfortable and reduces prosthetic utility.

### Step 2 — Substitute the persistence time

$$f_{refresh} \geq \dfrac{1}{200 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s}}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate

$$f_{refresh} \geq 5\;\text{Hz}$$

### Step 4 — Apply engineering safety margin

In practice, prosthetic systems operate at **30–60 Hz** to allow comfortable perception, accommodate inter-frame processing latency, and provide temporal smoothing. The chosen value is:

$$f_{refresh,\,design} = 60\;\text{Hz}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the design frame period

$$T_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{60} \approx 16.67\;\text{ms}$$

### Step 6 — Check latency budget

Available DSP + telemetry time per frame:

$$T_{budget} = T_{frame} - t_{stim\,settling} \approx 16.67 - 2 = 14.67\;\text{ms}$$

This is the **maximum allowable processing latency** per frame in the DSP pipeline.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Total System Power Budget

**Problem statement:** A 100-electrode cortical visual prosthesis operates at $f_{refresh} = 60\;\text{Hz}$, with each electrode receiving a biphasic pulse of amplitude $A = 100\;\mu\text{A}$, pulse width $PW = 200\;\mu s$ per phase, and compliance voltage $V = 5\;\text{V}$. Determine the **average stimulation power** assuming a duty factor $DF = 0.05$.

### Step 1 — Compute the charge per phase

$$Q = I \cdot PW = 100\;\mu\text{A} \times 200\;\mu s$$

$$Q = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{C} = 20\;\text{nC/phase}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the instantaneous power per electrode

$$P_{inst} = V \cdot I = 5\;\text{V} \times 100\;\mu\text{A} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{W} = 0.5\;\text{mW}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the average power per electrode

$$P_{avg,\,electrode} = P_{inst} \cdot DF = 0.5\;\text{mW} \times 0.05 = 25\;\mu\text{W}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the total power for 100 electrodes

$$P_{total,\,stim} = 100 \times 25\;\mu\text{W} = 2.5\;\text{mW}$$

### Step 5 — Add DSP and telemetry overhead

| Subsystem | Power |
|---|---|
| DSP processor (ASIC/FPGA) | **50 mW** |
| Telemetry (RF link) | **10 mW** |
| Stimulation driver | **2.5 mW** |
| Misc. biasing & housekeeping | **5 mW** |
| **Total** | **67.5 mW** |

### Step 6 — Interpret

The total implant power of **67.5 mW** is well within the safe dissipation limit for chronic cortical implants (typically < 100 mW to avoid thermal tissue damage; the **1°C tissue temperature rise rule** must be respected).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Telemetry Data Rate Requirement

**Problem statement:** A 1024-channel intracortical visual prosthesis (ICVP) stimulates each electrode at $f_{stim} = 100\;\text{Hz}$ with 16-bit pulse parameters. Compute the **minimum wireless data rate**.

### Step 1 — State the data rate equation

$$R = N_e \cdot f_{stim} \cdot b$$

**Explanation:** Each electrode independently requires $b$ bits of data per stimulation event, fired at frequency $f_{stim}$.

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

$$R = 1024 \times 100 \times 16$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate

$$R = 1{,}638{,}400\;\text{bits/s} \approx 1.64\;\text{Mbps}$$

### Step 4 — Apply protocol overhead

With ~30% overhead for error correction, framing, and synchronization:

$$R_{actual} = 1.64 \times 1.3 \approx 2.13\;\text{Mbps}$$

### Step 5 — Interpret

This data rate is achievable with modern **inductive/RF telemetry** (e.g., 13.56 MHz or 402–405 MHz MICS band) but mandates careful protocol design to maintain **BER < 10⁻⁶** for safety.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Trade-offs in Prosthetic Image Processing

| Design Parameter | Trade-off Partner | Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Spatial resolution ($N_e$) | Electrode impedance & power | Biocompatible electrode size |
| Frame rate ($f_{refresh}$) | Power dissipation & latency | Phosphene persistence |
| Pixel depth (bits) | Telemetry bandwidth | Wireless data rate |
| FOV (degrees) | Optical distortion | Camera lens & implant site |
| Stimulus amplitude | Charge density safety | **Shannon limit 30 μC/cm²** |
| Biphasic symmetry | Power efficiency | Charge balance ±5% |
| Image preprocessing complexity | DSP power & latency | < 100 ms total |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List and briefly explain the three principal phosphene mapping strategies used in DSP-based visual prosthetic systems. Under what condition is each strategy most appropriate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three principal phosphene mapping strategies are:

1. **Direct 1:1 Pixel-to-Electrode Mapping** *(1 mark)*: Each image pixel drives a single electrode. This produces a faithful spatial reproduction but requires a high electrode count. It is most appropriate when **$N_e \geq 1000$**, as in the **Illinois Intracortical Visual Prosthesis (ICVP)**.

2. **Log-Polar Foveated Mapping** *(1 mark)*: Image coordinates $(x, y)$ are transformed via $\rho = \ln(r/r_0)$, $\theta = q\phi$ to allocate high resolution centrally (fovea) and low resolution peripherally. This **mirrors the natural human retina** and is most appropriate for **simulating biological vision** with limited electrodes.

3. **Score-Based Saliency Mapping** *(1 mark)*: Only the top-K most salient image regions (edges, motion, faces) are rendered to phosphenes. This **minimizes power dissipation** and is most appropriate for **low-power, sparse-electrode implants** (e.g., 60-channel Argus II).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define chronaxie time. Why is it the optimal pulse width for charge-efficient neural stimulation in visual prostheses?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition** *(1 mark)*: Chronaxie time $t_c$ is the **minimum pulse width** at which a neural tissue requires **twice the rheobase current** to reach threshold. Mathematically, $t_c = \tau_m \cdot \ln 2 \approx 0.693\,\tau_m$.

- **Derivation context** *(1 mark)*: It is the inflection point of the **strength–duration curve** (Weiss–Lapicque) where the second derivative of threshold current with respect to pulse width is zero — i.e., the point of **minimum energy per pulse**.

- **Why optimal** *(1 mark)*: Operating at chronaxie delivers the **lowest charge per phase $Q$** required to excite the neuron, which **minimizes charge density** at the electrode–tissue interface, prevents electrochemical damage, and extends battery life of the implant.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** For each 14-mark question, two sub-parts are provided (7 marks each). Examiners award partial marks for diagrams, formula correctness, and unit usage.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete signal processing chain** of a DSP-based image processing system for visual prostheses. Identify the **function of each block** and the **latency contribution** of each stage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A cortical visual prosthesis uses a **10 × 10 electrode array** over a **20° field of view**. Calculate:
- (i) The **prosthetic visual acuity in pixels/degree** and equivalent Snellen rating.
- (ii) The **optimal pulse width** (chronaxie) given $\tau_m = 80\;\mu s$.
- (iii) The **rheobase current** given $V_{th} = 20\;\text{mV}$ and $R_m = 40\;\text{k}\Omega$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Block diagram (3 marks):**

Draw a 6-block pipeline with clear labels:
- (1) **Image sensor (CMOS/CCD)** — 1 mark
- (2) **DSP preprocessor** (filtering, normalization) — 0.5 mark
- (3) **Feature extraction** (edges, motion) — 0.5 mark
- (4) **Phosphene mapping** (log-polar, score-based) — 0.5 mark
- (5) **Stimulation parameter generator** (biphasic pulses) — 0.5 mark
- (6) **Telemetry + electrode driver** — 0.5 mark

**Step 2 — Function of each block with latency budget (3 marks):**

| Block | Function | Typical Latency |
|---|---|---|
| Image sensor | Convert light → digital frame | **< 5 ms** |
| DSP preprocessor | Noise reduction, histogram EQ | **< 10 ms** |
| Feature extraction | Edges, motion, saliency | **< 8 ms** |
| Phosphene mapping | Image → electrode coordinates | **< 3 ms** |
| Stimulus generator | Pulse parameter calculation | **< 2 ms** |
| Telemetry & driver | Wireless tx + electrode drive | **< 5 ms + ~50 ms neural** |

**Step 3 — Total latency check (1 mark):**

$$T_{lat,\,total} \approx 83\;\text{ms} < 100\;\text{ms} \;\;\checkmark$$

This meets the real-time perceptual constraint.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Visual acuity (3 marks):**

Phosphenes per degree:

$$\text{p/d} = \dfrac{\sqrt{N_e}}{FOV} = \dfrac{\sqrt{100}}{20} = 0.5\;\text{p/d}$$

Equivalent Snellen:

$$VA = 0.5 \times 20/20 = 20/800$$

**Interpretation:** Patient can detect large objects, navigate a room, but cannot read standard print.

**(ii) Chronaxie (2 marks):**

$$t_c = \tau_m \cdot \ln 2 = 80 \times 0.693 = 55.5\;\mu s$$

**(iii) Rheobase (2 marks):**

$$I_{rh} = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_m} = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{40 \times 10^{3}} = 0.5\;\mu\text{A}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the **Weiss–Lapicque strength–duration equation** for neural stimulation. Using this equation, show that the **chronaxie time** equals $\tau_m \ln 2$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **three principal image pre-processing operations** used in visual prosthetic DSP pipelines. For each, give the **mathematical form**, **engineering purpose**, and **impact on prosthetic perception**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Membrane model (1 mark):**

The neuronal membrane is modeled as a **resistor–capacitor parallel circuit** with membrane resistance $R_m$ and capacitance $C_m$, time constant $\tau_m = R_m C_m$.

**Step 2 — Apply a rectangular current pulse of amplitude $I$ and duration $t$ (1 mark):**

The membrane voltage evolves as:

$$V(t) = I R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Threshold condition (1 mark):**

An action potential fires when $V(t) = V_{th}$:

$$V_{th} = I_{th} R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $I_{th}$ (1 mark):**

$$I_{th}(t) = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$$

where $I_{rh} = V_{th}/R_m$ is the rheobase (minimum DC current).

**Step 5 — Apply the chronaxie definition (1 mark):**

Set $I_{th}(t_c) = 2 I_{rh}$:

$$2 I_{rh} = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t_c/\tau_m}}$$

$$1 - e^{-t_c/\tau_m} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

$$e^{-t_c/\tau_m} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Take natural log and solve (1 mark):**

$$-\dfrac{t_c}{\tau_m} = -\ln 2$$

$$\boxed{t_c = \tau_m \ln 2}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

The chronaxie is **independent of $V_{th}$ and $R_m$ individually** — it depends only on the membrane time constant. This makes it a **fundamental biophysical property** of the neuron, useful for designing implant-agnostic pulse widths.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

The three principal pre-processing operations are:

| Operation | Mathematical Form | Purpose | Perceptual Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian low-pass filtering** | $G(x,y) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} e^{-(x^2+y^2)/(2\sigma^2)}$ | Removes high-frequency sensor noise | Smooths phosphene grid, reduces jitter |
| **Histogram equalization** | $s_k = (L-1)\sum_{j=0}^{k} p_r(r_j)$ | Normalizes contrast across scenes | Improves phosphene brightness uniformity |
| **Sobel edge detection** | $G = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | Extracts object contours | Sharpens shape perception |

*Detailed marking:* 1.5 marks for each operation's math, 0.5 mark for purpose, 0.5 mark for impact.

**Examples of writing out the math (Gaussian — 1.5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Kernel definition:** A 2D Gaussian with standard deviation $\sigma$.

**Step 2 — Convolution with image:**

$$I_{filt}(x,y) = \sum_{u,v} G(u,v) \cdot I(x-u, y-v)$$

**Step 3 — Effect:** Attenuates spatial frequencies above $1/(2\pi\sigma)$ by the Gaussian's Fourier transform $\hat{G}(f_x, f_y) = e^{-2\pi^2\sigma^2(f_x^2 + f_y^2)}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in derived quantities (–1 to –2 marks):** Always write **$\mu$C/cm², $\mu$A, μs, ms, mW, p/deg**. Naked numbers = examiner's red pen.
>
> 2. **Confusing rheobase with threshold current (–1 mark):** Rheobase $I_{rh}$ is the **DC** current. Threshold $I_{th}(t)$ is **time-dependent** via Weiss–Lapicque.
>
> 3. **Omitting charge balance in biphasic pulses (–2 marks):** Every stimulus must be **charge-balanced** — examiners explicitly test this. State: $Q_{anodic} = Q_{cathodic}$.
>
> 4. **Missing the Shannon safety limit (–1 mark):** If you quote charge density without citing **Shannon's 30 μC/cm² limit**, marks are docked.
>
> 5. **Confusing retinal vs. cortical prosthesis:** Retinal = applied to surviving ganglion cells. Cortical = applied directly to V1. **Do not interchange** the stimulation targets.
>
> 6. **Ignoring latency budget:** A correct block diagram **without latency annotation** loses 1 mark. Always annotate the time budget per stage.
>
> 7. **Using Python/code in non-CS paper:** PEBMT743 is a Biomedical Engineering paper — **no code blocks allowed**. Use mathematical equations and block diagrams only.

---
