<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔌 Terminal Device Electronics — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Terminal Device Electronics** refers to the complete suite of active and passive electronic subsystems that reside at, or are directly coupled to, the **terminal electrode interface** of a neural prosthesis. It encompasses the hardware responsible for **stimulus generation, neural signal acquisition, signal conditioning, charge balancing, safety monitoring, and bidirectional telemetry** that bridges the biological tissue with the central processing unit of an implant.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is the **"front-end electronics"** sitting at the biotic–abiotic boundary — the physical point where engineered silicon meets living neural tissue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The terminal device electronics is governed by the *electrode–tissue interface model*, the *stimulus waveform parameters*, and the *safety envelope* defined by **charge per phase (Q)**, **charge density (k)**, and **charge per unit area per phase (D)**. A student must be able to derive, justify, and apply these parameters to real stimulator design problems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Handshake"

Imagine a translator standing between two people who speak completely different languages: one speaks "electrons" (the silicon chip) and the other speaks "ions" (the neuron). The translator's job is to:

1. **Listen carefully** without shouting (recording) — using ultra-low-noise amplifiers.
2. **Speak gently but clearly** (stimulation) — using controlled, charge-balanced current pulses.
3. **Never hurt either party** — enforcing strict safety limits on how much charge is delivered.

This translator is the **terminal device electronics**. It must operate reliably inside a salty, warm, electrically noisy, and mechanically dynamic environment — the human body — for years, often decades.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The terminal device is NOT just the electrode. The electrode is the *passive* contact; the electronics is the *active* "brain" of that contact. Both must be co-designed.

## 1.3 Standard Constants & Safety Metrics

The following are the **universally cited safety limits** that govern every design decision in terminal device electronics:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Safe Limit | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Charge per phase | Q | ≤ **0.4** | µC |
| Charge density | k | ≤ **30** | µC/cm² |
| Charge per unit area (Shannon limit) | log(D) | ≤ **1.85** | — |
| Stimulation frequency | f | **20 – 200** | Hz |
| Pulse width | PW | **50 – 500** | µs |
| Electrode impedance (1 kHz) | Z | **1 – 10** | kΩ |

These are **bold-emphasized** because KTU examiners explicitly test numerical compliance with these values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis — High-Yield Concept Map

## 2.1 The Electrode–Tissue Interface (ETI) Model

The ETI is the **cornerstone** of terminal device electronics. It is modelled as a **parallel RC network in series with a solution resistance (Rs)** — a classic **Randles equivalent circuit**.

### Why does this matter?
Because the electrode does NOT behave as a pure resistor. At the metal–electrolyte boundary, a **Helmholtz double layer** forms — essentially a nano-scale capacitor — which dominates the impedance at low frequencies.

### Operational Breakdown — How Stimulus Current Flows

1. **Driver stage (current source)** pushes electrons into the electrode.
2. Current crosses the **double-layer capacitance (Cdl)**.
3. Current flows through the **tissue bulk (Rs)** to the return electrode.
4. **Faradaic reactions** at the interface transfer charge via reduction/oxidation.
5. The **charge-balancing circuit** removes residual DC by applying an equal-and-opposite pulse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why biphasic pulses?** A monophasic pulse leaves a **net DC residual** at the electrode, causing electrolysis of water → H₂ and O₂ gas bubbles, pH shifts, and tissue damage. Biphasic, charge-balanced pulses reverse the charge and prevent this.

## 2.2 Stimulator Topologies — Voltage-Controlled vs Current-Controlled

| Feature | Voltage-Controlled | Current-Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| Output quantity | Constant Voltage (V) | Constant Current (I) |
| Charge control | Indirect (depends on Z) | **Direct (I × t = Q)** |
| Tissue response | Variable (Z changes over time) | **Predictable** |
| Safety | Less predictable | **Preferred for neural implants** |
| Complexity | Simpler | Requires Howland or op-amp current source |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expectation:** When asked "which is preferred and why", the model answer is **current-controlled stimulation** because charge delivery is independent of the variable electrode–tissue impedance — directly enabling safe, predictable neural activation.

## 2.3 Charge Balancing — The Three Canonical Methods

1. **Capacitor-Coupled (DC-blocking):** A series capacitor between stimulator and electrode blocks any DC offset. Simple, but C must be large enough to avoid saturation.
2. **Active Charge Balancing (Biphasic symmetrical):** Equal cathodic + anodic phases with no interphase delay (or a small one).
3. **Passive Discharge (Switched-resistor):** A resistor is switched across the electrode after the stimulus to bleed off residual charge.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Charge per phase | Coulombs (C) |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Charge density | C/cm² |
| 3 | $D = k \cdot \text{PW}$ | Charge per unit area per phase | µC·µs/cm² |
| 4 | $V_{\text{compliance}} = I \cdot Z_{\max} + V_{\text{interface}}$ | Required supply voltage | Volts |
| 5 | $P = V_{\text{compliance}} \cdot I$ | Static power dissipation | Watts |
| 6 | $Z = R_s + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{dl}} \parallel R_{ct}$ | Full ETI impedance | Ohms |
| 7 | $\log(D) + \log(Q/A) \le 1.85$ | Shannon's safety envelope | dimensionless |
| 8 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{C_{dl}}$ | Voltage developed across double layer | Volts |
| 9 | $E_{\text{stim}} = V_{\text{compliance}} \cdot I \cdot t$ | Energy per pulse | Joules |
| 10 | $f_{\text{Nyquist}} \ge 2 f_{\text{neural,max}}$ | ADC sampling requirement | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table notation rule:** All absolute-value and division bars use `\vert` / `\mid` (rendered as $Q/A$ above) to preserve markdown table integrity. Do NOT write `$Q/A$` with a pipe character inside a table.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Terminal device electronics is the enabling technology behind:

- **Cochlear implants** (CIs) — 22–24 channels of current stimulation on the auditory nerve.
- **Deep Brain Stimulators (DBS)** — high-frequency (130 Hz) biphasic pulses for Parkinson's.
- **Retinal prostheses (Argus II, PRIMA)** — 60–1500 electrode arrays stimulating the retina.
- **Sacral nerve stimulators** — for bladder control.
- **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCIs)** — e.g., Neuralink, Utah array recording front-ends.

Every one of these is bound by the same safety envelope and the same fundamental ETI physics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Exhaustive Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Derivation: Charge Per Phase and Charge Density

**Given:**
- Stimulus current amplitude: $I$ (in Amperes)
- Pulse width: $PW$ (in seconds)
- Electrode geometric surface area: $A$ (in cm²)

**Step 1 — Define current as rate of charge flow.**

By the fundamental definition of electric current:

$$I = \frac{dQ}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over the duration of a single phase.**

Assuming $I$ is constant during the cathodic phase (a standard rectangular pulse):

$$I = \frac{Q}{PW} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = I \cdot PW$$

**Step 3 — Express in practical units.**

For neural stimulation, currents are typically 10 µA – 1 mA and pulse widths 50 – 500 µs:

$$Q \;[\mu C] = I \;[\mu A] \times PW \;[\mu s] \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4 — Define charge density.**

$$k = \frac{Q}{A}$$

with $A$ in cm², giving $k$ in µC/cm². The KTU safety limit is $k \le 30\ \mu C/cm^2$ for safe chronic stimulation.

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-style).**

> A Pt-Ir microelectrode with $A = 0.01\ \text{cm}^2$ is driven with $I = 100\ \mu A$ for $PW = 200\ \mu s$. Find the charge per phase and the charge density.

$$Q = 100\ \mu A \times 200\ \mu s = 20{,}000 \times 10^{-12}\ C = 0.02\ \mu C$$

$$k = \frac{0.02\ \mu C}{0.01\ \text{cm}^2} = 2\ \mu C/\text{cm}^2$$

Both values are well below the safety limit ✅.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shannon's Safety Envelope

The Shannon limit combines charge density and charge per phase to capture **both** mechanisms of neural damage (electrochemical and excitotoxic).

**Step 1 — Define the combined metric.**

Shannon defined the dimensionless quantity $S$:

$$S = \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{k}{1\ \mu C/\text{cm}^2}\right)$$

where $D = k \times PW$ in $(\mu C \cdot \mu s / cm^2)$ and $k$ is in $\mu C/cm^2$.

**Step 2 — Apply the empirical boundary.**

Empirically, neural damage is avoided when:

$$S \le 1.85$$

**Step 3 — Verify with the example from §3.1.**

$D = 2\ \mu C/cm^2 \times 200\ \mu s = 400\ \mu C \cdot \mu s / cm^2$

$$\log_{10}(400) = 2.602$$

$$\log_{10}(2) = 0.301$$

$$S = 2.602 + 0.301 = 2.903$$

> [!WARNING]
> $S = 2.903 > 1.85$ — this combination would be **unsafe** despite $k$ alone being below 30 µC/cm²! This is the classic KTU "trap" question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Compliance Voltage

The stimulator's power supply must be high enough to drive the desired current through the maximum expected load.

**Step 1 — Model the load.**

The electrode–tissue interface impedance at the stimulation frequency is $Z_{\max}$ (worst case, usually at low frequency where Cdl is high).

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's law to the stimulus path.**

$$V_{\text{required}} = I \cdot Z_{\max}$$

**Step 3 — Account for the interface overpotential.**

Charge transfer at the metal–electrolyte interface requires a small extra voltage $V_{\text{int}}$ (typically 0.5 – 0.7 V):

$$V_{\text{compliance}} = I \cdot Z_{\max} + V_{\text{int}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

For $I = 1\ mA$, $Z_{\max} = 5\ k\Omega$, $V_{\text{int}} = 0.6\ V$:

$$V_{\text{compliance}} = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (5 \times 10^3) + 0.6 = 5.6\ V$$

A supply rail of **at least 6 V** is needed — a critical KTU design consideration.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Energy Per Stimulus Pulse

$$E_{\text{pulse}} = V_{\text{compliance}} \cdot I \cdot PW$$

For the example above with $PW = 200\ \mu s$:

$$E_{\text{pulse}} = 5.6\ V \cdot 1\ mA \cdot 200\ \mu s = 5.6 \times 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{\text{pulse}} = 1.12 \times 10^{-6}\ J = 1.12\ \mu J$$

For 100 pulses/s, the average power is:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = 1.12\ \mu J \times 100 / s = 112\ \mu W$$

This justifies the need for **highly efficient switch-mode stimulators** in chronic implants.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🧩 Block-Level Architecture — Mermaid Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Implantable Terminal-Device Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Coil RF Power] --> B[RF Rectifier and Regulator]
    B --> C[Internal Power Management Unit]
    C --> D[DSP Control Core]
    D --> E[DAC Stimulus Waveform Generator]
    E --> F[Current Driver Stage]
    F --> G[Charge Balancing Switch Matrix]
    G --> H[Electrode Tissue Interface]
    H --> I[Neural Tissue]
    H --> J[Neural Amplifier Front End]
    J --> K[Bandpass Filter 300Hz to 7kHz]
    K --> L[16 bit ADC]
    L --> D
    D --> M[Back Telemetry Uplink]
    M --> N[External Receiver]

    classDef power fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef signal fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef safety fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef bio fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000

    class A,B,C power
    class D,E,F,L,M,N signal
    class G safety
    class H,I bio
```

## 4.2 Stimulator Output Stage — Functional Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DAC Input] --> B[Voltage to Current Converter]
    B --> C[Cathodic Phase Switch]
    C --> D[Anodic Phase Switch]
    D --> E[DC Blocking Capacitor]
    E --> F[Electrode]
    F --> G[Tissue Return Path]
    G --> H[Charge Balance Monitor]
    H --> I[Safety Shutdown Comparator]
    I --> J[Microcontroller Interrupt]

    classDef drive fill:#ddeeff,stroke:#003366
    classDef protect fill:#ffdddd,stroke:#990000
    classDef bio fill:#ddffdd,stroke:#006600

    class A,B,C,D drive
    class E,H,I,J protect
    class F,G bio
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow During a Single Biphasic Pulse

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DSP as DSP Controller
    participant DAC as DAC Waveform Gen
    participant DRV as Current Driver
    participant ELEC as Electrode Tissue
    participant MON as Charge Monitor

    DSP->>DAC: Set cathodic amplitude I and width PW
    DAC->>DRV: Analog command voltage Vcmd
    DRV->>ELEC: Sink current I for time PW
    Note over ELEC: Cathodic phase delivers Q = I times PW
    DRV->>ELEC: Source current I for time PW
    Note over ELEC: Anodic phase removes Q returning to neutral
    ELEC->>MON: Electrode voltage sampled
    MON->>DSP: Residual DC check
    DSP->>DSP: Abort or continue based on threshold
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram style?** Per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard, all node IDs are alphanumeric (A, B, C…) and labels contain only plain text — no bold, italics, or HTML inside the double-quoted strings. This guarantees clean rendering on GitHub, VS Code, and the KTU e-learning portal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define "charge balancing" in a neural stimulator. Why is it mandatory in chronic implantable systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Charge balancing is the technique of ensuring that the **net DC current delivered to the electrode–tissue interface over one complete stimulation cycle is zero**. It is implemented via symmetric biphasic current pulses (cathodic followed by anodic of equal charge $Q = I \cdot PW$) or by using a series DC-blocking capacitor. It is **mandatory** because any DC residual causes electrochemical reactions at the electrode (water electrolysis, metal ion dissolution, pH shifts) leading to tissue damage and electrode corrosion. The Shannon limit framework (Section 3.2) is grounded in the prevention of such damage.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between voltage-controlled and current-controlled stimulation. State which is preferred for neural implants.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Voltage-controlled | Current-controlled |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Fixed V | Fixed I |
| Charge Q | Depends on Z(t) | Direct: $Q = I \cdot PW$ |
| Safety | Lower — Z varies with time and tissue response | Higher — predictable |
| KTU preference | Not preferred | **Preferred for neural implants** |

The **current-controlled topology** is preferred because the electrode–tissue impedance $Z$ is time-varying, encapsulation-dependent, and stimulus-dependent. Direct control of $I$ guarantees precise charge delivery independent of $Z$, which is essential for reproducible neural activation and meeting the Shannon safety envelope.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [Adapted from KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**A(a) [7 marks].** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a fully implantable current-controlled neural stimulator. Explain the role of every block.

**Model Solution:**

A current-controlled neural stimulator consists of the following functional blocks, mapped 1:1 to the diagram in §4.1 and §4.2 of these notes:

1. **Power Management Unit (PMU):** Receives RF energy via inductive link, rectifies and regulates it to provide stable supply rails (e.g., 5 V logic, ±6 V compliance).
2. **DSP / Microcontroller Core:** Generates timing, stimulus parameters, executes safety algorithms, and handles telemetry.
3. **Waveform Generator (DAC):** Produces a precise voltage command corresponding to the desired current amplitude.
4. **Voltage-to-Current Converter:** Typically a Howland current pump or op-amp + transistor network that converts the DAC voltage into a precisely regulated current $I$, independent of load impedance.
5. **H-Bridge / Switch Matrix:** Directs the current to flow cathodically (sink) or anodically (source) into the electrode — the basis of the biphasic waveform.
6. **DC-Blocking Capacitor:** Hardware-level safety; guarantees zero net DC even if the digital charge balance fails.
7. **Charge Monitor:** Samples electrode voltage after each phase; if residual voltage exceeds a threshold (typically ±50 mV), it triggers a safety discharge cycle.
8. **Back-Telemetry:** Encodes impedance, charge, and status data onto the inductive link for the external reader.

[Each block mapped to 1 mark ≈ 7 marks total.]

---

**A(b) [7 marks].** A platinum-iridium microelectrode of geometric area $A = 0.005\ \text{cm}^2$ is stimulated with a symmetric biphasic current pulse of $I = 250\ \mu A$ and $PW = 300\ \mu s$. Calculate (i) charge per phase, (ii) charge density, and (iii) verify Shannon's safety limit.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**(i) Charge per phase:**

$$Q = I \cdot PW = 250\ \mu A \times 300\ \mu s = 75{,}000 \times 10^{-12}\ C = 0.075\ \mu C$$

**(ii) Charge density:**

$$k = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.075\ \mu C}{0.005\ \text{cm}^2} = 15\ \mu C/\text{cm}^2$$

Since $k = 15 \le 30\ \mu C/\text{cm}^2$ — within safe limit ✅.

**(iii) Shannon limit check:**

$$D = k \times PW = 15\ \mu C/\text{cm}^2 \times 300\ \mu s = 4500\ \mu C \cdot \mu s / \text{cm}^2$$

$$\log_{10}(D) = \log_{10}(4500) = 3.653$$

$$\log_{10}(k) = \log_{10}(15) = 1.176$$

$$S = 3.653 + 1.176 = 4.83$$

> [!WARNING]
> **$S = 4.83 \gg 1.85$ — UNSAFE combination despite $k$ being individually safe!**
> This is a classic KTU trap. The student must reduce $I$ or $PW$ to bring $S$ below 1.85.

[Each sub-calculation ≈ 2–3 marks + final interpretation = 7 marks.]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**B(a) [7 marks].** Explain the Randles equivalent circuit model of the electrode–tissue interface. Derive the impedance expression and discuss its frequency dependence.

**Model Solution:**

The Randles model comprises three elements:

- **$R_s$ (solution/series resistance):** Bulk tissue resistance between the working and reference electrodes.
- **$C_{dl}$ (double-layer capacitance):** Models the Helmholtz double layer at the metal–electrolyte boundary, in the range of **nF to µF** depending on electrode area.
- **$R_{ct}$ (charge-transfer resistance):** Models Faradaic current paths; large for ideal (non-Faradaic) electrodes like TiN or IrOx.

**Impedance expression:**

$$Z(\omega) = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl}}$$

**Frequency behavior:**

- At **low frequency** ($\omega \to 0$): $Z \approx R_s + R_{ct}$ — dominated by charge transfer.
- At **high frequency** ($\omega \to \infty$): $Z \approx R_s$ — capacitor shorts out $R_{ct}$.
- At **intermediate frequency**: a constant-phase-element (CPE) behavior emerges, with a phase angle between 0° and −90°.

**Engineering significance:** The frequency dependence dictates the choice of stimulation frequency. Most neural stimulators operate between **20 Hz and 1 kHz** to remain in a regime where $C_{dl}$ dominates and charge transfer is minimized.

[Block diagram, formula derivation, frequency regime discussion = 7 marks.]

---

**B(b) [7 marks].** Design the compliance voltage for a stimulator driving $I = 800\ \mu A$ through a maximum tissue impedance of $Z_{\max} = 8\ k\Omega$. The interface overpotential is $0.65\ V$. What supply rail is required? If the pulse width is $250\ \mu s$ and stimulation rate is $50\ Hz$, compute the average power dissipation.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**(i) Compliance voltage:**

$$V_{\text{compliance}} = I \cdot Z_{\max} + V_{\text{int}}$$

$$V_{\text{compliance}} = (800 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (8 \times 10^3) + 0.65 = 6.4 + 0.65 = 7.05\ V$$

**Recommended supply rail:** A standard **±8 V or 9 V single rail** to give ≥1 V headroom.

**(ii) Energy per pulse:**

$$E_{\text{pulse}} = V_{\text{compliance}} \cdot I \cdot PW = 7.05 \cdot 800\ \mu A \cdot 250\ \mu s$$

$$E_{\text{pulse}} = 7.05 \times 800 \times 10^{-6} \times 250 \times 10^{-6} = 1.41 \times 10^{-6}\ J = 1.41\ \mu J$$

**(iii) Average power:**

$$P_{\text{avg}} = E_{\text{pulse}} \times f_{\text{stim}} = 1.41\ \mu J \times 50\ Hz = 70.5\ \mu W$$

**Conclusion:** The stimulator consumes about **70.5 µW** on average — easily supplied by an RF inductive link of a few mW.

[Each sub-calculation ≈ 2–3 marks + design justification = 7 marks.]

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Terminal Device Electronics:**
> 1. **Forgetting the overpotential term** $V_{\text{int}}$ in compliance voltage — costs ~2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing charge density $k$ with charge $Q$** — they have different units; examiners will deduct.
> 3. **Assuming Shannon's limit only involves $k$** — it is a *combined* metric; using $k$ alone is incomplete.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion** in $Q = I \cdot PW$: always convert µA·µs to Coulombs by multiplying by $10^{-12}$.
> 5. **Drawing the biphasic waveform as monophasic** in diagrams — instant 1-mark penalty.
> 6. **Not specifying "current-controlled"** when justifying neural implant preference — partial credit only.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Terminal device electronics** is the active hardware that sits at the electrode–tissue interface — encompassing stimulation, recording, charge balancing, and safety.
- The **electrode–tissue interface** is modelled as a **Randles circuit** with $R_s$, $C_{dl}$, and $R_{ct}$.
- **Current-controlled stimulation is preferred** over voltage-controlled because charge delivery $Q = I \cdot PW$ is independent of time-varying $Z$.
- **Charge balancing** (symmetric biphasic pulses or DC-blocking capacitors) is non-negotiable to prevent tissue damage.
- **Key safety limits:** $Q \le 0.4\ \mu C$, $k \le 30\ \mu C/\text{cm}^2$, and **Shannon's** $S \le 1.85$.
- **Compliance voltage** must account for both the ohmic drop and the interface overpotential: $V = I \cdot Z_{\max} + V_{\text{int}}$.
- **Power budget** for typical implants is in the **10s of µW** range — justifying wireless RF powering.
- **Neural recording front-ends** need bandwidth of **300 Hz – 7 kHz** and noise floor **< 5 µV rms** to capture spikes and LFPs.
- **Charge monitor + safety shutdown** is a mandatory redundant safety layer in all chronic implants.
- The **H-bridge / switch matrix** is the canonical topology for generating biphasic waveforms from a unipolar supply.
- **Biphasic pulses** can be *symmetric* (equal phases) or *asymmetric* (cathodic-leading, anodic-leading) — cochlear implants typically use cathodic-leading.
- **Interphase gap** (a few µs of zero current) reduces the simultaneous charge overlap and improves neural selectivity.
- **Hermetic packaging** (titanium, ceramic) is required to protect the electronics from the body's saline environment for decades.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Upper Limb Prosthetics and Pre-Prosthetic Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Upper Limb Prosthetics and Pre-Prosthetic Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Upper Limb Prosthetics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Upper Limb Prosthetics** is the specialized branch of rehabilitation engineering concerned with the design, fabrication, fitting, and clinical evaluation of artificial devices (prostheses) that replace all or part of a missing upper extremity due to congenital deficiency, trauma, disease (e.g., diabetes, vascular disorders, malignancy), or surgical amputation.

According to the **International Standards Organization (ISO 8548-1, 8549)**, an upper limb prosthesis is defined as a *externally applied device used to replace, wholly or in part, an absent or deficient upper limb segment, with the primary objectives of restoring form (cosmesis), function (prehension, manipulation), and psychological well-being of the user.*

### 1.1.1 Levels of Upper Limb Amputation (KTU Clinical Classification)

The level of amputation strictly dictates the prosthesis prescription, socket design, and control strategy. KTU examiners frequently test these levels:

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized Amputation Levels (Proximal → Distal):**
> 1. **Forequarter (Interscapulothoracic)** – Removal of entire arm, scapula, and clavicle
> 2. **Shoulder Disarticulation (SD)** – Separation at the glenohumeral joint
> 3. **Transhumeral (TH) / Above-Elbow (AE)** – Amputation between shoulder and elbow
> 4. **Elbow Disarticulation (ED)** – Separation at the elbow joint
> 5. **Transradial (TR) / Below-Elbow (BU)** – Amputation between elbow and wrist
> 6. **Wrist Disarticulation (WD)** – Separation at the radiocarpal joint
> 7. **Transcarpal** – Through the carpal bones
> 8. **Transmetacarpal** – Through the metacarpals
> 9. **Transphalangeal / Partial Hand** – Through the phalanges

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Tailored Suit"**
> Think of an upper limb prosthesis like a *custom-tailored business suit*. Before the tailor (prosthetist) cuts any fabric (fabricates a socket), he must first take detailed measurements (pre-prosthetic assessment), understand the client's lifestyle (occupation, hobbies, goals), check the client's posture (muscle strength, range of motion), and assess what kind of work the suit will be used for (body-powered vs. myoelectric). A suit made without these measurements will look terrible and be uncomfortable — similarly, a prosthesis prescribed without thorough pre-prosthetic assessment will be rejected by the amputee (a phenomenon clinically termed *prosthesis abandonment*).

### 1.3 Definition of Pre-Prosthetic Assessment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Prosthetic Assessment** is the systematic, multidisciplinary clinical evaluation performed *before* the prescription and fabrication of an upper limb prosthesis. It encompasses the patient's physical condition (residual limb status, joint range of motion, muscle strength, sensation), psychological readiness, vocational and avocational goals, social support, financial considerations, and the prescription of interim measures (e.g., compression therapy, desensitization, range-of-motion exercises).

### 1.4 Statistical & Clinical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Amputation Incidence**: Approximately **1.5 to 2.0 per 10,000** population in India experience upper limb loss annually (limb-specific incidence). Globally, trauma accounts for **~75%** of upper limb amputations.
> - **Prosthesis Abandonment Rate**: Approximately **30% to 50%** of upper limb prostheses are abandoned, primarily due to inadequate pre-prosthetic assessment, poor fit, or mismatched prescription.
> - **Ideal Prosthetic Fitting Time**: Post-surgical prosthetic fitting is ideally initiated within **30 to 90 days** for traumatic cases once wound healing is complete.
> - **Standard Residual Limb Shrinkage**: Compression therapy aims for approximately **3%–5% volume reduction** per week until limb volume stabilizes (typically 4–6 months post-amputation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-Prosthetic Assessment — Detailed Components

Pre-prosthetic assessment is **not a single event** but a multi-stage process that begins immediately post-amputation and continues until final prosthetic fitting. The clinical pathway is structured as follows:

### 2.1.1 Stage 1: Immediate Post-Operative Phase (0–3 weeks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Objectives:**
> 1. **Wound Healing Management** — Sterile dressing, edema control
> 2. **Pain Management** — Phantom limb pain (PLP) and residual limb pain control
> 3. **Rigid Removable Dressing (RRD)** or **Immediate Postoperative Prosthesis (IPOP)** application
> 4. **Desensitization Techniques** — Tapping, vibration, graded textures

### 2.1.2 Stage 2: Pre-Prosthetic Training Phase (3–12 weeks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Interventions:**
> - **Residual Limb Compression Therapy**: Elastic bandages, shrinker socks, or pneumatic post-amputation mobility (PPAM) aids
> - **Range of Motion (ROM) Exercises**: Target joints (e.g., shoulder, elbow, wrist)
> - **Strengthening Exercises**: Particularly of antagonist muscle groups
> - **Single-Handed Activity Training**: ADL (Activities of Daily Living) training
> - **Psychological Counseling**: Body image, grief, motivation
> - **Vocational Assessment**: Identifying work requirements

### 2.1.3 Stage 3: Prosthetic Prescription & Casting (12+ weeks)

This stage culminates in the **Prescription Writing** — a formal document that includes:
- Amputation level
- Patient demographics
- Specific component selection (terminal device, wrist unit, elbow unit, shoulder unit, control system, suspension, socket design)
- Material specifications

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all clinically and mathematically relevant formulas for upper limb prosthetics assessment. These are high-yield for KTU numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Residual Limb Volume Change** | $V_f = V_0 \cdot (1 - r)^n$ | $V_0$ = initial volume; $r$ = weekly shrinkage rate (~0.03–0.05); $n$ = number of weeks |
| 2 | **Body-Powered Cable Force Ratio** | $F_{TD} = F_{input} \cdot \frac{L_{lever}}{L_{cable}}$ | $F_{TD}$ = terminal device force; $L_{lever}$ = excursion lever arm; $L_{cable}$ = cable lever arm |
| 3 | **Harness Excursion Ratio (HER)** | $HER = \frac{\Delta L_{cable}}{\Delta L_{ROM}}$ | $\Delta L_{cable}$ = cable travel (mm); $\Delta L_{ROM}$ = body joint ROM (°) |
| 4 | **Myoelectric Signal (MES) Bandwidth** | $f_{cutoff} = 20\ \text{Hz}$ to $500\ \text{Hz}$ | Standard clinical filtering range |
| 5 | **Myoelectric Signal Amplitude** | $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$ | Typically **20 µV – 5 mV** at skin surface |
| 6 | **Terminal Device Opening Width (Hook)** | $W_{TD} = f(L_{finger})$ | Function of finger length; typically **5 cm – 9 cm** for adult hooks |
| 7 | **Static Grip Force (Hook-type TD)** | $F_{grip} = k \cdot x$ | $k$ = spring constant (N/mm); $x$ = displacement |
| 8 | **Socket Circumference Reduction** | $C_{socket} = C_{limb} \cdot (1 - \alpha)$ | $\alpha$ = reduction factor; typically **0.95–0.97** (3–5% negative) |
| 9 | **Prosthetic Forearm Length** | $L_{forearm} = L_{intact\ forearm} \pm 1\ \text{cm}$ | Symmetry rule for unilateral amputee |
| 10 | **Suspension Strap Tension** | $T_{strap} = \mu \cdot N$ | $\mu$ = friction coefficient; $N$ = normal force |
| 11 | **Cosmesis Acceptance Index (CAI)** | $CAI = \frac{\sum S_{cosmesis}}{N_{criteria}} \times 100\%$ | Used in patient satisfaction surveys |
| 12 | **Range of Motion (Shoulder Flexion)** | $\theta_{sf} \geq 0°\ \text{to}\ 180°$ | Minimum required for body-powered prosthesis |
| 13 | **Power Consumption (Myoelectric)** | $P = V \cdot I_{avg}$ | Typical battery: **7.2 V, 2000 mAh** Li-ion |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why These Concepts Matter to Biomedical Engineers:**
> 1. **Socket Design Engineers** use residual limb volume formulas to predict socket fit and time-to-refit cycles.
> 2. **Myoelectric System Designers** require accurate MES amplitude and frequency data to set amplifier gain and filter cutoffs.
> 3. **Prosthetists** apply excursion ratios to optimize harness geometry for body-powered devices.
> 4. **Rehabilitation Researchers** use acceptance indices to evaluate new prosthetic technologies against patient satisfaction metrics.

## 2.4 Why Pre-Prosthetic Assessment is the Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "3-Pillar" Model of Prosthetic Success:**
> 1. **Patient** (motivation, physical capacity, goals)
> 2. **Prosthetist** (clinical skills, fabrication quality)
> 3. **Prosthesis** (mechanical appropriateness, fit, function)
>
> If **any** pillar is weak — particularly the **Patient** pillar, which is established during pre-prosthetic assessment — the entire prosthetic outcome fails. KTU examiners often frame this as: *"A brilliant prosthesis prescribed for an unmotivated or poorly prepared patient is a clinical failure."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Residual Limb Volume Reduction Model

> This derivation explains the *exponential decay* of residual limb volume under compression therapy, used clinically to determine when the limb is ready for definitive socket casting.

### Step 1: Define Initial Conditions

Let $V_0$ be the residual limb volume immediately post-amputation (after suture removal). Under consistent compression therapy, the volume decreases at a fractional rate $r$ per week.

### Step 2: Set Up the Differential Equation

The rate of volume change is proportional to the current volume:

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = -r \cdot V(t)
$$

Where $r$ is the **volume decay constant** (per week), and $V(t)$ is the limb volume at time $t$ (in weeks).

### Step 3: Solve the Differential Equation

Separating variables and integrating:

$$
\int_{V_0}^{V(t)} \frac{1}{V}\,dV = -r \int_0^t dt
$$

Applying the limits:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{V(t)}{V_0}\right) = -r \cdot t
$$

### Step 4: Final Closed-Form Solution

Exponentiating both sides:

$$
V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-r \cdot t}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Discrete-Week Form (Clinical Utility)

For a discrete number of weeks $n$, the recurrence relation becomes:

$$
V_n = V_0 \cdot (1 - r)^n
$$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

> **Example:** A 35-year-old male with a transradial amputation has an initial residual limb volume of $V_0 = 800\ \text{cm}^3$. The clinician applies compression therapy with an observed weekly shrinkage rate of $r = 0.04$ (4% per week). Calculate the residual limb volume after **6 weeks**.

**Solution:**

$$
V_6 = 800 \cdot (1 - 0.04)^6
$$

$$
V_6 = 800 \cdot (0.96)^6
$$

$$
V_6 = 800 \cdot 0.78297...
$$

$$
V_6 \approx 626.4\ \text{cm}^3
$$

> **Result:** After 6 weeks of compression therapy, the residual limb volume has reduced to approximately **626.4 cm³** — a total volume reduction of **~21.7%**. The limb is approaching the volume-stability threshold (typically a <2% change between consecutive weekly measurements) suitable for definitive socket fabrication.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Body-Powered Cable Force Transmission

> This derivation quantifies how the force applied by the patient's shoulder/scapular motion is transmitted to the terminal device (e.g., hook) via the cable-and-harness system.

### Step 1: Define the Mechanical Model

The body-powered system consists of:
- A **figure-8 or 9 harness** anchored on the contralateral axilla
- A **control cable** routed over the shoulder
- A **terminal device** (hook or hand) attached at the distal end

### Step 2: Apply Lever Mechanics

The shoulder acts as a first-class lever. The cable attaches at a distance $L_{lever}$ from the shoulder pivot; the patient's musculature applies input force $F_{input}$.

For mechanical equilibrium about the shoulder pivot:

$$
F_{input} \cdot L_{lever} = F_{cable} \cdot L_{cable}
$$

### Step 3: Solve for Cable Force

$$
F_{cable} = F_{input} \cdot \frac{L_{lever}}{L_{cable}}
$$

### Step 4: Account for Friction and Efficiency

In practice, friction in the cable housing reduces force transmission. Let $\eta$ be the system efficiency (typically **0.7 to 0.9** for a well-maintained system):

$$
F_{cable} = \eta \cdot F_{input} \cdot \frac{L_{lever}}{L_{cable}}
$$

### Step 5: Terminal Device Output Force

If the cable directly drives the terminal device fingers:

$$
F_{TD} = F_{cable} = \eta \cdot F_{input} \cdot \frac{L_{lever}}{L_{cable}}
$$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

> **Example:** A body-powered transhumeral prosthesis has the following parameters: shoulder input force $F_{input} = 50\ \text{N}$, lever arm ratio $\frac{L_{lever}}{L_{cable}} = 1.5$, and system efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Calculate the force delivered at the terminal device.

**Solution:**

$$
F_{TD} = 0.85 \cdot 50 \cdot 1.5
$$

$$
F_{TD} = 63.75\ \text{N}
$$

> **Result:** The terminal device receives approximately **63.75 N** of force — sufficient to lift a **6.5 kg** object (assuming $1\ \text{kg} \approx 9.81\ \text{N}$ under gravity) in pinch grip.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Prosthetic Control System Selection

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix guides prosthetic prescription based on pre-prosthetic assessment findings — a high-yield KTU topic.

| Patient Factor | Body-Powered | Myoelectric | Hybrid | Cosmetic/Passive |
|----------------|--------------|-------------|--------|-----------------|
| **High physical demand (laborer)** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★ |
| **Low physical demand (desk job)** | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ |
| **Below-elbow amputation** | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |
| **Above-elbow amputation** | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ |
| **Limited shoulder ROM** | ★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Poor skin condition (sweating)** | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Younger patient (<30 yrs)** | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ |
| **Older patient (>60 yrs)** | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |
| **Cosmesis priority** | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Cost constraint** | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Heavy moisture environment** | ★★★★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |

### Interpretation Guide
- ★★★★★ = **Excellent** choice
- ★★★★ = **Very good** choice
- ★★★ = **Good** choice
- ★★ = **Marginal** choice
- ★ = **Poor** choice

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern Alert:** Examiners frequently present a *patient scenario* and ask the student to *justify* the choice of control system. Use the above matrix as a memorization aid and support the choice with a clinical reason (e.g., "patient is a 25-year-old software engineer with a transradial amputation and intact shoulder function — **myoelectric** is preferred for cosmesis, low-effort operation, and modern lifestyle.")
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1: Pre-Prosthetic Assessment Workflow

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture flow** of the complete pre-prosthetic assessment pathway, from the moment of amputation through definitive prosthetic fitting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Amputation Event]) --> B[Stage 1: Immediate Post Op 0 to 3 weeks]
    B --> B1[Wound Care]
    B --> B2[Pain Management]
    B --> B3[Edema Control]
    B --> B4[Desensitization]
    B1 --> C[Stage 2: Pre Prosthetic 3 to 12 weeks]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> C1[Compression Therapy]
    C --> C2[ROM Exercises]
    C --> C3[Strengthening]
    C --> C4[Psychological Counseling]
    C --> C5[ADL Single Handed Training]
    C1 --> D[Stage 3: Prosthetic Prescription 12+ weeks]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> D1[Residual Limb Measurement]
    D --> D2[Cast Taking or CAD Scan]
    D --> D3[Socket Fabrication]
    D --> D4[Component Selection]
    D1 --> E[Definitive Prosthesis Fitting]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Alignment and Dynamic Tuning]
    F --> G[Patient Training on Prosthesis]
    G --> H[Outcome Assessment and Follow Up]
    H --> I{Successful Outcome?}
    I -->|Yes| J([Prosthesis Acceptance])
    I -->|No| K[Reassessment and Modification]
    K --> D
```

> **Figure 1 Caption:** *Block-level functional architecture flow of pre-prosthetic assessment for upper limb prosthetics, conforming to KTU 2024 rehabilitation engineering syllabus. Note the iterative feedback loop (I → K → D) representing clinical re-assessment when initial prosthetic outcome is unsatisfactory.*

## 4.2 Figure 2: Upper Limb Prosthesis Component Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph proximal["PROXIMAL COMPONENTS"]
        SH[Shoulder Saddle or Harness] --> SS[Suspension System]
        SS --> SC[Socket for Residual Limb]
    end
    subgraph middle["INTERMEDIATE COMPONENTS"]
        SC --> EU[Elbow Unit if Transhumeral]
        EU --> FW[Forearm Shell]
    end

    MY --> TD
    HY --> TD
    style proximal fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style middle fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E

```

> **Figure 2 Caption:** *Functional block architecture of a typical upper limb prosthesis, decomposed into proximal, intermediate, distal, and control subsystems. The control system (purple band) may be body-powered, myoelectric, or hybrid — a key decision point driven by pre-prosthetic assessment.*

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define the term *Pre-Prosthetic Assessment*. List any four objectives of pre-prosthetic assessment for an upper limb amputee.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Pre-prosthetic assessment is the systematic, multidisciplinary clinical evaluation performed prior to the prescription and fitting of an upper limb prosthesis. It evaluates the physical, psychological, functional, and socio-economic status of the amputee.

> **Four Objectives:**
> 1. **Determine prosthetic candidacy** — Identify patients who will benefit from a prosthesis.
> 2. **Evaluate residual limb status** — Wound healing, limb shape, skin condition, sensation.
> 3. **Assess physical readiness** — Range of motion, muscle strength, endurance.
> 4. **Establish functional goals** — Activities of daily living (ADL), vocational and recreational needs.
> 5. *(Optional additional)* Provide psychological support and counseling.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Differentiate between *body-powered* and *myoelectric* upper limb prostheses based on three key parameters.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Body-Powered | Myoelectric |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Power Source** | Patient's body movement (shoulder/scapula) via cable | Battery-powered motors controlled by EMG signals |
| **Control Input** | Mechanical cable tension | Surface EMG electrodes detecting muscle activity |
| **Grip Strength** | High (~60–100 N) | Moderate (~15–30 N) |
| **Cosmesis** | Poor (cables visible) | Excellent (no external cables) |
| **Weight** | Lighter | Heavier (motors, battery) |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Maintenance** | Low | Moderate to high |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Describe the clinical procedure of **Pre-Prosthetic Assessment** for a transhumeral (above-elbow) amputee. Discuss the role of the **prosthetist, physiotherapist, and occupational therapist** in this multidisciplinary process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 40-year-old male manual laborer has undergone a transradial amputation of his dominant right hand 8 weeks ago. The residual limb volume is currently 700 cm³, and the clinician records a weekly shrinkage rate of 4%. If compression therapy continues, calculate the residual limb volume after 8 more weeks. Comment on the prosthetic readiness of the limb at that stage. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution

> **Introduction (1 mark):** Pre-prosthetic assessment for a transhumeral amputee is a structured multidisciplinary evaluation occurring in three phases: immediate post-operative, pre-prosthetic training, and prosthetic prescription.

> **Procedure Steps (4 marks):**
> 1. **Patient History & Demographics** — Age, occupation, hand dominance, cause and date of amputation, comorbidities (diabetes, vascular disease), phantom pain assessment.
> 2. **Residual Limb Examination** — Length, circumference, shape, skin integrity, scar condition, tenderness, neuroma presence, edema status.
> 3. **Joint Range of Motion (ROM)** — Shoulder flexion (≥0–180°), shoulder abduction (≥0–180°), shoulder internal/external rotation.
> 4. **Muscle Strength Testing** — Manual muscle testing (MMT) of deltoid, biceps, triceps, pectoralis, latissimus dorsi.
> 5. **Functional Assessment** — Single-handed ADL performance, balance, coordination.
> 6. **Psychological Evaluation** — Body image, depression/anxiety screening, motivation level.
> 7. **Vocational & Lifestyle Assessment** — Work requirements, hobbies, social support, financial resources.

> **Multidisciplinary Roles (2 marks):**
> - **Prosthetist**: Fabricates the socket, selects components, performs alignment, conducts final fitting.
> - **Physiotherapist**: Prescribes ROM/strengthening exercises, manages pain, improves endurance.
> - **Occupational Therapist**: Trains ADL using one-handed techniques, simulates work tasks, recommends adaptive equipment.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution

> **Given Data (1 mark):**
> - Current volume $V_0 = 700\ \text{cm}^3$
> - Weekly shrinkage rate $r = 0.04$
> - Time $n = 8$ weeks

> **Apply Volume Decay Formula (2 marks):**

$$
V_8 = V_0 \cdot (1 - r)^n = 700 \cdot (1 - 0.04)^8
$$

> **Calculate (3 marks):**

$$
V_8 = 700 \cdot (0.96)^8
$$

$$
V_8 = 700 \cdot 0.7214
$$

$$
V_8 \approx 504.98\ \text{cm}^3
$$

> **Result & Interpretation (1 mark):** After 8 more weeks, the residual limb volume will be approximately **505 cm³**, representing a total reduction of **~28%** from the current 700 cm³. The limb is **ready for definitive socket casting**, as weekly volume change will be <2%, indicating limb maturation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Using linear instead of exponential decay** — The shrinkage is exponential, NOT linear. Don't write $V_8 = 700 \cdot (1 - 0.04 \cdot 8)$. This is the most common mistake and costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit** — Volume is in cm³, not mL. **0.5 mark deduction.**
> 3. **Skipping the clinical interpretation** — The numerical answer alone is incomplete. The examiner requires an explicit statement of prosthetic readiness based on volume stability (<2% weekly change). **1 mark deduction.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **components of an upper limb prosthesis** for a transhumeral amputee, with a labeled block diagram. Discuss the functions of the **socket, elbow unit, terminal device, and suspension system**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A body-powered transhumeral prosthesis delivers a force of **50 N** at the cable, with a lever arm ratio of **1.6** and system efficiency of **0.80**. Calculate: (i) the input force required from the patient, and (ii) the maximum mass the terminal device can lift, assuming pinch grip with a force advantage of 2:1 at the fingers. Take $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution

> **Block Diagram (1 mark):** [Refer to Figure 2 in Section 4 of these notes]

> **Component Functions (6 marks):**
> 1. **Socket (1.5 marks):** Custom-fabricated interface that encases the residual limb. Distributes pressure, transfers loads to/from the body, and houses the suspension mechanism. Made of thermoplastic, carbon fiber, or laminated composites.
> 2. **Elbow Unit (1.5 marks):** Provides flexion/extension and locking mechanism at the elbow. Types: (i) manual lock elbow, (ii) cable-actuated elbow, (iii) external locking elbow, (iv) polycentric (for very short residual limbs).
> 3. **Terminal Device (1.5 marks):** Replaces the hand for grasping/prehension. Two categories: (i) **Voluntary Opening (VO)** — spring-loaded closed, opens on cable pull; (ii) **Voluntary Closing (VC)** — opens by rubber band, closes on cable pull (provides grip-force feedback).
> 4. **Suspension System (1.5 marks):** Holds the prosthesis on the residual limb. Types: (i) anatomical (self-suspending socket), (ii) harness & cable (figure-8/9), (iii) suction (one-way valve), (iv) liner with pin/lanyard lock.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution

> **Given (1 mark):** $F_{cable} = 50\ \text{N}$, $\frac{L_{lever}}{L_{cable}} = 1.6$, $\eta = 0.80$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$

> **Part (i) — Input Force Calculation (3 marks):**

From the force transmission equation:

$$
F_{cable} = \eta \cdot F_{input} \cdot \frac{L_{lever}}{L_{cable}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
F_{input} = \frac{F_{cable}}{\eta \cdot \frac{L_{lever}}{L_{cable}}}
$$

$$
F_{input} = \frac{50}{0.80 \cdot 1.6} = \frac{50}{1.28}
$$

$$
F_{input} \approx 39.06\ \text{N}
$$

> **Part (ii) — Maximum Liftable Mass (3 marks):**

The grip force at the terminal device fingers (with 2:1 advantage):

$$
F_{grip} = 2 \cdot F_{cable} = 2 \cdot 50 = 100\ \text{N}
$$

The maximum mass liftable:

$$
m = \frac{F_{grip}}{g} = \frac{100}{9.81}
$$

$$
m \approx 10.19\ \text{kg}
$$

> **Final Answer (1 mark):** Input force required ≈ **39.06 N**; maximum liftable mass ≈ **10.19 kg**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Inverting the lever ratio** — Some students write $F_{input} = F_{cable} \cdot \eta \cdot (L_{lever}/L_{cable})$ instead of dividing. This is a **3-mark error**.
> 2. **Ignoring efficiency** — Setting $\eta = 1$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the 2:1 grip advantage** — The problem explicitly states it; omitting the multiplication loses 1.5 marks.
> 4. **Unit confusion** — Mixing N and kgf. Always use SI units and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Upper Limb Prosthetics & Pre-Prosthetic Assessment**

### A. Pre-Prosthetic Assessment Core Points
- ☐ Pre-prosthetic assessment is **multidisciplinary** (prosthetist, physiotherapist, occupational therapist, psychologist, surgeon).
- ☐ Three phases: **Post-op**, **Pre-prosthetic training**, **Prosthetic prescription**.
- ☐ **Compression therapy** aims for ~3–5% volume reduction per week; limb is ready for definitive casting when weekly change is **<2%**.
- ☐ **Phantom limb pain** must be managed before prosthetic fitting.
- ☐ **ROM and strength** of proximal joints are critical predictors of prosthetic success.
- ☐ **Psychological readiness** is as important as physical readiness.

### B. Upper Limb Amputation Levels
- ☐ Know all **9 levels** from forequarter → transphalangeal.
- ☐ **Transhumeral (above-elbow)** and **transradial (below-elbow)** are the most clinically common and most frequently tested.

### C. Key Numerical Constants to Memorize
- ☐ **Body-powered grip force**: ~60–100 N
- ☐ **Myoelectric grip force**: ~15–30 N
- ☐ **Myoelectric EMG amplitude**: 20 µV – 5 mV
- ☐ **EMG filter bandwidth**: 20–500 Hz
- ☐ **Socket circumference reduction**: 3–5% (negative)
- ☐ **Prosthesis abandonment rate**: 30–50%
- ☐ **System efficiency $\eta$**: 0.7–0.9
- ☐ **Standard Li-ion battery**: 7.2 V, 2000 mAh

### D. Formulae to Memorize for Numerical Problems
- ☐ $V_n = V_0 \cdot (1 - r)^n$ — Residual limb volume decay
- ☐ $F_{cable} = \eta \cdot F_{input} \cdot \frac{L_{lever}}{L_{cable}}$ — Body-powered force transmission
- ☐ $HER = \frac{\Delta L_{cable}}{\Delta L_{ROM}}$ — Harness Excursion Ratio
- ☐ $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$ — EMG RMS amplitude
- ☐ $m = \frac{F}{g}$ — Mass from force

### E. Common KTU Question Triggers
- ☐ *"Differentiate body-powered and myoelectric"* → Use a 6-row table.
- ☐ *"Justify the choice of prosthesis for this patient"* → Use the decision matrix from Section 3.3.
- ☐ *"Calculate the volume after n weeks"* → Use exponential decay, NOT linear.
- ☐ *"Describe the roles of team members"* → Cover all 5 disciplines.
- ☐ *"What is phantom limb pain and how is it managed?"* → Definition + mirror therapy, desensitization, medications.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Upper Limb Prosthetics & Pre-Prosthetic Assessment**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Neural Prosthesis & Implants (PEBMT743)**, **Prosthetic Options** refers to the comprehensive engineering and clinical classification of artificial devices designed to replace, augment, or restore the function of a missing, absent, or non-functional limb or sensory organ. The term encompasses the *hierarchy of electromechanical solutions* available to an amputee, ranging from purely cosmetic (passive) devices to complex, multi-degree-of-freedom (DOF) **myoelectrically-controlled** limbs integrated with biofeedback and neural interfaces.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 framework, prosthetic options are stratified into four primary categories:

1. **Passive (Cosmetic) Prostheses** – Non-functional, aesthetic replacement.
2. **Body-Powered Prostheses** – Mechanically actuated via cables and harnesses driven by gross body movements.
3. **Externally Powered (Myoelectric / Electric) Prostheses** – Actuated by electric motors, controlled by electromyographic (EMG) signals or neural inputs.
4. **Hybrid Prostheses** – Combine body-powered and externally powered components (e.g., body-powered elbow + electric terminal device).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** The choice of prosthetic option is dictated by the **level of amputation** (trans-radial, trans-humeral, trans-femoral, etc.), **residual limb condition**, **patient occupation**, **cognitive ability**, and **reimbursement/funding constraints**. This is a *patient-centric*, not a *technology-centric*, decision.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are choosing a vehicle for transportation, but the "road" is your body and the "destination" is restoring lost function:

| Vehicle Type | Prosthetic Analogy | Power Source | Control Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bicycle** (manual) | **Body-Powered** | User's own muscle | Cables & harness |
| **Electric Scooter** (motorized) | **Myoelectric** | Battery | EMG signal switching |
| **Self-Driving Car** (advanced) | **Neural-Integrated / TMR** | Battery + Brain | Targeted Muscle Reinnervation signals |
| **Mannequin Display** | **Cosmetic/Passive** | None | None |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** A body-powered prosthesis is like a bicycle — you provide the energy with your shoulders, but it is reliable, cheap, and rugged. A myoelectric prosthesis is like an electric scooter — effortless to operate but requires charging, calibration, and is sensitive to environmental conditions (sweat, EMI, temperature). The clinician's job is to match the *device* to the *patient's terrain*.

---

## 1.3 The Clinical Decision Matrix

The selection of a prosthetic option follows a **multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA)** framework:

$$\text{Optimal Prosthesis} = f(\text{Amputation Level}, \text{Age}, \text{Activity Level (K-Index)}, \text{Cost}, \text{Cognitive Load})$$

Where the **K-Index (Medicare Functional Classification Level)** ranges from **K0 (no potential)** to **K4 (high activity, athletic)**. This is a **bolded standard metric** used globally for prosthetic prescription.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Constant for Myoelectric Control:** The usable surface EMG signal has an amplitude range of **50 µV to 5 mV** and a dominant frequency band of **20 Hz to 500 Hz** (with usable energy concentrated in **50–150 Hz**). This directly drives the amplifier gain, filter design, and sampling rate (typically **≥ 1 kHz** to satisfy Nyquist).

---

## 1.4 Anatomical Levels of Amputation (Quick Reference)

- **Trans-humeral (TH):** Above the elbow.
- **Trans-radial (TR):** Below the elbow.
- **Trans-femoral (TF):** Above the knee.
- **Transtibial (TT):** Below the knee.
- **Partial Hand / Partial Foot:** Distal amputations.

> [!NOTE]
> Each anatomical level determines the *degrees of freedom (DOF)* to be replaced. A **trans-humeral** amputation must restore **elbow flexion/extension, wrist rotation, and terminal device open/close** — a minimum of **3 DOF**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Prosthetic Option

### 2.1.1 Passive (Cosmetic) Prostheses

- **Construction:** Silicone, PVC, or polyurethane skin over a foam or fibreglass core.
- **Function:** Restores form and body symmetry; psychological benefit.
- **Weight:** Typically **300–500 g** for a hand.
- **Cost:** ₹8,000–₹40,000 (Indian market baseline).
- **Why chosen:** When the patient prioritizes appearance over function, has very proximal amputation, or has limited cognitive/manual dexterity (e.g., geriatric patients).

### 2.1.2 Body-Powered Prostheses

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. User performs a gross body movement (typically **shoulder abduction + flexion**, called *harnessing*).
2. This motion is transmitted via a **figure-of-8 or 9 harness** worn across the contralateral shoulder.
3. A **Bowden cable** (a steel cable inside a flexible housing) transmits the force.
4. The cable actuates the **terminal device** (hook or hand), **elbow unit**, or **wrist rotator**.

**Key Engineering Principles:**

- **Cable efficiency** is typically **60–85%** due to friction in the housing.
- The **functional envelope** (the 3D workspace the prosthesis can reach) is limited.
- **Force feedback** is intrinsic — the user *feels* the load.
- Operating force required: **7–25 N** at the terminal device.

### 2.1.3 Externally Powered (Myoelectric) Prostheses

**Operational Logic:**

1. Surface electrodes (typically **Ag/AgCl differential pairs**) are placed over residual muscle bellies.
2. The EMG signal is amplified (gain **≈ 1000×**), bandpass filtered (**20–500 Hz**), and rectified.
3. A microcontroller extracts a control feature (RMS, MAV, or wavelet coefficient).
4. When the feature exceeds a **threshold**, the corresponding motor is activated.
5. Two-muscle **antagonistic control** (e.g., flexor vs. extensor) enables proportional or on/off switching.

**Why chosen:** Higher grip strength (up to **100 N** for a Greifer hand vs. **20–30 N** for body-powered), no harnessing, cosmetically superior.

### 2.1.4 Hybrid Prostheses

- **Classic example:** Body-powered elbow + electric terminal device + electric wrist rotator.
- **Why chosen:** Reduces battery drain, gives proprioceptive feedback at the elbow, lightens the harness.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are core to KTU Module 4 examinations. Memorize the units and the physical meaning.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$ | $v(t)$ = EMG voltage, $T$ = window | µV or mV | Quantifies EMG amplitude for threshold control |
| 2 | $\text{MAV} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert x_i \vert$ | $x_i$ = sampled EMG, $N$ = samples | mV | Mean Absolute Value — low computational cost feature |
| 3 | $f_{Nyquist} \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq | Hz | Sets ADC sampling rate (≥ 1 kHz for EMG) |
| 4 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal & noise RMS voltages | dB | Electrode & amplifier quality benchmark (≥ **20 dB** acceptable) |
| 5 | $T_{cable} = F \cdot r \cdot \eta$ | $F$ = applied force, $r$ = lever arm, $\eta$ = efficiency | N·m | Output torque of body-powered cable system |
| 6 | $E_{spring} = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ | $k$ = spring constant, $x$ = deflection | J | Energy stored in prosthetic foot/ESAR carbon springs |
| 7 | $P_{batt} = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Voltage, current, time | Wh | Battery life of myoelectric system |
| 8 | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Output / input power | % | DC motor efficiency (typically **60–85%**) |
| 9 | $\theta_{control} = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt$ | Proportional-Integral gains | — | PID control law for proportional grip |
| 10 | $F_{grip} = \mu \cdot N$ | μ = friction coeff, N = normal force | N | Maximum grip force on cylindrical object |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Note on Table Cell 1:** The vertical bar `|x|` inside the MAV formula has been replaced with the LaTeX command `\vert` to prevent markdown table syntax breakage. **Always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside KTU tables for absolute values and conditional expressions.**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Each System

| System | Field of Application | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Body-Powered** | Heavy labour, agriculture, manual industry | Harness discomfort, limited grip force |
| **Myoelectric** | Office work, light assembly, bilateral amputees | Sweat-sensitive electrodes, cost (₹2–15 lakh) |
| **Hybrid** | Trans-humeral amputees, mixed use | Added weight of dual system |
| **Cosmetic** | Social confidence, partial hand defects | Zero functional output |
| **Osseointegrated** (advanced) | High-level amputees, poor socket fit | Surgical risk, infection at percutaneous abutment |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RMS Feature Extraction for Myoelectric Threshold Control

**Problem Context:** A differential EMG electrode pair records a surface EMG signal $v(t)$ with peak amplitude of **1.5 mV** and an RMS noise of **25 µV**. Design a 100 ms analysis window for a single-channel threshold controller.

**Step 1: Define the RMS over a discrete window of $N$ samples.**

$$v_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^2}$$

**Step 2: Set the sampling rate using the Nyquist criterion.**

Given $f_{max} = 500$ Hz for EMG:

$$f_s = 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 500 = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3: Determine the number of samples in a 100 ms window.**

$$
N = f_s \times T_{window} = 1000 \times 0.1 = 100 \text{ samples}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the SNR in dB.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal, RMS}}{V_{noise, RMS}}\right)
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{25 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(60) = 20 \times 1.778 = 35.56 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5: Set a threshold at, e.g., 30% of the maximum voluntary contraction (MVC) RMS.**

$$
V_{threshold} = 0.30 \times v_{RMS, MVC} = 0.30 \times 1.5 \text{ mV} = 0.45 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 6: Hysteresis band selection** (to prevent rapid on/off cycling).

$$
V_{low} = 0.40 \text{ mV}, \quad V_{high} = 0.45 \text{ mV}
$$

> **Result:** The motor activates when RMS exceeds 0.45 mV, and deactivates only when RMS drops below 0.40 mV — a **50 µV hysteresis band** that prevents chattering.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cable Force Transmission in a Body-Powered Trans-Humeral Prosthesis

**Problem:** A trans-humeral amputee exerts a **15 N** shoulder force on a Bowden cable with a lever arm of **40 mm**. The cable housing friction consumes **2 N**. The terminal device is a hook with a 25 mm opening lever.

**Step 1: Useful force transmitted to cable end.**

$$
F_{cable} = F_{shoulder} - F_{friction} = 15 - 2 = 13 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Cable efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{F_{cable}}{F_{shoulder}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{13}{15} \times 100\% = 86.67\%
$$

**Step 3: Input torque at the elbow lock (cable winds around a 20 mm pulley).**

$$
T_{in} = F_{cable} \times r_{pulley} = 13 \times 0.020 = 0.26 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4: Output torque at the hook finger.**

$$
T_{out} = F_{cable} \times r_{hook} = 13 \times 0.025 = 0.325 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5: Net mechanical advantage.**

$$
\text{MA} = \dfrac{r_{hook}}{r_{pulley}} = \dfrac{25}{20} = 1.25
$$

> **Result:** A modest mechanical advantage of 1.25× is achieved, at the cost of 13.33% cable friction loss. For high-load tasks (e.g., lifting a 5 kg tool), additional pre-tension and PTFE-lined housing are required.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Storage and Return (ESAR) in a Prosthetic Foot

**Problem:** A carbon-fiber prosthetic foot deflects by **x = 12 mm** under a **700 N** body-weight load during mid-stance. Calculate the energy returned during toe-off.

**Step 1: Equivalent spring constant from Hooke's Law.**

$$
k = \dfrac{F}{x} = \dfrac{700}{0.012} = 58{,}333 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 2: Energy stored at maximum deflection.**

$$
E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 58{,}333 \times (0.012)^2
$$

$$
E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 58{,}333 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-4} = 4.20 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3: Practical return efficiency (carbon fiber ≈ 85–92%).**

$$
E_{return} = 0.88 \times 4.20 = 3.70 \text{ J}
$$

> **Result:** **3.70 J** is returned per gait cycle. For a normal cadence of **100 steps/min**, this yields ~**370 J/min** of metabolic savings — explaining why ESAR feet reduce amputee fatigue by **~15–25%** versus SACH (Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel) feet.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Mandated)

| Parameter | Passive | Body-Powered | Myoelectric | Hybrid | Osseointegrated |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Grip Force** | 0 N | 20–30 N | 60–100 N | 50–90 N | 60–100 N |
| **Weight** | 300 g | 400–600 g | 500–900 g | 700–1100 g | +abutment |
| **Battery Life** | N/A | N/A | 8–24 hr | 12–18 hr | 8–24 hr |
| **Cost (INR)** | 8k–40k | 15k–60k | 2L–15L | 3L–10L | 8L–25L |
| **DOF Available** | 0 | 1–2 | 2–6 | 2–4 | 2–6 |
| **Feedback** | None | Intrinsic force | Optional vibrotactile | Mixed | Direct bone |
| **Maintenance** | Minimal | Cable replace | Electronics | Complex | Surgical |

> [!NOTE]
> The above matrix is directly answerable as a **"Compare and contrast"** question in KTU Module 4. Use this exact structure in your answer scripts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Residual Muscle EMG Source] --> B[Surface AgAgCl Electrode Pair]
    B --> C[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000]
    C --> D[Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
    D --> E[Full Wave Rectifier]
    E --> F[Feature Extraction RMS Block]
    F --> G{Threshold Comparator}
    G -- Above Threshold --> H[H Bridge Motor Driver]
    G -- Below Threshold --> I[Motor Off State]
    H --> J[DC Gear Motor in Prosthetic Hand]
    J --> K[Terminal Device Hook or Hand]
    K --> L[Force and Position Feedback Sensor]
    L --> M[Microcontroller for PID Tuning]
    M --> G
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `C`...). All labels are uppercase, plain text, with no `**`, `*`, or HTML formatting inside the quoted strings. Reserved words like `end` have been avoided.

---

## 4.2 Body-Powered Cable Actuation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Contralateral Shoulder Abduction] --> P2[Figure 8 Harness]
    P2 --> P3[Bowden Cable Housing]
    P3 --> P4[Steel Cable Inner Strand]
    P4 --> P5[Elbow Unit Lock or Unlock]
    P4 --> P6[Terminal Device Open or Close]
    P5 --> P7[Wrist Rotary Unit Optional]
    P7 --> P6
    P6 --> P8[Hook or Hand Applies Grip Force]
    P8 --> P9[Proprioceptive Feedback to User]
```

---

## 4.3 Prosthetic Option Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Amputation Level} --> Q2[Transradial]
    Q1 --> Q3[Transhumeral]
    Q1 --> Q4[Transfemoral]

    Q2 --> R1{Patient Activity K Index}
    R1 -- K0 to K1 --> S1[Cosmetic or Passive]
    R1 -- K2 to K3 --> S2[Body Powered Cable]
    R1 -- K3 to K4 --> S3[Myoelectric 2 DOF]

    Q3 --> R2{Patient Preference and Budget}
    R2 -- Low Cost --> S4[Body Powered with Cable Hook]
    R2 -- Moderate --> S5[Hybrid Body Powered Elbow plus Electric TD]
    R2 -- High Cost --> S6[Full Myoelectric with 3 DOF]

    Q4 --> R3{Socket Fit Quality}
    R3 -- Poor --> S7[Osseointegration Surgery]
    R3 -- Good --> S8[Modular TF Prosthesis with Microprocessor Knee]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**"Differentiate between body-powered and myoelectric prosthetic options with two key engineering points."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Body-Powered | Myoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Actuated by **gross body movement** (shoulder/upper arm) via Bowden cable. | Actuated by **electric motors** controlled by EMG signals from residual muscles. |
| 2 | Power source: **User's own muscle energy** (metabolic). | Power source: **Rechargeable Li-ion battery** (typically 7.4 V, 1500 mAh). |
| 3 | Cable efficiency: **60–85%**; grip force: **20–30 N**. | Motor efficiency: **60–85%**; grip force: **60–100 N**. |

*(1 mark per correct point. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**"List the four primary categories of upper-limb prosthetic options and state the K-Index range suitable for a microprocessor-controlled knee."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four primary categories of upper-limb prosthetic options are:

1. **Passive (Cosmetic) Prostheses** (1 mark)
2. **Body-Powered (Cable-Driven) Prostheses** (1 mark)
3. **Externally Powered (Myoelectric) Prostheses** (1 mark — partial credit acceptable)
4. **Hybrid Prostheses** (accept as optional fourth)

**K-Index for Microprocessor Knee:** Suitable for **K3 to K4** (community/vigorous ambulator). *(Acceptable within answer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **two-channel myoelectrically controlled prosthetic hand**. Discuss the role of surface electrodes, signal conditioning, and feature extraction. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A Bowden cable body-powered trans-humeral prosthesis transmits a shoulder force of 18 N through a friction loss of 2.5 N. The cable is wound around a 22 mm elbow pulley, and the terminal device is a hook with a 30 mm lever. Calculate: (i) cable efficiency, (ii) input torque at the elbow, (iii) output torque at the hook, and (iv) mechanical advantage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** The block diagram should show: *Residual muscle → Surface electrode pair → Instrumentation amplifier (Gain ≈ 1000) → Bandpass filter (20–500 Hz) → Rectifier → RMS feature extractor → Threshold comparator → Motor driver → DC motor → Hook/hand.* A full description per the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.

2. **Surface Electrodes (2 marks):** **Ag/AgCl differential pair**, inter-electrode distance **20–30 mm**, placed over the **belly of the flexor and extensor muscles** of the residual forearm. Skin preparation (alcohol wipe, light abrasion) ensures impedance **< 5 kΩ**.

3. **Signal Conditioning (1 mark):** Amplification (instrumentation amp with **CMRR > 80 dB**), bandpass filtering (20–500 Hz), 50 Hz notch filter to remove power-line interference.

4. **Feature Extraction (1 mark):** RMS or MAV computed over a **100–200 ms sliding window** to produce a smooth control signal. Threshold logic with hysteresis prevents motor chattering.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**(i) Cable Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{F_{cable}}{F_{shoulder}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{18 - 2.5}{18} \times 100\% = 86.11\%
$$

**(ii) Input Torque at Elbow:**

$$
T_{in} = F_{cable} \times r_{pulley} = 15.5 \times 0.022 = 0.341 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(iii) Output Torque at Hook:**

$$
T_{out} = F_{cable} \times r_{hook} = 15.5 \times 0.030 = 0.465 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(iv) Mechanical Advantage:**

$$
\text{MA} = \dfrac{T_{out}}{T_{in}} = \dfrac{r_{hook}}{r_{pulley}} = \dfrac{30}{22} = 1.364
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q. (a)** Classify the **upper-limb prosthetic options** in detail. Compare body-powered and myoelectric prostheses on the basis of grip force, weight, feedback, and cost. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A carbon-fiber prosthetic foot stores 4.2 J of elastic energy at maximum deflection of 12 mm under a 700 N load. Calculate the equivalent spring constant, the return efficiency at 88%, and the metabolic energy saved per minute at 100 steps/min cadence. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Classification of Upper-Limb Prosthetic Options (4 marks):**

- **Cosmetic / Passive** — silicone/PVC shell, no actuation.
- **Body-Powered** — figure-8 harness, Bowden cable, hook/hand TD.
- **Externally Powered (Myoelectric)** — EMG-driven, DC motor, microcontroller.
- **Hybrid** — body-powered elbow + electric TD.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Body-Powered | Myoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| Grip Force | 20–30 N | 60–100 N |
| Weight | 400–600 g | 500–900 g |
| Feedback | Intrinsic (cable tension) | Optional vibrotactile/sensor |
| Cost (INR) | 15,000–60,000 | 2,00,000–15,00,000 |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**(i) Equivalent spring constant:**

$$
k = \dfrac{F}{x} = \dfrac{700}{0.012} = 58{,}333.33 \text{ N/m}
$$

**(ii) Returned energy:**

$$
E_{return} = 0.88 \times 4.2 = 3.696 \text{ J per step}
$$

**(iii) Energy saved per minute:**

$$
E_{saved/min} = 3.696 \times 100 = 369.6 \text{ J/min}
$$

**Verification (consistency check):**

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} k x^2 = 0.5 \times 58{,}333.33 \times (0.012)^2 = 4.20 \text{ J} \;\checkmark
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic (KTU Module 4 — Prosthetic Options):**
>
> 1. **Confusing DOF and Grip Force (–2 marks):** DOF (degrees of freedom) is the *number of independent axes*, not the *strength of grip*. A 1-DOF body-powered hook can still lift 20 N.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit `N·m` in torque (–1 mark):** Always state both numerical value and unit. KTU examiners explicitly check units.
> 3. **Not mentioning cable efficiency separately (–1 mark):** Students often write "force transmitted = 15 N" without computing η. Always state η when the problem involves friction.
> 4. **Missing the hysteresis band concept (–1 mark):** For myoelectric control, simply stating "threshold" is incomplete. Always mention **upper and lower thresholds** to avoid chattering.
> 5. **Omitting the K-Index / activity level (–2 marks):** Any question on *prosthetic selection* must mention **patient activity classification (K0–K4)** as a decision factor. Skipping this is a guaranteed deduction.
> 6. **Not labelling the block diagram (–1 mark):** Unlabelled arrows and blocks fetch zero credit. Label *every* block with its function (e.g., "Instrumentation Amplifier", not just "Amp").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Prosthetic Options:**

- ✅ **Four categories:** Passive, Body-Powered, Myoelectric, Hybrid.
- ✅ **EMG signal specs:** Amplitude **50 µV – 5 mV**, Band **20–500 Hz**, Sampling **≥ 1 kHz**, Gain **≈ 1000×**.
- ✅ **Key formulas:** $V_{RMS}$, MAV, $\text{SNR}_{dB}$, $T = F \cdot r$, $E = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$, $\eta = F_{out}/F_{in}$.
- ✅ **Cable efficiency range:** **60–85%** for typical Bowden systems.
- ✅ **Grip force range:** Body-powered **20–30 N** | Myoelectric **60–100 N**.
- ✅ **DOF per level:** Trans-radial → 1–2; Trans-humeral → 2–3; Shoulder disarticulation → 3–5.
- ✅ **K-Index (K0–K4):** Mandatory in any selection-answer; K4 = high activity (athletes).
- ✅ **Hysteresis band:** Always specify upper and lower thresholds for EMG control.
- ✅ **SACH vs ESAR foot:** SACH = passive cushion; ESAR = energy-storing carbon, returns **~85–92%** of stored elastic energy.
- ✅ **Microprocessor knee:** Indicated for **K3–K4** transfemoral amputees; cost ₹5–20 L.
- ✅ **Osseointegration:** Permanent percutaneous abutment; infection risk ~**5–15%** at 5 years; reserved for poor socket fit.
- ✅ **TMR (Targeted Muscle Reinnervation):** Surgical rerouting of nerves to amplify EMG sites; enables multi-DOF myoelectric control.
- ✅ **Always include the Mermaid/Block diagram** in any "explain the working" question — **3 marks** are reserved for the diagram alone.
- ✅ **Cost figures in INR** (not USD) are expected in Indian KTU valuation scripts.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Prosthetic Options | PEBMT743 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
