topics.title: Components for the Upper Limb Prosthesis: Hands, Work Hands, and Cosmetic Covers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components for the Upper Limb Prosthesis: Hands, Work Hands, and Cosmetic Covers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **terminal device (TD)** is the most distal functional component of any upper limb prosthesis, replicating the biological role of the human hand. It interfaces directly with the environment and converts the user's biomechanical intent (via body-powered cables, myoelectric signals, or hybrid control) into grasp, manipulation, or stabilization actions. KTU 2024 Scheme classifies terminal devices into three engineering tiers:

1. **Prosthetic Hands** – anthropomorphic, multi-articulated graspers mimicking human hand kinematics.
2. **Work Hands (Hooks / Specialty Terminal Devices)** – non-anthropomorphic, high-utility grippers optimized for durability, lifting capacity, and tool use.
3. **Cosmetic Covers (Passive Shells & Gloves)** – aesthetic restorations prioritizing visual realism over active function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must distinguish between **functional terminal devices** (active grasp) and **cosmetic restorations** (passive aesthetic). Examiners frequently test the engineering trade-off between *anthropomorphism* and *functional utility*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **Swiss Army Knife** mounted at the end of a robotic arm. The knife has:
- A **multi-function hand** (the corkscrew/blade – looks like a hand, can grasp gently).
- A **work hook** (the pliers – ugly, but can lift 50 kg of groceries).
- A **cosmetic shell** (the polished wooden casing – hides the mechanisms, looks elegant).

A unilateral arm amputee essentially carries this entire toolkit on their residual limb, choosing the right "tool" for the right task. The **wrist unit** acts as the connector (the knife's pivot), and the **socket** acts as the handle.

### 1.3 Engineering Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Anthropometric Metric:** An average adult human male hand has a **maximum grip aperture of ~95–110 mm**, a **tip pinch force of ~70 N**, and a **total mass of ~400 g**. Prosthetic terminal devices are benchmarked against these reference values (per the University of New Brunswick's UNB Test Protocol and ISO 8548-1).

| Tier | Primary Design Goal | Typical User |
|------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Anthropomorphic Hand** | Aesthetic + functional grasp | Bilateral amputee, social priority |
| **Work Hand / Hook** | Mechanical efficiency + lifting | Unilateral amputee, manual laborer |
| **Cosmetic Cover** | Visual fidelity | Passive cosmetic restoration |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Prosthetic Hands (Anthropomorphic Terminal Devices)

#### 2.1.1 Passive Hands
- **No actuation**; purely cosmetic with a flexible inner core (foam, silicone, or soft PVC over an aluminum or plastic skeleton).
- Finger joints are pre-positioned and held by friction.
- Used for bilateral amputees where one limb is functional and the other serves as a "paperweight" or assistive stabilizer.

#### 2.1.2 Mechanical (Body-Powered) Hands
- Actuated by **harness-cable tension** generated via shoulder, scapular, or chest excursion.
- Typically feature a **single motor axis** at the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joint, producing a 3-jaw-chuck pinch (thumb + index + middle finger).
- Prehension modes: **voluntary opening (VO)** is standard (cable pull opens fingers; rubber bands or springs close them).
- Typical pinch force: **50–100 N**; opening aperture: **60–90 mm**.

#### 2.1.3 Externally Powered (Electric / Myoelectric) Hands
- Driven by **DC motors or servo actuators** powered by Li-ion or NiMH battery packs.
- Control inputs: **EMG signals** from residual forearm muscles (e.g., Ottobock SensorHand, Bebionic, i-Limb, Michelangelo).
- Modern variants use **multi-articulated fingers** with up to 6 independent degrees of freedom (DOF) and **pattern-recognition control** for grip selection (power, precision, lateral, hook, index).

#### 2.1.4 Activity-Specific Hands
- **Sports hands**: specialized designs for swimming, baseball, billiards, golf, archery.
- Examples: TRS (Therapeutic Recreational Systems) ball-claws, swim fins, billiards bridges.

### 2.2 Work Hands (Hooks and Industrial Terminal Devices)

Work hands are the **engineering workhorses** of upper-limb prosthetics. They sacrifice anthropomorphism for **mechanical advantage, durability, and sensory feedback**.

#### 2.2.1 Voluntary Opening (VO) Hooks
- Default state: **closed** (held by rubber bands or springs).
- Cable pull **opens** the fingers against the elastic force.
- Release upon cable slack provides **automatic grip** (huge functional advantage for sustained hold).
- Standard band tensions: **1.5 lb, 3 lb, 5 lb, 7 lb, 9 lb** (force to fully open).
- Iconic examples: **Hosmer Dorrance Model 5 (adult), Model 5XA (child), APRL hook, CAPP hook**.

#### 2.2.2 Voluntary Closing (VC) Hooks
- Default state: **open**.
- Cable pull **closes** the fingers; user controls grip force proportionally.
- Provides **variable grip force** (graded prehension) but requires sustained cable tension to maintain hold.
- Higher cognitive load; preferred for tasks requiring modulated force (e.g., holding an egg, then a hammer).

#### 2.2.3 Materials and Coatings
- **Body**: Stainless steel, aluminum alloy (6061-T6), or titanium (Ti-6Al-4V).
- **Grip surfaces**: Neoprene, rubber, nitrile coating for friction enhancement and shock absorption.

### 2.3 Cosmetic Covers (Passive Shells & Gloves)

#### 2.3.1 PVC Cosmetic Gloves
- **Most common** cosmetic restoration worldwide.
- Stockinette-lined interior fits over the prosthetic hand skeleton.
- Available in standard Caucasian, African-American, Hispanic, and Asian skin tones.
- Lifespan: **6–12 months** before UV degradation, staining, and color fading.
- Manufacturers: Ottobock, Fillauer, Steeper, TRS.

#### 2.3.2 Silicone Custom Covers
- **Premium tier** – hand-crafted, custom-painted to match contralateral limb.
- Multi-layer silicone with embedded pigments to mimic dermal depth.
- Realistic details: **fingerprints, veins, knuckle wrinkles, hair (individually punched), freckles, nail beds**.
- Lifespan: **3–5 years**.
- Cost: **5–10× higher** than PVC gloves.

#### 2.3.3 Foam and Fabric Covers
- Used in developing-country or pediatric contexts.
- Lightweight, washable, low-cost.
- Sacrifices realism for affordability.

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Numerical Relationships** for KTU numerical problems. All values are derived from ISO 8548, UNB Test Protocol, and clinical literature on upper-limb prosthetics.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Equation | Unit |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|------|
| Mechanical Advantage (Cable System) | $MA$ | $MA = \dfrac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \dfrac{d_{in}}{d_{out}}$ | dimensionless |
| Cable Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{F_{out} \cdot d_{out}}{F_{in} \cdot d_{in}}$ | dimensionless |
| Pinch Force | $F_p$ | $F_p = F_{cable} \cdot MA$ | N |
| Opening Aperture | $\theta$ | $60 \le \theta \le 110$ | mm |
| Hook Band Tension (VO) | $F_b$ | $1.5, 3, 5, 7, 9$ | lb (lbf) |
| Hand Mass (Std. Adult) | $m$ | $250 \le m \le 500$ | g |
| Battery Runtime (Myoelectric) | $t$ | $8 \le t \le 24$ | h |
| Cable Bow Travel (Excursion) | $e$ | $50 \le e \le 75$ | mm |
| Grip Cycle Time | $T_c$ | $0.3 \le T_c \le 1.5$ | s |

**CRITICAL NOTATION NOTE:** All absolute value expressions are written using `\vert` (e.g., $\vert F_{in} \vert$) to comply with the markdown table safety protocol.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Prosthetic hands serve as the canonical benchmark for **dexterous manipulation** research in humanoid robotics.
- **Industrial Design:** Hook mechanisms inform the design of **gripper end-effectors** in factory automation.
- **Biomimetics:** Cosmetic cover realism drives advances in **silicone pigmentation, multi-material 3D printing, and haptic skin** development.
- **Sports Prosthetics:** Activity-specific work hands enable **Paralympic performance** (e.g., carbon-fiber running blades originated from prosthetic socket engineering).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Mechanical Advantage of a Cable-Driven Hook System

**Problem Context:** A unilateral transradial amputee uses a Hosmer Model 5 voluntary-opening hook. The cable excursion is **e = 70 mm**, the cable input force is **F_in = 25 N**, and the hook is required to deliver a tip pinch force of **F_out = 100 N** when gripping a 30 mm diameter cylindrical object.

**Step 1 — Define Work-Energy Conservation in an Ideal Frictionless System:**

For an ideal (lossless) cable system, the work input by the user equals the work output at the hook tip.

$$W_{in} = W_{out}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Force-Distance Products:**

$$F_{in} \cdot d_{in} = F_{out} \cdot d_{out}$$

where $d_{in}$ is the cable excursion distance and $d_{out}$ is the displacement of the hook tip.

**Step 3 — Rearrange to Solve for Mechanical Advantage:**

$$\frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \frac{d_{in}}{d_{out}} = MA$$

$$\boxed{MA = \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Numerical MA:**

$$MA = \frac{100 \text{ N}}{25 \text{ N}} = 4.0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Hook Tip Displacement:**

Rearranging the work equation:

$$d_{out} = \frac{F_{in} \cdot d_{in}}{F_{out}} = \frac{25 \text{ N} \cdot 70 \text{ mm}}{100 \text{ N}}$$

$$d_{out} = 17.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Verify Physical Plausibility:**

A 17.5 mm tip displacement for a 30 mm diameter object is consistent with a partial closure through the cylindrical object (the hook is still partially open around the cylinder). Verified ✓.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cable Efficiency with Frictional Losses

**Step 1 — Acknowledge Frictional Losses in the Real System:**

$$\eta = \frac{F_{out} \cdot d_{out}}{F_{in} \cdot d_{in}}$$

**Step 2 — Express Output Force in Terms of Input Force and Efficiency:**

$$F_{out} = \eta \cdot MA_{ideal} \cdot F_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Standard Engineering Assumption for Bowden Cable:**

For a well-lubricated Bowden cable with $MA = 4$ and $\eta = 0.85$:

$$F_{out} = 0.85 \cdot 4.0 \cdot 25 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{out} = 85 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result:**

The actual delivered pinch force is **85 N**, which is 15% lower than the ideal 100 N due to friction in the cable housing. This is the basis of KTU problem-solving: students must account for $\eta < 1$ in realistic prosthetic designs.

### 3.3 Derivation: Voluntary Opening Hook Band Selection

**Step 1 — User Intent vs. Band Force:**

For a VO hook, the user must generate cable tension greater than the band force $F_b$ to fully open. The required user input force is:

$$F_{user} = \frac{F_b}{MA \cdot \eta}$$

**Step 2 — Case Analysis with Standard Band Tensions:**

Assume $MA = 4$, $\eta = 0.9$:

| Band | $F_b$ (N) | $F_{user}$ (N) | User Capability |
|------|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1.5 lb | 6.7 | 1.85 | Easily achievable (light objects) |
| 3 lb | 13.4 | 3.72 | Standard daily use |
| 5 lb | 22.2 | 6.17 | Moderate grip on tools |
| 7 lb | 31.1 | 8.65 | Heavy grasp (e.g., grocery bag) |
| 9 lb | 40.0 | 11.12 | Maximum grip (athletic use) |

**Step 3 — Prescription Logic:**

The prosthetist selects the heaviest band the user can comfortably overcome, since heavier bands provide **automatic grip** (the elastic force holds the object without sustained user effort). This is a classic KTU trade-off question.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hand vs. Work Hand vs. Cosmetic Cover

| Criterion | Prosthetic Hand | Work Hand / Hook | Cosmetic Cover |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Actuation** | Mechanical / Myoelectric / Passive | Mechanical (cable) | None (passive) |
| **Aesthetic Realism** | High (anatomical) | Low (industrial look) | Very high (silicone) |
| **Functional Grip Force** | 50–150 N | 100–300 N | 0 N (passive) |
| **Mass** | 250–500 g | 80–200 g | 50–150 g |
| **Maintenance** | High (electronics) | Low (mechanical) | Medium (UV degradation) |
| **Cost (INR approx.)** | ₹30,000 – ₹25,00,000 | ₹5,000 – ₹80,000 | ₹3,000 – ₹2,00,000 |
| **Lifespan** | 3–5 years | 5–10 years | 0.5–5 years |
| **Best User** | Bilateral / aesthetic priority | Unilateral / labor | Passive restoration |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Upper Limb Prosthesis Terminal Device Subsystem

This block diagram maps the engineering subsystems of the three terminal device categories, illustrating their control inputs, mechanical linkages, and environmental interfaces.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["BIOMECHANICAL INPUT LAYER"]
        nodeA1["Shoulder Excursion"]
        nodeA2["Scapular Abduction"]
        nodeA3["EMG Residual Muscles"]
        nodeA4["Push Button Switch"]
    end

    subgraph TRANSMISSION_LAYER["CONTROL TRANSMISSION LAYER"]
        nodeB1["Harness and Cable Bowden"]
        nodeB2["Linear Actuator DC Motor"]
        nodeB3["Pattern Recognition Controller"]
    end

    subgraph TERMINAL_LAYER["TERMINAL DEVICE LAYER"]
        nodeC1["Prosthetic Hand Multi Articulated"]
        nodeC2["Work Hand VO Hook"]
        nodeC3["Work Hand VC Hook"]
        nodeC4["Cosmetic Cover Passive Shell"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["FUNCTIONAL OUTPUT LAYER"]
        nodeD1["Pinch and Power Grasp"]
        nodeD2["Hook Suspension and Tool Hold"]
        nodeD3["Aesthetic Visual Restoration"]
    end

    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeA3 --> nodeB3

    nodeC3 --> nodeD2

```

### 4.2 Decision Logic Flow: Prosthetist Terminal Device Selection

This flowchart encodes the clinical decision pathway used by certified prosthetists (per American Board for Certification in Orthotics, Prosthetics and Pedorthotics - ABC) when prescribing a terminal device.

```mermaid

    nodeRes6["Prescribe Pediatric Soft Foam Cover"]

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a terminal device in the context of upper limb prosthetics and list the three main categories with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A terminal device is the most distal component of an upper limb prosthesis that interfaces with the environment to perform grasp, manipulation, or stabilization tasks.
The three main categories are:
1. **Prosthetic (Anthropomorphic) Hands** – e.g., Ottobock SensorHand, Hosmer mechanical hand.
2. **Work Hands / Hooks** – e.g., Hosmer Model 5 VO hook, TRS CAPP VC hook.
3. **Cosmetic Covers** – e.g., PVC cosmetic glove, custom silicone cover.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for categories with examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between voluntary opening (VO) and voluntary closing (VC) hook mechanisms in upper limb prosthetics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Voluntary Opening (VO):** Default state is *closed*; cable pull *opens* the hook against elastic bands; provides automatic grip on release. E.g., Hosmer Model 5.
- **Voluntary Closing (VC):** Default state is *open*; cable pull *closes* the hook proportionally, providing graded grip force. E.g., TRS CAPP hook.
- **Trade-off:** VO = lower cognitive load, automatic grip; VC = modulated force, higher user effort.

*(2 marks for VO/VC distinction + 1 mark for trade-off)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the design and functional characteristics of a **Hosmer Dorrance voluntary-opening (VO) hook** used as a work hand in upper limb prosthetics. Include in your answer:
- Materials of construction
- Standard band tension values
- Cable actuation mechanism
- Clinical advantages and limitations **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transradial amputee uses a VO hook with a cable mechanical advantage of $MA = 4.5$ and cable efficiency $\eta = 0.88$. The user applies a cable input force of 30 N through a 65 mm shoulder excursion.
- Calculate the output pinch force delivered at the hook tip.
- Determine the output displacement of the hook tip.
- Comment on the engineering trade-off between band tension selection and user fatigue. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) VO Hook Design and Functional Characteristics (7 marks):**

The **Hosmer Dorrance Model 5** is the most widely prescribed adult VO hook worldwide. Key design features:

- **Materials:** Stainless steel (finger hooks), aluminum alloy (fork and connection plate), neoprene coating on grip surfaces. Some models (5XA) use titanium for pediatric lightweight construction.
- **Band Tension System:** Replaceable rubber bands threaded through the hook fingers. Standard tensions of **1.5, 3, 5, 7, and 9 pounds** (lbf) allow graded grip strength prescription. Heavier bands provide stronger automatic grip but require greater user force to open.
- **Cable Actuation:** A stainless steel Bowden cable connects the harness to the hook's anchor pin. When the user abducts the scapula, cable tension rotates the finger levers about their pivots, opening the hook against the band.
- **Force Transmission:** The mechanical advantage (typically 4–5×) amplifies the user's cable force to a tip pinch force of **100–200 N**.
- **Clinical Advantages:** High durability, low maintenance, intuitive control, automatic grip (no sustained user effort), low cost, modular interchangeability.
- **Limitations:** Non-anthropomorphic appearance, social stigma, limited to 2-finger pinch (no spherical grasp), band replacement required every 6–12 months, not waterproof in standard configuration.

*(1.5 marks materials + 1.5 marks band + 1.5 marks actuation + 1.5 marks trade-offs + 1 mark clinical summary)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate the Output Pinch Force:**

The output force equation accounting for cable efficiency is:

$$F_{out} = \eta \cdot MA \cdot F_{in}$$

Substituting $MA = 4.5$, $\eta = 0.88$, $F_{in} = 30$ N:

$$F_{out} = 0.88 \cdot 4.5 \cdot 30 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{out} = 118.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Output Displacement:**

Using the work-energy principle (with efficiency correction):

$$d_{out} = \eta \cdot \frac{F_{in} \cdot d_{in}}{F_{out}} = \eta \cdot \frac{d_{in}}{MA}$$

$$d_{out} = 0.88 \cdot \frac{65 \text{ mm}}{4.5}$$

$$d_{out} = 12.71 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Trade-off Discussion:**

The user must generate sufficient cable force to overcome the selected band tension. The required user input force is:

$$F_{user} = \frac{F_{band}}{MA \cdot \eta}$$

- A **1.5 lb band (6.7 N)** requires only ~1.7 N of user input — minimal fatigue, but the hook will not grip heavy objects.
- A **9 lb band (40 N)** requires ~10 N of user input — significant sustained effort, causing shoulder fatigue over prolonged use.

**Conclusion:** The prosthetist should select the **heaviest band the user can comfortably operate for typical daily tasks** (usually 3–5 lb), balancing automatic grip strength against shoulder fatigue. This is a classic KTU-style trade-off analysis question.

*(2 marks force calc + 2 marks displacement + 3 marks trade-off analysis)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **PVC cosmetic gloves** and **custom silicone cosmetic covers** used as passive restorations in upper limb prosthetics. Cover materials, manufacturing process, realism, durability, cost, and clinical indications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a functional block diagram, describe the **control architecture of a myoelectric prosthetic hand**. Explain how EMG signals from residual forearm muscles are processed to produce multi-articulated finger movements. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PVC vs. Silicone Cosmetic Cover Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | PVC Glove | Silicone Custom Cover |
|-----------|-----------|------------------------|
| **Base Material** | Polyvinyl chloride with plasticizer | Medical-grade silicone elastomer (e.g., Dragon Skin, Ecoflex) |
| **Manufacturing** | Injection molding of stock sizes | Hand-poured multi-layer mold of contralateral hand cast |
| **Color Realism** | Limited stock tones (Caucasian, African-American, Asian, Hispanic) | Custom color-mixed pigments matched spectrophotometrically |
| **Surface Detail** | Smooth or lightly textured | Fingerprints, veins, knuckle wrinkles, hair (individually punched) |
| **Durability** | 6–12 months (UV degradation, staining) | 3–5 years |
| **Cost (INR)** | ₹3,000 – ₹15,000 | ₹50,000 – ₹2,00,000 |
| **Tear Resistance** | Moderate (punctures at high stress) | High (multi-layer reinforcement) |
| **Cleaning** | Soap and water | Soap and water; alcohol-safe |
| **Clinical Indication** | Standard post-fitting, low-budget, pediatric | High-priority aesthetic, social/work reintegration, public-facing professionals |

**Conclusion:** PVC gloves dominate the global market due to cost and accessibility; silicone covers are reserved for patients with high aesthetic priority, long-term wear, and adequate financial coverage.

*(1 mark per major parameter + 1 mark conclusion)*

**(b) Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand Control Architecture (7 marks):**

The myoelectric control architecture follows a **sense-process-actuate** pipeline:

**1. Signal Acquisition:** Surface EMG electrodes (typically 2 channels, sometimes 4–8 for pattern recognition) are placed over antagonist muscle pairs in the residual forearm — commonly the **flexor digitorum superficialis** (closes hand) and **extensor digitorum** (opens hand).

**2. Signal Conditioning:** The raw EMG signal (amplitude 50 µV – 5 mV, frequency 20–500 Hz) is:
- Amplified (gain ~1000×)
- Bandpass filtered (20–500 Hz)
- Rectified (full-wave)
- Envelope detected (RMS or moving average)
- Threshold-detected to yield a binary or proportional control signal

**3. Control Decision:**
- **Two-site control:** Co-contraction of both muscles triggers a mode switch (e.g., hand open/close → wrist rotation).
- **Pattern recognition:** Machine learning (LDA, SVM, or neural networks) classifies multiple grip intents (power, precision, lateral, hook, index).

**4. Actuation:** A microcontroller sends PWM signals to DC motors within the hand. Each finger may have its own micro-actuator, or a single motor drives all fingers through a differential mechanism. Position feedback from encoders closes the loop.

**5. Power Supply:** A 7.4 V Li-ion battery pack housed in the socket or cosmetic cover provides 8–24 hours of operation.

**Functional Block Diagram (textual form for clarity):**

`Residual Muscle → EMG Electrode → Amplifier → Filter → Rectifier → Threshold/Classifier → Microcontroller → DC Motor Driver → Finger Actuator → Hand Movement`

**Clinical Advantage:** No harness or cable required, full shoulder range preserved, intuitive control.

**Limitation:** Susceptible to sweat, electrode shift, and electromagnetic interference; battery-dependent.

*(1.5 marks acquisition + 1.5 marks processing + 1.5 marks actuation + 1.5 marks clinical + 1 mark block diagram)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Terminal Device Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing VO and VC mechanics** — Examiners award 0 marks if the student writes "VO hook closes when cable is pulled." Memorize: VO = **O**pening on pull; VC = **C**losing on pull.
> 2. **Forgetting the efficiency factor ($\eta$)** — Many students compute $F_{out} = MA \cdot F_{in}$ and lose 1 mark for not including frictional losses. Always state $\eta$ explicitly.
> 3. **Misidentifying hook components** — The "band" in a VO hook is **not** the cable; it is the **elastic restoring element**. This confuses ~30% of students.
> 4. **Unit errors** — Band tension is given in **pounds (lbf)**, not Newtons. Convert carefully: $1 \text{ lbf} = 4.448$ N.
> 5. **Skipping the trade-off discussion** — A pure numerical answer without engineering trade-off commentary loses at least 2 marks in Part B.
> 6. **Ignoring bilateral vs. unilateral context** — A bilateral amputee is *much more likely* to receive an aesthetic myoelectric hand, whereas a unilateral amputee is *more likely* to receive a work hook. Examiners test this clinical reasoning.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Terminal Device (TD)** is the distal end-effector of an upper limb prosthesis; the three tiers are **Hands, Work Hands, and Cosmetic Covers**.
- **Prosthetic Hands** come in passive, mechanical (body-powered), and myoelectric variants; key spec is **pinch force (50–150 N)** and **opening aperture (60–110 mm)**.
- **Work Hands (Hooks)** are cable-actuated; **VO** = cable pull opens, **VC** = cable pull closes; standard band tensions are **1.5, 3, 5, 7, 9 lbf**.
- **Mechanical Advantage** $MA = F_{out}/F_{in} = d_{in}/d_{out}$ — higher MA = more force, less displacement.
- **Cable Efficiency** $\eta < 1$ (typically 0.85–0.92) accounts for frictional losses in Bowden cables.
- **Cosmetic Covers** are passive; PVC is the workhorse (cheap, short life), silicone is premium (expensive, long life, custom realism).
- **Activity-specific hands** exist for sports (baseball, billiards, swimming, golf, archery).
- **Myoelectric control** uses EMG from residual forearm muscles, processed via amplification, filtering, rectification, and threshold/pattern recognition, then drives DC motor actuators.
- **Standard pediatric considerations** require lighter materials (titanium, aluminum), smaller apertures, and lower force bands.
- **Clinical decision pathway**: Bilateral + active → myoelectric hand; Unilateral + laborer → VO hook; Unilateral + aesthetic priority → silicone cover; Pediatric + low-budget → foam/PVC.
- **Key KTU keywords to memorize**: voluntary opening, voluntary closing, Band tension, Bowden cable, Mechanical advantage, Cable efficiency, Myoelectric control, Pattern recognition, Multi-articulated, Anthropomorphic, Cosmetic restoration, Silicone elastomer, PVC cosmetic glove.
- **Units cheat sheet**: Force in N (or lbf for bands), displacement in mm, mass in g, runtime in h, frequency in Hz, voltage in V (typically 7.4 V for Li-ion packs).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS

# Module 4 — Electric Wrist Rotation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electric Wrist Rotation** is a functional electrical stimulation (FES) technique used in upper-limb neuroprosthetics in which externally applied, controlled electrical pulses are delivered to the peripheral motor nerves or directly to the muscles that produce pronation and supination of the forearm, with the aim of restoring functional rotational wrist/forearm movement in individuals with upper-limb paralysis caused by spinal cord injury (SCI), stroke, cerebral palsy, or other central nervous system disorders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEBMT743, Module 4):**
> *Electric Wrist Rotation* refers to the FES-driven restoration of pronation–supination torque about the radioulnar joints using surface, percutaneous, or implanted electrodes, governed by recruitment curves, force–frequency characteristics, and closed-loop sensory feedback.

The two physiological movements targeted are:

- **Pronation** — inward rotation of the forearm that turns the palm to face **downward / posteriorly** (~$80^\circ$ to $90^\circ$ of range).
- **Supination** — outward rotation of the forearm that turns the palm to face **upward / anteriorly** (~$80^\circ$ to $90^\circ$ of range).

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike the wrist **flexion/extension** hinge joint (radiocarpal), wrist *rotation* occurs primarily at the **proximal and distal radioulnar joints**. This anatomical distinction is critical when selecting electrode placement and stimulation strategies.

### 1.2 Key Muscles and Their Innervation

| Movement | Primary Muscle | Nerve | Spinal Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pronation | Pronator teres | Median nerve | C6–C7 |
| Pronation | Pronator quadratus | Anterior interosseous (median) | C8–T1 |
| Pronation (assist) | Flexor carpi radialis | Median nerve | C6–C7 |
| Supination | Supinator | Deep branch of radial nerve | C5–C6 |
| Supination (power) | Biceps brachii | Musculocutaneous nerve | C5–C6 |

> [!TIP]
> The **biceps brachii** is a powerful supinator only when the elbow is flexed beyond ~$90^\circ$. For pure FES-induced supination with the arm extended, the deeper *supinator* muscle must be selectively targeted — this is one of the central engineering challenges of electric wrist rotation.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine turning a doorknob. If your brain could not send the "turn the knob" signal to your forearm, an **electric wrist rotation** neuroprosthesis acts like a *translator*:

1. The **brain's intent** (or a manual trigger button) is read by a controller.
2. The **controller** decides *which muscle to twitch* and *how hard*.
3. Tiny **electrical pulses** are sent through electrodes placed on or near the muscle.
4. The **muscle contracts**, producing rotation just like a healthy arm would.
5. **Sensors** (position, EMG, or accelerometers) tell the controller if the desired motion has been achieved.

In short: it is a *bypass circuit* around the damaged nerve pathway — electricity replaces the missing neural command.

### 1.4 Standard Quantitative Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Stimulation frequency** for tetanic fusion: **$f = 20$–$50~\text{Hz}$**
- **Pulse width**: **$PW = 100$–$400~\mu\text{s}$**
- **Current amplitude**: **$I = 5$–$50~\text{mA}$**
- **Maximum voluntary isometric pronation torque**: **$\approx 4$–$7~\text{Nm}$**
- **Maximum voluntary isometric supination torque**: **$\approx 5$–$9~\text{Nm}$**
- **Range of Motion (ROM)**: **$\approx 160^\circ$ to $180^\circ$ total arc**
- **Charge per phase limit (safe)**: **$Q_{ph} \leq 0.4~\mu\text{C}$** (Shannon limit, for safe neural stimulation)

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Steps of an Electric Wrist Rotation FES System

The system can be broken into **six functional stages**, each with a clear "why" and "how":

1. **Intent Acquisition** — *Why?* The paralyzed patient cannot voluntarily move the limb, so the controller must read an external command (push-button, joystick, residual EMG from a partially spared muscle, or cortical/EEG signal).
   *How?* A command sensor (digital input, residual EMG amplifier, or brain–computer interface) generates a reference signal $r(t)$.

2. **Trajectory Generation** — *Why?* Smooth functional rotation (e.g., to drink from a cup) needs a desired angular profile $\theta_d(t)$, not an instant step.
   *How?* A minimum-jerk or sigmoid trajectory is generated:
   $\theta_d(t) = \theta_{final} \cdot \left[ 10\left(\tfrac{t}{T}\right)^3 - 15\left(\tfrac{t}{T}\right)^4 + 6\left(\tfrac{t}{T}\right)^5 \right]$ for $0 \le t \le T$.

3. **Inverse Musculoskeletal Model** — *Why?* The controller must convert desired joint torque into the muscle stimulation that produces it.
   *How?* Compute required pronator/supinator activation $a_p(t)$ and $a_s(t)$ using joint torque:
   $\tau_{req}(t) = I_{arm} \cdot \ddot{\theta}_d(t) + B \cdot \dot{\theta}_d(t)$

4. **Stimulation Pulse Generation** — *Why?* Skeletal muscle requires a train of pulses to tetanize (sustain a contraction).
   *How?* Generate a biphasic, charge-balanced pulse train at frequency $f$ with adjustable pulse width $PW$ and current amplitude $I$ to control activation.

5. **Muscle Recruitment and Contraction** — *Why?* Motor units are recruited in an orderly, fixed sequence (size principle).
   *How?* Increasing $I$ and $PW$ recruits more motor units; increasing $f$ increases fused tetanic force.

6. **Feedback / Closed-Loop Correction** — *Why?* Muscle properties (fatigue, spasm, posture-dependent moment arms) cause variability.
   *How?* A position/velocity sensor (goniometer, IMU) feeds back $\theta(t)$ for PID correction:
   $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$, where $e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta(t)$.

### 2.2 The Three Fundamental Stimulation Laws

**(A) Recruitment Law — Force vs. Stimulation Amplitude (or Pulse Width):**
At a fixed frequency above tetanic fusion, force rises approximately linearly with stimulus intensity once threshold is exceeded:

$$
F(I) =
\begin{cases}
0, & I < I_{th} \\[4pt]
F_{max} \cdot \dfrac{I - I_{th}}{I_{max} - I_{th}}, & I_{th} \le I \le I_{max} \\[6pt]
F_{max}, & I > I_{max}
\end{cases}
$$

**(B) Fusion Law — Force vs. Stimulation Frequency:**
At a fixed supramaximal amplitude, isometric force grows with pulse frequency until full tetanic fusion:

$$
F(f) \;=\; F_{tet} \cdot \left[\, 1 - e^{-(f - f_{min})/\tau_f} \,\right] \quad \text{for } f \ge f_{min}
$$

where $f_{min} \approx 8$–$12~\text{Hz}$ (single-twitch onset) and full tetanus occurs at $f \approx 30$–$50~\text{Hz}$.

**(C) Fatigue Law — Force Decay Over Time:**
Sustained FES causes force to decay approximately exponentially:

$$
F(t) \;=\; F_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{fat}}
$$

Typical $\tau_{fat}$ for FES-evoked contractions: **$30$–$90$ seconds**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = F_{max} \cdot \dfrac{I - I_{th}}{I_{max} - I_{th}}$ | Recruitment (linear) | N | $I_{th} \approx 5$–$10~\text{mA}$ |
| 2 | $F(f) = F_{tet} \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-(f - f_{min})/\tau_f} \,]$ | Force–frequency | N | $f = 10$–$50~\text{Hz}$ |
| 3 | $F(t) = F_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{fat}}$ | Muscle fatigue | N | $\tau_{fat} = 30$–$90~\text{s}$ |
| 4 | $Q_{ph} = I_{amp} \cdot PW$ | Charge per phase | $\mu\text{C}$ | $\le 0.4~\mu\text{C}$ (Shannon) |
| 5 | $k = \log_{10}(Q/A)$ | Shannon $k$-index | – | $k \le 1.5$ for safe stim |
| 6 | $\tau_{joint} = F_{muscle} \cdot d_{MA}$ | Joint torque | Nm | $d_{MA} \approx 1$–$3~\text{cm}$ |
| 7 | $I_{arm} \cdot \ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} = \tau_{muscle} - \tau_{load}$ | Forearm dynamics | Nm, rad | $I_{arm} \approx 0.005$–$0.02~\text{kgm}^2$ |
| 8 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot e$ | PID controller | V or mA | Tuned per patient |
| 9 | $f_{tetanus} \approx 1/T_{twitch}$ | Tetanic fusion | Hz | $T_{twitch} \approx 80$–$120~\text{ms}$ |
| 10 | $\eta_{stim} = W_{out}/E_{stim}$ | FES efficiency | % | $1$–$10\%$ typical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations form the bedrock of *every* FES controller in clinical use — including the **Freehand System** (Cleveland FES Center), **Bioness H200**, and **NESS H200 Handmaster**. They also underpin the tuning of closed-loop implantable systems such as the **STIMuGRIP** and the **MANUS** arm.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rehabilitation Robotics** — Hybrid FES + robotic exoskeleton for stroke recovery.
- **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** — Restoring the ability to turn a key, hold a cup, or use a phone.
- **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — Decoded motor intent drives FES for wrist rotation.
- **Prosthetic Hand Control** — Combined wrist rotation + grasp with one controller.
- **Space Medicine Research (NASA)** — Countermeasure for forearm deconditioning in microgravity.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Recruitment Curve from First Principles

**Goal:** Derive the linear relation between applied current amplitude $I$ and resulting muscle force $F$.

**Step 1 — Motor Unit Size Principle (Henneman 1957).**
In a healthy muscle, motor units are recruited from smallest (slow, fatigue-resistant) to largest (fast, fatigable) as excitatory drive increases. In FES, the *applied* current spatially summates excitatory postsynaptic potentials at the axon hillock. A motor unit is recruited when the local depolarization exceeds its rheobase-threshold.

**Step 2 — Threshold Distribution.**
Let the threshold current for the $n^{th}$ motor unit be $I_{th,n}$, distributed uniformly over $[I_{th,min}, I_{th,max}]$ with density $\rho(I_{th}) = \dfrac{N}{\Delta I}$ where $\Delta I = I_{th,max} - I_{th,min}$ and $N$ is the total number of motor units.

**Step 3 — Number of Active Motor Units at Current $I$.**
The number of motor units whose threshold is exceeded:

$$
n_{active}(I) = \int_{0}^{I} \rho(I_{th})\, dI_{th} = N \cdot \dfrac{I - I_{th,min}}{\Delta I}
$$

This is valid only when $I_{th,min} \le I \le I_{th,max}$; outside this range, $n_{active}=0$ or $n_{active}=N$.

**Step 4 — Force Proportional to Active Motor Units.**
Assuming equal force contribution per motor unit, $F_{MU}$, total force is:

$$
F(I) = n_{active}(I) \cdot F_{MU} = N \cdot F_{MU} \cdot \dfrac{I - I_{th,min}}{\Delta I}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Maximal Force and Threshold.**
At $I = I_{th,max}$, all $N$ motor units are active, giving $F_{max} = N \cdot F_{MU}$.
Substituting and identifying $I_{th,min} = I_{th}$:

$$
\boxed{\, F(I) \;=\; F_{max} \cdot \dfrac{I - I_{th}}{I_{th,max} - I_{th}} \,}
$$

**Conclusion:** This confirms the linear recruitment model. The slope $\dfrac{F_{max}}{I_{th,max} - I_{th}}$ defines the *recruitment gain* of the FES system — a key tuning parameter in clinical stimulators.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force–Frequency Relationship and Tetanic Fusion

**Goal:** Derive the steady-state tetanic force as a function of pulse frequency.

**Step 1 — Single Twitch Force Profile.**
A single supramaximal pulse produces a twitch with force profile approximated as:

$$
f_{tw}(t) = F_{tw,peak} \cdot \dfrac{t}{T_{rise}} \cdot e^{1 - t/T_{rise}} \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le T_{fall}
$$

where $T_{rise} \approx 30$–$60~\text{ms}$ and $T_{fall} \approx 80$–$150~\text{ms}$.

**Step 2 — Linear Summation of Twitches.**
For a train of pulses at inter-pulse interval $T = 1/f$, when $T$ is short, twitches superimpose. The summation factor $S(f)$ for a regular train is approximately:

$$
S(f) \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{1 - e^{-(f - f_{min})/\tau_f}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Tetanic Force.**
The fused tetanic force is the steady-state plateau where successive twitches fully overlap:

$$
F(f) = F_{tw,peak} \cdot S_{max} \cdot \left[\, 1 - e^{-(f - f_{min})/\tau_f} \,\right]
$$

Identifying $F_{tet} = F_{tw,peak} \cdot S_{max}$:

$$
\boxed{\, F(f) \;=\; F_{tet} \cdot \left[\, 1 - e^{-(f - f_{min})/\tau_f} \,\right] \quad f \ge f_{min} \,}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (typical pronator teres).**
Given $f_{min} = 10~\text{Hz}$, $\tau_f = 5~\text{Hz}$, $F_{tet} = 50~\text{N}$:

At $f = 20~\text{Hz}$:
$F(20) = 50 \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-(20-10)/5} \,] = 50 \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-2} \,] = 50 \cdot 0.8647 = 43.2~\text{N}$

At $f = 35~\text{Hz}$:
$F(35) = 50 \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-(35-10)/5} \,] = 50 \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-5} \,] = 50 \cdot 0.9933 = 49.7~\text{N}$ (≈$99\%$ fused).

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of $f$ in clinical FES is a **trade-off** — higher $f$ gives smoother, stronger force but **accelerates fatigue**. Most clinical wrist-rotation systems operate at $f = 20$–$30~\text{Hz}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Forearm Rotational Dynamics and Required Torque

**Goal:** Derive the joint torque needed to achieve a desired pronation–supination trajectory.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram of the Forearm.**
Treating the forearm + hand as a rigid body rotating about the long axis of the radius:

Forces and torques acting:
- Muscle torque from pronators/supinators: $\tau_{muscle}(t)$
- Gravitational torque (varies with arm posture): $\tau_{grav}$
- External load torque (e.g., a cup): $\tau_{load}$
- Joint damping: $B \cdot \dot{\theta}(t)$

**Step 2 — Newton's Second Law for Rotation.**

$$
I_{arm} \cdot \ddot{\theta}(t) \;=\; \tau_{muscle}(t) - B \cdot \dot{\theta}(t) - \tau_{grav}(\theta) - \tau_{load}(\theta, t)
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to Express Required Muscle Torque.**

$$
\tau_{muscle}(t) \;=\; I_{arm} \cdot \ddot{\theta}_d(t) + B \cdot \dot{\theta}_d(t) + \tau_{grav}(\theta_d) + \tau_{load}(\theta_d, t)
$$

**Step 4 — Convert Torque to Stimulation via Inverse Model.**
With moment arm $d_{MA}$ (lever arm of pronator teres ≈ $1.8~\text{cm}$ at neutral, supinator ≈ $2.1~\text{cm}$):

$$
F_{muscle}(t) \;=\; \dfrac{\tau_{muscle}(t)}{d_{MA}}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Recruitment Model to Compute Current.**
Inverting the linear recruitment law:

$$
I(t) \;=\; I_{th} + \left(I_{max} - I_{th}\right) \cdot \dfrac{F_{muscle}(t)}{F_{max}}
$$

**Numerical Example.**
Suppose a patient with a paralyzed forearm (mass $m = 1.2~\text{kg}$, length $L = 0.26~\text{m}$) is asked to supinate against gravity from $0^\circ$ to $90^\circ$ in $T = 1.5~\text{s}$ using a minimum-jerk trajectory.

Moment of inertia (uniform rod about end): $I_{arm} = \dfrac{1}{3} m L^2 = \dfrac{1}{3}(1.2)(0.26)^2 = 0.027~\text{kgm}^2$

Peak angular acceleration (from min-jerk): $\ddot{\theta}_{max} \approx \dfrac{30 \theta_{final}}{T^2}$ (approximation):

$$
\ddot{\theta}_{max} \;\approx\; \dfrac{30 \cdot (\pi/2)}{(1.5)^2} \;=\; \dfrac{47.12}{2.25} \;\approx\; 20.9~\text{rad/s}^2
$$

Required peak inertial torque:

$$
\tau_{peak} = I_{arm} \cdot \ddot{\theta}_{max} = 0.027 \cdot 20.9 \approx 0.56~\text{Nm}
$$

Required muscle force (with $d_{MA} = 2.0~\text{cm} = 0.02~\text{m}$):

$$
F_{muscle,peak} = \dfrac{0.56}{0.02} = 28~\text{N}
$$

This is well within the capability of the supinator (max force $\approx 100$–$150~\text{N}$).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Charge-Balanced Biphasic Stimulus and Safety

**Goal:** Show that biphasic stimulation ensures zero net DC charge, preventing electrode and tissue damage.

**Step 1 — Cathodic (excitation) phase.** Current $-I_c$ for duration $PW_c$.
**Step 2 — Anodic (recovery) phase.** Current $+I_a$ for duration $PW_a$.

Charge balance requires equal magnitude of charge in each phase:

$$
I_c \cdot PW_c \;=\; I_a \cdot PW_a
$$

**Step 3 — Symmetric biphasic case.** $I_c = I_a = I$, $PW_c = PW_a = PW$:

$$
Q_{cathode} = I \cdot PW = Q_{anode}
$$

$$
Q_{net,DC} = Q_{cathode} - Q_{anode} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Shannon Safety Limit.**
For safe chronic stimulation, the **Shannon $k$-index** must satisfy:

$$
k \;=\; \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{Q_{ph}}{A}\right) \;\le\; 1.5
$$

where $Q_{ph}$ is charge per phase in $\mu\text{C}$ and $A$ is electrode area in $\text{mm}^2$.

For $PW = 300~\mu\text{s}$ and $I = 10~\text{mA}$ on a $A = 50~\text{mm}^2$ electrode:

$$
Q_{ph} = 10 \cdot 0.3 = 3~\mu\text{C}
$$

$$
k = \log_{10}(3/50) = \log_{10}(0.06) = -1.22 \ll 1.5 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Safe.}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always design **charge-balanced biphasic** waveforms for FES. DC residual charge causes electrochemical corrosion of the electrode and electrochemical damage to the tissue — a critical safety failure mode in implantables.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Engineering Design Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | Best Clinical Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency $f$ | Force, smoothness | Fatigue life | $20$–$30~\text{Hz}$ |
| Pulse width $PW$ | Recruitment, force | Selectivity | $200$–$300~\mu\text{s}$ |
| Amplitude $I$ | Force, recruitment | Patient comfort | $10$–$25~\text{mA}$ |
| Electrode size | Current spread, comfort | Selectivity | $20$–$50~\text{mm}^2$ |
| Inter-electrode distance | Deeper penetration | Selectivity | $2$–$3~\text{cm}$ |
| Stimulation mode (open-loop) | Simplicity | Accuracy | Used in rehab only |
| Stimulation mode (closed-loop) | Accuracy, fatigue mgmt | Complexity | Used in implanted FES |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEBMT743]**
*List the two primary muscles responsible for (a) pronation and (b) supination of the forearm, with their nerve supply.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(a) **Pronation**:
1. **Pronator teres** — supplied by the **median nerve** (C6–C7).
2. **Pronator quadratus** — supplied by the **anterior interosseous branch of the median nerve** (C8–T1).

(b) **Supination**:
1. **Supinator** — supplied by the **deep branch of the radial nerve** (C5–C6).
2. **Biceps brachii** (power supinator when elbow is flexed) — supplied by the **musculocutaneous nerve** (C5–C6).

> *(Award 0.5 marks per correct muscle, 0.5 marks per correct nerve; 3 marks total.)*

---

|---|---|

| Pulse width $PW$ | **$100$–$400~\mu\text{s}$** (most common: $200$–$300~\mu\text{s}$) |
| Current amplitude $I$ | **$5$–$50~\text{mA}$** (most common: $10$–$25~\text{mA}$) |
