### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Module internal choice is mandatory. *Answer ANY ONE FULL QUESTION from the module.* Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Electrophysiology and Recruitment Design**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop functional electrical stimulation (FES) system for restoring wrist rotation in a patient with C5–C6 spinal cord injury. Clearly label the feedback sensor, controller, stimulator, and muscle group. [7 marks]*

**(b)** *A linear recruitment model is given by: $F(I) = F_{max} \cdot \dfrac{I - I_{th}}{I_{max} - I_{th}}$. For the pronator teres muscle of a patient: $F_{max} = 60~\text{N}$, $I_{th} = 6~\text{mA}$, $I_{max} = 30~\text{mA}$.*
*(i) Plot (describe) the recruitment curve.*
*(ii) Calculate the force produced when $I = 12~\text{mA}$ and when $I = 24~\text{mA}$.*
*(iii) If the moment arm of the pronator teres is $d_{MA} = 2.0~\text{cm}$, find the joint torque in each case. [7 marks]*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working of a closed-loop FES system (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark) Patient input source:** Intent is sensed from residual EMG (e.g., from the trapezius or sternocleidomastoid) or via push-button/joystick.
2. **(1 mark) Trajectory generator:** A reference angular trajectory $\theta_d(t)$ is produced (e.g., a min-jerk profile from $0^\circ$ to $80^\circ$ supination over 1.5 s).
3. **(1 mark) Sensor feedback:** A biaxial goniometer or IMU mounted on the dorsal forearm measures actual rotation $\theta(t)$; the error $e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta(t)$ is computed.
4. **(1 mark) Controller:** A PID controller computes $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$.
5. **(1 mark) Stimulator output:** A biphasic, charge-balanced current pulse train is generated with adjustable $I$, $PW$, and $f$.
6. **(1 mark) Electrode–muscle interface:** Electrodes over the pronator teres (pronation channel) and supinator (supination channel) deliver pulses.
7. **(1 mark) Closed-loop operation:** Output angle is continuously fed back; the loop automatically increases $I$ if the patient is fatigued, and decreases it on spasm or overshoot.

**Block diagram (to be drawn by student):**
[Input intent] → [Reference generator] → [Comparator $+/-$] ← [Goniometer]
                       ↓ (error $e$)
              [PID controller] → [Stimulator] → [Electrodes] → [Muscles] → [Joint rotation] → [Task]

**(b) Recruitment calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) Recruitment curve description (2 marks):**
Linear, passing through $(I_{th}, 0) = (6, 0)$ and $(I_{max}, F_{max}) = (30, 60)$. Slope = $\dfrac{60}{30-6} = 2.5~\text{N/mA}$. The curve is flat at $F=0$ for $I<6~\text{mA}$ and saturates at $F=60~\text{N}$ for $I>30~\text{mA}$.

**(ii) Force calculations (3 marks):**

For $I = 12~\text{mA}$:

$$
F(12) = 60 \cdot \dfrac{12 - 6}{30 - 6} = 60 \cdot \dfrac{6}{24} = 60 \cdot 0.25 = \mathbf{15~\text{N}}
$$

For $I = 24~\text{mA}$:

$$
F(24) = 60 \cdot \dfrac{24 - 6}{30 - 6} = 60 \cdot \dfrac{18}{24} = 60 \cdot 0.75 = \mathbf{45~\text{N}}
$$

**(iii) Joint torque (2 marks):**

$\tau = F \cdot d_{MA}$, with $d_{MA} = 0.02~\text{m}$:

- $\tau(12) = 15 \cdot 0.02 = \mathbf{0.30~\text{Nm}}$
- $\tau(24) = 45 \cdot 0.02 = \mathbf{0.90~\text{Nm}}$

**Verification against max voluntary torque ($\approx 4$–$7~\text{Nm}$):** Even at maximal stimulation, the FES-evoked torque (with surface electrodes only) is below physiological maximum — illustrating why surface FES is often inadequate for strong functional tasks, motivating **implanted** systems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Stimulation Parameters and Safety**

**(a)** *Explain the force–frequency relationship in FES-evoked muscle contraction. Using the equation $F(f) = F_{tet} \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-(f - f_{min})/\tau_f} \,]$, compute the force for $f = 20, 30,$ and $50~\text{Hz}$, given $F_{tet} = 50~\text{N}$, $f_{min} = 10~\text{Hz}$, $\tau_f = 5~\text{Hz}$. Discuss why clinical FES does not use $f > 50~\text{Hz}$. [7 marks]*

**(b)** *Design a charge-balanced biphasic stimulation pulse for a wrist rotation FES system. Given: current amplitude $I = 15~\text{mA}$, pulse width $PW = 250~\mu\text{s}$, electrode area $A = 25~\text{mm}^2$. Verify Shannon safety limit and explain the role of the interphase delay. [7 marks]*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Force–frequency relationship and calculations (7 marks):**

**Theory (3 marks):**
As the pulse frequency $f$ increases above $f_{min}$ (typically $8$–$12~\text{Hz}$), individual muscle twitches fuse. With increasing $f$, fused force rises asymptotically toward the maximum tetanic force $F_{tet}$. The time constant $\tau_f$ characterizes how quickly the muscle reaches full fusion. Higher $f$ produces smoother, stronger force but accelerates metabolic fatigue and can cause patient discomfort.

**Calculations (3 marks):**

At $f = 20~\text{Hz}$:

$$
F(20) = 50 \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-(20-10)/5} \,] = 50 \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-2} \,] = 50 \cdot 0.8647 = \mathbf{43.2~\text{N}}
$$

At $f = 30~\text{Hz}$:

$$
F(30) = 50 \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-(30-10)/5} \,] = 50 \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-4} \,] = 50 \cdot 0.9817 = \mathbf{49.1~\text{N}}
$$

At $f = 50~\text{Hz}$:

$$
F(50) = 50 \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-(50-10)/5} \,] = 50 \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-8} \,] = 50 \cdot 0.99966 = \mathbf{49.98~\text{N}} \approx \mathbf{50~\text{N}}
$$

**Discussion — Why $f \le 50~\text{Hz}$ (1 mark):**
- Beyond $f = 50~\text{Hz}$, force gains are negligible (saturated).
- Fatigue rate increases sharply with $f$ (greater ATP consumption, faster metabolite accumulation).
- Patient discomfort increases; risk of reflex spasm rises.
- Charge delivery per unit time increases, potentially approaching unsafe limits.

**(b) Biphasic pulse design and Shannon safety (7 marks):**

**Charge per phase (2 marks):**

$$
Q_{ph} = I \cdot PW = (15 \times 10^{-3}~\text{A}) \cdot (250 \times 10^{-6}~\text{s}) = 3.75 \times 10^{-6}~\text{C} = \mathbf{3.75~\mu\text{C}}
$$

**Shannon $k$-index (2 marks):**

$$
k = \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{Q_{ph}}{A} \right) = \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{3.75~\mu\text{C}}{25~\text{mm}^2} \right) = \log_{10}(0.15) = \mathbf{-0.82}
$$

Since $k = -0.82 \ll 1.5$, the stimulation is **well within safe limits**. (1 mark)

**Pulse design (1.5 marks):**
The biphasic symmetric pulse has two equal phases of $250~\mu\text{s}$ at $\pm 15~\text{mA}$, separated by an interphase delay of typically $50$–$100~\mu\text{s}$.

**Role of interphase delay (1.5 marks):**
- A short gap (e.g., $50$–$100~\mu\text{s}$) between cathodic and anodic phases allows the cathodic charge to clear the electrode–tissue interface before the anodic phase reverses the polarization.
- Prevents the anodic phase from immediately cancelling the neural excitation.
- Reduces electrochemical stress on the electrode.
- Improves the safety and longevity of implanted electrodes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Electric Wrist Rotation Questions — Where Marks Are Lost:**
>
> 1. **Confusing biceps as a flexor only.** The biceps brachii is a *powerful supinator* when the elbow is flexed. Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks in muscle-list questions.
> 2. **Omitting units.** Always write $\text{Hz}$, $\mu\text{s}$, $\text{mA}$, $\text{N}$, $\text{Nm}$ explicitly. KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 3. **Confusing pronation and supination directions.** Pronation = palm *down/back*; supination = palm *up/forward*. A wrong direction in a clinical question = full mark deduction.
> 4. **Using monophasic waveforms in safety questions.** FES for chronic use **must** be biphasic and charge-balanced. Stating "monophasic" loses the safety marks.
> 5. **Skipping the recruitment threshold.** In any force calculation, students often write $F = F_{max} \cdot I / I_{max}$, forgetting the $-I_{th}$ term. This causes a $20$–$25\%$ error in computed force.
> 6. **Drawing arrows on diagrams.** Block diagrams in KTU answers should be unidirectional arrows with text labels. Misdirected or unlabeled arrows lose 1 mark.
> 7. **Ignoring fatigue in long-answer questions.** Any time a controller is discussed, a one-sentence mention of *muscle fatigue and its compensation* (e.g., via closed-loop control or stimulation rotation across multiple electrode sites) scores an extra mark.
> 8. **Forgetting the proximal vs. distal radioulnar joint distinction.** Wrist rotation is *not* at the radiocarpal joint. This anatomical error costs 1 mark in design questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electric Wrist Rotation**

- ⚡ **Definition:** FES-driven restoration of pronation and supination in paralyzed forearms.
- 💪 **Pronators:** Pronator teres (median, C6–C7), Pronator quadratus (anterior interosseous, C8–T1).
- 🔄 **Supinators:** Supinator (deep radial, C5–C6), Biceps brachii (musculocutaneous, C5–C6) — *power supinator only with elbow flexed*.
- 🦴 **Joints involved:** Proximal and distal **radioulnar** joints (NOT radiocarpal).
- 📏 **Range of motion:** $\sim 80^\circ$–$90^\circ$ in each direction; total arc $\sim 160^\circ$–$180^\circ$.
- ⚙️ **Typical FES parameters:** $f = 20$–$50~\text{Hz}$; $PW = 100$–$400~\mu\text{s}$; $I = 5$–$50~\text{mA}$.
- 📈 **Recruitment:** Linear model $F = F_{max} \cdot (I - I_{th})/(I_{max} - I_{th})$.
- 📊 **Force–frequency:** Exponential saturation $F(f) = F_{tet} \cdot [\, 1 - e^{-(f - f_{min})/\tau_f} \,]$.
- ⏳ **Fatigue:** Exponential decay $F(t) = F_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{fat}}$; $\tau_{fat} = 30$–$90~\text{s}$.
- 🛡️ **Safety:** Always **biphasic, charge-balanced**; verify Shannon $k \le 1.5$.
- 🎯 **Electrode choice:** Deep muscles (supinator, pronator quadratus) → **percutaneous or implanted** electrodes; superficial muscles (biceps, pronator teres) → surface acceptable.
- 🧠 **Control:** Open-loop for rehab, **closed-loop PID** with goniometer/IMU feedback for functional use.
- 🏥 **Clinical use:** C5–C6 SCI (Freehand, Bioness H200), stroke rehabilitation, BCI-driven FES.
- 🚫 **Pitfall:** Higher $f$ gives more force but **drastically increases fatigue** — clinical systems cap at $\sim 30~\text{Hz}$.
- 🔋 **Charge balance:** $I_c \cdot PW_c = I_a \cdot PW_a$; net DC charge must be zero.
- 📐 **Torque:** $\tau = F \cdot d_{MA}$, with $d_{MA} \approx 1.5$–$2.5~\text{cm}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hook — "PPS-BMN":**
> **P**ronator **P**alisades (Pronator teres + Pronator quadratus) → **B**iceps **M**edian-**N**erve sets the muscle map.
> Use this mnemonic to recall: Pronation = two pronator muscles; Biceps does both elbow flexion AND supination.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wrist Flexion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wrist Flexion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Wrist Flexion** is defined as the **rotational movement of the hand in the sagittal plane about the transverse axis of the wrist joint**, resulting in a decrease of the angle formed between the **volar (palmar) surface of the hand** and the **anterior surface of the forearm**. In standard anatomical position (forearm supinated, palm facing forward), wrist flexion is the motion that brings the palm of the hand toward the anterior aspect of the forearm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Wrist flexion is a planar, single-degree-of-freedom (1-DOF) movement that takes place predominantly at the **radiocarpal joint** (between the distal radius and the proximal row of carpal bones — scaphoid, lunate, and triquetrum). It is fundamentally distinct from *ulnar deviation* (frontal plane) and *radial deviation* (frontal plane), which must not be conflated during clinical analysis.

### Quantitative Boundaries of the Movement

| Parameter | Standard Value | Clinical/Engineering Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Active Range of Motion (AROM)** | **0° to 80°** | Beyond 80° is considered abnormal/hyperflexion |
| **Total Passive Range (PROM)** | **0° to 90°** | Passive end-feel assessment |
| **Functional Range for ADLs** | **0° to 40°** | Eating, writing, typing |
| **Mean Angular Velocity (Normal)** | **~150°/s** | Used in FES controller design |
| **Joint Centre (Axis of Rotation)** | **Capitate Head** | Kinematic reference point |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Laptop Screen"**
> Think of your forearm as the **keyboard base** of a laptop, and your hand as the **screen**. Wrist flexion is exactly the motion you perform when you **close the laptop lid** — bringing the palm surface down toward the forearm. The hinge of the laptop corresponds to the **radiocarpal joint** (the wrist), and the "force" that closes the screen comes from the **flexor muscles** (the cables inside the laptop hinge), which are electrically activated by your brain via the **median and ulnar nerves** (the control signals running through the cables).

### A Second Analogy — "The Fishing Rod Tip"
Imagine a fishing rod held horizontally:
- The **rod** = the forearm
- The **flexible tip** = the hand
- When you **curl the tip downward** toward the rod → that bending is **wrist flexion**
- The **force** applied to the tip comes from the fishing line (muscles/tendons) being pulled by a hand (the brain) somewhere up the rod.

This curling action is what FES (Functional Electrical Stimulation) systems attempt to *recreate* in patients who have lost volitional control due to spinal cord injury (SCI) or stroke.

## 1.3 Why Wrist Flexion Matters in Neural Prosthetics

In the context of **PEBMT743 — Neural Prosthesis & Implants**, wrist flexion is a *foundational motor function* because:

1. **Tenodesis Effect**: Wrist flexion couples mechanically with finger closure — when the wrist flexes ~30°, the fingers naturally curl, enabling grip without active finger flexor contraction. This is exploited in **tendon-transfer surgeries** and in **FES grasp-restoration systems**.
2. **Object Manipulation**: Most Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) — holding a cup, brushing teeth, writing — require at least 30–40° of wrist flexion.
3. **Implant Target**: The flexor muscles (especially FCR and FCU) are large, superficial, and easy to stimulate via surface or implanted electrodes, making them prime targets for **neuroprosthetic grasp systems** (e.g., the Freehand system by NeuroControl Corp.).
4. **Closed-Loop Control Benchmark**: Wrist flexion is a simple 1-DOF system, making it the *gold standard* test-bed for validating new FES controllers (PID, fuzzy logic, neural-network-based controllers).

## 1.4 Key Terminology Mastery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinguish these terms — KTU examiners frequently test them:**
> - **Wrist Flexion** — Palmar-ward bending in sagittal plane (0° to 80°)
> - **Wrist Extension** — Dorsal-ward bending in sagittal plane (0° to 70°)
> - **Radial Deviation (Abduction)** — Thumb-side movement in frontal plane (0° to 20°)
> - **Ulnar Deviation (Adduction)** — Little-finger-side movement in frontal plane (0° to 30°)
> - **Pronation / Supination** — Rotations of the *forearm* (not wrist)
> - **Tenodesis** — Passive finger closure produced by wrist extension (the *opposite* mechanical coupling, but conceptually paired)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biomechanics of the Wrist Joint

The wrist is a **biaxial (2-DOF) ellipsoidal joint** formed by the articulation of:
- The **distal radius** (and the **triangular fibrocartilage complex**, TFCC) proximally
- The **proximal carpal row** (scaphoid, lunate, triquetrum) distally
- The **distal carpal row** (trapezium, trapezoid, capitate, hamate) and the **base of the metacarpals** further distally

Wrist flexion is mechanically coupled with **slight ulnar deviation and forearm pronation** — this is termed the **dart-throwing motion (DTM)** and is critical in functional tasks. In a pure FES system, this coupling must be accounted for to avoid unintended co-movements.

## 2.2 Primary Muscles (Wrist Flexors) — The "Flexor Group"

| Muscle | Abbreviation | Primary Nerve | Origin | Insertion | Cross-Sectional Area (approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flexor Carpi Radialis** | FCR | Median | Medial epicondyle of humerus | Base of 2nd & 3rd metacarpals | ~**3.5 cm²** |
| **Flexor Carpi Ulnaris** | FCU | Ulnar | Medial epicondyle & olecranon | Pisiform, hamate, 5th metacarpal | ~**3.2 cm²** |
| **Palmaris Longus** | PL | Median | Medial epicondyle | Flexor retinaculum & palmar aponeurosis | ~**1.0 cm²** |
| **Flexor Digitorum Superficialis** | FDS | Median | Medial epicondyle, ulnar collateral ligament, coronoid process | Middle phalanges of digits 2–5 | ~**4.5 cm²** |
| **Flexor Digitorum Profundus** | FDP | Median (digits 2–3) & Ulnar (digits 4–5) | Proximal ulna & interosseous membrane | Distal phalanges of digits 2–5 | ~**5.0 cm²** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Synergistic Action Insight:** FCR and FCU are the **primary pure wrist flexors** (they do not cross the fingers, only the wrist). FDS and FDP act as **secondary flexors** because they cross both the wrist and the finger joints. In FES engineering, stimulating FCR and FCU gives **cleaner wrist-only flexion**, while stimulating FDS/FDP risks unwanted finger curling (which can be a *feature* for grasp, but a *bug* for isolated wrist control).

## 2.3 Neural Control Pathway

The motor command for wrist flexion travels along the following hierarchy:

1. **Primary Motor Cortex (M1)** — Precentral gyrus, lateral region (homunculus hand area)
2. **Corticospinal Tract** — Descends through internal capsule, cerebral peduncle, pons, medullary pyramids
3. **Decussation at Medullary Pyramids** — ~85% of fibres cross to the contralateral side
4. **Lateral Corticospinal Tract** — Descends in the lateral funiculus of the spinal cord
5. **Lower Motor Neurons** — Exit at spinal levels **C7–T1** (root values), travelling via the **brachial plexus** (lateral and medial cords) to form the **median nerve** and **ulnar nerve**
6. **Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ)** — The final synapse onto the muscle fibres of FCR, FCU, PL, FDS, FDP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Concept — "Lesion Level Determines Prosthesis Strategy":**
> - **C5–C6 lesion (Tetraplegia):** Shoulder and elbow control preserved, but wrist and hand are paralysed → FES systems target FCR, FCU, ECR (extensors) to restore **tenodesis grasp**
> - **C7 lesion:** Wrist extension preserved, but finger flexion weak → Strategy uses **active tenodesis** with FES augmentation
> - **C8–T1 lesion:** Intrinsic hand muscles lost → FES for thumb opposition and finger flexion becomes critical

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CHEAT-SHEET: All formulas tested for wrist flexion / FES wrist systems**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = F_m \cdot d \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | **Moment Arm Model** — Torque produced by a flexor muscle at joint angle $\theta$ | $\tau$: torque (Nm); $F_m$: muscle force (N); $d$: moment arm (m); $\theta$: joint angle (rad) | Estimating isometric torque capacity of FCR/FCU |
| 2 | $F_m = \sigma \cdot \text{PCSA} \cdot \text{FL} / \text{OptFL}$ | **Hill-Type Muscle Model** — Muscle force from cross-sectional area | $\sigma$: specific tension (~**30 N/cm²**); PCSA: physiological CSA (cm²); FL: fascicle length; OptFL: optimal fascicle length | Forward dynamics simulation |
| 3 | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \int_0^t \omega(\tau) \, d\tau$ | **Joint Angle from Angular Velocity** | $\theta$: joint angle (rad); $\omega$: angular velocity (rad/s) | FES feedback-loop design |
| 4 | $I \ddot{\theta} + B \dot{\theta} + K\theta = \tau_m - \tau_e$ | **Equation of Motion (Pendulum Model of Wrist)** | $I$: forearm-hand inertia (~**0.05 kg·m²**); $B$: damping; $K$: stiffness; $\tau_m$: muscle torque; $\tau_e$: external torque | State-space FES controller design |
| 5 | $u(t) = A \cdot \text{PW} \cdot f$ | **FES Stimulation Dose** | $u$: charge per second; $A$: pulse amplitude (mA); PW: pulse width ($\mu$s); $f$: frequency (Hz) | Programming FES stimulator |
| 6 | $\tau_{\text{stim}} = K_{\text{stim}} \cdot u(t - t_d)$ | **Recruitment Model** (1st-order lag) | $K_{\text{stim}}$: gain; $t_d$: neuromuscular delay (~**25–50 ms**) | Predicting torque response |
| 7 | $\lambda(t) = \alpha \cdot e^{-t/\tau_1} + (1-\alpha) e^{-t/\tau_2}$ | **Muscle Fatigue Model** (bi-exponential) | $\tau_1, \tau_2$: time constants (~**5 s, 60 s**); $\alpha$: weighting factor | Fatigue compensation in closed-loop FES |
| 8 | $\text{RMS}_{\text{EMG}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}$ | **EMG Amplitude (muscle activation index)** | $x_i$: raw EMG samples (mV) | Proportional FES control from residual EMG |
| 9 | $\text{MAV} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert$ | **Mean Absolute Value of EMG** | Same as above | Onset detection, simple threshold trigger |
| 10 | $\text{ARV} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert$ (with $\vert \cdot \vert$ being abs) | **Average Rectified Value of EMG** | Same as above | Direct drive of stimulation amplitude |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX NOTE:** In the above table, the expression $\vert x_i \vert$ uses the LaTeX absolute-value command `\vert` (NOT the ASCII pipe `|`). This avoids breaking the markdown table syntax. **Students writing notes must do the same when copying.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The wrist flexion model is the **benchmark 1-DOF system** used to validate:

- **PID Controllers** for FES (e.g., the work of Crago, Mortimer, and Peckham at Case Western, foundational to the Freehand System)
- **Iterative Learning Controllers (ILC)** that "learn" the patient's specific recruitment curves over repeated cycles
- **EMG-triggered FES systems** (e.g., the **NessH200** by Bioness) where residual volitional EMG in partially paralysed muscles triggers stimulation of the same muscle
- **Implanted BION (Bionic Neuron) microstimulators** — single-channel injectable stimulators placed near the FCR/FCU motor points
- **Brain–Computer Interface (BCI) controlled FES** — where decoded motor cortex signals drive the wrist flexor stimulator (e.g., the **BrainGate2 + Freehand** pipeline)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Torque Produced by the Flexor Carpi Radialis (FCR) at a Given Wrist Angle

### Given Assumptions (typical textbook values, KTU-acceptable)

- PCSA of FCR: $A_{\text{FCR}} = 3.5 \,\text{cm}^2 = 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{m}^2$
- Specific tension of skeletal muscle: $\sigma = 30 \,\text{N/cm}^2 = 30 \times 10^{4} \,\text{N/m}^2 = 3 \times 10^{5} \,\text{N/m}^2$
- Optimal fascicle length: $\text{OptFL} = 8 \,\text{cm} = 0.08 \,\text{m}$
- Instantaneous fascicle length at neutral wrist: $\text{FL} = 0.08 \,\text{m}$
- Moment arm of FCR at neutral (0°): $d = 1.5 \,\text{cm} = 0.015 \,\text{m}$
- Wrist flexion angle: $\theta = 30°$ (functional range)

### Step 1 — Calculate the Maximum Isometric Muscle Force

The Hill-type model states that the maximum isometric force is proportional to the physiological cross-sectional area and the specific tension, scaled by the ratio of fascicle length to optimal fascicle length (the latter ratio is unity when the muscle is at its optimal length on the length-tension curve).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\max} &= \sigma \cdot A_{\text{FCR}} \cdot \frac{\text{FL}}{\text{OptFL}} \\
&= (3 \times 10^{5} \,\text{N/m}^2) \cdot (3.5 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{m}^2) \cdot \frac{0.08}{0.08} \\
&= 3 \times 10^{5} \times 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0 \\
&= 105 \,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The FCR, when fully recruited, can produce a maximum isometric force of **105 N**. This matches published cadaveric and MRI-based estimates (typically 90–120 N).

### Step 2 — Adjust the Moment Arm Using the Cosine Function

The moment arm of a wrist flexor varies with joint angle. A first-order model treats the tendon path as approximately straight over the wrist, so the perpendicular distance from the joint centre to the line of action changes as the sine of the angle between the tendon and the long axis of the bone. At neutral (0°), the moment arm is at its reported value of 1.5 cm. At 30° of flexion, the effective moment arm changes by the cosine factor (depending on the convention used). We use the simplified model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(\theta) &= d_0 \cdot \cos(\theta) \\
&= 0.015 \cdot \cos(30°) \\
&= 0.015 \cdot 0.8660 \\
&= 0.01299 \,\text{m} \\
&\approx 0.013 \,\text{m} \; (\text{or } 1.3 \,\text{cm})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** At 30° of flexion, the moment arm of the FCR decreases from 1.5 cm to approximately **1.3 cm**. This reflects the geometric reality that as the hand curls, the tendon wraps differently across the wrist.

### Step 3 — Calculate the Flexion Torque at 30°

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= F_m \cdot d(\theta) \cdot \sin(90°) \quad [\text{since } F_m \text{ is perpendicular to } d] \\
&= 105 \,\text{N} \cdot 0.013 \,\text{m} \cdot 1.0 \\
&= 1.365 \,\text{Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the sine factor is 1:** When the muscle force vector is perpendicular to the moment arm vector (i.e., the muscle is pulling tangentially around the joint centre), the sine of the angle between them is 1. In the simplified wrist model, this is the assumed configuration. More advanced models (e.g., tendon-excursion models) would use $\sin(\theta + \phi)$ for some offset $\phi$.

### Step 4 — Compare to the Required Torque to Hold a 1 kg Object in the Hand

The torque required to hold a 1 kg mass at the hand's centre of mass (approximately 12 cm distal to the wrist joint) at 30° of flexion is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{load}} &= m \cdot g \cdot L \cdot \sin(\theta) \\
&= 1.0 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.12 \cdot \sin(30°) \\
&= 9.81 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 0.5886 \,\text{Nm} \\
&\approx 0.59 \,\text{Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Safety / Reserve Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reserve} &= \frac{\tau_{\text{FCR}}}{\tau_{\text{load}}} \\
&= \frac{1.365}{0.5886} \\
&\approx 2.32
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** A fully activated FCR produces approximately **2.3× the torque** needed to statically hold a 1 kg object at 30° of wrist flexion. This is why a healthy individual can comfortably lift heavier objects. For a paralysed patient with only **20–40% of the FCR motor units intact** (e.g., a C6 tetraplegic), the available torque drops to ~0.27–0.55 Nm, which is **insufficient** to lift 1 kg — justifying the need for FES to recruit the *remaining* motor units electrically. **This is the core engineering justification for an FES wrist prosthesis.**

## 3.2 Derivation: FES Stimulation Charge Required to Produce a Target Torque

### Given
- Target flexion torque: $\tau_{\text{target}} = 0.5 \,\text{Nm}$
- Patient's FCR has been characterised and the recruitment curve shows that a charge per pulse of $Q_p = 0.5 \,\mu\text{C}$ produces $F_m = 50 \,\text{N}$ at 30° of flexion
- Pulse width (PW) used: $300 \,\mu\text{s}$
- Required force: $F_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{target}}}{d(\theta)} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.013} = 38.46 \,\text{N}$

### Step 1 — Compute Required Pulse Amplitude

The charge per pulse is the product of amplitude and pulse width:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_p &= I \cdot \text{PW} \\
\Rightarrow I &= \frac{Q_p}{\text{PW}} = \frac{0.5 \,\mu\text{C}}{300 \,\mu\text{s}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{300 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.67 \,\text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Scale Up Using the Recruitment Linearity Assumption

Since recruitment is approximately linear in the operating range, the required charge scales proportionally with required force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_{\text{req}}}{Q_p} &= \frac{F_{\text{req}}}{F_m} \\
Q_{\text{req}} &= Q_p \cdot \frac{F_{\text{req}}}{F_m} = 0.5 \,\mu\text{C} \cdot \frac{38.46}{50} = 0.5 \cdot 0.7692 = 0.385 \,\mu\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute New Pulse Amplitude (keeping PW = 300 µs)

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{req}} &= \frac{Q_{\text{req}}}{\text{PW}} = \frac{0.385 \,\mu\text{C}}{300 \,\mu\text{s}} = \frac{0.385 \times 10^{-6}}{300 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 0.001283 \,\text{A} = 1.283 \,\text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Select Stimulation Frequency (Tetany vs. Smoothness)

To produce **smooth, fused (tetanic) contraction**, the stimulation frequency must exceed the fusion frequency of the FCR (typically 20–30 Hz). For comfort and reduced fatigue, **20 Hz** is the lower bound; for strong fused contraction, **30–40 Hz** is standard.

$$
f_{\text{stim}} = 30 \,\text{Hz}
$$

### Step 5 — Total Charge Per Second (the "stimulation dose")

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(t) &= Q_{\text{req}} \cdot f_{\text{stim}} \\
&= 0.385 \,\mu\text{C} \cdot 30 \,\text{Hz} \\
&= 11.55 \,\mu\text{C/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 11.55 µC/s value is the "stimulation dose" that drives the FCR to produce 0.5 Nm of flexion torque. In a real-time closed-loop FES system, the controller would adjust $I$ (or PW) every 10–20 ms to maintain the target torque despite muscle fatigue, spasticity, and voluntary interference.

## 3.3 Closed-Loop FES Controller — Conceptual Derivation

The goal is to drive the wrist to a desired angle $\theta_d(t)$ using a controller. The error is:

$$
e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta(t)
$$

A simple proportional-integral (PI) controller outputs a stimulation intensity $u(t)$:

$$
u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

The muscle then produces torque $\tau_m = G \cdot u(t - t_d)$ (with $G$ as the muscle gain and $t_d$ as the delay), which drives the joint dynamics:

$$
I \ddot{\theta} + B \dot{\theta} + K \theta = \tau_m - \tau_{\text{ext}}
$$

**Tuning the gains:** Empirically, for wrist flexion, $K_p$ in the range of **5–20 mA/rad** and $K_i$ in the range of **0.5–5 mA/(rad·s)** yield stable tracking in most C5–C6 tetraplegic patients (per published Freehand System tuning data).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Closed-Loop FES System for Wrist Flexion

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the signal flow and component interactions of a complete FES system designed to restore wrist flexion in a C5–C6 tetraplegic patient. The diagram follows the standard **KTU neural prosthesis block-architecture** pattern (sensors → controller → stimulator → muscle → joint → feedback).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Command Source: Residual EMG or BCI or Push-button] --> B1[Signal Conditioning: Amplify, Filter 20-450 Hz, Rectify, Smooth]
    B1 --> C1[Feature Extractor: MAV or RMS over 200 ms window]
    C1 --> D1[Controller: PI or Fuzzy or Neural-Network]
    D1 --> E1[Stimulator Output: Adjustable Amplitude 0-100 mA, PW 50-500 us, Freq 20-50 Hz]
    E1 --> F1[Electrode Interface: Surface Pads or Implanted Cuff or Intramuscular]
    F1 --> G1[Target Muscle: Flexor Carpi Radialis FCU FDS]
    G1 --> H1[Wrist Joint Mechanics: Torque, Angle, Velocity]
    H1 --> I1[Patient Hand: Functional Task e.g., Grasping Cup]
    I1 --> J1[External Load: Object weight, gravity, spasticity]
    J1 --> H1
    H1 --> K1[Angle Sensor: Goniometer or IMU or Encoder]
    K1 --> L1[Feedback Signal to Controller: theta, omega]
    L1 --> D1
    H1 --> M1[EMG Sensor: Voluntary residual activity]
    M1 --> B1
```

### Diagram Walk-Through (KTU Examiner's Reading Key)

| Block | KTU Function | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **A1** | Command intent | Source of "I want to flex" signal |
| **B1** | Signal conditioning | Removes 50/60 Hz line noise, motion artefact |
| **C1** | Feature extraction | Converts raw EMG into a single control value (e.g., MAV) |
| **D1** | Controller brain | Computes the stimulation intensity needed |
| **E1** | Stimulator hardware | The actual current source producing biphasic pulses |
| **F1** | Electrode interface | The point of contact with the body |
| **G1** | Target muscle | The biological actuator |
| **H1** | Joint mechanics | The biomechanical system (mass, damping, stiffness) |
| **I1** | Functional output | The actual movement/task |
| **J1** | External load | Disturbance (object weight, spasticity) |
| **K1** | Angle sensor | Provides feedback |
| **L1** | Feedback loop | Closes the loop to the controller |
| **M1** | Residual EMG | Re-injects volitional intent for hybrid control |

## 4.2 Neural Pathway Diagram — From Brain to Wrist Flexor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Primary Motor Cortex M1 Hand Area] --> N2[Corona Radiata]
    N2 --> N3[Internal Capsule Posterior Limb]
    N3 --> N4[Cerebral Peduncle]
    N4 --> N5[Pons]
    N5 --> N6[Medullary Pyramid]
    N6 --> N7[Decussation 85 percent cross]
    N7 --> N8[Lateral Corticospinal Tract]
    N8 --> N9[Spinal Cord Segments C7 C8 T1]
    N9 --> N10[Brachial Plexus Lateral and Medial Cords]
    N10 --> N11[Median Nerve]
    N10 --> N12[Ulnar Nerve]
    N11 --> N13[Neuromuscular Junction FCR PL FDS]
    N12 --> N14[Neuromuscular Junction FCU FDP to digits 4-5]
    N13 --> N15[Muscle Fibre Depolarization and Contraction]
    N14 --> N15
    N15 --> N16[Wrist Flexion Movement]
```

### Reading Key for the Neural Pathway

- **N1 → N6**: Upper motor neuron (UMN) pathway. Lesion here = stroke, traumatic brain injury.
- **N6 (Decussation)**: Why the left brain controls the right hand. A *left-MCA-stroke* causes *right-hand* weakness.
- **N7 → N9**: Lateral corticospinal tract. Damage below the decussation produces *ipsilateral* (same-side) paralysis.
- **N9 (C7–T1)**: The **crucial segment for wrist/hand control**. A C5–C6 SCI spares some of C7–T1, leaving residual innervation that FES exploits.
- **N11 (Median)** vs. **N12 (Ulnar)**: Median innervates FCR, PL, FDS, and the lateral half of FDP. Ulnar innervates FCU and the medial half of FDP. **In an FES system, stimulating both nerves (via multi-contact cuff electrodes) is the most complete way to recruit all wrist flexors.**

## 4.3 Joint Anatomy Cross-Section Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Distal Radius Triangular Fibrocartilage Complex] --> S2[Radiocarpal Joint Capsule]
    S2 --> S3[Proximal Carpal Row: Scaphoid, Lunate, Triquetrum]
    S3 --> S4[Midcarpal Joint]
    S4 --> S5[Distal Carpal Row: Trapezium, Trapezoid, Capitate, Hamate]
    S5 --> S6[CM joint: Bases of Metacarpals 2-5]
    S1 --> S7[Distal Radioulnar Joint]
    S7 --> S8[Ulna Head]
    S3 --> S9[Axis of Flexion-Extension through Capitate]
    S2 --> S10[FCR Tendon Sheath Volar]
    S2 --> S11[FCU Tendon Sheath Ulnar]
    S2 --> S12[PL Tendon Volar Midline]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Note:** This is an *adapted* schematic, as a true anatomical cross-section would require photographic or detailed line-art rendering. The block diagram conveys the joint hierarchy and the location of the major flexor tendons relative to the axis of rotation. The **axis of flexion-extension passes through the capitate head**, which is the anatomical reference point for goniometric measurement.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define wrist flexion. State the normal active range of motion and identify the primary muscles and nerves responsible.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Wrist flexion is the rotational movement of the hand in the sagittal plane about the transverse axis of the wrist, decreasing the angle between the palmar surface of the hand and the volar surface of the forearm.
>
> **Range of motion (1 mark):** The normal active range of motion is **0° to 80°**, with functional ADL range being **0° to 40°**.
>
> **Primary muscles and nerves (1 mark):** The primary muscles are the **Flexor Carpi Radialis (FCR)** and **Flexor Carpi Ulnaris (FCU)**, with secondary contributions from **Palmaris Longus (PL)**, **Flexor Digitorum Superficialis (FDS)**, and **Flexor Digitorum Profundus (FDP)**. These are innervated primarily by the **median nerve** (FCR, PL, FDS, lateral FDP) and the **ulnar nerve** (FCU, medial FDP).

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the "tenodesis effect" and how is it exploited in FES systems designed to restore grasp in C6 tetraplegic patients?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The tenodesis effect is the **passive mechanical coupling** between wrist position and finger position — when the wrist is **extended** by ~30°, the fingers and thumb **passively flex** and the hand closes; conversely, wrist **flexion** causes passive finger opening.
>
> **Mechanism (1 mark):** This occurs because the finger flexor tendons cross the wrist joint; when the wrist extends, the tendons are stretched and pull the fingers into flexion, even without active finger-muscle contraction.
>
> **FES exploitation (1 mark):** In C6 tetraplegic patients who retain **active wrist extension (via C6-innervated ECRL/ECR)** but lack finger flexion, an FES system can stimulate the **finger flexors (FDS, FDP)** only when the patient voluntarily extends the wrist. The wrist angle sensor (or residual EMG from ECR) triggers the stimulation, producing a **coordinated grasp** without requiring intact finger control.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has an **internal choice** — answer EITHER Option A OR Option B. Each option has two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question B — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`

#### (a) Describe in detail the biomechanics of wrist flexion. Include the joint involved, axis of rotation, primary and secondary muscles, their nerve supply, and the normal range of motion. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Joint and Axis of Rotation (1.5 marks):**
Wrist flexion occurs predominantly at the **radiocarpal joint**, with secondary contribution from the **midcarpal joint**. The axis of rotation for flexion-extension passes through the **head of the capitate bone** in the coronal plane. This joint is a **biaxial ellipsoidal joint** providing two degrees of freedom: flexion-extension (sagittal plane) and radial-ulnar deviation (frontal plane).

**2. Normal Range of Motion (1 mark):**
The normal **active** range is **0° to 80°** (with some sources giving 0° to 60° or 0° to 90°). The **passive** range extends to ~90°. The **functional** range for ADLs is **0° to 40°**.

**3. Primary Muscles (2.5 marks):**
The **primary** wrist flexors are the **Flexor Carpi Radialis (FCR)** and **Flexor Carpi Ulnaris (FCU)**, both of which cross the wrist but not the fingers. The **secondary** flexors — Palmaris Longus (PL), Flexor Digitorum Superficialis (FDS), and Flexor Digitorum Profundus (FDP) — cross both the wrist and finger joints, so they contribute to wrist flexion but also flex the fingers.

| Muscle | Nerve | PCSA (approx.) | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| FCR | Median | 3.5 cm² | Primary |
| FCU | Ulnar | 3.2 cm² | Primary |
| PL | Median | 1.0 cm² | Secondary (weak) |
| FDS | Median | 4.5 cm² | Secondary |
| FDP | Median + Ulnar | 5.0 cm² | Secondary |

**4. Nerve Supply (1 mark):**
The **median nerve** (root values C6–C7, lateral and medial cords of brachial plexus) supplies FCR, PL, FDS, and the lateral half of FDP. The **ulnar nerve** (root values C7–C8, medial cord) supplies FCU and the medial half of FDP.

**5. Functional Significance (1 mark):**
Wrist flexion is essential for ADLs such as feeding, grooming, and writing. In tetraplegia, the loss of wrist flexion eliminates the tenodesis-driven grasp, and FES targeting FCR and FCU is a key rehabilitation strategy.

---

#### (b) With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a closed-loop FES system for restoring wrist flexion in a C5–C6 tetraplegic patient. Describe each block and the role of feedback. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. System Justification (1 mark):**
A C5–C6 spinal cord injury spares the **C5–C6 roots**, allowing the patient to retain shoulder abduction, flexion, and elbow flexion. However, the **C7–T1 roots** (which innervate the wrist flexors) are partially or fully damaged, leaving the patient unable to flex the wrist volitionally. A **closed-loop FES system** electrically stimulates the residual wrist flexors (FCR, FCU) to restore controlled flexion.

**2. Block Diagram (3 marks):**
The system consists of the following blocks (presented as a flow):

**Command Source** → **Signal Conditioner** → **Feature Extractor** → **Controller** → **Stimulator** → **Electrode** → **Muscle** → **Wrist Joint** → **Functional Task**

with a **feedback loop** from a **goniometer (angle sensor)** and **EMG sensor** returning to the controller.

- **Command Source:** Residual volitional EMG from partially spared muscles (e.g., PL, FCR itself, or a more proximal muscle), or a BCI signal, or a manual push-button for the most severe cases.
- **Signal Conditioner:** Instrumentation amplifier with gain ~1000, band-pass filter 20–450 Hz, and 50/60 Hz notch filter to remove line noise.
- **Feature Extractor:** Computes the **Mean Absolute Value (MAV)** or **Root Mean Square (RMS)** of the EMG over a sliding 100–300 ms window to produce a single control value.
- **Controller:** A **PI controller** (or fuzzy / neural-network controller) that compares the desired angle (from the command) with the actual angle (from the goniometer) and computes a stimulation intensity.
- **Stimulator:** A **constant-current biphasic stimulator** delivering pulses of 0–100 mA, pulse width 50–500 µs, and frequency 20–50 Hz.
- **Electrode:** Either **surface pads** (non-invasive) or **implanted epimysial / cuff electrodes** (invasive, more selective).
- **Muscle and Joint:** The biological actuators.
- **Feedback:** A **bipolar goniometer** placed across the wrist measures the actual joint angle $\theta(t)$. An **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** can also be used. Feedback is critical because muscle response to the same stimulation varies with **fatigue**, **spasticity**, **electrode impedance changes** (due to sweat, movement), and **postural changes**.

**3. Why Closed-Loop (1.5 marks):**
- **Open-loop** FES produces unpredictable, drifting movements because muscle properties change.
- **Closed-loop** FES uses feedback to **automatically adjust** the stimulation intensity to maintain the desired joint angle despite disturbances.
- The controller must be tuned to be **robust** to time-varying muscle dynamics and **safe** under all conditions (e.g., no excessive stimulation that could cause burns or fractures in osteoporotic limbs).

**4. Real-World Example (1.5 marks):**
The **Freehand System** (NeuroControl Corp., FDA-approved 1997, discontinued 2001) used an **8-channel implanted stimulator** with epimysial electrodes on the finger and thumb flexors/extensors and the wrist extensors. A **shoulder-position sensor** (joystick mounted on the sternum) served as the command source, and **EMG from the wrist extensor** (ECRL) was used to trigger hand opening. Modern successors include the **NessH200 (Bioness)** and the **STIMuGRIP (FESIA)**.

---

### **Question B — Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]`

#### (a) Explain the design considerations for stimulating the wrist flexors via surface versus implanted electrodes. Compare advantages and disadvantages, and list the typical stimulation parameters used. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Surface Electrode Design (3 marks):**

**Working Principle:** Self-adhesive gel electrodes (typically Ag/AgCl) placed on the skin over the motor point of the target muscle (FCR, FCU).

**Advantages:**
- Non-invasive, no surgery required
- Easy to apply and remove
- Low cost, suitable for home use
- Re-positionable if ineffective

**Disadvantages:**
- **Selective recruitment is poor** — surface electrodes activate all muscles beneath them, including unwanted finger flexors
- **Daily positioning is non-reproducible** — even a 1 cm shift can dramatically change the recruitment
- **Stimulus artefact** contaminates EMG recordings (a major issue for EMG-triggered FES)
- Skin irritation and discomfort over long sessions
- Cannot reach deep muscles (e.g., FDP)

**2. Implanted Electrode Design (3 marks):**

**Working Principle:** Electrodes (epimysial, intramuscular, or nerve cuff) are surgically placed on or near the target muscle or its motor nerve.

**Advantages:**
- **Highly selective** — epimysial electrodes on FCR recruit FCR with minimal FCU co-activation
- **Reproducible stimulation day-to-day** — fixed position
- **No stimulus artefact** in surface EMG recordings
- **Lower current** required (1–10 mA vs. 20–80 mA for surface), reducing fatigue
- Cosmetic — fully implanted, no external hardware other than a small coil

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires **surgical implantation** (risk of infection, nerve damage)
- **Lead fatigue and breakage** — the wires between the stimulator and the electrode can fracture with repeated flexing
- **Battery replacement** requires surgery every 5–10 years
- High cost
- Not suitable for patients with significant spasticity (electrode may dislodge)

**3. Typical Stimulation Parameters (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Typical Value | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Pulse waveform | Biphasic, charge-balanced | Prevents DC accumulation, tissue damage |
| Pulse amplitude | 5–100 mA (surface) or 0.5–10 mA (implanted) | Above threshold, below pain |
| Pulse width | 50–500 µs | Modulates recruitment depth |
| Frequency | 20–50 Hz (commonly 25–35 Hz) | Above fusion frequency, below fatigue threshold |

---

#### (b) Calculate the maximum isometric flexion torque produced by the FCR at 30° of wrist flexion, given the following data: PCSA = 3.5 cm², specific tension σ = 30 N/cm², moment arm at neutral = 1.5 cm, and assume the moment arm varies with the cosine of the joint angle. State one engineering conclusion. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute maximum muscle force (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\max} &= \sigma \cdot \text{PCSA} \cdot \frac{\text{FL}}{\text{OptFL}} \\
&= 30 \,\text{N/cm}^2 \cdot 3.5 \,\text{cm}^2 \cdot 1.0 \\
&= 105 \,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the effective moment arm at θ = 30° (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(30°) &= d_0 \cdot \cos(30°) \\
&= 0.015 \,\text{m} \cdot 0.866 \\
&= 0.01299 \,\text{m} \approx 0.013 \,\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the flexion torque (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{FCR}} &= F_{\max} \cdot d(30°) \\
&= 105 \,\text{N} \cdot 0.013 \,\text{m} \\
&= 1.365 \,\text{Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering conclusion (1 mark):**
The fully activated FCR produces a maximum flexion torque of **~1.37 Nm** at 30° of wrist flexion. This is sufficient to lift a 1 kg object held in the hand at the same angle (which requires only ~0.59 Nm). For a patient with 30% residual innervation (typical of C6 tetraplegia), the available torque drops to ~0.41 Nm, which is **borderline insufficient**, justifying the need for **FES to augment** the remaining volitional control. **Practical FES systems therefore target not only FCR but also co-recruit FCU, FDS, and (in grasp-mode) FDP to generate the 2–3 Nm of total flexion torque** typically required for functional tasks such as holding a 1.5 L water bottle.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Wrist-Flexion Questions**
> 1. **Confusing wrist flexion with finger flexion** — FCR and FCU flex the *wrist*, not the fingers. FDS and FDP flex the *fingers* and *assist* wrist flexion. Examiners mark **zero** for the wrong muscle-action mapping.
> 2. **Using `|` (pipe) inside a markdown table** — this breaks the table syntax and the answer renders incorrectly. Always use the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` command.
> 3. **Forgetting the cosine factor in moment-arm calculation** — many students write $d$ as constant and lose 1–2 marks in derivations.
> 4. **Confusing radial nerve with median/ulnar nerve** — the **radial nerve** innervates *extensors* (ECRL, ECRB, ECU), not flexors. Examiners frequently include this as a distractor.
> 5. **Stating ROM as 0–90°** without specifying *active vs. passive* — the active range is 0–80°; passive is 0–90°. Vague answers lose the "specification" mark.
> 6. **Ignoring the stimulus parameters in FES questions** — even if the question is theoretical, you must state amplitude, pulse width, and frequency. FES parameters are **always** part of a complete answer.
> 7. **Omitting the closed-loop justification** — in FES controller questions, always explain *why* closed-loop is necessary (muscle fatigue, spasticity, time-varying dynamics), not just *what* the components are.
> 8. **Using non-SI units** (e.g., kgf instead of N, cm² without converting to m²) — examiners deduct for unit errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Read this 10 minutes before the exam**

- **Definition:** Wrist flexion = palmar-ward rotation in sagittal plane, around the capitate-head axis, ROM 0–80° (active), 0–90° (passive), 0–40° (functional).
- **Joint:** Radiocarpal (primary) + midcarpal (secondary); biaxial ellipsoidal.
- **Primary Muscles (Pure Wrist Flexors):** FCR, FCU.
- **Secondary Muscles (Wrist + Finger):** PL, FDS, FDP.
- **Nerves:** Median (FCR, PL, FDS, lateral FDP) + Ulnar (FCU, medial FDP).
- **Spinal Levels:** C7, C8, T1.
- **Tenodesis Effect:** Wrist *extension* → passive finger flexion; Wrist *flexion* → passive finger extension. **C6 tetraplegics exploit this with FES.**
- **C5–C6 SCI:** Wrist flexors paralysed → FES targets FCR + FCU for grasp restoration.
- **C7 SCI:** Wrist extensors preserved → active tenodesis is feasible.
- **FES Parameters:** Biphasic, charge-balanced; 5–100 mA (surface) / 0.5–10 mA (implanted); PW 50–500 µs; Freq 20–50 Hz (commonly 25–35 Hz).
- **NMJ Delay:** ~25–50 ms — must be accounted for in closed-loop controller design.
- **Fusion Frequency:** ~20–30 Hz — operation below this produces unfused, "twitchy" contraction.
- **Muscle Force Formula:** $F_m = \sigma \cdot \text{PCSA} \cdot (\text{FL} / \text{OptFL})$; with $\sigma \approx 30 \,\text{N/cm}^2$.
- **Torque Formula:** $\tau = F_m \cdot d \cdot \sin(\theta)$ or simplified to $F_m \cdot d \cdot \cos(\theta)$ for moment-arm variation.
- **EMG Features:** MAV (mean absolute value) and RMS (root mean square) are the two most common feature extractors for EMG-triggered FES.
- **Surface vs. Implanted Electrodes:** Surface = non-invasive but non-selective; Implanted = selective but surgical.
- **Freehand System:** Historical 8-channel implanted FES for grasp; precursor to Bioness H200 and Fesia STIMuGRIP.
- **BION (Bionic Neuron):** Single-channel injectable microstimulator, injectable via a 12-gauge needle — future direction for wrist flexor stimulation.
- **Closed-Loop Need:** Muscle fatigue, spasticity, electrode impedance drift, postural changes → require feedback control (PI, fuzzy, or neural-network based).
- **PCSA Values (approx.):** FCR 3.5, FCU 3.2, PL 1.0, FDS 4.5, FDP 5.0 — in cm².
- **Engineering Reserve Factor:** A healthy FCR produces ~2.3× the torque needed to hold 1 kg at 30° flexion → justifies FES for weaker patients.
- **Corticospinal Decussation:** 85% of fibres cross at the medullary pyramids → left-brain lesion causes right-hand weakness.
- **Equation of Motion (Wrist Pendulum Model):** $I \ddot{\theta} + B \dot{\theta} + K\theta = \tau_m - \tau_e$, with $I \approx 0.05 \,\text{kg·m}^2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: ELBOW COMPONENTS IN NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Elbow Components** in the context of neural prosthesis and implants refer to the integrated electromechanical, biomechanical, and neuroelectronic subsystems that work in concert to either (a) **restore functional elbow movement in paralyzed limbs** via Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES), or (b) **replace a missing limb** through a prosthetic elbow unit. The component set typically comprises the stimulation/actuation hardware, the electrode–nerve interface, the sensor feedback chain, the mechanical coupling, and the embedded controller.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Re-wired Robotic Arm" Intuition

Imagine the human elbow as a sophisticated **door hinge with a built-in motor, brake, and speed sensor**. In a healthy person, the brain sends electrical signals down nerves to muscles (biceps/triceps) that pull the forearm up or push it down. In neural prosthetics, two scenarios arise:

- **Scenario A (Paralysis – FES):** The "wiring from the brain" is broken. We **bypass the damaged nerve** and deliver controlled electrical pulses directly to the muscles (or to the nerve) using implanted electrodes. The muscle itself is still healthy — we are essentially *re-wiring the lamp* to a different switch.
- **Scenario B (Amputation – Prosthetic):** The entire "door hinge assembly" is missing. We install a **mechanical robotic hinge** (the prosthetic elbow) controlled by the user's intent — captured via surface EMG signals from residual muscles.

In both cases, the **elbow components** are the toolbox: electrodes, leads, pulse generator, controller, sensors, mechanical frame, and coupling socket.

### 1.2 Anatomical & Functional Substrate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The elbow is a **compound synovial hinge joint** comprising three articulations: humero-ulnar, humero-radial, and proximal radio-ulnar. It permits **flexion–extension (0°–150°)** and **pronation–supination (≈ ±80°)**. This dual function dictates that elbow components must address **two degrees of freedom (DOF)**, often in a coupled manner.

| Motion | Primary Muscle | Antagonist Muscle | Nerve Supply |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Flexion | Biceps Brachii, Brachialis | Triceps Brachii | Musculocutaneous |
| Extension | Triceps Brachii | Biceps Brachii | Radial |
| Pronation | Pronator Teres/Quadratus | Supinator | Median / Radial |
| Supination | Supinator, Biceps | Pronator Teres | Radial / Musculocutaneous |

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Normal elbow flexion range:** **0° to ~145°–150°**
- **Functional flexion arc for daily tasks (eating, grooming):** **30° to 130°**
- **Biceps moment arm at 90° flexion:** **≈ 4–5 cm**
- **Maximum voluntary elbow flexion torque in adult male:** **≈ 60–80 Nm**
- **Typical FES pulse parameters:** amplitude **20–150 mA**, pulse width **100–400 µs**, frequency **20–50 Hz**
- **Body-powered prosthetic elbow cable excursion:** **~5–7 cm** for full flexion

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a question asks to "list the elbow components" or "explain the architecture of an FES elbow system," always structure the answer in **five functional blocks**: (1) Command Source, (2) Controller, (3) Stimulator/Actuator, (4) Electrode–Tissue Interface, (5) Sensor Feedback. This is the standard KTU valuation key format.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Decomposition of Elbow Components

The elbow component set in any neural prosthesis/orthosis can be deconstructed into **five interdependent sub-systems**. The rigorous understanding of *why* each exists is critical for KTU Part B (14-mark) answers.

### 2.1.1 Command Source (Intent Detection Block)
- **Purpose:** Decode *user intention* — does the patient want to flex, extend, lock, or release the elbow?
- **Methods:**
  - **Surface EMG (sEMG)** from biceps/triceps of residual limb
  - **Implanted myoelectric sensors (IMES)**
  - **Cortical / peripheral nerve recordings (ENG)** via cuff or intrafascicular electrodes
  - **Shoulder-motion-encoded switches (body-powered harness)**
  - **Voice, inertial (IMU), or sip-and-puff controllers** for high-level amputees

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EMG dominates KTU questions:** It is non-invasive, robust, and forms the basis of nearly all commercial myoelectric elbows. The signal amplitude is typically **20 µV to 5 mV** in the 20–500 Hz band.

### 2.1.2 Controller (Signal Processing & Decision Block)
- **Function:** Maps the command signal → stimulus parameters (amplitude/pulse-width/frequency) or actuator commands.
- **Algorithm classes:**
  - **Threshold-based on/off controllers** (simple, 2-state)
  - **Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID)** for smooth tracking
  - **Finite State Machines (FSM)** for multi-mode orthoses
  - **Neural-network / fuzzy-logic** for nonlinear muscle recruitment
- **Modulation strategies:**
  - **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM):** pulse width ∝ desired torque
  - **Amplitude Modulation (AM):** current amplitude ∝ desired torque
  - **Frequency Modulation (FM):** 10–50 Hz for fused tetanic contraction

### 2.1.3 Stimulator / Actuator (Power Output Block)
- **For FES:** an implantable or surface **pulse generator** (current- or voltage-controlled) delivering biphasic charge-balanced pulses to avoid electrochemical damage.
- **For Prosthetic:** **DC motor + harmonic-drive gear**, **pneumatic (CO₂) actuator**, or **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** for compliance.
- **KTU Hot-Question:** Compare the **energy density of FES muscle** vs. an **electric motor** — the answer is that biological muscle ≈ **0.04–0.1 kW/kg** whereas a brushed DC motor ≈ **0.15–0.3 kW/kg**, but FES is *fatigue-limited*.

### 2.1.4 Electrode–Tissue Interface (Signal Delivery Block)
- **Surface electrodes** (hydrogel, Ag/AgCl) — non-invasive, high impedance, low selectivity.
- **Percutaneous (fine-wire) electrodes** — temporary, better selectivity.
- **Epimysial electrodes** — sutured onto muscle epimysium.
- **Intramuscular (IM) electrodes** — fine wires within muscle belly.
- **Cuff electrodes** — wrapped around peripheral nerve.
- **Utah / Michigan (intrafascicular) arrays** — high-resolution neural recording/stimulation.

### 2.1.5 Sensor Feedback (Closed-Loop Block)
- **Position sensors:** goniometer, Hall-effect, optical encoder, IMU (MEMS accelerometer + gyroscope).
- **Force/torque sensors:** strain gauges, FSR (Force-Sensitive Resistor).
- **Slip & grasp sensors** for hand coupling.
- **EMG feedback (residual or stimulated)** for fatigue estimation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relationship | Description | Typical Units |
|---|------------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $T = F \times r$ | Torque = Force × moment arm | Nm |
| 2 | $E_{pulse} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{pw}$ | Energy per stimulation pulse | Joules |
| 3 | $Q = I \cdot t_{pw}$ | Per-pulse charge (must be < 1 µC for safe stimulation) | Coulombs |
| 4 | $f_{tet} \ge \frac{1}{T_{twitch}}$ | Minimum tetanic fusion frequency | Hz |
| 5 | $F_{muscle} = F_{max} \cdot \sigma(V)$ | Hill-type muscle force model with activation $\sigma(V)$ | N |
| 6 | $\theta_{elbow} = \int_{0}^{t} \omega(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Elbow angle from angular velocity | rad |
| 7 | $K_{t} = \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta I}$ | Motor torque constant | Nm/A |
| 8 | $\tau_{mech} = J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega$ | Mechanical time constant of geared actuator | — |
| 9 | $Z_{electrode} = \frac{V}{I}\bigg\vert_{f}$ | Electrode–tissue impedance at frequency f | Ω |
| 10 | $SNR_{EMG} = 20 \log_{10}\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}$ | Myoelectric signal quality | dB |
| 11 | $V_{RMS,EMG} \propto T_{muscle}$ | Surface EMG RMS amplitude correlates with muscle torque | — |
| 12 | $\eta_{gear} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Gearbox efficiency (harmonic drive: 0.7–0.85) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $E_{cumulative} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} V_k I_k t_{pw,k}$ | Total charge delivered (monitor for ≤ 1 µC/cm²) | Coulombs |
| 14 | $P_{muscle} = \frac{E_{metabolic}}{t} \approx \frac{V O_2}{t}$ | Metabolic power from gas exchange (fatigue index) | Watts |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU pitfall:** When writing $\vert V/I \vert$ for impedance, **always** use `\vert` inside LaTeX blocks; raw `|` will break markdown tables. The above table already complies.

## 2.3 Why This System Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Clinical impact:** Restores 30°–130° functional reach arc, enabling self-feeding (ADL — Activities of Daily Living).
- **Industrial parallel:** FES elbow systems borrow heavily from **robot manipulator control** (impedance/admittance, hybrid force-position).
- **Energy-efficiency argument:** Body-powered (cable-driven) elbows use **~5 J per cycle** (metabolically), while myoelectric elbows consume **~10–20 J per cycle** from the battery — a key KTU comparative question.
- **Regulatory link:** All implant components must comply with **ISO 14708** (active implantable medical devices) and electrodes with **ISO 10993** (biocompatibility).
- **Why closed-loop?** Open-loop FES fatigues muscle within 2–5 minutes; **closed-loop position/force feedback** extends functional use to **>30 minutes**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hill-Type Muscle Model for FES Elbow Torque

> The Hill-type muscle model is the *canonical biomechanical equation* for FES elbow analysis and is a high-yield derivation in KTU ESE.

We start with the standard **Hill three-element model**: a contractile element (CE) in parallel with a passive elastic element (PE), both in series with a series elastic element (SE).

**Step 1 — Define the contractile element force:**
$$F_{CE} = F_{max} \cdot \sigma(V) \cdot f_{FV}(v) \cdot f_{FL}(L)$$

Here, $\sigma(V)$ is the activation level driven by stimulation $V$ (or $I$), $f_{FV}(v)$ is the force–velocity relationship, and $f_{FL}(L)$ is the force–length relationship.

**Step 2 — Activation dynamics (first-order):**
$$\tau_{act} \frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \sigma(t) = \sigma_{target}(V(t))$$

This means the muscle cannot instantly reach its target — it takes time constant $\tau_{act}$ (≈ 10–40 ms) for activation, which determines the **FES response lag**.

**Step 3 — Force–velocity (Hill hyperbolic):**
$$f_{FV}(v) = \frac{F_{max} - a \cdot v}{F_{max} + b \cdot v}$$

where $a$ and $b$ are Hill constants with $a/b$ ratio ≈ 0.25 for elbow flexors.

**Step 4 — Project force into elbow torque:**
Using moment arm $r(\theta)$:
$$T_{elbow} = F_{CE} \cdot r(\theta)$$

**Step 5 — Total elbow torque (CE + PE):**
$$T_{elbow} = F_{CE} \cdot r(\theta) + F_{PE}(L) \cdot r(\theta) + T_{load}$$

**Step 6 — Combine with limb inertia $J$ and damping $B$:**
$$J \ddot{\theta} + B \dot{\theta} + F_{PE}(\theta) = F_{CE} \cdot r(\theta) - T_{load}(\theta)$$

This is the **dynamic equation of an FES-driven elbow** and forms the plant model for any PID/Fuzzy controller.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) Recruitment Law

> Pulse-width modulation is the most common clinical technique to grade muscle force in FES.

**Step 1 — Define the strength–duration (SD) curve:**
The threshold charge $Q_{th}$ to recruit a motor unit:
$$Q_{th} = I_{rh} \cdot t_{pw,th} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\tau_{m}}{\tau_{pw}}\right)$$

where $\tau_m$ is the membrane time constant (~100–200 µs for peripheral nerve).

**Step 2 — Chronaxie $t_c$ and rheobase $I_{rh}$:**
At pulse width equal to chronaxie, the threshold current equals **2 × rheobase**. Chronaxie is a key KTU viva question.

**Step 3 — Recruitment curve as a function of $t_{pw}$:**
$$\text{Recruitment}(\%) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-k(t_{pw} - t_{50})}}$$

where $t_{50}$ is the pulse width at 50% recruitment and $k$ is the slope.

**Step 4 — Equivalent torque expression:**
$$T_{elbow}(t) = T_{max} \cdot \text{Recruitment}(t_{pw}(t)) \cdot \sigma(t)$$

**Step 5 — Control law (proportional):**
$$t_{pw}(t) = t_{pw,min} + K_p \cdot (\theta_{desired}(t) - \theta_{actual}(t))$$

**Step 6 — Charge-balance check:**
$$Q_{phase} = I \cdot t_{pw} \le 1 \, \mu C$$
A symmetric biphasic pulse ensures $Q_{anode} = Q_{cathode}$ to prevent DC accumulation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Motor Selection for a Prosthetic Elbow Actuator

> This is a classic KTU design problem: "Design a motor to lift a 2 kg forearm through 90° in 0.8 s."

**Step 1 — Required torque:**
$$T = m g r = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 = 4.905 \text{ Nm}$$

Add a safety factor of 2 (for inertia, friction, and shock loading):
$$T_{design} = 9.81 \text{ Nm}$$

**Step 2 — Required angular velocity:**
$$\omega = \frac{\pi/2}{0.8} = 1.963 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Power required at output shaft:**
$$P = T \cdot \omega = 9.81 \times 1.963 = 19.25 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Account for gearbox efficiency $\eta = 0.80$:**
$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{19.25}{0.80} = 24.06 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Motor torque constant $K_t$ (typical DC: 0.02–0.05 Nm/A):**
$$I = \frac{T_{motor}}{K_t} = \frac{9.81/100}{0.03} = 3.27 \text{ A}$$

(where motor torque is reduced by gear ratio of 100:1).

**Step 6 — Battery sizing (Li-ion, 7.4 V nominal):**
$$I_{draw} = \frac{P}{V \eta_{driver}} = \frac{24}{7.4 \times 0.85} \approx 3.8 \text{ A}$$
A 1500 mAh pack allows ≈ 0.4 h continuous elbow use — typical KTU numerical answer.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Table

| Parameter | Body-Powered | Myoelectric (Motor) | FES (Muscle) | Pneumatic |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|--------------|-----------|
| Energy source | User body | Battery | User metabolism | CO₂ cartridge |
| Weight (forearm) | 250–400 g | 400–700 g | 50–100 g (stim) | 500–900 g |
| Cosmesis | Poor | Good | Excellent | Bulky |
| Grip force | 100+ N | 16–100 N | 20–40 N | 100+ N |
| Latency | 50–100 ms | 100–300 ms | 200–500 ms | 50–200 ms |
| Endurance | Unlimited | 8–24 h | 5–30 min | 1–2 days |
| Cost | Low | High | Very High | Medium |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. List the five functional blocks of a functional electrical stimulation (FES) elbow system and state the role of the controller block.**
*(CO4, K2 — Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
The five blocks are (i) Command Source (EMG/ENG), (ii) Controller (modulation logic), (iii) Pulse Stimulator (current source), (iv) Electrode–Tissue Interface, and (v) Sensor Feedback. The **controller** decodes the user's intent and converts it into proper pulse parameters (amplitude, pulse width, frequency) so that the muscle produces the required torque. It uses algorithms like thresholding, PID, or FSM to ensure smooth and stable elbow motion. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between epimysial and intrafascicular electrodes used in elbow FES systems.**
*(CO4, K3 — Apply)*

**Model Answer:**
**Epimysial** electrodes are sutured onto the outer surface (epimysium) of the muscle — they are stable, easy to implant, and have moderate selectivity. **Intrafascicular** electrodes (e.g., Utah/Michigan arrays) are inserted *within* the nerve fascicle, offering very high selectivity and access to single motor units but with higher surgical risk and possible nerve damage. Epimysial is preferred for gross elbow flexion/extension; intrafascicular is used for fine graded control. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a myoelectric prosthetic elbow**. Explain the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A myoelectric prosthetic elbow consists of **five functional blocks**:

1. **EMG Acquisition Block** — surface Ag/AgCl electrodes detect residual biceps/triceps EMG (20 µV–5 mV, 20–500 Hz).
2. **Signal Conditioning Block** — instrumentation amplifier (gain ≈ 1000), 50 Hz notch filter, band-pass 20–500 Hz, full-wave rectifier.
3. **Feature Extraction & Classification Block** — computes RMS, MAV, waveform length; classifies into "flex / extend / lock / unlock" using LDA or ANN.
4. **Motor Drive Block** — H-bridge PWM driver controlling a brushed DC motor coupled via **harmonic-drive** gearbox (ratio 100:1, efficiency ≈ 0.8).
5. **Feedback Block** — Hall-effect position sensor and strain-gauge torque sensor feeding back to the controller for closed-loop position/force regulation.

The **power source** (Li-ion 7.4 V, 1500 mAh) supplies all blocks, with separate regulation for the low-power logic and high-power motor stage.

**Diagrammatic representation (textual):**
$$\text{EMG} \rightarrow \text{Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Filter} \rightarrow \text{Classifier} \rightarrow \text{PWM Driver} \rightarrow \text{Motor} \rightarrow \text{Elbow Coupling}$$
$$\uparrow \hspace{8cm} \downarrow$$
$$\text{Encoder + Torque Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Controller}$$

**(7 marks — split: 2 for block diagram, 3 for block-wise explanation, 2 for control flow & power)**

---

**(b)** A prosthetic elbow is required to lift a **2.0 kg forearm** through **90° in 0.8 s**. The forearm centre of mass is at **0.25 m** from the joint. Compute the **(i)** required torque, **(ii)** required power at the joint, **(iii)** required motor current if $K_t = 0.03$ Nm/A and gear ratio is 100:1. Assume 80% gearbox efficiency and 7.4 V supply. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Required torque:**
$$T = m g r = 2.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 = 4.905 \text{ Nm}$$
With safety factor of 2:
$$T_{design} = 9.81 \text{ Nm}$$

**(ii) Required power at joint:**
Angular velocity:
$$\omega = \frac{\pi/2}{0.8} = 1.963 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$P_{out} = T_{design} \cdot \omega = 9.81 \times 1.963 = 19.26 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Motor current:**
After gearbox, motor torque is reduced by factor 100:
$$T_{motor} = \frac{9.81}{100} = 0.0981 \text{ Nm}$$
$$I_{motor} = \frac{T_{motor}}{K_t} = \frac{0.0981}{0.03} = 3.27 \text{ A}$$

**Check against supply voltage** (motor back-EMF + $IR$ drop):
$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{19.26}{0.80} = 24.08 \text{ W}$$
$$I_{draw} = \frac{P}{V \cdot \eta_{driver}} = \frac{24.08}{7.4 \times 0.85} \approx 3.83 \text{ A}$$

The 3.83 A draw is within the design current — selection is feasible. **(7 marks — split: 2 + 2 + 3)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Hill-type muscle model** used in FES elbow systems. Derive the dynamic equation relating elbow torque to muscle activation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Hill-type model represents skeletal muscle as a **contractile element (CE)**, a **parallel elastic element (PE)**, and a **series elastic element (SE)**. The contractile element force is:
$$F_{CE} = F_{max} \cdot \sigma(V) \cdot f_{FV}(v) \cdot f_{FL}(L)$$

where $\sigma(V)$ is the activation from stimulation, $f_{FV}$ is the force–velocity function, and $f_{FL}$ is the force–length function.

**Activation dynamics (first-order lag):**
$$\tau_{act}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \sigma = \sigma_{target}(V)$$

**Force–velocity (Hill hyperbola):**
$$f_{FV} = \frac{F_{max} - a v}{F_{max} + b v}$$

**Projecting force to torque via moment arm $r(\theta)$:**
$$T_{CE} = F_{CE} \cdot r(\theta)$$

**Adding passive elastic torque and load:**
$$T_{elbow} = F_{CE} r(\theta) + F_{PE}(\theta) r(\theta) - T_{load}(\theta)$$

**Applying Newton's second law for rotation:**
$$J\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + F_{PE}(\theta) r(\theta) = F_{CE} r(\theta) - T_{load}(\theta)$$

This is the **plant model** used in FES elbow control design. **(7 marks — split: 2 for model diagram, 3 for CE/SE/PE, 2 for final equation)**

---

**(b)** Compare **FES, body-powered, myoelectric, and pneumatic elbow systems** with respect to endurance, weight, grip force, cosmesis, and clinical applicability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | FES | Body-Powered | Myoelectric | Pneumatic |
|-----------|-----|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Endurance** | 5–30 min (muscle fatigue) | Unlimited (cable) | 8–24 h (battery) | 1–2 days (CO₂) |
| **Weight** | 50–100 g (stim) | 250–400 g | 400–700 g | 500–900 g |
| **Grip force** | 20–40 N | 100+ N | 16–100 N | 100+ N |
| **Cosmesis** | Excellent (no external mech.) | Poor (cables/harness) | Good (silent) | Bulky (canister) |
| **Clinical use** | SCI, stroke | Low-level amputee | Mid–high level amputee | Heavy-duty (industrial) |

**Inferences (for full marks):**
- FES is **best cosmetically** but **fatigue-limited** → suited for short-duration ADL training.
- Body-powered is **most reliable**, cheapest, but cosmetically poor.
- Myoelectric is the **mainstream compromise** for transhumeral amputees.
- Pneumatic is reserved for **high-force vocational** use.

**(7 marks — 2 for table + 3 for inferences + 2 for clinical recommendation)**

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks on Elbow Components questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the closed-loop feedback block** — KTU key always awards 2 marks for the sensor feedback chain; open-loop diagrams lose these.
> 2. **Confusing "epimysial" with "intramuscular"** — epimysial = on the *surface*; IM = *within* the muscle belly. Mixing them is a common 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Not stating units** in numerical problems — every torque must be in **Nm**, every current in **A**, every angle in **rad or °**. Missing units = −0.5 mark per subpart.
> 4. **Ignoring charge-balance** when describing biphasic pulses — must explicitly state $Q_{anode} = Q_{cathode}$.
> 5. **Skipping the safety factor** in motor design — always multiply raw torque by **≥ 2** to account for dynamic loading; examiners deduct 1 mark if omitted.
> 6. **Confusing "prosthesis" with "orthosis"** — prosthesis *replaces* a missing limb; orthosis *assists* an existing limb. FES elbow = orthosis; myoelectric elbow = prosthesis. This distinction appears in nearly every KTU paper.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Elbow has **2 DOF** (flexion/extension + pronation/supination); component design must address both.
- ✅ **Five functional blocks:** Intent → Controller → Stimulator/Actuator → Interface → Feedback.
- ✅ **FES parameters:** 20–50 Hz, 100–400 µs pulse width, 20–150 mA amplitude, **charge ≤ 1 µC per phase**.
- ✅ **Biphasic, charge-balanced pulses** are mandatory for safe stimulation (ISO 14708).
- ✅ **Hill muscle model** gives the canonical plant equation:
  $J\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + F_{PE}r = F_{CE}r - T_{load}$.
- ✅ **PWM** of pulse width is the standard clinical method to grade force in FES.
- ✅ **Myoelectric elbow** uses residual **sEMG** → amplifier → classifier → motor driver → gearbox.
- ✅ **Body-powered** systems: cable excursion ~5–7 cm; myoelectric: 8–24 h battery; FES: 5–30 min endurance.
- ✅ **Electrode types (in increasing selectivity):** Surface → Percutaneous → Epimysial → Intramuscular → Cuff → Intrafascicular.
- ✅ **Closed-loop control extends FES endurance** from 2–5 min (open-loop) to >30 min.
- ✅ **Motor design rule of thumb:** Raw torque × **2** (safety), power ÷ **0.8** (gearbox), current = torque / $K_t$ (post-gear).
- ✅ **Standards to remember:** **ISO 14708** (active implants), **ISO 10993** (biocompatibility), **IEC 60601** (medical electrical equipment).
- ✅ **Clinical benefit:** Restoring the 30°–130° flexion arc enables eating, grooming, and face-touching — the **functional reach envelope**.
- ✅ **Fatigue mitigation strategies:** interleaved stimulation, frequency modulation, doublet pulses, and closed-loop feedback.
- ✅ **KTU high-yield acronyms:** FES, ENG, EMG, sEMG, IMES, SEA, FSM, PID, MAV, RMS, LDA, ANN, PWM, AM, FM, ISO, DOF, ADL.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Neural Prosthesis and Implants**, a **Control System** is an engineered arrangement of interconnected components that regulates, directs, or commands the output of a neural stimulator or actuator so that a desired physiological response is achieved and maintained, despite disturbances or variations in the biological load. The system accepts one or more reference inputs (e.g., intent signals from the brain, residual EMG, or external sensors) and produces controlled outputs (e.g., stimulation current amplitude, pulse width, frequency) delivered to the target neural tissue.

According to KTU 2024 scheme terminology, control systems for neural prosthetics are classified based on:

- **Loop Architecture**: Open-loop (feed-forward) vs. Closed-loop (feedback)
- **Signal Domain**: Continuous-time vs. Discrete-time (digital) controllers
- **Adaptivity**: Fixed-gain, Adaptive, or Learning-based controllers (e.g., neural network controllers)
- **Linearity**: Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) models vs. Non-linear models of neural tissue

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A neural prosthesis is fundamentally a **biomedical mechatronic system**. Without a robust control architecture, parameters such as muscle fatigue, electrode impedance drift, and neural plasticity render the prosthesis unstable and unusable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Exact Terminology):**
> A *Closed-Loop Neural Control System* is a feedback system in which the controller continuously compares the actual neural/physiological output (controlled variable) with the desired reference and adjusts the stimulation parameters to minimize the error signal, ensuring precision, stability, and safety.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **neural prosthesis like a smart thermostat controlling your room temperature**, but instead of temperature, it is controlling the *contraction level of a paralyzed muscle*:

| Smart Thermostat Analogy | Neural Prosthesis Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Set-point temperature (e.g., 22°C) | Desired muscle force or joint angle |
| Room temperature sensor | Neural recording electrode / EMG sensor |
| Thermostat controller | Implantable pulse generator (IPG) |
| Heater/Cooler | Stimulation electrode on nerve/muscle |
| Window opening (disturbance) | Muscle fatigue, impedance change |

### Plain English Explanation:

Imagine you have a **prosthetic hand**. You *think* about closing your fingers. Your brain sends weak signals. These signals are picked up by surface electrodes on your skin (sensors). A small computer (controller) processes these signals and sends commands to motors or stimulates muscles in your forearm (actuator). The fingers close.

**But what if the grip is too weak?** Without feedback, the system cannot know. With feedback (force sensors on fingertips telling the controller how hard you are gripping), the controller can automatically increase stimulation until the desired grip is achieved. **This is closed-loop control.**

If the system simply fires pre-programmed stimulation regardless of actual output, it is **open-loop**.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are standard in neural prosthesis control systems:
