# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RMS EMG as the Control Variable

**Starting premise:** The processor must decide, in real-time, how hard the user is trying to contract the muscle. The DC component of EMG is essentially zero (it is a zero-mean stochastic signal). The standard scalar feature used is the **Root-Mean-Square** over a sliding window of length $T$.

**Step 1 — Start from the energy of a finite-length EMG window:**

$$
E = \int_{0}^{T} v_{emg}^2(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Define the mean-square value (energy per unit time):**

$$
\overline{v^2} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v_{emg}^2(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root to obtain RMS:**

$$
V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v_{emg}^2(t)\, dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Approximate the integral as a discrete sum over $N$ ADC samples (sampling frequency $f_s$):**

$$
T = \frac{N}{f_s}
$$

So:

$$
V_{RMS} \approx \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_{emg}^2(i)}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**

A typical EMG window of $N = 200$ samples at $f_s = 1$ kHz gives $T = 200$ ms. This window is short enough for real-time control (< 5 Hz bandwidth for the control command) yet long enough to average out the random noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RMS and not peak?** Peak detection is fragile: a single artefact spike triggers a false command. RMS averages the energy, making it the **most reliable** estimate of voluntary contraction strength.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Motor Current from Required Grip Force

**Given:** A prosthetic finger must apply a tangential grip force $F_g$ on a cylindrical object of diameter $D$ via a tendon wrapped around a pulley of radius $r_p$.

**Step 1 — Mechanical torque required at the finger joint:**

$$
\tau_{joint} = F_g \cdot r_p
$$

**Step 2 — The motor's output shaft, after the gearhead (gear ratio $G$, efficiency $\eta$), must produce:**

$$
\tau_{motor} = \frac{\tau_{joint}}{G \cdot \eta}
$$

**Step 3 — The DC motor's torque constant $K_t$ (in Nm/A) relates current to torque:**

$$
I = \frac{\tau_{motor}}{K_t} = \frac{F_g \cdot r_p}{G \cdot \eta \cdot K_t}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example for KTU board problem:**

Suppose $F_g = 50$ N, $r_p = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m, $G = 100$, $\eta = 0.7$, $K_t = 0.02$ Nm/A.

Substitute:

$$
I = \frac{50 \times 0.01}{100 \times 0.7 \times 0.02}
$$

$$
I = \frac{0.5}{1.4} \approx 0.357\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 5 — Power drawn from battery (assuming $V_{batt} = 7.4$ V):**

$$
P = V \cdot I = 7.4 \times 0.357 \approx 2.64\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 6 — Battery life from a 1500 mAh Li-Po pack:**

$$
\text{Energy} = 7.4 \times 1.5 = 11.1\ \text{Wh}
$$

$$
\text{Runtime} = \frac{11.1}{2.64} \approx 4.2\ \text{hours}
$$

This matches real-world Ottobock / i-Limb runtime specifications of **3 – 5 hours of active use**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pattern Recognition Decision Boundary (LDA)

For a **two-class** grasp classification (e.g., "Power Grip" vs "Precision Pinch") with feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^2$, **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** finds the optimal projection direction $\mathbf{w}$ that maximally separates the two class means $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2$ while minimising within-class scatter.

**Step 1 — Within-class scatter matrix:**

$$
S_W = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T
$$

**Step 2 — Between-class scatter matrix:**

$$
S_B = (\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)^T
$$

**Step 3 — Fisher criterion (to be maximised):**

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}}
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate and set to zero to find optimal $\mathbf{w}$:**

$$
\mathbf{w}^* = S_W^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)
$$

**Step 5 — Decision rule for a new feature vector $\mathbf{x}_{new}$:**

$$
y = \mathbf{w}^{*T} \mathbf{x}_{new}
$$

$$
\text{Class} = \begin{cases}
\text{Power Grip}, & y \geq y_{th} \\
\text{Precision Pinch}, & y < y_{th}
\end{cases}
$$

Where the threshold $y_{th}$ is set as the midpoint of the projected class means.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why LDA for KTU exams?** LDA is the de-facto **first algorithm** taught in pattern-recognition modules because it is mathematically simple, has a closed-form solution, and runs in $< 1$ ms on embedded hardware. Modern systems (COAPT, Infinite Biomedical) still use LDA on 8-channel EMG with > 95 % accuracy.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Skin-Electrode Impedance Model

The skin-electrode interface behaves like a **resistor in parallel with a capacitor**, often in series with a second RC combination (the deeper dermis and underlying tissue).

**Step 1 — Simplified two-element model:**

$$
Z_{skin}(\omega) = \frac{R}{1 + (\omega RC)^2} - j\frac{\omega R^2 C}{1 + (\omega RC)^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude and phase:**

$$
\vert Z_{skin}(\omega) \vert = \frac{R}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}
$$

$$
\phi(\omega) = -\arctan(\omega RC)
$$

**Step 3 — At low frequency ($\omega \to 0$):** $\vert Z \vert \to R$ (resistive), dominated by the outer stratum corneum.
**Step 4 — At high frequency ($\omega \to \infty$):** $\vert Z \vert \to 0$ (capacitive short).

**Step 5 — Practical consequence:** For 50 Hz interference, lowering the skin impedance by **lightly abrading the skin and using conductive gel** reduces mains pickup and improves SNR dramatically.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "Myoelectrically Controlled Hand". List any four key components of the system.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A myoelectrically controlled hand is a battery-powered, externally-actuated upper-limb prosthesis whose drive commands are derived from the surface or implanted EMG bioelectric signals of the residual muscles in the amputated limb.

**Four key components (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Electrode assembly** (Ag/AgCl surface or intramuscular needle) for EMG acquisition.
2. **Signal conditioning unit** comprising instrumentation amplifier, band-pass filter, and 50 Hz notch filter.
3. **Controller / Processor** (microcontroller with pattern-recognition algorithm or amplitude-decision logic).
4. **Actuator sub-system** (DC gearmotor + Bowden cable + articulated fingers + battery).

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Direct Control and Pattern Recognition Control of a myoelectric hand.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

|---|---|---|
| **EMG channels** | 1 or 2 | 4 to 16 |
| **Feature used** | Single RMS or MAV threshold | Multi-dimensional feature vector (MAV, RMS, ZC, WL, AR coefficients) |
| **Classifier** | Threshold comparator | LDA, SVM, Random Forest, CNN, RNN |
| **Grasp classes** | 1 (or 2 with co-contraction switching) | 4 – 10 simultaneous or switched |
| **Latency** | < 30 ms | 50 – 200 ms |
| **Re-calibration** | Not required | Daily or weekly |
| **KTU keyword** | "Classical myoelectric control" | "Intelligent / AI-based myoelectric control" |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — 14-Mark Comprehensive Question

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4, Q8(A)]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a myoelectrically controlled hand. Explain the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the materials used in the construction of a prosthetic hand with reference to biocompatibility, mechanical strength, and weight. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram (2 marks):**

The student must draw a labelled block diagram showing: **Residual muscle → Surface electrodes → Instrumentation amplifier → Bandpass filter → Notch filter → Rectifier → Smoother → Threshold/Classifier → Motor driver → DC motor → Cable → Fingers → Object → Force sensor → Feedback**.

**Working description (5 marks):**

1. **Signal source (0.5):** Voluntary contraction of residual flexor/extensor muscles generates EMG (50 μV – 5 mV, 20–500 Hz).
2. **Acquisition (0.5):** A pair of Ag/AgCl surface electrodes placed on the skin picks up the differential EMG.
3. **Amplification (0.5):** An instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128, AD620) with CMRR $\geq$ 100 dB boosts the signal by a gain of 1000.
4. **Filtering (0.5):** 20 Hz high-pass removes motion artefact, 500 Hz low-pass prevents aliasing, 50 Hz notch removes mains interference.
5. **Rectification & smoothing (0.5):** Full-wave rectification + integrator yields an envelope proportional to muscle effort.
6. **Decision logic (0.5):** A threshold detector (direct) or pattern classifier (intelligent) generates open/close/grasps.
7. **Actuation (0.5):** H-bridge driver energises a 6 – 12 V DC gearmotor, which pulls a tendon (Bowden cable) inside the finger, closing it around the object.
8. **Feedback (1.0):** A force-sensitive resistor at the fingertip detects slip; this modulates grip force automatically (closed-loop proportional control) to prevent crushing fragile objects (eggs, paper cups) yet retain a glass bottle firmly.

---

|---|---|---|---|
| **Finger skeleton / linkages** | **Titanium Ti-6Al-4V ELI** | Biocompatible (ISO 5832-3), high strength-to-weight ratio ($\sigma_y \approx 880$ MPa, density 4.43 g/cm³), non-magnetic (MRI-safe) | 1.5 |
| **Outer skin / cosmetic glove** | **Medical-grade silicone elastomer (MED-4950, NuSil)** | Biocompatible (USP Class VI), Shore A 10 – 40 (soft-touch), waterproof, can be pigmented to match skin tone | 1.5 |

| **Battery** | **Lithium-Polymer (Li-Po), 7.4 V, 1500 – 2200 mAh** | High energy density ($\sim 200$ Wh/kg), rechargeable, low self-discharge | 1.0 |

### Question B (14 Marks) — 14-Mark Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4, Q8(B)]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between surface EMG electrodes and implanted (intramuscular) electrodes for myoelectric control. List the advantages and disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR)? How does it improve the controllability of a myoelectric hand? Mention the surgery and signal-routing steps. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

| Aspect | Surface Electrodes | Implanted (Intramuscular) Electrodes |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | On skin surface | Inside the muscle belly (epimysial / intramuscular needles) |
| **Signal amplitude** | 50 μV – 5 mV | 0.5 – 10 mV (5 – 10× higher) |
| **SNR** | 20 – 30 dB | 40 – 60 dB |
| **Bandwidth** | Limited by skin (low-pass) | Full physiological bandwidth (up to 1 kHz) |
| **Cross-talk** | High (picks up adjacent muscles) | Low (localised to single motor unit territory) |
| **Stability** | Affected by sweat, electrode shift | Highly stable; chronically implantable |
| **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive, don/doff daily | Requires minor surgery; lead wires pass through skin |
| **Longevity** | Days (adhesive) – months (textile) | Years (with proper hermetic encapsulation) |
| **Cost** | ₹500 – ₹5,000 (India) | ₹50,000 – ₹5,00,000 (with implant-grade packaging) |
| **KTU keyword** | "Non-invasive myoelectric" | "Implantable Myoelectric Sensor (IMS)" |

**Conclusion (1 mark for any):** Surface electrodes dominate clinical use today; implanted electrodes (e.g., the **IMES** — Implanted Myoelectric Sensing System developed under the DARPA "Revolutionising Prosthetics" program) are the future.

---

### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** **Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR)** is a surgical procedure in which the residual motor nerves of an amputated limb (which previously innervated the hand) are surgically transferred onto remnant muscle targets (e.g., pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, or remaining biceps/triceps). When the patient *thinks* "close hand," the brain's motor command now travels down the re-routed nerve and causes a contraction in the new target muscle.

**Surgical steps (2 marks):**
1. Identify the four major nerves in the residual limb — **median, ulnar, radial, musculocutaneous**.
2. Dissect the nerves and split them into motor fascicles.
3. Reinnervate a segment of remaining muscle tissue that is not used for other functions.
4. Allow 3 – 6 months for axonal regeneration.

**Signal-routing steps (1.5 marks):**
1. Place surface or implanted electrodes over each newly reinnervated muscle site.
2. Each site now represents a unique intended movement (e.g., pectoralis 1 = hand close, pectoralis 2 = hand open, latissimus 1 = wrist flexion, etc.).
3. The controller is trained to map 4 – 6 EMG signals to multiple grasps.

**Benefits (2 marks):**
1. **Intuitive control**: The user simply *thinks* about the movement; no co-contraction tricks needed.
2. **Multi-DOF simultaneous control**: Up to 6 DoF from 6 channels — power, precision, lateral, hook, open, plus wrist rotate.
3. **Reduced phantom pain**: TMR was originally invented to treat phantom-limb pain (Dr. Todd Kuiken, 2004) and serendipitously yielded intuitive prosthetic control.
4. **Higher accuracy**: Pattern recognition accuracy improves from ~85 % (2-channel) to > 95 % (6-channel TMR).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Confusing "myoelectric" with "bioelectric":** Myoelectric refers **only** to muscle-derived electrical signals (EMG). It does **not** include EEG (brain), EOG (eye), or ECG (heart). Marks lost for loose terminology.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting units:** RMS values without $\mu$V or mV will lose **0.5 marks**. Always write "$V_{RMS} = 250\ \mu V$", not just "250".
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Skipping the bandpass filter:** A surprising number of students draw a block diagram without a 50 Hz notch filter. This is an **automatic 1-mark deduction** in valuation.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Calling pattern recognition "AI":** Be precise — name the algorithm (LDA / SVM / CNN). Generic "AI" gets partial credit only.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Omitting biocompatibility standards:** When listing materials, **always** quote the standard (ISO 10993 for biocompatibility, USP Class VI for silicone, ASTM F75 for Co-Cr alloy). Naming a material without its standard loses a mark.
>
> **Pitfall 6 — Confusing "TMR" with "RPNI" (Regenerative Peripheral Nerve Interface):** Both are surgical nerve-routing techniques, but TMR uses native muscle targets while RPNI uses free muscle grafts. Examiners will deduct for interchange.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- ✅ A **myoelectric hand** = EMG-driven, battery-powered, externally actuated upper-limb prosthesis.
- ✅ **EMG** amplitude = **50 μV – 5 mV**, bandwidth = **20 – 500 Hz**.
- ✅ **CMRR of amplifier** must be $\geq$ **100 dB** to reject 50 Hz mains.
- ✅ **Three essential filters** = high-pass (20 Hz) + low-pass (500 Hz) + notch (50 Hz).
- ✅ **Direct control** uses 1–2 channels and a threshold; **Pattern recognition** uses 4–16 channels and a classifier.
- ✅ **LDA** = the most commonly used classifier in commercial pattern-recognition hands (COAPT, Infinite Biomedical).
- ✅ **Co-contraction** = simultaneous activation of two antagonistic muscles; used as a "switch" to cycle grasp modes.
- ✅ **Materials**: Titanium (skeleton), Silicone (cosmetic glove), UHMWPE (bearings), 316L SS (tendons), Carbon-fibre composite (socket), Li-Po (battery).
- ✅ **Biocompatibility standards**: ISO 10993, USP Class VI, ASTM F75.
- ✅ **TMR** = surgical re-routing of residual nerves onto new muscle targets, enabling intuitive 4–6 DoF control.
- ✅ **Battery**: typically **7.4 V Li-Po**, ~1500 – 2200 mAh, runtime 3 – 5 hours of active use.
- ✅ **Grip force** of commercial hands: **0 – 100 N** (Ottobock Michelangelo); closing time: **0.5 – 1.5 s**.
- ✅ **Weight of hand unit**: typically **< 500 g** for cosmesis and shoulder-girdle comfort.
- ✅ **Force / slip feedback** is achieved by **FSR (Force-Sensitive Resistor)** at the fingertip + **vibrotactile** or **TENS** feedback to the skin.
- ✅ **DARPA "Revolutionising Prosthetics" program** produced the **DEKA "Luke" Arm** (10 DoF, FDA-cleared 2014).
- ✅ **COAPT COMPLETE CONTROL** is the first FDA-cleared **pattern-recognition** controller (2017).
- ✅ **Ottobeck Michelangelo**, **i-Limb Quantum (Ossur)**, **bebionic (Ottobock)**, **Taska**, and **Hero Arm (Open Bionics)** are the most-tested commercial myoelectric hands in KTU viva voce.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line Board-Exam Golden Statement:**
> *"A myoelectrically controlled hand converts the RMS amplitude of the EMG signal of a voluntarily-contracted residual muscle into a proportional actuation command for an electric motor that drives an articulated finger, with materials chosen for biocompatibility (ISO 10993), mechanical strength (Ti-6Al-4V, CFRP), and low weight (< 500 g)."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

topics.title: Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Neural Prosthesis and Implants**, an **Actuator** is defined as the output stage of a bidirectional neural interface system that converts processed neural or electronic control signals into a deliberate physiological action — namely, the delivery of electrical, chemical, mechanical, or optical energy to excitable neural tissue (nerve, muscle, or sensory end-organ) to restore, replace, or augment a lost biological function.

The actuator subsystem forms the **efferent limb** of a closed-loop neural prosthesis, complementing the **afferent limb** represented by sensors/recording electrodes. It consists of three mandatory sub-blocks:

1. **Stimulus Generator / Driver Circuitry** — produces controlled-current or controlled-voltage waveforms.
2. **Lead-Conductor Network** — routes the stimulus from the source to the tissue.
3. **Active Electrode Interface** — the electrode–tissue boundary where charge transfer occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** An actuator in a neural prosthesis is **not** a generic motor or solenoid. It is a **biocompatible, charge-balanced, energy-controlled bioelectronic interface** whose primary function is to safely depolarize (excite) or hyperpolarize (inhibit) neuronal membranes without causing electrochemical tissue damage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to communicate with a deaf person using a **tap on the shoulder**:

- Your **brain decision** = "I want to say hello."
- Your **hand movement** = the signal processing in your motor cortex.
- Your **fingers touching the shoulder** = the actuator — the **physical interface** that finally delivers the message.

In a neural prosthesis, the actuator plays the **exact same role as the fingers on the shoulder**. It is the final messenger. If the tap is too soft (sub-threshold), the person won't respond. If it is too hard (excessive charge), it may cause injury. Therefore, an actuator must deliver **just the right amount of energy, at the right time, in the right place**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following parameters are **universally required** in any KTU answer concerning neural actuators:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Threshold charge | $Q_{th}$ | **0.1 – 1.0** | $\mu C$ |
| Charge per phase | $Q$ | **0.05 – 5** | $\mu C$ |
| Charge density | $D$ | **< 30** | $\mu C / cm^2$ |
| Current density | $J$ | **< 1000** | $\mu A / cm^2$ |
| Stimulus pulse width | $PW$ | **50 – 500** | $\mu s$ |
| Stimulation frequency | $f$ | **20 – 200** | Hz |
| Phase voltage | $V$ | **1 – 10** | V |
| Shannon's safety limit | $k = \log(D) + \log(J)$ | **< 1.85** | — |
| Faraday constant | $F$ | **96485** | C/mol |
| Neural membrane time constant | $\tau_m$ | **≈ 10** | ms |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Role of Actuators in a Closed-Loop Neural Prosthesis

A complete neural prosthesis is a **closed-loop bioelectronic system**. The actuator closes the loop on the output side.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Signal Acquisition** — Neural signals (EEG, ECoG, spike trains) are recorded by sensors.
2. **Decoding** — Algorithms translate neural intent (e.g., "I want to move my arm").
3. **Command Generation** — A control unit produces an instruction pulse.
4. **Actuator Activation** — The actuator delivers energy to the target tissue, evoking:
   - **Motor output** (muscle contraction via FES),
   - **Sensory feedback** (e.g., cochlear/retinal stimulation),
   - **Modulation** (e.g., deep brain stimulation for Parkinson's).
5. **Feedback** — Sensory reafference or external sensors close the loop.

### 2.2 Classification of Actuators

Actuators used in neural prosthetics are classified by the **energy modality** they employ:

- **Electrical Actuators** — most prevalent; deliver controlled charge pulses.
- **Chemical Actuators** — drug delivery pumps (e.g., intrathecal baclofen).
- **Mechanical Actuators** — pneumatic/hydraulic cuffs, tendon stimulators.
- **Optical Actuators** — optogenetic stimulation using light (research-grade).
- **Magnetic Actuators** — transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) coils (non-implanted).
- **Ultrasonic Actuators** — focused ultrasound for deep brain modulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, the **vast majority** of marks are awarded to **electrical actuators** because they are the only fully implantable and clinically mature modality.

### 2.3 Electrical Stimulator Circuit Topologies

Electrical actuators can be implemented as:

- **Voltage-Controlled (VC) Stimulator** — simpler but suffers from variable charge delivery due to changing tissue impedance.
- **Current-Controlled (CC) Stimulator** — preferred in KTU answers; delivers **constant current**, ensuring reproducible charge regardless of impedance drift.
- **Capacitively-Coupled (CCoupled) Stimulator** — delivers charge via a capacitor; inherently **charge-balanced**; safe for chronic use.

### 2.4 Stimulation Waveforms

The single most examinable waveform concept in KTU is the **stimulus pulse shape**:

- **Monophasic Pulse** — current flows in one direction only; **unsafe** for chronic use; causes net DC → electrochemical damage.
- **Biphasic Pulse** — symmetric or asymmetric two-phase current; **safe and clinically mandatory**.
- **Charge-Balanced Biphasic** — the integral of current over the cathodic phase equals that over the anodic phase:

$$Q_{cathode} = \int_{0}^{PW_c} I_c(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{PW_a} I_a(t)\,dt = Q_{anode}$$

- **High-Frequency Block Waveforms** — used for nerve blocking instead of activation.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the following high-yield equations. They appear almost every year in KTU model papers.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = I \cdot PW$ | Charge per phase | $\mu C$ |
| 2 | $D = Q / A_{electrode}$ | Charge density | $\mu C / cm^2$ |
| 3 | $J = I / A_{electrode}$ | Current density | $\mu A / cm^2$ |
| 4 | $V = I \cdot Z_{tissue}$ | Ohm's law across tissue | V |
| 5 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Instantaneous power delivered | W |
| 6 | $E_{pulse} = P \cdot PW$ | Energy per pulse | J |
| 7 | $P_{avg} = E_{pulse} \cdot f$ | Average power | W |
| 8 | $Q_{rev} = n \cdot F$ | Reversible charge via Faraday's law | C |
| 9 | $m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of species produced at electrode | g |
| 10 | $k = \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(J)$ | Shannon safety parameter | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$ | Membrane time constant | ms |
| 12 | $V_{th} = I_{th} \cdot R_m$ | Threshold membrane depolarization | mV |
| 13 | $V_{rest} \approx -70$ | Neuronal resting potential | mV |
| 14 | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Sampling for feedback signals | Hz |
| 15 | $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}\right)^2}$ | Tissue impedance (parallel RC) | $\Omega$ |

### 2.6 Why These Equations Matter

- Equations 1–3 define the **safety envelope** — exceed them and tissue dies.
- Equation 10 (Shannon's $k < 1.85$) is the **single most cited safety criterion** in neural stimulation literature.
- Equations 11–12 describe the **neural recruitment** mechanism — how a stimulus pulse above $V_{th}$ triggers an action potential.
- Equation 9 is the Faraday-derived **electrochemical damage predictor** — irreversible faradaic reactions begin when charge exceeds $Q_{rev}$.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cochlear implants** — actuators deliver biphasic current pulses to auditory nerve fibers representing specific frequency bands.
- **Retinal prostheses (Argus II, Alpha-IMS)** — epiretinal/subretinal electrode arrays stimulate remaining retinal ganglion cells.
- **Deep Brain Stimulators (DBS)** — chronic high-frequency stimulation of the subthalamic nucleus for Parkinson's disease.
- **Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES)** — restores grasp, gait, and respiration in spinal-cord-injured patients.
- **Sacral nerve stimulation** — treats incontinence.
- **Vagus nerve stimulation (VNS)** — treats epilepsy and depression.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Charge Per Phase from Current and Pulse Width

This is the most fundamental derivation in KTU neural actuator questions.

**Given:**
- Constant stimulating current: $I$
- Cathodic pulse width: $PW_c$
- Tissue modeled as a purely resistive load during the pulse.

**Step 1 — Define current as rate of charge flow.**
By definition, electric current is the time-rate of change of charge:

$$I = \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over the duration of the cathodic phase.**
For a constant current $I$ over interval $[0, PW_c]$:

$$Q_c = \int_{0}^{PW_c} I\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Pull the constant out of the integral.**
Since $I$ is constant:

$$Q_c = I \cdot \int_{0}^{PW_c} dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$Q_c = I \cdot \big[t\big]_{0}^{PW_c} = I \cdot (PW_c - 0)$$

**Step 5 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{Q_c = I \cdot PW_c}$$

This result expresses charge in coulombs when $I$ is in amperes and $PW_c$ is in seconds. For typical neural prosthetic values ($I = 100 \, \mu A$, $PW_c = 200 \, \mu s$):

$$Q_c = (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times (200 \times 10^{-6}) = 20 \times 10^{-9} \, C = 20 \, nC = 0.02 \, \mu C$$

This is comfortably below the threshold charge $Q_{th} \approx 0.1 \, \mu C$ activation range of small-diameter fibers, but sufficient when paired with appropriate electrode geometry.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Charge Balancing Condition for a Biphasic Waveform

**Given:**
- Cathodic current amplitude $I_c$ over duration $PW_c$.
- Anodic current amplitude $I_a$ over duration $PW_a$.

**Step 1 — State the definition of charge balance.**
A stimulus waveform is charge-balanced when the net charge transferred per cycle is zero:

$$\Delta Q_{cycle} = Q_c - Q_a = 0$$

**Step 2 — Express both charges.**
Using $Q = I \cdot PW$:

$$Q_c = I_c \cdot PW_c$$
$$Q_a = I_a \cdot PW_a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the balance equation.**

$$I_c \cdot PW_c - I_a \cdot PW_a = 0$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the design constraint.**

$$\boxed{I_c \cdot PW_c = I_a \cdot PW_a}$$

**Step 5 — Three practical implementations.**
1. **Symmetric biphasic:** $I_c = I_a$ and $PW_c = PW_a$.
2. **Current-asymmetric:** $I_a = 0.5 \, I_c$, hence $PW_a = 2 \, PW_c$.
3. **Active discharge:** cathodic pulse followed by a passive resistor-discharge anodic pulse with $I_a$ small and $PW_a$ long.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Shannon's Safety Limit

**Given:**
- Charge density $D = Q/A$ in $\mu C / cm^2$.
- Current density $J = I/A$ in $\mu A / cm^2$.
- Empirical threshold observed across multiple animal studies.

**Step 1 — Define the safety parameter $k$.**

$$k = \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(J)$$

**Step 2 — Apply log identity.**

$$\boxed{k = \log_{10}(D \cdot J)}$$

**Step 3 — Insert safe-limit values.**
$D = 30 \, \mu C / cm^2$ and $J = 1000 \, \mu A / cm^2$:

$$k = \log_{10}(30 \times 1000) = \log_{10}(30000) \approx 4.48$$

This is the *maximum* end of the safety zone. The conservative safe-zone upper bound quoted by KTU is $k < 1.85$, which corresponds to lower stimulation levels used in chronic implants.

**Step 4 — Operational interpretation.**
- If $k < 1.85$: **safe** for chronic stimulation.
- If $1.85 \le k < 2.0$: marginal — short-duration use only.
- If $k \ge 2.0$: **unsafe** — risk of electrochemical damage and tissue necrosis.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Threshold Membrane Voltage for Action Potential Initiation

**Given:**
- Neural membrane modeled as a parallel RC circuit with membrane resistance $R_m$ and capacitance $C_m$.
- Stimulus current $I_{stim}$ injected via intracellular or extracellular electrode.

**Step 1 — Membrane time constant.**

$$\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$$

For a typical mammalian myelinated axon: $R_m \approx 10 \, k\Omega \cdot cm^2$, $C_m \approx 1 \, \mu F / cm^2$, giving $\tau_m \approx 10 \, ms$.

**Step 2 — Charging equation for a step current.**
When a sub-threshold constant current $I$ is injected, the membrane voltage changes according to:

$$V_m(t) = I \cdot R_m \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state value.**

$$V_{m,ss} = I \cdot R_m$$

**Step 4 — Threshold condition.**
An action potential is initiated when $V_m$ reaches approximately:

$$V_{th} \approx +55 \, mV \;\; \text{(from rest of} \, -70 \, mV\text{)}$$

So the required depolarization is $\Delta V = 125 \, mV$.

**Step 5 — Solve for threshold current.**
Assuming pulse width $PW \gg \tau_m$:

$$\boxed{I_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{th}}{R_m}}$$

If $R_m = 10 \, k\Omega$, then:

$$I_{th} = \dfrac{125 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^3} = 12.5 \, \mu A$$

For shorter pulses ($PW < \tau_m$), the strength-duration relationship applies:

$$I_{th} = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m}}$$

where $I_{rh}$ is the rheobase current.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Position of the Actuator in a Closed-Loop Neural Prosthesis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BRAIN_OR_NERVE] --> B[SENSOR_BLOCK]
    B --> C[AMPLIFIER_AND_FILTER]
    C --> D[ADC_DIGITIZER]
    D --> E[SIGNAL_PROCESSOR_DECODER]
    E --> F[CONTROLLER_AND_SAFETY_UNIT]
    F --> G[STIMULUS_GENERATOR]
    G --> H[LEAD_AND_CONDUCTOR_NETWORK]
    H --> I[ACTIVE_ELECTRODE_INTERFACE]
    I --> J[TARGET_TISSUE_NERVE_OR_MUSCLE]
    J --> K[PHYSIOLOGICAL_RESPONSE_MOTION_OR_SENSATION]
    K --> A
```

This flow demonstrates the **placement of the actuator** between the digital command unit and the biological tissue. The feedback arrow (K → A) confirms that actuators operate inside a **closed loop**, not in isolation.

---

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Neural Prosthesis Actuators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[ACTUATORS] --> A1[ELECTRICAL]
    A0 --> A2[CHEMICAL]
    A0 --> A3[MECHANICAL]
    A0 --> A4[OPTICAL]
    A0 --> A5[MAGNETIC]
    A0 --> A6[ULTRASONIC]
    A1 --> B1[CURRENT_CONTROLLED_CC]
    A1 --> B2[VOLTAGE_CONTROLLED_VC]
    A1 --> B3[CAPACITIVELY_COUPLED_CC]
    B1 --> C1[COCHLEAR_IMPLANT]
    B1 --> C2[RETINAL_PROSTHESIS]
    B1 --> C3[DEEP_BRAIN_STIMULATOR]
    B1 --> C4[FES_FUNCTIONAL_ELEC_STIM]
    A2 --> D1[INTRATHECAL_PUMP]
    A2 --> D2[IONTOPHORETIC_PATCH]
    A4 --> E1[OPTOGENETICS]
    A5 --> E2[TMS_COIL]
    A6 --> E3[FOCUSED_ULTRASOUND]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Electrical Stimulator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[POWER_SUPPLY_BATTERY] --> P2[VOLTAGE_REGULATOR]
    P2 --> P3[DIGITAL_CONTROL_LOGIC]
    P3 --> P4[CURRENT_SOURCE_OR_SINK_DRIVER]
    P4 --> P5[OUTPUT_STAGE_H_BRIDGE]
    P5 --> P6[ELECTRODE_INTERFACE]
    P6 --> P7[TISSUE_LOAD]
    P3 --> P8[CHARGE_BALANCE_MONITOR]
    P8 --> P3
    P3 --> P9[SAFETY_SHUTDOWN_CIRCUIT]
```

This is the canonical **functional architecture** of any implantable neural actuator. Note the **charge balance monitor** feeding back to the digital control — it is the most critical safety block.

---

### 4.4 Stimulus Waveform Diagram (Biphasic Charge-Balanced)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIPHASIC["BIPHASIC_CHARGE_BALANCED_WAVEFORM"]
        direction LR
        W1[QUIESCENT_BASELINE] --> X1[CATHODIC_PHASE_NEGATIVE_PULSE]
        X1 --> X2[INTERPHASE_DELAY_US]
        X2 --> X3[ANODIC_PHASE_POSITIVE_PULSE]
        X3 --> W1
    end
```

For clarity: in the cathodic phase the active electrode is negative (depol arizes the neuron); in the anodic phase the polarity reverses to restore charge balance.

---

### 4.5 Cochlear Implant Actuator Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[MICROPHONE_INPUT] --> S1[BANDPASS_FILTER_BANK]
    S1 --> S2[ENVELOPE_DETECTOR]
    S2 --> S3[CHANNEL_MAPPING_ALGORITHM]
    S3 --> S4[CURRENT_STIMULATOR_OUTPUT_STAGE]
    S4 --> S5[ELECTRODE_ARRAY_INSERTED_INTO_COCHLEA]
    S5 --> S6[AUDITORY_NERVE_FIBERS]
    S6 --> S7[BRAIN_AUDITORY_CORTEX]
    S7 --> S8[PERCEPTION_OF_SOUND]
```

This functional architecture shows how a **cochlear implant actuator** ultimately drives the auditory cortex. Every stage in this pipeline maps to a component tested in KTU viva questions.

---

### 4.6 Retinal Prosthesis Actuator Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[EXTERNAL_CAMERA] --> R1[VIDEO_PROCESSOR_EYEGLASSES_MOUNTED]
    R1 --> R2[RF_TELEMETRY_LINK]
    R2 --> R3[IMPLANTED_STIMULATOR_CASE]
    R3 --> R4[ELECTRODE_ARRAY_EPIRETINAL_OR_SUBRETINAL]
    R4 --> R5[REMAINING_RETINAL_GANGLION_CELLS]
    R5 --> R6[OPTIC_NERVE]
    R6 --> R7[VISUAL_CORTEX]
    R7 --> R8[PERCEPTION_OF_LIGHT_PATTERN]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]`
**Define the term "actuator" in the context of a neural prosthesis. List any two examples of actuators used clinically.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An actuator in a neural prosthesis is the output subsystem that converts electrical control signals into a physiological response by delivering controlled energy — typically charge-balanced current pulses — to excitable tissue such as neurons or muscles.

Two clinical examples:
1. **Cochlear implant electrode array** — delivers biphasic current to auditory nerve fibers to restore hearing.
2. **Deep brain stimulator (DBS)** lead — delivers high-frequency pulses to the subthalamic nucleus to suppress Parkinsonian tremor.
*(A third valid example: Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) for restoring grasp.)*

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition: **1 mark**
- Two examples with brief justification: **2 marks (1 each)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State Shannon's safety criterion for electrical stimulation and explain its physical meaning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Shannon's safety parameter is defined as:

$$k = \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(J) = \log_{10}(D \cdot J)$$

where $D$ is charge density ($\mu C / cm^2$) and $J$ is current density ($\mu A / cm^2$).

- **Physical meaning:** $k$ combines the two principal electrochemical stressors (charge and current density) into a single scalar that predicts the onset of **irreversible faradaic reactions** at the electrode–tissue interface.
- **Safe limit:** $k < 1.85$ for chronic stimulation; values approaching 2.0 indicate tissue damage risk.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Formula: **1 mark**
- Variable identification: **1 mark**
- Safety limit and physical meaning: **1 mark**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an **electrical stimulator** used as the actuator in a neural prosthesis. Clearly label the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **charge-balance condition** for a biphasic stimulation waveform and explain why charge balancing is mandatory in chronic implants. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Q.A(a)

The electrical stimulator actuator consists of the following functional blocks (matches the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3):

1. **Power Supply (Battery):** A lithium-iodine or rechargeable Li-ion cell providing 2.5 – 3.5 V. Inductively recharged across the skin in modern implants.
2. **Voltage Regulator:** Stabilizes the supply against battery droop and temperature drift.
3. **Digital Control Logic:** A microcontroller or ASIC that generates timing, amplitude, and pulse-width commands. Often includes **safety watchdog timers**.
4. **Current Source / Sink Driver:** Produces the commanded constant current $I$ through a Howland current source or op-amp + transistor topology.
5. **Output Stage (H-Bridge):** Switches polarity to deliver either cathodic or anodic phase without changing lead connections.
6. **Electrode Interface:** The metal–electrolyte boundary where charge transfer occurs. Materials include Pt, Pt-Ir, iridium oxide, and conducting polymers (PEDOT).
7. **Charge Balance Monitor:** Senses the residual voltage on coupling capacitors; if imbalance exceeds a threshold, the safety circuit intervenes.
8. **Safety Shutdown Circuit:** Disables stimulation on detection of fault conditions.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Block diagram: **2 marks**
- Functional description of all 8 blocks: **4 marks (0.5 each)**
- Mention of charge-balance monitor: **1 mark**

---

#### Model Answer for Q.A(b)

**Derivation (5 marks):**
Let $I_c$ be cathodic current amplitude, $PW_c$ its duration, and $I_a$, $PW_a$ the corresponding anodic values.

Charge per cathodic phase:

$$Q_c = I_c \cdot PW_c$$

Charge per anodic phase:

$$Q_a = I_a \cdot PW_a$$

Charge-balance requires net DC charge per cycle = 0:

$$\Delta Q = Q_c - Q_a = 0$$

Substituting:

$$I_c \cdot PW_c - I_a \cdot PW_a = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{I_c \cdot PW_c = I_a \cdot PW_a}$$

**Why charge balancing is mandatory (2 marks):**

If $Q_c \neq Q_a$, a **net DC offset** appears across the electrode–tissue interface. This causes:
1. **Electrolysis of water** — producing $H^+$ and $OH^-$ ions and shifting local pH, killing neurons.
2. **Dissolution of electrode metal** — releasing toxic Pt or Ir ions into tissue.
3. **Growth of fibrous encapsulation** — increasing impedance and raising stimulation thresholds over time.
4. **Generation of toxic reaction products** (e.g., chlorine gas at the counter electrode).

Thus charge balancing is **not optional** — it is a regulatory and clinical prerequisite for any chronically implanted actuator.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **electrode–tissue interface model** for a neural stimulation actuator. Include the equivalent circuit and the role of the **Helmholtz double layer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **charge per phase**, **charge density**, and **current density** for an electrode of area $A = 0.05 \, cm^2$, stimulated with $I = 200 \, \mu A$ for $PW = 300 \, \mu s$. Comment on whether the values are within safe limits. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Q.B(a)

The electrode–tissue interface is modeled by the **Randles equivalent circuit**:

- **$C_{dl}$** — Helmholtz double-layer capacitance, formed by the separation of charges (electrons in metal, ions in electrolyte) at the interface. Typical value: $1 – 10 \, \mu F / cm^2$.
- **$R_{ct}$** — Charge-transfer resistance, representing faradaic (redox) reactions.
- **$R_s$** — Solution (tissue bulk) resistance, typically $200 – 1000 \, \Omega$.
- **$Z_w$** (Warburg impedance) — diffusion-limited impedance, significant at low frequencies.

The **Helmholtz double layer** acts as a **capacitor in series** with the tissue. During a stimulation pulse, charge accumulates on this layer before any faradaic reaction occurs — this is the basis of **reversible (capacitive) stimulation**, which is inherently safe because no electrons actually cross the metal–electrolyte boundary.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Equivalent circuit diagram: **2 marks**
- Identification of $C_{dl}, R_{ct}, R_s, Z_w$: **3 marks (1 each minus penalty)**
- Role of double layer in safe stimulation: **2 marks**

---

#### Model Answer for Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- $I = 200 \, \mu A = 200 \times 10^{-6} \, A$
- $PW = 300 \, \mu s = 300 \times 10^{-6} \, s$
- $A = 0.05 \, cm^2$

**Step 1 — Charge per phase:**

$$Q = I \cdot PW = (200 \times 10^{-6})(300 \times 10^{-6}) = 60000 \times 10^{-12} \, C = 0.06 \, \mu C$$

**Step 2 — Charge density:**

$$D = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.05} = 1.2 \, \mu C / cm^2$$

**Step 3 — Current density:**

$$J = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{0.05} = 4000 \, \mu A / cm^2 = 4 \, mA / cm^2$$

**Step 4 — Shannon's safety parameter:**

$$k = \log_{10}(D \cdot J) = \log_{10}(1.2 \times 4000) = \log_{10}(4800) \approx 3.68$$

**Step 5 — Safety Comment:**
- Charge density $D = 1.2 \, \mu C / cm^2$ is **safe** (limit < 30).
- Current density $J = 4000 \, \mu A / cm^2$ is **above the typical 1000 $\mu A/cm^2$ limit**.
- The combined $k \approx 3.68$ is **well above 1.85** — this stimulation regime is **unsafe for chronic use**.

**Recommendation:** Use a **larger electrode** (e.g., $A = 0.2 \, cm^2$) or **reduce current to 50 $\mu A$**.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct Q calculation: **2 marks**
- Correct D and J calculations: **2 marks**
- Correct Shannon $k$ computation: **2 marks**
- Safety interpretation: **1 mark**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in actuator questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing actuator with sensor.** An actuator is the *output* device (e.g., stimulating electrode). A sensor is the *input* device (e.g., recording microelectrode). Marks are deducted when these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Writing $Q = I \cdot t$ without converting microamperes and microseconds properly — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unit errors.
> 3. **Drawing monophasic waveforms in chronic implant answers.** Monophasic pulses are unsafe; a biphasic charge-balanced waveform is mandatory.
> 4. **Omitting the charge-balance monitor block** in stimulator block diagrams. This is a high-weightage item.
> 5. **Not mentioning Shannon's $k < 1.85$** when discussing safety. This single formula can earn 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Writing "voltage" instead of "current"** in current-controlled stimulator answers — examiners are strict about this distinction.
> 7. **Confusing Faraday's constant value.** Use $F = 96485 \, C/mol$, not $96500$ in some texts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Actuators**
>
> - An **actuator** in a neural prosthesis is the output energy-delivery subsystem, **not** a generic motor.
> - The **three canonical sub-blocks** are: stimulus generator, lead network, and active electrode interface.
> - **Biphasic charge-balanced waveforms** are mandatory for safety; **monophasic** is forbidden chronically.
> - Master the **charge equation** $Q = I \cdot PW$ and its derivatives $D = Q/A$, $J = I/A$.
> - **Shannon's safety limit:** $k = \log(D) + \log(J) < 1.85$.
> - **Charge-balance condition:** $I_c \cdot PW_c = I_a \cdot PW_a$.
> - Know the **electrode–tissue equivalent circuit** (Randles model) and the role of the **Helmholtz double layer**.
> - **Threshold current** $I_{th} = \Delta V_{th} / R_m$; for typical axons, $\sim 10 \, \mu A$.
> - **Strength–duration relationship:** $I_{th} = I_{rh} / (1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m})$.
> - Materials for stimulating electrodes: **Pt, Pt-Ir (90/10), iridium oxide, PEDOT, TiN**.
> - Clinical actuators: **cochlear implant, retinal prosthesis (Argus II), DBS, FES, VNS, sacral stimulator**.
> - **Capacitively-coupled stimulation** is inherently charge-balanced and safest.
> - **Faraday's law of electrolysis:** $m = Q \cdot M / (nF)$ — governs electrode dissolution.
> - Always state **both the clinical use and the safety bound** when describing any actuator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor (Biomedical Definition):** A *sensor* is a transducer element that detects a physical, chemical, or biological stimulus from the human body (or its environment) and converts it into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or impedance change) suitable for processing by an implanted or external prosthetic/electronic system.

In the context of **Neural Prosthesis & Implants**, sensors form the *afferent (input) limb* of the prosthesis — they capture biological information that the implant will ultimately use to:
- **Stimulate** the nervous system (feed-forward sensing), or
- **Modulate** stimulation parameters in real time (closed-loop / feedback sensing), or
- **Restore** a lost sensory modality (sensory substitution — e.g., retinal photodiodes, cochlear microphones).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Classification Hook:** Sensors used in neural implants are classified along three orthogonal axes:
> 1. **Power mode** → *Active* (self-generating, e.g., piezoelectric) vs *Passive* (modulating, e.g., resistive strain gauge).
> 2. **Signal domain** → *Physical* (pressure, temperature, motion) vs *Chemical* (pH, pO₂, glucose, neurotransmitters) vs *Bioelectric* (EMG, ENG, ECoG, LFP).
> 3. **Contact mode** → *Invasive* (intracortical, epimysial) vs *Semi-invasive* (subcutaneous, epicortical) vs *Non-invasive* (surface).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **security alarm system** in a building:
- *Motion detectors, glass-break sensors, and smoke detectors* are all "sensors" — each watches for one specific kind of event.
- When triggered, they produce a standardized signal (an electrical pulse or a voltage change) that the *alarm panel* understands, regardless of what the original event was.

**A biomedical sensor in a neural prosthesis works exactly the same way.** Your body is the building, the biological event (a muscle contraction, a rise in blood glucose, a pressure spike inside the skull, light hitting the retina) is the "event," and the sensor is the specialized detector that translates that event into a clean electrical signal the implant electronics can "read."

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the "3-Question Test" for any neural-implant sensor:**
> 1. *What is the biological input?* (Pressure? Chemical? Electric potential? Light?)
> 2. *What is the transduction principle?* (Piezoelectric? Resistive? Capacitive? Electrochemical? Photoelectric?)
> 3. *What is the output the implant electronics can use?* (Voltage? Current? Frequency? Impedance?)

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold = KTU High-Yield)

Any sensor used in a neural implant is graded on these standard metrics — **examiners love them**:

- **Sensitivity (S):** Output change per unit input change — typically *mV/mmHg*, *mV/°C*, *nA/ppm*.
- **Selectivity:** Ability to respond *only* to the target stimulus in the presence of interferents.
- **Linearity:** How closely the output follows a straight line vs input (quantified by *R²*).
- **Dynamic Range:** Ratio between the largest and smallest detectable input.
- **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change in input.
- **Response Time / Time Constant (τ):** Speed of the sensor — *critical* for closed-loop DBS and retinal implants (must be < *10 ms*).
- **Drift:** Unwanted change in output when the input is held constant (sensor *aging*).
- **Hysteresis:** Difference in output for the same input reached from opposite directions.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** *S/N* ratio, often reported in *dB*.
- **Biocompatibility:** Must not provoke toxic/immune response (ISO 10993).
- **Long-term Stability:** Crucial for implants expected to function *> 10 years*.

## 1.4 Standard Physical / Engineering Constants

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Body core temperature | T_core | **37 °C** (310.15 K) | Thermal sensor reference |
| Normal intracranial pressure | ICP | **7–15 mmHg** | Pressure sensor range |
| Normal intraocular pressure | IOP | **10–21 mmHg** | Glaucoma sensor range |
| Blood pH (arterial) | pH | **7.35–7.45** | Chemical sensor range |
| Arterial pO₂ | pO₂ | **80–100 mmHg** | Oxygen sensor range |
| Blood glucose (normal) | – | **70–110 mg/dL** | Glucose sensor range |
| ECG amplitude (surface) | – | **0.5–5 mV** | Biopotential reference |
| EEG amplitude | – | **10–100 µV** | Biopotential reference |
| ENG (nerve) amplitude | – | **1–50 µV** | Neural signal reference |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic Sensor Model — The Transfer Function

Every sensor, regardless of physics, is mathematically modelled as:

$$y(t) = f\bigl(x(t)\bigr)$$

Where:
- $x(t)$ = the input measurand (stimulus).
- $y(t)$ = the electrical output.
- $f(\cdot)$ = the **transfer function** determined by the transduction principle.

For a *linear* sensor (the assumption KTU problems always use unless told otherwise):

$$y(t) = S \cdot x(t) + y_0$$

- $S$ = static **sensitivity** (slope of the calibration line).
- $y_0$ = output offset (often set to zero by calibration).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** If a KTU problem gives you two calibration points, you solve for $S$ and $y_0$ from a *two-point straight line*. This is the single most common 3-mark trick on this module.

## 2.2 Static Characteristics of a Sensor

| Characteristic | Definition | KTU Typical Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Closeness of output to *true* value | Compare to a calibrated standard |
| **Precision** | Repeatability of the output | Standard deviation over N readings |
| **Sensitivity** $S$ | $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Slope of best-fit line |
| **Linearity** | Maximum deviation from a straight line | Nonlinearity $\%NL = \dfrac{\vert y_{actual}-y_{ideal} \vert_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ |
| **Hysteresis** | Output difference between ascending & descending input | $H\% = \dfrac{\vert y_{asc}-y_{desc} \vert_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ |
| **Drift** | Slow change in output with time at constant input | mV/hour or %FS/hour |
| **Dead zone** | Range of input that produces no output | Threshold behaviour |
| **Saturation** | Maximum output the sensor can produce | Clipping |

## 2.3 Dynamic Characteristics — First-Order Sensor Model

Most biomedical sensors are well-modelled as **first-order systems** with transfer function:

$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

The **step response** is:

$$y(t) = K \cdot x_0 \bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$$

Where:
- $K$ = static sensitivity (DC gain).
- $\tau$ = **time constant** — the time to reach *63.2 %* of the final value.
- For a rising exponential, the sensor reaches *95 %* of final value at $t = 3\tau$, and *98.2 %* at $t = 4\tau$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl — 3τ Rule:** In a KTU numerical, if the question states "the sensor reaches 95 % of final value," assume $t = 3\tau$ and back-solve for $\tau$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Memorize This Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical table — note the use of `\vert` instead of the pipe character `\|` to keep markdown tables safe.**

| # | Sensor / Principle | Governing Equation | Output Quantity | Typical Use in Neural Prosthesis |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wheatstone bridge (general)** | $V_o = V_{ex}\!\left[\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}-\dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right]$ | Voltage | Strain gauge, RTD, force sensors |
| 2 | **Quarter bridge (1 active arm)** | $V_o \approx V_{ex}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$ | Voltage | Single-element resistive sensors |
| 3 | **Full bridge (4 active arms)** | $V_o = V_{ex}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Voltage | Maximises sensitivity ×4 |
| 4 | **Piezoelectric sensor (charge mode)** | $Q = d_{33}\cdot F$ | Charge (C) | Accelerometer, vibration, cochlear mic |
| 5 | **Piezoelectric sensor (voltage mode)** | $V_o = \dfrac{Q}{C_p} = \dfrac{d_{33}\cdot F}{C_p}$ | Voltage | Same, after charge amp |
| 6 | **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)** | $R(T) = R_0\,[1 + \alpha(T-T_0)]$ | Resistance | Implant thermometry, hyperthermia |
| 7 | **Thermistor (NTC, β-model)** | $R(T) = R_0\cdot\exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}-\dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | Resistance | High-sensitivity temp sensing |
| 8 | **Thermocouple (Seebeck)** | $V = S_{AB}\,(T_h - T_c)$ | Voltage | Small, passive temp probes |
| 9 | **Strain gauge (piezoresistive)** | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f\cdot\varepsilon$ | Resistance | Bone-strain, intracranial pressure |
| 10 | **Capacitive sensor** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r A}{d} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = -\dfrac{\Delta d}{d}$ | Capacitance | Pressure, displacement |
| 11 | **ISFET / ChemFET** | $V_{th} = V_{th,0} - \alpha\cdot pH$ | Threshold voltage | pH, ion sensing in vivo |
| 12 | **Clark oxygen electrode** | $I = n\,F\,A\,J_{O_2}$ | Current | Cerebral oxygen monitoring |
| 13 | **Amperometric glucose sensor** | $I = n\,F\,A\,k\cdot C_{glucose}$ | Current | Continuous glucose monitor (CGM) |
| 14 | **Generic biosensor response** | $\text{Signal} = S\cdot C + b$ | Concentration | Neurotransmitter, lactate |
| 15 | **SNR (dB form)** | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | Sensor front-end design |

**Legend of symbols (for quick recall):**
- $V_{ex}$ = bridge excitation voltage.
- $R, R_0$ = nominal resistance; $\Delta R$ = change.
- $d_{33}$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient (*pC/N*).
- $C_p$ = piezoelectric element + cable capacitance.
- $\alpha$ = RTD temperature coefficient of resistance (*≈ 0.00385 /°C* for platinum).
- $\beta$ = thermistor material constant (*typically 3000–5000 K*).
- $G_f$ = gauge factor (*≈ 2.0* for metal, *100–200* for semiconductor).
- $\varepsilon$ = mechanical strain.
- $S_{AB}$ = Seebeck coefficient (*µV/°C*).
- $n$ = electrons per reaction; $F$ = Faraday constant (*96485 C/mol*).
- $J_{O_2}$ = oxygen flux through membrane.

## 2.5 Classification of Sensors (Exam-Ready Tree)

**A. By Energy / Power Mode**
- **Active sensors** → self-generating: *piezoelectric, thermoelectric, photovoltaic, bioelectric*. Need **no** external power to produce a signal.
- **Passive sensors** → modulating: *resistive, capacitive, inductive*. Require **excitation** (bridge voltage, AC carrier).

**B. By Signal Domain**
- *Physical:* pressure, temperature, force, displacement, acceleration.
- *Chemical:* pH, pO₂, pCO₂, glucose, ions, neurotransmitters.
- *Bioelectric:* EMG, EEG, ECG, ENG, ECoG, LFP, single-unit spikes.

**C. By Output Electrical Form**
- *Resistive → Wheatstone bridge.*
- *Capacitive → AC bridge / charge amplifier.*
- *Inductive → LVDT, eddy current.*
- *Voltage-generating → direct amplification.*
- *Current-generating → transimpedance amplifier.*
- *Frequency-generating → direct digital counting.*

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Neural Prosthetic Systems

| Subsystem | Sensors Deployed | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Cochlear implant** | Microphone (acoustic), accelerometer (vibration mode) | Convert sound / bone vibration to electrical stimulation pattern |
| **Retinal prosthesis (epiretinal / subretinal)** | External CMOS camera, photodiodes (subretinal) | Replace photoreceptor function |
| **DBS — closed loop** | Local field potential (LFP) sensing electrodes | Detect beta-band power → adapt stimulation |
| **Bionic limb (myoelectric)** | Surface / implantable EMG electrodes | Intent recognition from muscle activity |
| **Cortical visual prosthesis** | Utah-array microelectrodes (recording) + camera (input) | Capture and write to visual cortex |
| **Intracranial pressure monitor** | Strain-gauge or capacitive pressure sensor | Hydrocephalus, TBI monitoring |
| **Continuous glucose monitor** | Amperometric enzymatic electrode | Diabetes management |
| **Vestibular prosthesis** | Gyroscope / IMU | Restore balance via head-motion sensing |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition below is shown explicitly. **No step is skipped.** Multi-line equations are wrapped in a single `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` block with a blank line before and after the `$$` fences.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Voltage of a Wheatstone Bridge

A Wheatstone bridge is the **universal readout circuit** for passive resistive sensors. It is therefore the most-asked derivation on this module.

**Step 1 — Define the bridge topology.**
Four resistors form a diamond. A DC excitation $V_{ex}$ is applied between the top and bottom nodes (a–c). The output is taken between the two side nodes (b–d).

```
        R1
   a ----/\/\---- b
   |              |
Vex|              | Vo
   |              |
   c ----/\/\---- d
        R3
   (R2 connects a–d; R4 connects b–c)
```

**Step 2 — Express each side as a voltage divider.**
The voltage at node **b** (relative to ground at c) is set by the divider $R_1$–$R_2$:

$$V_b = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The voltage at node **d** is set by the divider $R_3$–$R_4$:

$$V_d = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 3 — The differential output voltage is the difference.**

$$V_o = V_b - V_d = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} \right]$$

This is the **general bridge equation** — formula (1) of the cheat sheet.

**Step 4 — Special case: balanced bridge at $V_o = 0$.**
For balance, the two divider ratios must be equal:

$$\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} \;\Longrightarrow\; R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$$

This is the classic *null-balance condition* used in Wheatstone-bridge instruments.

**Step 5 — Special case: quarter-bridge (single active arm).**
Let $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ and $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ (only one resistor changes). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R+\Delta R}{R+\Delta R + R} - \frac{R}{R+R} \right] \\[4pt]
    &= V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R+\Delta R}{2R+\Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right] \\[4pt]
    &= V_{ex} \left[ \frac{2(R+\Delta R) - (2R+\Delta R)}{2(2R+\Delta R)} \right] \\[4pt]
    &= V_{ex} \left[ \frac{2R + 2\Delta R - 2R - \Delta R}{2(2R+\Delta R)} \right] \\[4pt]
    &= V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R+\Delta R)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the small-signal approximation $\Delta R \ll R$.**
Then $2R + \Delta R \approx 2R$, giving the textbook form:

$$\boxed{\,V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}\,}$$

This is **formula (2)** of the cheat sheet.

**Step 7 — Sensitivity.**
The bridge sensitivity is:

$$S_{bridge} = \frac{\partial V_o}{\partial R_1}\bigg\vert_{R_1=R} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{(R_1+R_2)^2}\bigg\vert_{R_1=R_2=R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4R}$$

So the *quarter-bridge sensitivity* is $V_{ex}/(4R)$. Doubling to half-bridge (2 active arms) gives $V_{ex}/(2R)$, and full-bridge (4 active arms) gives $V_{ex}/R$ — a **4× gain** over quarter bridge.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output of a Piezoelectric Pressure / Force Sensor

Piezoelectric sensors are *active* — they generate charge directly when mechanically stressed. They are heavily used in accelerometers and in some cochlear-implant microphones.

**Step 1 — Direct piezoelectric effect (charge generation).**
For a force $F$ applied along the polarization axis (the "3" direction) of a piezoelectric element:

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F$$

Where:
- $Q$ = generated charge (Coulombs).
- $d_{33}$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N, typically 100–500 pC/N for PZT).
- $F$ = applied force (N).

**Step 2 — Output voltage when connected to a capacitive load.**
The piezo element itself has a capacitance $C_p$, and the connecting cable adds $C_c$. The total is $C = C_p + C_c$. The open-circuit voltage is:

$$V_o = \frac{Q}{C_p + C_c} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_p + C_c}$$

**Step 3 — If a charge amplifier is used (standard practice in implants).**
A charge amplifier with feedback capacitor $C_f$ and op-amp gives an output voltage that is **independent of cable capacitance** (because the op-amp virtual ground holds the piezo terminal at 0 V):

$$V_{out,ch} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d_{33}\cdot F}{C_f}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A follow-up question often asks *"Why is a charge amplifier preferred over a voltage amplifier for piezoelectric sensors in implants?"* — answer: cable capacitance noise dominates a voltage-mode amp, but a charge amplifier rejects it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RTD Temperature-to-Resistance Conversion

A platinum RTD follows a near-linear relationship:

$$R(T) = R_0\,[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$$

Where:
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (usually 0 °C or 25 °C).
- $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient of resistance, for platinum **$\alpha \approx 0.00385 \; /°\text{C}$** (the *European curve* / IEC 60751 standard).
- $T, T_0$ in °C (or K — the slope is the same).

**Step 1 — Express the change in resistance.**

$$\Delta R = R(T) - R_0 = \alpha R_0 (T - T_0)$$

**Step 2 — Plug into the quarter-bridge formula.**
Using $V_o \approx V_{ex}\,\Delta R / (4R)$ and $R \approx R_0$ for small $\Delta R$:

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4R_0}\cdot \alpha R_0 (T - T_0) = \frac{\alpha V_{ex}}{4}(T - T_0)$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity of the RTD-in-bridge readout.**

$$\boxed{\,S_{RTD,bridge} = \frac{\partial V_o}{\partial T} = \frac{\alpha V_{ex}}{4}\,}$$

For a typical $V_{ex} = 5$ V and $\alpha = 0.00385$:

$$S_{RTD,bridge} \approx \frac{0.00385 \times 5}{4} \approx 4.81 \; \text{mV/°C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example the KTU examiner may adapt:**
> *A PT100 RTD is connected in a quarter-bridge with $V_{ex} = 5$ V. Find the bridge output at $T = 50$ °C, given $R_0 = 100 \; \Omega$ at 0 °C.*
> *Answer:* $\Delta R = 0.00385 \times 100 \times 50 = 19.25 \; \Omega$. Therefore $V_o \approx 5 \times 19.25 / 400 \approx 0.241$ V = **241 mV**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capacitive Pressure Sensor Sensitivity

For a parallel-plate capacitive sensor with plate area $A$, gap $d$, and dielectric permittivity $\varepsilon$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon A}{d}$$

**Step 1 — Sensitivity to gap change.**
Differentiate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC}{dd} &= -\frac{\varepsilon A}{d^2} = -\frac{C}{d} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad \frac{\Delta C}{C} &= -\frac{\Delta d}{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — For a pressure sensor where gap deformation is $\Delta d = k \cdot P$** (with $k$ = diaphragm compliance, m/Pa):

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C} = -\frac{k \cdot P}{d} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad S_{cap} = \left\vert \frac{\partial C}{\partial P} \right\vert = \frac{C \cdot k}{d}$$

This is the **basis of capacitive intracranial and intraocular pressure sensors**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Sensor for an Implant

| Application | Stimulus | Best Sensor Choice | Transduction | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intracranial pressure | Mechanical (CSF pressure) | Strain-gauge bridge or capacitive | Piezoresistive / capacitive | Linear, low drift, biocompatible encapsulation |
| Body-core temperature (hyperthermia implant) | Thermal | RTD (Pt) or thermistor | Resistive | High accuracy, stable over years |
| Cortical neural recording | Bioelectric | Utah-array microelectrode | Bioelectric (Faradaic) | High SNR, single-unit resolution |
| Cochlear implant (vibration mode) | Acoustic/mechanical | Piezoelectric accelerometer | Piezoelectric | Wide bandwidth, low power |
| Retinal prosthesis (subretinal) | Photonic | Silicon photodiode | Photovoltaic | No external power, micro-scale |
| Continuous glucose monitor | Chemical | Amperometric enzyme electrode | Electrochemical | Specific to glucose via GOX enzyme |
| Vestibular prosthesis | Angular velocity | MEMS gyroscope | Coriolis (capacitive) | Small, low-power, 3-axis |
| Blood pH (intracranial) | Chemical | ISFET | Field-effect | Miniaturized, solid-state |
| Myoelectric hand | Bioelectric | Epimysial / implantable EMG electrode | Bioelectric | High selectivity, chronic stability |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse *bioelectric sensors* (which *measure* neural/muscular potentials) with *stimulation electrodes* (which *deliver* current). In KTU questions, a "sensor electrode" always **records**; a "stimulating electrode" always **injects**. The same physical hardware is sometimes used in both modes (e.g., DBS leads), so read the question stem carefully.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 4.1** renders the *block-level functional architecture* of a sensor front-end in a closed-loop neural implant. **Diagram 4.2** is the sensor classification tree. All node IDs follow the Mermaid safe-identifier rule (alphanumeric, letter-prefixed, no reserved keywords).

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Sensor Front-End in a Closed-Loop Neural Prosthesis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Biological Stimulus]:::bio --> inputB[Sensor Element]:::sens
    inputB --> inputC[Signal Conditioning]:::cond
    inputC --> inputD[Analog to Digital Converter]:::adc
    inputD --> inputE[Digital Signal Processor]:::dsp
    inputE --> inputF{Decision Logic}:::logic
    inputF -- Above Threshold --> inputG[Stimulus Generator]:::stim
    inputF -- Below Threshold --> inputH[HOLD State]:::hold
    inputG --> inputI[Stimulating Electrode]:::elec
    inputI --> inputJ[Neural Tissue]:::tissue
    inputJ -. Feedback Path .-> inputA

    classDef bio fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef sens fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F
    classDef cond fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D
    classDef adc fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#4338CA,color:#312E81
    classDef dsp fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#831843
    classDef logic fill:#FFE4E6,stroke:#BE123C,color:#881337
    classDef stim fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6B21A8,color:#581C87
    classDef hold fill:#F1F5F9,stroke:#475569,color:#1E293B
    classDef elec fill:#CFFAFE,stroke:#0E7490,color:#164E63
    classDef tissue fill:#FCE7E7,stroke:#9F1239,color:#7F1D1D
```

**Read-out:** The biological stimulus (e.g., local field potential in a DBS patient, glucose concentration in interstitial fluid, or pressure in the cranium) is captured by the sensor element, amplified and filtered, digitised, processed in firmware, and used by a decision block to decide *whether* and *how strongly* to deliver a stimulation pulse. The dashed feedback arrow emphasises the **closed-loop** nature — the implanted electrode both stimulates the tissue and (in some architectures) senses the resulting neural response.

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Biomedical Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Sensors]:::root --> rootB[By Energy Mode]:::mode
    rootA --> rootC[By Signal Domain]:::dom
    rootA --> rootD[By Contact Mode]:::cont

    rootB --> modeA[Active self generating]:::act
    rootB --> modeB[Passive modulating]:::pas

    modeA --> modeA1[Piezoelectric]:::act
    modeA --> modeA2[Thermoelectric]:::act
    modeA --> modeA3[Photovoltaic]:::act
    modeA --> modeA4[Bioelectric electrode]:::act

    modeB --> modeB1[Resistive bridge]:::pas
    modeB --> modeB2[Capacitive bridge]:::pas
    modeB --> modeB3[Inductive LVDT]:::pas

    rootC --> domA[Physical]:::domn
    rootC --> domB[Chemical]:::domn
    rootC --> domC[Bioelectric]:::domn

    domA --> domA1[Pressure strain]:::domn
    domA --> domA2[Temperature]:::domn
    domA --> domA3[Acceleration motion]:::domn
    domA --> domA4[Light]:::domn

    domB --> domB1[pH ISFET]:::domn
    domB --> domB2[pO2 Clark electrode]:::domn
    domB --> domB3[Glucose amperometric]:::domn
    domB --> domB4[Neurotransmitter]:::domn

    domC --> domC1[EEG]:::domn
    domC --> domC2[EMG]:::domn
    domC --> domC3[ENG ECoG]:::domn
    domC --> domC4[Single unit spikes]:::domn

    rootD --> contA[Invasive]:::contn
    rootD --> contB[Semi invasive]:::contn
    rootD --> contC[Non invasive]:::contn

    classDef root fill:#0F172A,stroke:#0F172A,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef mode fill:#1E293B,stroke:#0F172A,color:#F1F5F9
    classDef act fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F
    classDef pas fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D
    classDef dom fill:#581C87,stroke:#581C87,color:#F5F3FF
    classDef cont fill:#7F1D1D,stroke:#7F1D1D,color:#FEE2E2
    classDef domn fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6B21A8,color:#581C87
    classDef contn fill:#FFE4E6,stroke:#BE123C,color:#881337
```

## 4.3 Sensor Placement Map in a Representative Neural Prosthesis (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bioenv[Human Body]:::body --> s1[ICP Pressure Sensor]:::sens
    bioenv --> s2[LFP Sensing Electrode]:::sens
    bioenv --> s3[Glucose Sensor]:::sens
    bioenv --> s4[Accelerometer Gyro]:::sens
    bioenv --> s5[EMG Electrode]:::sens

    s1 --> mcu1[Implant MCU]:::mcu
    s2 --> mcu1
    s3 --> mcu1
    s4 --> mcu1
    s5 --> mcu1

    mcu1 --> tx1[Wireless Telemetry]:::tx
    mcu1 --> stim1[Stim Driver]:::stim
    stim1 --> elec1[Stimulating Electrode]:::elec
    elec1 -. Feedback .-> s2

    classDef body fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#7F1D1D,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef sens fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F
    classDef mcu fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef tx fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D
    classDef stim fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6B21A8,color:#581C87
    classDef elec fill:#CFFAFE,stroke:#0E7490,color:#164E63
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing sensor and transducer:** A *sensor* detects, a *transducer* converts energy forms. They are not always the same — a piezoelectric crystal is a sensor for force but a transducer for mechanical→electrical energy.
> 2. **Mixing up active vs passive:** Piezoelectric is *active* (self-generating); thermistor and strain gauge are *passive* (need excitation). A surprising number of students mark these wrong in 1-mark MCQs.
> 3. **Forgetting the small-signal approximation in bridge analysis:** Without $\Delta R \ll R$, the answer will be visibly wrong. Always state the assumption.
> 4. **Wrong units in RTD problems:** The temperature coefficient $\alpha$ is in *per °C* (or *per K*) — do **not** write just "/°C" in the final answer line.
> 5. **Confusing "sensitivity" of a sensor with "sensitivity" of a bridge readout circuit:** The sensor $S$ is in mV/unit; the bridge readout gain is $V_{ex}/(4R)$. The overall system sensitivity is the product.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 (Model)]

**Define a biomedical sensor. List the four most important static characteristics used to specify a sensor for an implant application.**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

A *biomedical sensor* is a device that detects a biological measurand (physical, chemical, or electrical) and converts it into a proportional electrical signal for measurement or prosthesis control. In implants, the four most important static characteristics are:
1. **Sensitivity** — output change per unit input change (mV/mmHg, mV/°C, nA/mM, etc.).
2. **Selectivity** — discrimination against interfering species/signals.
3. **Linearity** — closeness of the calibration curve to a straight line (reported as %NL).
4. **Drift / long-term stability** — slow change of output at constant input, critical for implants that must operate for *years* without recalibration.

Other secondary characteristics: accuracy, precision, hysteresis, resolution, dynamic range.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]

**With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Wheatstone bridge used for the readout of a resistive biosensor. Derive the balance condition.**

**Model Answer (≈ 130 words + diagram):**

A Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ in a diamond configuration. A DC excitation $V_{ex}$ is applied between the top node *a* and bottom node *c*. The differential output is taken between nodes *b* and *d*.

From the voltage-divider rule:

$$V_b = V_{ex} \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}, \qquad V_d = V_{ex} \frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}$$

The output voltage is:

$$V_o = V_b - V_d = V_{ex}\!\left[\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}-\frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right]$$

For **balance** ($V_o = 0$):

$$\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}=\frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3}$$

A small change $\Delta R$ in one arm (e.g., a strain gauge in $R_1$) produces a small output $V_o \approx V_{ex}\,\Delta R/(4R)$, which is linearly proportional to the stimulus.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Note for students:** KTU Module-Internal-Choice means: in the exam, you will be given **both** Question A and Question B; you must answer **exactly one** of them. The two questions below are mutually independent, each broken into 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 (Model)]

**(a)** *With the help of a circuit diagram, derive the output voltage expression of a Wheatstone bridge. From this, obtain the small-signal expression for a quarter-bridge configuration where only one resistor changes by $\Delta R$. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *A platinum RTD (PT100) is connected in a quarter-bridge circuit with excitation $V_{ex} = 5$ V. The nominal resistance is $R_0 = 100 \; \Omega$ at $T_0 = 0$ °C, and the temperature coefficient is $\alpha = 0.00385 /°\text{C}$. Calculate:*
  *(i) the bridge output voltage at $T = 60$ °C;*
  *(ii) the sensitivity in mV/°C;*
  *(iii) the temperature when the bridge output reads 0.150 V. **(7 marks)***

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

A Wheatstone bridge has four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$. With excitation $V_{ex}$ across nodes a (top) and c (bottom), the output is taken between b and d.

By the voltage divider rule:

$$V_b = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}, \qquad V_d = V_{ex}\cdot\frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}$$

Differential output:

$$V_o = V_b - V_d = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}-\frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right] \quad \text{...(1)}$$

For a quarter-bridge, let $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ and $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= V_{ex}\!\left[\frac{R+\Delta R}{R+\Delta R + R} - \frac{R}{R+R}\right] \\[4pt]
    &= V_{ex}\!\left[\frac{R+\Delta R}{2R+\Delta R} - \frac{1}{2}\right] \\[4pt]
    &= V_{ex}\!\left[\frac{2(R+\Delta R) - (2R+\Delta R)}{2(2R+\Delta R)}\right] \\[4pt]
    &= V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R+\Delta R)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the small-signal regime $\Delta R \ll R$:

$$\boxed{V_o \approx V_{ex}\cdot\frac{\Delta R}{4R}} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Mark split:** Circuit diagram — 2 marks. General bridge equation — 2 marks. Quarter-bridge substitution + simplification — 2 marks. Final small-signal result + assumption stated — 1 mark.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $V_{ex} = 5$ V, $R_0 = 100 \; \Omega$, $T_0 = 0$ °C, $\alpha = 0.00385 /°\text{C}$.

**(i) Output at $T = 60$ °C (3 marks):**

Resistance at 60 °C:

$$R(60) = R_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)] = 100\,[1 + 0.00385 \times 60]$$

Compute step by step:

$$0.00385 \times 60 = 0.231$$

$$R(60) = 100 \times (1 + 0.231) = 100 \times 1.231 = 123.1 \; \Omega$$

Therefore $\Delta R = 23.1 \; \Omega$. Substituting into the quarter-bridge formula:

$$V_o = 5 \times \frac{23.1}{4 \times 100} = 5 \times \frac{23.1}{400} = \frac{115.5}{400}$$

$$\boxed{V_o = 0.2888 \; \text{V} \approx 288.8 \; \text{mV}}$$

**(ii) Sensitivity in mV/°C (2 marks):**

From the derivation in Section 3.3:

$$S_{RTD,bridge} = \frac{\alpha\, V_{ex}}{4} = \frac{0.00385 \times 5}{4} = \frac{0.01925}{4}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.004813 \; \text{V/°C} = 4.81 \; \text{mV/°C}}$$

**(iii) Temperature for $V_o = 0.150$ V (2 marks):**

Rearranging the bridge formula $V_o = V_{ex}\,\alpha(T-T_0)/4$:

$$T - T_0 = \frac{4 V_o}{\alpha\, V_{ex}} = \frac{4 \times 0.150}{0.00385 \times 5}$$

$$T - T_0 = \frac{0.600}{0.01925} \approx 31.17 \; °\text{C}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 31.17 \; °\text{C}}$$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]

**(a)** *Explain the principle of operation of a piezoelectric sensor. Derive the expression for the output voltage when it is connected to a charge amplifier. Why is a charge amplifier preferred over a voltage amplifier in implantable applications? **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *A MEMS accelerometer based on a piezoelectric element is to be used as the motion sensor in a vestibular prosthesis. The piezoelectric coefficient is $d_{33} = 250$ pC/N, the proof-mass sensitivity is $0.5 \; \mu\text{g}$ per $\mu\text{m}$ displacement, the static capacitance of the piezo element is $C_p = 1.2$ nF, and the cable capacitance is $C_c = 0.3$ nF. A charge amplifier with feedback capacitor $C_f = 100$ pF is used. Find:*
  *(i) the charge generated per g of acceleration;*
  *(ii) the output voltage of the charge amplifier per g;*
  *(iii) the open-circuit voltage that would have appeared without the charge amplifier (cable connected). **(7 marks)***

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Principle:** Certain crystals (PZT, quartz, PVDF, BaTiO₃) are *non-centrosymmetric*. When mechanically stressed, the positive and negative charge centres separate, producing a net surface charge — this is the **direct piezoelectric effect**.

For a force $F$ applied along the polarisation axis (the "3" axis):

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Where $d_{33}$ is the *longitudinal piezoelectric charge coefficient* in C/N.

**Charge amplifier derivation:** The charge amplifier is an op-amp circuit with the piezo element connected between the inverting input and ground, and a feedback capacitor $C_f$ from output to inverting input. The non-inverting input is grounded.

Because the op-amp input draws no current, all charge from the piezo flows into $C_f$:

$$Q = C_f \cdot (V_{in}^+ - V_{out}) = C_f (0 - V_{out})$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d_{33}\cdot F}{C_f}} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Why charge amplifier over voltage amplifier (2 marks):**
1. The output is **independent of cable capacitance** $C_c$ — important because $C_c$ varies with cable flexure in vivo, causing huge drift in voltage-mode readouts.
2. Better **noise immunity** — the op-amp virtual ground minimises the input noise contribution.
3. **Lower output impedance** — easier to drive subsequent ADC stages.

**Mark split:** Principle explanation — 2 marks. Charge equation — 1 mark. Charge amplifier derivation — 2 marks. Three advantages — 2 marks.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $d_{33} = 250$ pC/N, $C_p = 1.2$ nF, $C_c = 0.3$ nF, $C_f = 100$ pF.

**(i) Charge generated per g (2 marks):**

Force on the proof mass under 1 g acceleration ($g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$): if the effective proof mass is $m$, then $F = m \cdot g$. The problem does not give $m$ explicitly; however, in the standard textbook formulation we directly use the *force sensitivity* of the piezo. Treating "1 g" as the unit force-equivalent in this problem (the problem is conceptually simplified):
