## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — End-to-End EMG Reference Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete functional architecture of a myoelectric reference control system, from physiological intent to mechanical actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BRAIN Motor Cortex] --> B[Descending Corticospinal Tract]
    B --> C[Residual Skeletal Muscle]
    C --> D[Surface EMG Electrodes Ag/AgCl Bipolar]
    D --> E[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain approx 1000]
    E --> F[Band Pass Filter 20 Hz to 500 Hz]
    F --> G[Notch Filter 50 Hz Mains Rejection]
    G --> H[Full Wave Rectifier Absolute Value]
    H --> I[Low Pass Smoother 4th Order Butterworth fc 5 Hz]
    I --> J[Feature Extraction MAV RMS WL ZC SSC]
    J --> K{Control Strategy}
    K --> L1[Threshold On or Off]
    K --> L2[Proportional Linear]
    K --> L3[Classifier LDA SVM ANN]
    L1 --> M[Actuator Driver PWM H Bridge]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    M --> N[Prosthetic Actuator Motor or FES]
    N --> O[Mechanical Output Hand Grip Wrist Rotation]
    O --> P[Sensory Feedback Visual or Vibrotactile]
    P --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Pattern Recognition Training vs. Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TR[TRAINING PHASE OFFLINE]
        T1[Recorded EMG Database Multiple Gestures] --> T2[Sliding Window Segmentation 200 ms]
        T2 --> T3[Feature Vector F per Window]
        T3 --> T4[Labeled Training Set F and Class C]
        T4 --> T5[Classifier Training LDA Weights W and b]
    end
    subgraph IN[INFERENCE PHASE REAL TIME]
        I1[Live EMG Stream] --> I2[Same Windowing and Feature Extraction]
        I2 --> I3[Trained LDA Forward Pass]
        I3 --> I4[Predicted Class Label]
        I4 --> I5[Command Decoder Maps Class to Actuator]
    end
    T5 -. weights transferred .-> I3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Feedback Loop and Error Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Signal rEMG Envelope] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    SUM --> CTRL[P Controller Gain Kp]
    CTRL --> PLANT[Plant Actuator and Load]
    PLANT --> Y[Actual Output yActual]
    Y --> SUM
    Y --> FB[Sensory Feedback Channel to Patient]
    FB --> R
    SUM -- error eTrack rEMG minus yActual --> CTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above are *functional architecture maps*, not anatomical drawings. In KTU 14-mark answers, a hand-drawn equivalent block diagram (boxes + arrows, no detail) typically earns **3–4 marks** by itself — never skip it.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define EMG. List any four time-domain features used in EMG-based prosthetic control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
EMG (Electromyography) is the **recording of the electrical activity produced by skeletal muscles during contraction**. It is the bioelectric manifestation of the summed Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs) detected via surface or intramuscular electrodes.
Four time-domain features are:
1. **MAV** – Mean Absolute Value of the signal in a window.
2. **RMS** – Root Mean Square, representing signal power.
3. **WL** – Waveform Length, the cumulative sum of absolute sample-to-sample differences.
4. **ZC** – Zero Crossings, the number of times the signal crosses zero (frequency proxy).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of an instrumentation amplifier in EMG signal conditioning? State its typical gain and CMRR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The instrumentation amplifier performs **differential amplification** of the small differential EMG voltage (µV range) measured between two surface electrodes, while rejecting the large common-mode voltage (mains interference, body potential).
- **Typical Gain:** **1000 (≈ 60 dB)**.
- **CMRR:** **> 80 dB** (clinical systems aim for 100–110 dB).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an EMG-based reference control system for a myoelectric prosthetic hand. Discuss the various signal-processing stages and the control strategies used.**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram & Signal Processing Stages (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**
A myoelectric prosthetic hand is a powered device in which the patient's residual muscle EMG provides the *reference command* for actuation. The block diagram (see Section 4.1) consists of:

1. **Acquisition Stage:** A pair of Ag/AgCl surface electrodes is placed on the agonist–antagonist muscle pair of the forearm (e.g., flexor carpi radialis and extensor carpi radialis). A reference electrode is placed on a bony prominence (olecranon).

2. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Provides differential gain ≈ 1000 with CMRR ≥ 100 dB. Converts the µV-level EMG to a mV-level signal.

3. **Band-Pass Filter (20–500 Hz):** Removes DC offset and high-frequency noise outside the EMG band. A 50 Hz notch filter is added for India/Europe mains.

4. **Rectifier:** Full-wave rectification ($x_{rect} = \vert x \vert$) makes the bipolar signal unipolar.

5. **Smoother / Envelope Detector:** A 4th-order Butterworth LPF with cutoff 3–8 Hz produces the **linear envelope** $e(t) \propto$ muscle force.

6. **Feature Extraction:** A sliding window of 100–300 ms extracts MAV, RMS, WL, etc., reducing the data rate to a few features per window.

7. **Controller:** Maps features to actuator commands (see Part b).

8. **Actuator:** A DC motor or servo drives the prosthetic fingers via tendons or linkages.

#### Part (b) — Control Strategies (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**
The three classical control strategies are:

1. **Threshold (On/Off) Control:** A single EMG channel drives a single DOF. When the envelope exceeds a preset threshold $\tau$, the actuator is ON at fixed speed; otherwise OFF. Simple, robust, but no proportional gradation.

2. **Proportional Control:** The actuator speed/torque is linearly proportional to the envelope:
$$v_{actuator}(t) = K \cdot e(t)$$
where $K$ is calibrated per patient via the MVC normalization. Provides intuitive grip-force modulation. Limited to one DOF per muscle site.

3. **Pattern Recognition Control:** With 4–8 EMG channels and a sliding feature window, a classifier (LDA, SVM, or ANN) is trained to recognize $K$ discrete gestures. The predicted class is decoded into a multi-DOF command. This is the current clinical standard for multi-function hands (e.g., 6 grip patterns).

4. **Regression-Based Control (Advanced):** A continuous mapping $y = f(F)$ allows simultaneous proportional control of multiple DOFs — the emerging state of the art.

**Comparator Table:**

| Strategy | DOF | Intuitiveness | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Threshold | 1 | Low | Negligible |
| Proportional | 1 | Medium | Low |
| Pattern Recognition | 5–10 | High | Medium |
| Regression | Continuous | Very High | Medium–High |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Explain in detail the feature-extraction and pattern-recognition techniques used in EMG reference control. Discuss the LDA classifier with its mathematical formulation.**

#### Part (a) — Feature Extraction (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**
Feature extraction condenses a window of $N$ EMG samples $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\}$ into a compact vector $\mathbf{F} = [f_1, f_2, \dots, f_M]^{T}$ that is sensitive to the user's intent and robust to noise.

**Time-Domain Features (most common clinically):**

1. **MAV:** $\quad MAV = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert x_i \vert$

2. **RMS:** $\quad RMS = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}x_i^{2}}$

3. **WL:** $\quad WL = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1}\vert x_{i+1} - x_i \vert$

4. **ZC:** $\quad ZC = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1}[\text{sgn}(-x_i \cdot x_{i+1}) \text{ AND } \vert x_i - x_{i+1}\vert > \epsilon]$

5. **SSC:** Slope Sign Changes — number of times the slope of the waveform changes sign, with a threshold $\epsilon$ to ignore noise.

**Frequency-Domain Features:**
- **Mean Frequency (MNF)** and **Median Frequency (MDF)** obtained from the Power Spectral Density (PSD) — sensitive to muscle fatigue.
- Computed via FFT or autoregressive (AR) modelling.

**Time-Frequency Features:**
- **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** spectrograms.
- **Wavelet coefficients** (e.g., Daubechies-4) at multiple scales.

**Hudgins' Feature Set (1993, classic):** MAV, MAV slope, ZC, SSC, WL — the canonical 5-feature set used in nearly every textbook example.

#### Part (b) — LDA Classifier — Mathematical Formulation (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**
Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) is a **supervised, linear, generative** classifier that finds the hyperplane best separating $K$ classes in feature space.

**Step 1 — Within-Class Scatter Matrix $S_W$:**

$$S_W = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_k} (\mathbf{f}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)(\mathbf{f}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^{T}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ is the mean feature vector of class $k$:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_k} \mathbf{f}_i$$

**Step 2 — Between-Class Scatter Matrix $S_B$:**

$$S_B = \sum_{k=1}^{K} N_k (\boldsymbol{\mu}_k - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_k - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is the global mean.

**Step 3 — Fisher's Criterion — Projection Vector $\mathbf{w}$:**

The optimal projection maximizes the ratio of between-class to within-class scatter:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w}}$$

Setting $\frac{dJ}{d\mathbf{w}} = 0$ yields the **generalized eigenvalue problem**:

$$S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w}$$

The projection vector is the **eigenvector** corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}$.

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:**
For a new feature vector $\mathbf{f}_{new}$, the classifier projects onto the discriminant axis and assigns to the class with the highest posterior:

$$y_{pred} = \arg\max_k \; \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{f}_{new} + b_k$$

where $b_k$ is the class-specific bias term derived from the class means.

**Why LDA is the KTU-favoured choice:**
- Closed-form solution — no iterative training required.
- Real-time inference in < 1 ms on a microcontroller.
- Robust to EMG amplitude variability (uses covariance structure, not absolute scale).
- Empirically achieves **> 95 % accuracy** on 6-class grasp recognition with Hudgins' features.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the notch filter** — KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark if 50 Hz notch is not mentioned when discussing EMG conditioning for India.
> 2. **Confusing MAV and RMS** — MAV is the *mean* of absolute values; RMS is the *square root* of the *mean of squares*. They are *not* equal. Spelling this out in the answer earns a ½-mark bonus.
> 3. **Skipping the block diagram** in 14-mark answers — You will lose **2–3 marks** for omitting the system block diagram. Always draw it, even if rough.
> 4. **Omitting MVC normalization** — When deriving the linear envelope, always show the normalization step $e_{norm} = e / e_{MVC}$ to demonstrate patient-independence.
> 5. **Writing `|x|` inside a markdown table** — Use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX to avoid table breakage; this is a *technical presentation* deduction.
> 6. **Calling LDA "Linear Discriminant ANALYSIS" only — it is both analysis and classifier.** State the full name and the role.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **EMG** = bioelectric signal from muscle contraction; **bandwidth 20–500 Hz**; **amplitude 50 µV – 5 mV** (surface).
- ✅ **CMRR** of the IA must be **> 80 dB**; gain ≈ **1000**.
- ✅ The **50 Hz notch filter** is mandatory in India.
- ✅ **Full-wave rectification** is preferred over half-wave.
- ✅ **Envelope cutoff** $f_c$ = **3–8 Hz** (4th-order Butterworth).
- ✅ The five canonical time-domain features (Hudgins' set): **MAV, MAV-slope, ZC, SSC, WL**.
- ✅ **MVC normalization** is essential for patient-independent calibration.
- ✅ **Three control schemes:** Threshold, Proportional, Pattern Recognition.
- ✅ **LDA** is the clinical gold-standard classifier; solves a generalized eigenvalue problem $S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w}$.
- ✅ **Regression-based** control is the modern state-of-the-art for multi-DOF intuitive prostheses.
- ✅ Always draw the **block diagram** in 14-mark answers — it is worth **2–3 marks** by itself.
- ✅ Mention **sampling rate ≥ 1 kHz** and **window length 100–300 ms** when discussing real-time EMG.
- ✅ Trade-off triangle: **DOFs ↔ Intuitiveness ↔ Computational Cost** — knowing this wins bonus marks.
- ✅ Always cite **CMRR, SNR, and bandwidth** in numerical answer questions.
- ✅ Final answer tip: For 14-mark questions, use the structure **Diagram → Stages → Math → Comparator Table → Conclusion**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Artificial Prehension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Artificial Prehension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Artificial Prehension

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Prehension** is the engineering and biomedical discipline dedicated to designing, fabricating, and controlling artificial devices (prosthetic hands, terminal devices, and grippers) that replicate, restore, or augment the human hand's natural ability to grasp, hold, manipulate, and release objects of varying size, shape, weight, and texture.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **Neural Prosthesis & Implants (PEBMT743)**, artificial prehension is studied as the **functional endpoint** of upper-limb neural interfacing — the place where bioelectric signals (EMG, EEG, peripheral nerve signals) are ultimately translated into coordinated mechanical grasping actions.

### Formal Classification (Per Standard Prosthetics Literature)

Artificial prehension systems can be hierarchically classified into the following three categories:

1. **Cosmetic (Passive) Devices** — non-actuated, used purely for appearance.
2. **Body-Powered Devices** — actuated by cables, harnesses, and body movements (cable-driven).
3. **Externally Powered Devices** — actuated by electric motors (myoelectric), pneumatic systems, or hybrid mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The primary engineering focus of Module 4 is on the **signal acquisition → decoding → actuation** chain, with emphasis on **myoelectric control**, **pattern recognition**, and **multi-DOF (Degree of Freedom) prosthetic hand design**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Car Driver and the Steering Wheel**
> 
> Think of artificial prehension like giving a person a **remote-controlled robotic hand** to replace a lost one. The "remote control" is no longer a joystick — it is the **person's own muscles or nerves**. Every time an amputee flexes a phantom muscle, an electrode "listens" to that electrical signal, a small computer "decides" what motion is intended, and motors in the prosthetic hand "execute" the grasp.
> 
> In short: **Brain/Nerve → Electrical Signal → Decoder → Motor → Grasp Action.**

### The Prehension Loop in Plain English

The human grasping action involves a continuous closed-loop cycle:

- **Sensation** (feeling the object) → **Decision** (what grip type?) → **Action** (muscle contraction) → **Grasp** → **Feedback** (pressure, slip).

An artificial prehension system must replicate as much of this loop as possible. Modern devices incorporate **pressure sensors, slip detectors, and vibrotactile feedback** to close the loop, mimicking the natural sensory feedback that lets us hold a paper cup without crushing it or a wet glass without dropping it.

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics for Prehension Devices:**
> 
> - **Grip Force Range:** 0 – 200 N (for activities of daily living, ADL)
> - **Operating Speed:** 0.5 – 1.5 seconds for full open-to-close cycle
> - **Number of Active Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** 1 (simple hook) to 20+ (advanced anthropomorphic hands)
> - **Power Source:** Li-ion battery (typical **3.7 V**, 600–2000 mAh)
> - **Motor Type:** DC micromotors (typical **6 V – 12 V** range, gear ratio 30:1 to 300:1)
> - **EMG Signal Bandwidth:** **20 Hz – 500 Hz** (surface EMG)
> - **Control Latency:** Target ≤ **100 ms** for natural feel

---

## 1.3 Why Artificial Prehension Matters

Loss of a hand results in approximately **60% loss of whole-body function** for bilateral tasks. Prehension is fundamental to:

- **Activities of Daily Living (ADL):** eating, dressing, hygiene
- **Vocational tasks:** typing, tool use, lifting
- **Social and psychological well-being:** self-image and independence

The global prosthetic hand market exceeds **USD 1.2 billion annually**, with an estimated **2 million** upper-limb amputees in the world, providing strong engineering motivation for advancing this field.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Human Prehension Framework (Theoretical Foundation)

The human hand performs prehension through a tightly coordinated biomechanical system. The foundational taxonomy of human grasps was established by **Napier (1956)**, who classified all grips into two fundamental categories:

### A. Power Grip (Force Closure Grip)
The object is held by **flexor force** with the thumb acting as a buttress. The object is clamped between the palm and fingers, maximizing **force transmission** at the expense of dexterity. Examples: holding a hammer, doorknob, or tennis racket.

### B. Precision Grip (Form Closure Grip)
The object is held between the **thumb and fingertips**, maximizing **dexterity and manipulation** at the expense of force. Examples: holding a pen, needle, or coin.

### C. Hook Grip
A special case — fingers flex around an object while the thumb remains passive. Example: carrying a briefcase.

### D. Pinch Grips (Sub-classification)
- **Tip Pinch** — thumb tip to index tip (force ≈ 5–10 N typical)
- **Lateral Pinch (Key Pinch)** — thumb pad to lateral side of index finger (force ≈ 15–25 N)
- **Three-Jaw Chuck (Tripod)** — thumb, index, middle
- **Five-Jaw Chuck** — thumb plus all four fingers

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all key engineering equations, metrics, and parameters essential for KTU examinations on Artificial Prehension. Pay special attention to units and symbol conventions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Grasp Force Equation | $F_{grip} = \mu \cdot F_{normal}$ | $F_{grip}$ = grip friction force (N), $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $F_{normal}$ = normal force (N) | Basic Coulomb friction; minimum grip force to prevent slip |
| 2 | Slip Prevention Condition | $F_{grip} \geq \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \mu}$ | $W$ = object weight (N), $n$ = number of contact points | Minimum grip force to hold an object against gravity |
| 3 | Motor Torque Required | $\tau = F_{grip} \cdot L_{eff}$ | $\tau$ = torque (N·m), $L_{eff}$ = effective lever arm (m) | Torque needed at finger joint to produce grip force |
| 4 | Gear-Reduced Torque | $\tau_{out} = \tau_{motor} \cdot G \cdot \eta$ | $G$ = gear ratio, $\eta$ = gearbox efficiency (typically 0.4 – 0.7) | Output torque after gear reduction |
| 5 | Power Consumption (DC Motor) | $P = V \cdot I = \dfrac{\tau \cdot \omega}{\eta_{total}}$ | $V$ = voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) | Power drawn during grasping action |
| 6 | EMG Signal RMS | $EMG_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}$ | $x_i$ = EMG sample, $N$ = number of samples | Energy feature used to estimate muscle activation |
| 7 | Mean Absolute Value (MAV) | $MAV = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert$ | $x_i$ = EMG sample | Simpler feature; linear with muscle force |
| 8 | Classification Accuracy | $Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $TP$ = true positive, etc. | Quality metric for pattern-recognition-based control |
| 9 | Battery Life Estimate | $t_{batt} = \dfrac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}}$ | $C_{batt}$ = battery capacity (mAh), $I_{avg}$ = average current draw (mA) | Operating time per charge |
| 10 | Degrees of Freedom (DOF) | $DOF = 3(L-1) - 2J - H$ (for spatial mechanisms) | $L$ = links, $J$ = joints, $H$ = higher pairs (Kutzbach criterion) | Number of independent motions of the prosthetic hand |
| 11 | Control Latency Budget | $t_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{process} + t_{actuate}$ | $t_{sense}$ = sensing time, etc. | End-to-end delay from intent to motion |
| 12 | Slip Detection Threshold | $\Delta f > f_{threshold}$ (vibration-based) | $\Delta f$ = frequency change (Hz) | Vibrotactile sensor signal indicating slip onset |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The friction coefficient $\mu$ is critical. For **silicone-rubber fingertips**, $\mu \approx 0.8$–$1.2$ (dry) and $\mu \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ (wet). A **safety factor of 2× to 3×** is added to the minimum grip force to account for dynamic loading and uncertainty.

---

## 2.3 The Artificial Prehension System — Block Architecture

Every artificial prehension system, regardless of sophistication, contains the following six functional blocks:

1. **Residual Limb / Nerve Interface** — source of bioelectric signals.
2. **Signal Acquisition Stage** — electrodes (surface or implanted) + amplification + filtering.
3. **Signal Processing / Decoder** — feature extraction + classification (for pattern recognition).
4. **Controller / Decision Module** — translates intent into motor commands.
5. **Actuation Stage** — DC motors, pneumatic artificial muscles (PAMs), or shape-memory alloys.
6. **Terminal Device (End Effector)** — the mechanical fingers and thumb that physically contact the object.

> [!TIP]
> **Closed-Loop Additions** in advanced devices:
> - **Force Sensors** in fingertips (FSR — Force Sensitive Resistor)
> - **Slip Detectors** (vibration / accelerometer-based)
> - **Position Sensors** (encoders at joints)
> - **Haptic Feedback** (vibrotactile or electrotactile stimulators)

---

## 2.4 Control Strategies (KTU Focus Area)

### 2.4.1 Direct Control (On/Off, Two-Site)
Two muscle sites (e.g., flexor and extensor) are mapped to two opposing commands (open/close). Simplest approach; **1 DOF** functional control.

### 2.4.2 Proportional Control
Grip force or closing speed is proportional to the **amplitude of the EMG signal**, giving smoother, more natural motion.

$$v_{motor} \propto EMG_{RMS} \quad \text{or} \quad v_{motor} = K \cdot EMG_{RMS}$$

where $K$ is a gain constant tuned for the patient.

### 2.4.3 Pattern Recognition Control
Multiple EMG channels (typically 6–16) are processed using machine learning classifiers such as **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Support Vector Machines (SVM), or Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** to recognize multiple grasp patterns. This enables **multi-DOF intuitive control** without mode switching.

### 2.4.4 Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR) & Regenerative Peripheral Nerve Interfaces (RPNI)
Nerves that previously controlled the missing hand are surgically redirected to spare muscles. The reinnervated muscles now produce distinct EMG patterns corresponding to the lost hand's intended motions, dramatically improving intuitive control.

### 2.4.5 Brain–Computer Interface (BCI) Based Prehension
In severe cases (e.g., high-level amputations, locked-in patients), **EEG-based BCIs** or **intracortical microelectrode arrays** (e.g., Utah Array) are used to decode motor intent directly from the brain.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Artificial Prehension

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical & Industrial Impact:**
> 
> - **Medical Rehabilitation:** Restoration of ADL independence for amputees.
> - **Industrial Automation:** Theoretical principles of grasp force, friction, and slip detection inform **robotic grippers** in factories (e.g., food handling, electronics assembly).
> - **Space & Defense:** NASA's **Robonaut** and prosthetic-inspired exoskeletons for astronaut glove assistance.
> - **Assistive Robotics:** AI-driven grasping for the elderly and disabled.
> - **Surgical Robotics:** Slip-sensing principles from prosthetic fingertips are now used in **Da Vinci surgical graspers**.

The convergence of **neural engineering, materials science, and AI** makes artificial prehension one of the most interdisciplinary topics in modern biomedical engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Grip Force to Prevent Object Slip

This is the most fundamental analytical problem in prehension engineering. It uses the classical **Coulomb friction model**.

### Step 1 — State the Physical Problem

We have a flat object of weight $W$ held between two parallel fingertips with normal (grip) force $F_n$ applied at each contact. We want to find the minimum total normal force such that the object does not slip.

### Step 2 — Identify the Forces Acting on the Object

Four contact points exist (two fingers, two contact patches each). For simplicity, we consider two opposing contact points (one on each side), so $n = 2$ contact surfaces.

The forces acting on the object are:

1. **Weight** $W$ acting downward.
2. **Normal force** $F_n$ acting horizontally inward from each finger.
3. **Friction force** $F_f$ acting vertically upward (opposing downward slip).

### Step 3 — Apply Equilibrium Conditions

For static equilibrium (no slip, no acceleration):

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \cdot F_f = W$$

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{n,left} = F_{n,right} = F_n$$

### Step 4 — Apply Coulomb's Law of Friction

The maximum static friction force at each contact is:

$$F_{f,max} = \mu \cdot F_n$$

To prevent slip, we require:

$$F_f \leq \mu \cdot F_n$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Minimum Normal Force

Setting the friction force at its maximum (worst-case):

$$n \cdot \mu \cdot F_{n,min} = W$$

Solving for $F_{n,min}$:

$$F_{n,min} = \frac{W}{n \cdot \mu}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Safety Factor (Engineering Practice)

In real prosthetic applications, dynamic loads, surface variations, and patient-specific factors necessitate a safety factor $SF$ (typically 2 to 3):

$$F_{n,design} = SF \cdot \frac{W}{n \cdot \mu}$$

### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example

> **Example:** An amputee picks up a 1.2 kg water bottle. The fingertips are silicone with $\mu = 0.9$ (dry). Two contact points are used. Find the minimum grip force required. Take $SF = 2.5$.

**Step 7a — Calculate the weight of the object:**

$$W = m \cdot g = 1.2 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 11.77 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7b — Calculate the minimum normal force:**

$$F_{n,min} = \frac{W}{n \cdot \mu} = \frac{11.77}{2 \times 0.9} = \frac{11.77}{1.8} = 6.54 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7c — Apply safety factor:**

$$F_{n,design} = 2.5 \times 6.54 = 16.35 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7d — Each fingertip must therefore deliver approximately 16.35 N of normal force, totaling 32.7 N of grip force across both fingers.**

This is well within the capability of a standard DC-geared motor prosthetic hand (which can typically produce 30–70 N per finger).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Motor Sizing for a Single Finger

We now derive the motor torque required to produce a given grip force at the fingertip.

### Step 1 — Geometric Model

Consider a finger as a simple lever rotating about a pivot (the knuckle/MCP joint). The effective lever arm $L_{eff}$ is the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line of action of the contact force.

### Step 2 — Torque Equation

The torque $\tau$ required at the joint to produce normal force $F_n$ at the fingertip is:

$$\tau = F_n \cdot L_{eff}$$

### Step 3 — Substituting Design Force

Using the safety-factored design force from the previous derivation:

$$\tau_{joint} = F_{n,design} \cdot L_{eff} = SF \cdot \frac{W}{n \cdot \mu} \cdot L_{eff}$$

### Step 4 — Gear Reduction Required

Small DC motors produce high speed but low torque. A **planetary gearbox** with ratio $G$ and efficiency $\eta$ is used:

$$\tau_{out} = \tau_{motor} \cdot G \cdot \eta$$

Therefore, the required motor torque is:

$$\tau_{motor} = \frac{SF \cdot W \cdot L_{eff}}{n \cdot \mu \cdot G \cdot \eta}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

> **Example:** With $W = 11.77$ N, $L_{eff} = 0.04$ m (4 cm), $n = 2$, $\mu = 0.9$, $G = 100$, $\eta = 0.6$, $SF = 2.5$:

$$\tau_{motor} = \frac{2.5 \times 11.77 \times 0.04}{2 \times 0.9 \times 100 \times 0.6}$$

$$\tau_{motor} = \frac{1.177}{108} = 0.0109 \text{ N·m}$$

This is well within the capability of a standard **6 V DC micromotor** (rated torque ~0.02–0.05 N·m), confirming the feasibility of the design.

### Step 6 — Power and Current Draw

If the motor operates at 6000 RPM = 628 rad/s unloaded and the load draws the motor to half this speed ($\omega = 314$ rad/s):

$$P = \tau \cdot \omega = 0.0109 \times 314 = 3.42 \text{ W}$$

$$I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{3.42}{6} = 0.57 \text{ A}$$

A 1500 mAh battery would last:

$$t_{batt} = \frac{1500}{570} \approx 2.6 \text{ hours of continuous use}$$

In practice, grasps are intermittent, so daily use is achievable on a single charge.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Prehension Control Strategies

The following table maps **control strategy** to **information channel, complexity, intuitiveness, and KTU-relevant trade-offs**.

| Control Strategy | Input Signal | Classifier / Decoder | DOF Possible | Intuitiveness | KTU Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct (On/Off) | 2 EMG channels | Threshold comparator | 1 | Low | Cheap, robust, but no proportional control |
| Proportional | 2 EMG channels | Linear scaling | 1 | Moderate | Smoother, still single DOF |
| Pattern Recognition (LDA) | 6–16 EMG channels | Linear Discriminant Analysis | 2–6 | High | Real-time, but requires training data |
| Pattern Recognition (CNN) | High-density EMG | Deep neural network | 6–10 | Very High | Computationally heavy; needs embedded GPU |
| TMR-based | Re-routed EMG | LDA or SVM | 5–8 | Very High | Requires surgery, training |
| RPNI-based | Reinnervated muscle EMG | Pattern recognition | 5–10 | Very High | Multiple surgical interfaces |
| Cortical BCI (Utah Array) | Spikes / LFP | Kalman filter / RNN | Up to 10 | Very High | Invasive, expensive, signal drift |
| EEG-BCI (Non-invasive) | EEG motor imagery | CSP + LDA | 1–3 | Moderate | Low bandwidth, slow |
| Osseointegration | EMG + bone-anchored | Direct or pattern recognition | Up to 6 | High | Infection risk, surgical complexity |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** When asked to compare strategies, always address the three pillars: **intuitiveness, dexterity (DOF), and invasiveness.** This triad is the standard valuation framework for KTU 2024 Scheme essay questions on prosthetics.

---

## 3.4 EMG Feature-Extraction Derivation (for Pattern Recognition)

A frequently asked KTU derivation: how is the EMG signal converted into a classifiable feature vector?

### Step 1 — Window the Signal
EMG is segmented into windows of length $N$ (typically 100–250 ms, with 50% overlap).

### Step 2 — Extract Time-Domain Features

For window $k$ with samples $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$:

**Root Mean Square (RMS):**

$$EMG_{RMS}[k] = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}$$

**Mean Absolute Value (MAV):**

$$MAV[k] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert$$

**Waveform Length (WL):**

$$WL[k] = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \vert x_{i+1} - x_i \vert$$

**Zero Crossings (ZC):**

$$ZC[k] = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \left[ \text{sgn}(-x_i \cdot x_{i+1}) \text{ and } \vert x_i - x_{i+1} \vert \geq \epsilon \right]$$

### Step 3 — Build the Feature Vector

For $C$ EMG channels and $F$ features per channel, the feature vector has dimension $C \times F$:

$$\mathbf{x}[k] = \begin{bmatrix} RMS_1 \\ MAV_1 \\ WL_1 \\ \vdots \\ RMS_C \\ MAV_C \\ WL_C \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Classification (e.g., LDA)

LDA finds a projection vector $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes the between-class scatter ratio:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}}$$

where $S_B$ is the between-class scatter matrix and $S_W$ is the within-class scatter matrix. The optimal $\mathbf{w}$ is found by solving the generalized eigenvalue problem:

$$S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w}$$

The class label is then decided by the nearest projected centroid in the reduced feature space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is KTU high-yield.** Pattern recognition + LDA is one of the most frequently asked 14-mark questions in Module 4 of the Neural Prosthesis & Implants paper.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Artificial Prehension System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the full closed-loop architecture of a modern myoelectric prosthetic hand, from neural intent to grasp execution and back.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BRAIN] --> B[PERIPHERAL NERVES]
    B --> C[RESIDUAL MUSCLE]
    C --> D[EMG ELECTRODES]
    D --> E[SIGNAL CONDITIONING]
    E --> F[FEATURE EXTRACTION]
    F --> G[PATTERN CLASSIFIER LDA]
    G --> H[CONTROL DECISION MODULE]
    H --> I[MOTOR DRIVER]
    I --> J[DC GEARED MOTORS]
    J --> K[PROSTHETIC FINGERS AND THUMB]
    K --> L[OBJECT GRASPED]
    L --> M[FORCE SENSORS FSR]
    L --> N[SLIP DETECTORS]
    M --> O[MICROCONTROLLER FEEDBACK]
    N --> O
    O --> P[HAPTIC FEEDBACK STIMULATOR]
    P --> A
    H -. feedback .-> O
```

### Explanation of the Block Diagram

- **BRAIN** issues the motor command (e.g., "grasp the cup").
- **PERIPHERAL NERVES** carry the command.
- **RESIDUAL MUSCLE** contracts, generating EMG potentials.
- **EMG ELECTRODES** (surface or implanted) pick up the signals.
- **SIGNAL CONDITIONING** amplifies, filters (20–500 Hz bandpass), and rectifies the signal.
- **FEATURE EXTRACTION** computes RMS, MAV, WL, etc.
- **PATTERN CLASSIFIER (LDA)** identifies the intended grasp type.
- **CONTROL DECISION MODULE** selects motor commands.
- **MOTOR DRIVER** powers the **DC GEARED MOTORS**, which actuate the **PROSTHETIC FINGERS AND THUMB**.
- The **OBJECT GRASPED** triggers **FORCE SENSORS (FSR)** and **SLIP DETECTORS**.
- The **MICROCONTROLLER** processes sensor data and triggers a **HAPTIC FEEDBACK STIMULATOR** (e.g., vibratory motor on the skin) to provide sensory feedback to the user — closing the loop back to the **BRAIN**.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Grasp Taxonomy

This diagram maps the Napier prehension taxonomy into the engineering choices for prosthetic design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HUMAN PREHENSION] --> B[POWER GRIP]
    A --> C[PRECISION GRIP]
    A --> D[HOOK GRIP]
    A --> E[PINCH GRIPS]
    B --> B1[CYLINDRICAL GRIP]
    B --> B2[SPHERICAL GRIP]
    B --> B3[HOOK GRIP]
    C --> C1[TIP PINCH]
    C --> C2[TRIPOD PINCH]
    C --> C3[FIVE JAW CHUCK]
    E --> E1[LATERAL KEY PINCH]
    E --> E2[THREE JAW CHUCK]
    A --> F[PROSTHETIC MAPPING]
    F --> F1[HOOK TERMINAL DEVICE]
    F --> F2[MYOELECTRIC HAND 1 DOF]
    F --> F3[ANTHROPOMORPHIC MULTI DOF]
    F1 --> G[BODY POWERED]
    F2 --> H[MYOELECTRIC DIRECT CONTROL]
    F3 --> I[PATTERN RECOGNITION CONTROL]
```

### Explanation

- The diagram separates **human prehension types** (Napier classification) from the **prosthetic engineering implementations** that attempt to replicate them.
- A **hook terminal device** is typically body-powered and provides only power grip.
- A **myoelectric 1-DOF hand** offers power grip with proportional force.
- An **anthropomorphic multi-DOF hand** (e.g., 6-DOF) attempts to replicate the full prehension taxonomy using pattern-recognition-based control.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Comparative Control Strategies

This diagram contrasts the three primary control paradigms in artificial prehension.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EMG SOURCES] --> B1[DIRECT CONTROL]
    A --> B2[PROPORTIONAL CONTROL]
    A --> B3[PATTERN RECOGNITION]
    A --> B4[TMR OR RPNI]
    A --> B5[CORTICAL OR EEG BCI]
    B1 --> C1[1 DOF ON OFF]
    B2 --> C2[1 DOF PROPORTIONAL]
    B3 --> C3[2 TO 6 DOF LDA OR SVM]
    B4 --> C4[5 TO 10 DOF INTUITIVE]
    B5 --> C5[UP TO 10 DOF BRAIN DIRECTED]
    C1 --> D[PROSTHETIC ACTUATION]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[GRASPING ACTION]
```

### Explanation

- The diagram is arranged horizontally to show the **progression of sophistication** from direct control (left) to brain-computer interface (right).
- All strategies converge at the **PROSTHETIC ACTUATION** stage before producing the final **GRASPING ACTION**.
- This visualization helps KTU students answer comparison questions by clearly mapping control methods to the resulting **dexterity (DOF)** achievable.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Slip Detection and Closed-Loop Force Modulation

This flowchart details the sensor-driven decision logic for **slip prevention** during a grasp.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OBJECT IN GRIP] --> B[VIBRATION SENSOR READS f]
    B --> C{f exceeds slip threshold}
    C -- NO --> D[MAINTAIN CURRENT GRIP FORCE]
    C -- YES --> E[INCREMENT GRIP FORCE BY DELTA F]
    E --> F[RECHECK SLIP AFTER t ms]
    F --> G{STILL SLIPPING}
    G -- YES --> E
    G -- NO --> D
    D --> H[CONTINUE MONITORING]
    H --> B
```

### Explanation

- The system continuously monitors vibration frequency $f$ from a fingertip-mounted accelerometer or piezoelectric sensor.
- If $f$ exceeds the **slip threshold** (typically 20–30 Hz for many materials), the controller increments the grip force in small discrete steps.
- This is a real-time, **event-driven control loop** that prevents both slip (insufficient force) and crush damage (excessive force), implementing a key safety feature of modern prehension devices.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEBMT743]

> **Define Artificial Prehension. List any four types of human prehension patterns.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Artificial Prehension** is the branch of biomedical engineering concerned with the design, development, and control of artificial devices — such as prosthetic hands, terminal devices, and robotic grippers — that replicate or restore the human hand's ability to grasp, hold, manipulate, and release objects of varying shape, size, and texture.

**Four types of human prehension patterns (Napier Classification):**

1. **Power Grip** — full-hand clamp for force (e.g., holding a hammer).
2. **Precision Grip** — fingertip control for fine tasks (e.g., holding a pen).
3. **Hook Grip** — passive thumb, fingers flexed (e.g., carrying a briefcase).
4. **Pinch Grip** — thumb-finger contact (e.g., tip pinch, lateral/key pinch).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEBMT743]

> **What is a myoelectric prosthesis? State two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **myoelectric prosthesis** is an externally powered artificial limb that is controlled by the electrical activity (EMG) generated by the patient's residual muscles.

**Advantages:**

1. **No harnessing or cables** — improves comfort and cosmetic appearance.
2. **Proportional control possible** — grip force scales with muscle contraction.

**Limitation:**

1. Susceptible to **electrode-skin impedance variations** due to sweat, hair, and motion artifacts, requiring recalibration.

---

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Module 4 generally features an internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below, fully solved, to maximize exam preparedness.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

> **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the closed-loop architecture of a modern myoelectric prosthetic hand. Label the major signal-processing and actuation blocks. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 1.5 kg object is to be grasped by a two-finger gripper. The coefficient of friction between the silicone fingertip and the object is 0.85. The effective lever arm of the finger is 4.5 cm, and a planetary gearbox with ratio 120:1 and efficiency 0.55 is used. Calculate: (i) the minimum grip force, (ii) the required motor torque, and (iii) the operating current if the motor runs at 7.2 V and the loaded speed is 250 rad/s. Use a safety factor of 2.5. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

The closed-loop myoelectric prosthetic hand consists of the following functional stages:

1. **Signal Source (1 mark):** Residual muscles generate EMG potentials corresponding to the user's intent. For amputees, Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR) may be employed to amplify and diversify the EMG sources.

2. **Signal Acquisition (1 mark):** Surface or implanted electrodes (typically Ag/AgCl for surface, intramuscular needle electrodes for implanted) capture the EMG. Signals are differentially amplified (gain 1000–10,000), bandpass filtered (20–500 Hz), and notch filtered (50/60 Hz) to remove power-line interference.

3. **Feature Extraction & Classification (2 marks):** Time-domain features (RMS, MAV, WL, ZC) are extracted from windowed EMG (window: 150–250 ms, 50% overlap). A classifier such as LDA, SVM, or a lightweight CNN maps the feature vector to one of $K$ grasp classes (e.g., power, precision, lateral, tripod, hook).

4. **Controller & Motor Driver (1.5 marks):** The decoded class activates the appropriate motor command, which is sent via PWM to H-bridge motor drivers (e.g., DRV8871, L293D). Microcontroller (e.g., STM32) handles real-time scheduling.

5. **Actuation (1 mark):** Geared DC motors (or pneumatic artificial muscles) actuate the prosthetic fingers through tendon-driven or linkage mechanisms. Position encoders and force-sensitive resistors (FSR) on fingertips provide feedback.

6. **Sensory Feedback (0.5 mark):** Slip detection (via vibration sensors) and grip force (via FSR) are processed; vibrotactile or electrotactile stimulators on the skin provide closed-loop feedback to the user.

A **labelled block diagram** is mandatory and earns full marks.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $m = 1.5$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\mu = 0.85$, $n = 2$ (two contact points)
- $L_{eff} = 0.045$ m, $G = 120$, $\eta = 0.55$
- $SF = 2.5$, $V = 7.2$ V, $\omega = 250$ rad/s

**(i) Minimum Grip Force (2 marks):**

Weight of object:

$$W = m \cdot g = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \text{ N}$$

Minimum grip force (sum of normal forces at two contacts):

$$F_{grip,min} = SF \cdot \frac{W}{n \cdot \mu} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{14.715}{2 \times 0.85}$$

$$F_{grip,min} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{14.715}{1.7} = 2.5 \cdot 8.656 = 21.64 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Required Motor Torque (3 marks):**

Torque at finger joint:

$$\tau_{joint} = F_{grip,min} \cdot L_{eff} = 21.64 \times 0.045 = 0.974 \text{ N·m}$$

Wait — note that the grip force is the *sum* of two fingers, so torque per joint (per finger) is:

$$\tau_{joint,per\,finger} = F_{n,per\,finger} \cdot L_{eff} = \frac{21.64}{2} \times 0.045 = 10.82 \times 0.045 = 0.487 \text{ N·m}$$

Required motor torque (per finger):

$$\tau_{motor} = \frac{\tau_{joint,per\,finger}}{G \cdot \eta} = \frac{0.487}{120 \times 0.55} = \frac{0.487}{66} = 7.38 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{motor} \approx 7.4 \text{ mN·m}}$$

**(iii) Operating Current (2 marks):**

Mechanical power output (per motor):

$$P = \tau_{joint,per\,finger} \cdot \omega = 0.487 \times 250 = 121.7 \text{ W}$$

(Note: This assumes the loaded joint speed is 250 rad/s, which is the post-gearbox shaft speed. We must convert back to motor shaft if necessary; the standard convention is to use the joint power.)

Current draw:

$$I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{121.7}{7.2} \approx 16.9 \text{ A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students often forget to apply the safety factor in part (i) and lose 1 mark. Always re-read the question for "design force" vs "minimum force" wording.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Many students confuse *grip force per finger* with *total grip force*. The 0.974 N·m result is the total joint torque, not the per-finger value.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** Forgetting to multiply by $\eta$ (gearbox efficiency) is the single most common error in motor-sizing problems. Always include the efficiency term in your final answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR)** and **Pattern Recognition-based myoelectric control**. Discuss how they collectively improve prosthetic prehension. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the following control strategies for a multi-DOF prosthetic hand: Direct On/Off control, Proportional control, Pattern Recognition control, and Cortical BCI control. Use a comparison matrix covering DOF, intuitiveness, invasiveness, and computational cost. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — TMR + Pattern Recognition (7 marks):**

**Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR):** (3 marks)

TMR is a **surgical neural-prosthetic interface** technique in which nerves that previously innervated the missing limb (e.g., median, ulnar, radial nerves of the arm) are surgically redirected to spare, denervated muscles of the residual limb or chest. After 3–6 months of reinnervation, these muscles produce **distinct, separable EMG patterns** corresponding to the original (now phantom) hand's intended motions. For example:

- Redirecting the **median nerve** to a chest muscle causes EMG bursts on the chest when the patient attempts a **precision pinch**.
- Redirecting the **radial nerve** produces EMG signals associated with **wrist extension / power grip opening**.

TMR dramatically **increases the number of independent EMG control sites** from the typical 1–2 (biceps/triceps) to 5–8, enabling **multi-DOF intuitive control** without requiring the patient to learn unrelated muscle contractions.

**Pattern Recognition-based Myoelectric Control:** (3 marks)

Pattern recognition complements TMR by decoding the multi-channel EMG in real time. The processing pipeline is:

1. **Acquisition** — $C$ EMG channels ($C = 6$–$16$ for high-density arrays).
2. **Windowing** — overlapping windows of 100–250 ms.
3. **Feature Extraction** — RMS, MAV, WL, ZC, AR coefficients.
4. **Classification** — LDA, SVM, or shallow CNN identifies the grasp class.
5. **Output** — discrete or continuous motor command for each finger.

**Collective Synergy:** (1 mark)

TMR provides **more independent, physiologically intuitive EMG sources**, while pattern recognition provides the **computational intelligence** to decode them. Together, they enable an amputee to control a multi-DOF hand with **near-natural thought-based grasping**, representing the current state-of-the-art in upper-limb prosthetics.

**Part (b) — Comparison Matrix (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Direct On/Off | Proportional | Pattern Recognition | Cortical BCI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input Signal | 2 EMG channels | 2 EMG channels | 6–16 EMG channels | Cortical spikes / EEG |
| DOF Achievable | 1 | 1 | 2–10 | Up to 10 |
| Intuitiveness | Low (mode switching) | Moderate | High | Very High |
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive (surface) | Non-invasive | Non-invasive to minimally invasive | Highly invasive (intracortical) |
| Computational Cost | Very Low | Very Low | Moderate (real-time) | High (GPU often needed) |
| Training Required | None | None | Yes (per-user calibration) | Yes + surgical recovery |
| Reliability | High | High | Moderate (sensitive to electrode shift) | Low (signal drift, scar tissue) |
| Latency | < 50 ms | < 50 ms | 80–150 ms | 100–300 ms |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** For comparison questions, students often list features without **justifying** the trade-offs. Each entry must be accompanied by a one-line reasoning. For instance, "Pattern recognition is more intuitive **because** it eliminates mode switching by directly classifying grasp intent from natural EMG patterns."

---

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Artificial Prehension (Module 4):**

- **Definition:** Artificial prehension = restoration/replication of human grasp using engineered devices.
- **Napier Classification:** Power grip, Precision grip, Hook grip, Pinch grips (tip, lateral, tripod, 5-jaw chuck).
- **Three categories of prosthetic devices:** Cosmetic, Body-powered, Externally powered (myoelectric).
- **EMG Bandwidth:** 20–500 Hz; sampling rate typically **1 kHz** or higher (Nyquist criterion).
- **Slip prevention equation:** $F_{n,min} = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \mu}$; always apply a safety factor of **2× to 3×**.
- **Motor sizing:** $\tau_{motor} = \dfrac{SF \cdot W \cdot L_{eff}}{n \cdot \mu \cdot G \cdot \eta}$.
- **Pattern recognition pipeline:** Windowing → Feature extraction (RMS, MAV, WL, ZC) → Classification (LDA/SVM/CNN) → Motor command.
- **Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR):** Surgical rerouting of nerves to spare muscles to create intuitive EMG control sites.
- **RPNI:** Regenerative Peripheral Nerve Interface — grafts of muscle tissue to amplify nerve signals.
- **Closed-loop feedback:** Force sensors (FSR), slip detectors (vibration), position encoders, haptic feedback (vibrotactile/electrotactile).
- **Standard grip force range:** 0–200 N for ADL; design target 30–70 N per finger.
- **Control latency target:** ≤ 100 ms for natural feel.
- **Friction coefficient for silicone:** $\mu \approx 0.8$–$1.2$ (dry), 0.3–0.5 (wet).
- **Degrees of freedom:** Range from 1 (hook) to 20+ (advanced anthropomorphic, e.g., IH2 Azzurra, Shadow Hand).
- **Modern advanced hands:** Bebionic, Michelangelo, LUKE Arm, Hero Arm — 6–10 DOF.
- **Future direction:** Osseointegration, sensory feedback via peripheral nerve stimulation, machine-learning-driven adaptive grasp selection, soft robotics grippers.
- **Clinical metrics:** Acceptance rate of body-powered ≈ 60–70%, myoelectric ≈ 30–50%, TMR-based ≈ 80–90%.
- **KTU exam weightage:** Module 4 typically carries **15–20 marks** in the End Semester Exam (ESE), with at least one 14-mark question from Artificial Prehension or Bionic Limbs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brain Control and Sensing of Artificial Limbs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT743 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Brain Control and Sensing of Artificial Limbs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Brain Control and Sensing of Artificial Limbs** refers to the bidirectional engineering discipline concerned with the acquisition, decoding, and translation of **central nervous system (CNS) neural activity** into command signals for prosthetic actuators, and the **artificial generation of somatosensory feedback** (touch, pressure, proprioception, temperature) delivered back to the brain or peripheral nerves via electrical, optical, or chemical means. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this sits within the domain of **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI)** and **Brain–Machine Interfaces (BMI)**, and forms the closed-loop core of a modern **neuroprosthetic limb**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> A *brain-controlled artificial limb* is a mechatronic prosthetic system in which **motor intent** is decoded from neural signals (cortical, electrocorticographic, or peripheral) and **sensory percepts** are elicited by direct neural stimulation, thereby restoring both the *efferent* (command) and *afferent* (feedback) channels of the natural sensorimotor loop.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Two-Way Radio" Analogy

Imagine your natural arm as a **two-way radio conversation** between a base station (the brain) and a field agent (the hand). When you reach for a cup:

- The **base station broadcasts a command** (motor cortex fires, "pick up cup").
- The **field agent replies with reports** (hand sensors send pressure, temperature, joint angle back).
- Both stations constantly adjust based on the other's chatter.

A traditional myoelectric prosthesis is like a **one-way megaphone** — the amputee shouts "close hand" to the muscle, but the hand cannot whisper back about grip force, so the user must rely on vision alone (the "**vision-dominated control paradigm**").

A **brain-controlled, sensory-enabled prosthetic** rebuilds the full duplex radio:
- The **efferent (outgoing) channel** is captured by electrodes reading the brain's motor plan.
- The **afferent (incoming) channel** is reconstructed by stimulating somatosensory cortex or peripheral nerves with patterns that the brain interprets as natural touch.

The result is a limb that *thinks through you* and *feels for you*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants are examinable in numerical problems:
>
> - **Information Transfer Rate (ITR):** measured in **bits/min**, typical BCI target ≥ 30 bits/min.
> - **Charge per phase (Q):** safe limit **< 0.4 µC/phase** for cortical microstimulation (Shannon 1992 limit).
> - **Charge density:** safe limit **< 30 µC/cm²·phase** for chronic intracortical electrodes.
> - **Neural recording bandwidth:** single units **300 Hz – 7 kHz**, LFP **0.5 – 300 Hz**, ECoG **0.5 – 500 Hz**, EEG **0.5 – 100 Hz**.
> - **Microelectrode impedance:** **50 – 500 kΩ** at **1 kHz** for typical tungsten or Michigan probes.
> - **Phantom limb pain prevalence in amputees:** **50 – 80 %**, motivating sensory feedback research.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

Module 4 of PEBMT743 is the **integration module** — it ties together electrodes (Module 1), stimulating circuits (Module 2), and physiological models (Module 3) into a clinically deployable system. Board questions frequently test:

- The classification of recording modalities (invasive / semi-invasive / non-invasive).
- Decoding strategies (population vector, Kalman filter, deep learning).
- Stimulation paradigms for sensory feedback.
- The bi-directional *closed-loop* architecture and its safety constraints.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Brain-Controlled Limbs

A modern bidirectional prosthetic is engineered around **four interlocking functional pillars**. Understanding them is essential for any 14-mark KTU question.

#### Pillar 1 — Neural Signal Acquisition (The Efferent Read)

- **Why?** The brain's motor plan must be captured before the limb can act.
- **How?** Electrodes transduce ionic currents in neurons into measurable voltages.
- **Recording Hierarchy (least → most invasive):**
  - **EEG (Electroencephalography)** — scalp, non-invasive, low spatial resolution (~cm), high motion artefact.
  - **ECoG (Electrocorticography)** — subdural grid, ~1 mm spatial resolution, 0.5–500 Hz bandwidth, requires craniotomy.
  - **Intracortical Microelectrodes** — Utah Array / Michigan Probe, single-unit & multi-unit activity (SUA / MUA), 30 000+ neurons sampled possible.
  - **Peripheral Nerve Interfaces** — TMR, RPNI, longitudinal intrafascicular electrodes (LIFE) capturing residual motor signals.

#### Pillar 2 — Neural Decoding (The Translation Engine)

- **Why?** Raw spike trains or field potentials are stochastic; the limb needs deterministic commands.
- **How?** Statistical models map neural population activity $\mathbf{x}(t)$ to kinematic state $\mathbf{y}(t)$.
- **Common Decoders in KTU syllabus:**
  - **Population Vector Algorithm (PVA)** — Georgopoulos, 1986; cosine-tuned directional tuning.
  - **Linear Filter / Wiener Filter** — least-squares optimal for Gaussian signals.
  - **Kalman Filter** — recursive Bayesian, the clinical workhorse (e.g., BrainGate trials).
  - **Recurrent Neural Networks / LSTM** — emerging for adaptive non-linear control.

#### Pillar 3 — Artificial Actuation (The Motor Output)

- **Why?** The decoded command must move a physical or virtual limb.
- **How?** DC motors, servo motors, pneumatic muscles, or **ECAs (Electroactive polymers)** receive pulse-width-modulated (PWM) drive.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Human hand has ~22 DOF; modern prosthetic hands realistically restore 6–10 active DOF (DEKA LUKE arm, Modular Prosthetic Limb).

#### Pillar 4 — Sensory Feedback (The Afferent Write)

- **Why?** Without feedback, the user rejects the limb within 6–12 months (rejection rates historically > 30 %).
- **How?** Sensors on the prosthetic (force, slip, temperature) drive a stimulator that activates:
  - **Primary somatosensory cortex (S1)** via intracortical microstimulation (ICMS).
  - **Peripheral nerve fibers** via intrafascicular electrodes (TIME, FINE, Cuff electrodes).
  - **Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR)** skin — non-invasive sensory substitution.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the high-yield formula bank for Module 4. Board problems almost always involve one of these equations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols & Units | Validity / Note |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Shannon–Hartley Information Rate** | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $C$ = channel capacity (bits/s), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = SNR (dimensionless) | Sets theoretical upper bound for BCI bit rate. |
| 2 | **BCI Information Transfer Rate (ITR, bits/min)** | $B_{\text{ITR}} = 60\!\left(\log_{2}N + P\log_{2}P + (1-P)\log_{2}\dfrac{1-P}{N-1}\right)$ | $N$ = number of classes, $P$ = classification accuracy | Wolpaw's formula, used in all EEG-based BCI evaluation. |
| 3 | **Charge per Phase (Stimulation Safety)** | $Q = I \cdot t_{pw}$ | $Q$ in µC, $I$ in µA, $t_{pw}$ in ms | Shannon limit $Q \le 0.4 \,\mu\text{C/phase}$ for cortical microelectrodes. |
| 4 | **Charge Density** | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A_{elec}}$ | $D$ in µC/cm², $A_{elec}$ in cm² | Limit $D \le 30 \,\mu\text{C/cm}^2$ for chronic electrodes. |
| 5 | **Population Vector (Decoding Direction)** | $\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(\mathbf{c}_i - b_i\right)\mathbf{d}_i$ | $\mathbf{c}_i$ = firing rate of neuron $i$, $b_i$ = baseline, $\mathbf{d}_i$ = preferred direction | Georgopoulos 1986; sums unit vectors weighted by directional tuning. |
| 6 | **Kalman Filter State Update** | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k\!\left(\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}\right)$ | $\mathbf{K}_k$ = Kalman gain, $\mathbf{z}_k$ = neural measurement | Predictor–corrector; standard in BrainGate clinical decoders. |
| 7 | **Tuning Curve (Cosine Model)** | $f(\theta) = f_0 + k \cos(\theta - \theta_{\text{pref}})$ | $f$ = firing rate (Hz), $\theta$ = reach direction, $k$ = modulation depth | Foundation of motor cortex decoding. |
| 8 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (EEG)** | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{ERP}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | $V$ in µV | Used to evaluate P300 / SSVEP BCIs. |
| 9 | **Electrode Impedance (Warburg Model)** | $Z(\omega) = R_s + \dfrac{R_{ct}}{1 + j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl}}$ | $R_s$ = solution resistance, $R_{ct}$ = charge-transfer, $C_{dl}$ = double-layer | Determines recording quality at 1 kHz. |
| 10 | **Phantom Limb Pain Relief (Mirror Therapy Analogue)** | $\Delta\text{PLP} = \alpha N_{\text{fb}} + \beta$ | $N_{\text{fb}}$ = number of feedback channels, $\alpha < 0$ | Empirical; bidirectional feedback shown to reduce PLP. |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These four pillars are not academic abstractions. They power:

- **BrainGate2 clinical trials** — tetraplegic patients controlling 7-DOF robotic arms with intracortical Utah arrays.
- **DARPA HAPTIX program** — restoring naturalistic touch via peripheral nerve stimulation.
- **Synchron Stentrode™** — endovascular BCI, no craniotomy, currently in clinical trials.
- **Closed-loop deep brain stimulators (DBS)** for Parkinson's — decoding beta-band LFP to adapt stimulation amplitude in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A complete PEBMT743 graduate must be able to *trace a signal from a cortical pyramidal neuron → amplifier → ADC → decoder → motor driver → actuator*, and the *sensory return path from a force sensor → DAC → current stimulator → afferent nerve → S1 cortex*. The board loves to test this round-trip.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon–Hartley Theorem Applied to a BCI Channel

We derive the maximum error-free information rate the brain→prosthetic channel can carry.

**Step 1 — State the channel capacity definition.**

For a continuous Gaussian channel of bandwidth $B$ (Hz) and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$, the capacity $C$ (bits/s) is the maximum of the mutual information.

$$
C = \max_{P(x)} I(X;Y) = B \int_{0}^{B} \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S(f)}{N(f)}\right) df
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the white-noise assumption.**

If the noise is white and the signal is band-limited with flat PSD $N_0/2$ and signal power $S$, then $S(f)/N(f) = S/N$ is constant across the band.

$$
C = B \int_{0}^{B} \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) df = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \int_{0}^{B} df
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

The integration is over frequency; the integrand is constant, so the integral equals $B$.

$$
\int_{0}^{B} df = B
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back and obtain the canonical form.**

$$
C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits/s]}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to a BCI-relevant per-minute rate.**

Multiply by 60 to obtain a per-minute ITR upper bound.

$$
C_{\min} = 60\,B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits/min]}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check for EEG-based BCI.**

Take $B = 40\,\text{Hz}$ (mu/beta band), $S/N = 100$ (40 dB, achievable with averaged ERPs).

$$
C_{\min} = 60 \times 40 \times \log_{2}(101) \approx 60 \times 40 \times 6.658 \approx 15{,}979 \,\text{bits/min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The *practical* ITR of an EEG P300 speller is only 20–40 bits/min due to non-ideal channel coding and human reaction time. The Shannon bound tells us the gap is not in the channel — it is in the *human interface* and *classifier*.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Population Vector Decoder (Georgopoulos, 1986)

We show how a population of directionally tuned motor cortical neurons yields a single decoded reach vector.

**Step 1 — Define the preferred direction.**

For neuron $i$, the preferred direction $\mathbf{d}_i$ is the 3-D unit vector in Cartesian hand-space along which firing rate is maximum.

$$
\| \mathbf{d}_i \| = 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N
$$

**Step 2 — Define the tuning curve (cosine model).**

The instantaneous firing rate is baseline $b_i$ plus cosine modulation.

$$
f_i(\theta) = b_i + k_i \cos\!\left(\theta - \theta_{\text{pref},i}\right)
$$

This can be written vectorially.

$$
f_i(\theta) = b_i + k_i\, \mathbf{d}_i \cdot \hat{\mathbf{v}}_{\theta}
$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{v}}_{\theta}$ is the unit vector of the actual hand velocity.

**Step 3 — Invert the linear system.**

For an instantaneous hand velocity $\mathbf{v}$ of magnitude $v = \|\mathbf{v}\|$, the directional preference can be expressed in Cartesian form by writing $\mathbf{d}_i \cdot \mathbf{v} = \|\mathbf{v}\|\cos(\theta - \theta_{\text{pref},i})$.

$$
f_i - b_i = k_i\, \mathbf{d}_i \cdot \mathbf{v}
$$

**Step 4 — Stack the equations into matrix form.**

$$
\mathbf{f} - \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{D}\, \mathbf{v}
$$

where $\mathbf{f} = (f_1,\ldots,f_N)^T$, $\mathbf{b} = (b_1,\ldots,b_N)^T$, and $\mathbf{D}$ is the $N \times 3$ matrix of preferred direction components.

**Step 5 — Pseudoinverse solution (least-squares estimate).**

For an over-determined system ($N \gg 3$), use the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \left(\mathbf{D}^T \mathbf{D}\right)^{-1} \mathbf{D}^T (\mathbf{f} - \mathbf{b})
$$

**Step 6 — Original Georgopoulos form (equal weights).**

Assuming all $k_i$ are equal and $N$ is large, the pseudoinverse reduces to the intuitive sum of weighted unit vectors.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(f_i - b_i\right) \mathbf{d}_i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** This equation is a *favourite* 7-mark derivation. Memorise Steps 4–6; the *vectorised pseudoinverse form* is the modern version the board expects.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Safe Stimulation Charge Calculation

We compute whether a stimulating microelectrode operates within the Shannon safety envelope.

**Step 1 — Stimulating pulse parameters.**

A typical ICMS pulse is biphasic, charge-balanced, cathodic-first.

$$
I_{\text{stim}} = 50 \,\mu\text{A}, \quad t_{pw} = 200 \,\mu\text{s}, \quad A_{\text{elec}} = 2000 \,\mu\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Charge per phase.**

$$
Q = I_{\text{stim}} \cdot t_{pw} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \,\text{C} = 0.01 \,\mu\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert electrode area to cm².**

$$
A_{\text{elec}} = 2000 \,\mu\text{m}^2 = 2000 \times 10^{-8} \,\text{cm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{cm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Charge density.**

$$
D = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{elec}}} = \frac{0.01 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \,\mu\text{C/cm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with safety limit.**

$$
D = 5 \times 10^{-4} \,\mu\text{C/cm}^2 \;\ll\; 30 \,\mu\text{C/cm}^2 \;\checkmark
$$

The stimulation is well within the chronic safe envelope; tissue damage and electrode corrosion are unlikely.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** forgetting to convert $\mu\text{m}^2$ to $\text{cm}^2$. A factor of $10^{8}$ difference in final answer is the typical 2-mark deduction.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Kalman Filter State Estimate

We derive the recursive update for a brain-controlled cursor state $\mathbf{x}_k = (x, y, v_x, v_y)^T$ from neural firing rates $\mathbf{z}_k$.

**Step 1 — Linear Gaussian state-space model.**

$$
\mathbf{x}_{k} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{w}_{k-1}, \quad \mathbf{w} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q})
$$

$$
\mathbf{z}_{k} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}_{k} + \mathbf{v}_{k}, \quad \mathbf{v} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R})
$$

**Step 2 — Prediction step.**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\mid k-1}
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_{k-1\mid k-1}\mathbf{A}^T + \mathbf{Q}
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation (measurement residual).**

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_k = \mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Innovation covariance.**

$$
\mathbf{S}_k = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R}
$$

**Step 5 — Kalman gain.**

$$
\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{S}_k^{-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Posterior update.**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_k
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}) \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}
$$

**Step 7 — Closed-loop interpretation for prosthetics.**

At every 50–100 ms time step, the Kalman filter fuses the **predicted cursor trajectory** with the **newly decoded neural firing rates** to produce a smooth, minimum-variance estimate of intended hand movement. The BrainGate2 clinical trial achieved ~ 91 % target hit-rate using this exact framework.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bidirectional Brain-Controlled Prosthetic Limb

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **complete closed-loop data flow** from cortical intent to mechanical action and back to somatosensory cortex. Each block is annotated with its KTU syllabus counterpart.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Primary Motor Cortex M1] -->|Spike trains + LFP| B[Intracortical Microelectrode Array 96 channel Utah Array]
    B --> C[Neural Amplifier 80 dB gain 300 Hz to 7 kHz]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter 16 bit 30 kHz sampling]
    D --> E[Feature Extraction Band pass 300 to 3000 Hz Spike sorting]
    E --> F[Decoder Kalman Filter or Population Vector]
    F --> G[Motor Controller PWM Driver]
    G --> H[Prosthetic Actuator DC servo / ECA muscle]
    H --> I[Artificial Hand Force and position sensors]
    I --> J[Sensory Encoder Maps force to stimulus amplitude]
    J --> K[Current Stimulator Charge balanced biphasic]
    K --> L[Peripheral Nerve Interface TIME or FINE electrode]
    L --> M[Primary Somatosensory Cortex S1 Elicited touch percept]
    M -.->|Feedback modulates motor plan| A

    subgraph PowerAndSafety
        PS1[Wireless Power Transfer 13.56 MHz] -.-> C
        PS2[Safety Watchdog Charge density limit] -.-> K
    end
```

### 4.2 Decoding & Stimulation Algorithm Flow (Detailed Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw Neural Signal] --> R2{Band Selection}
    R2 -->|High gamma 75 200 Hz| R3[True decoding input]
    R2 -->|Delta Theta| R4[Discard artifact]
    R3 --> R5[Window 100 ms bins]
    R5 --> R6[Z score normalization]
    R6 --> R7[Decoder module]
    R7 --> R8[Predicted velocity]
    R8 --> R9[Integrate to position]
    R9 --> R10[Send to prosthetic]
    R10 --> R11[Servo command]
    R11 --> R12[Actuator moves]

    S1[Force sensor on fingertip] --> S2[ADC sample]
    S2 --> S3[Threshold linearizer]
    S3 --> S4[Map to stimulation amplitude]
    S4 --> S5[Stimulator output stage]
    S5 --> S6[Peripheral nerve or S1 microstimulation]
    S6 --> S7[User perceives touch]
    S7 -.-> S8[Modulates grip force in next motor cycle]
    S8 -.-> R1
```

### 4.3 Comparison Matrix — Recording Modalities

| Property | EEG (Non-invasive) | ECoG (Subdural) | Intracortical (Utah Array) | Peripheral Nerve (TMR/RPNI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Spatial resolution** | ~ 2–3 cm | ~ 1 mm | ~ 50 µm (single neuron) | ~ fascicle level |
| **Signal type** | Field potentials (volume conducted) | Local field potentials + high gamma | Single & multi-unit spikes | EMG from reinnervated muscles |
| **Invasiveness** | None | Craniotomy + dural opening | Penetrating electrodes (∼1 mm) | Nerve dissection |
| **Bandwidth** | 0.5–100 Hz | 0.5–500 Hz | 300–7 000 Hz | EMG 20–500 Hz |
| **Stability** | High (gel dries) | Months–years | 1–5 years (gliosis) | Years (regenerating nerve) |
| **Information rate (typical)** | 20–40 bits/min | 60–120 bits/min | 200+ bits/min | 30–80 bits/min |
| **Clinical examples** | P300 speller, motor imagery | High-gamma BCI for speech | BrainGate, Neuralink | TMR for touch |
| **KTU 2024 board weight** | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
