## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define Brain–Computer Interface (BCI) and Brain–Machine Interface (BMI). Differentiate between them in two lines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Brain–Computer Interface (BCI)** is a direct communication pathway between the brain's electrical activity and an external device, *bypassing* the normal neuromuscular output channels. A **Brain–Machine Interface (BMI)** is a subset of BCI that specifically targets the *restoration of motor function* by decoding motor intent and driving actuators such as robotic limbs. **Difference:** BCI is the broader discipline (covers communication, monitoring, neurofeedback); BMI is *motor-restoration-specific* and includes the closed-loop sensory feedback path.
(Board expects: 1 mark definition BCI, 1 mark definition BMI, 1 mark difference.) ✓

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List the three non-invasive, semi-invasive, and invasive neural recording techniques used for prosthetic control. State the typical frequency band of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Non-invasive — EEG (Electroencephalography):** bandwidth **0.5–100 Hz**, captures summed cortical dendritic activity via scalp electrodes. (1 mark)
- **Semi-invasive — ECoG (Electrocorticography):** bandwidth **0.5–500 Hz**, uses subdural electrode grids on the cortical surface. (1 mark)
- **Invasive — Intracortical Microelectrodes (Utah Array / Michigan Probe):** bandwidth **300–7 000 Hz**, records single-unit and multi-unit action potentials. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete closed-loop architecture of a brain-controlled, sensory-enabled prosthetic limb**. Label every block from motor cortex to actuator and back. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Shannon–Hartley information capacity** of a neural recording channel and compute the maximum ITR for an EEG system with $B = 30$ Hz and $S/N = 50$. Comment on why practical BCI rates are far below this theoretical bound. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A (a) — Closed-Loop Architecture (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the two halves of the loop.**
The system has a **motor (efferent) channel** and a **sensory (afferent) channel**. (½ mark)

**Step 2 — Motor channel (forward path):**
The chain is **Primary Motor Cortex (M1) → Intracortical Microelectrode Array (e.g., 96-channel Utah Array) → Neural Amplifier (×80 dB gain, 300 Hz–7 kHz bandpass) → Analogue-to-Digital Converter (16-bit, 30 kHz) → Feature Extraction (spike sorting / band-power) → Decoder (Kalman / Population Vector) → Motor Controller (PWM, 20 kHz) → Actuator (DC servo / ECA) → Artificial Hand**. (3 marks, ½ mark per correct block)

**Step 3 — Sensory channel (return path):**
**Fingertip Force Sensor (FSR) / Position Encoder → ADC → Sensory Encoder (force-to-amplitude map) → Charge-Balanced Current Stimulator (biphasic, < 0.4 µC/phase) → Peripheral Nerve Interface (TIME/FINE/cuff) OR S1 Intracortical Microstimulation → Elicited Somatosensory Percept**. (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Closed-loop justification.**
The sensory percept modulates M1 activity in the next cycle, completing the feedback loop. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Safety and power subsystems (bonus mention).**
Wireless power telemetry, charge-density watchdog, hermetic titanium enclosure. (½ mark)

#### Solution to Question A (b) — Shannon–Hartley Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State the formula.** (½ mark)

$$
C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Derive from mutual information integral.** (2 marks)

Starting from $C = B\int_0^B \log_2(1+S(f)/N(f))\,df$, apply the white-noise, band-limited assumption so the integrand is constant, giving the canonical form.

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.** (1 mark)

Given $B = 30$ Hz, $S/N = 50$.

$$
C = 30 \log_2(51) = 30 \times 5.672 \approx 170.2 \text{ bits/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bits/min.** (1 mark)

$$
C_{\min} = 60 \times 170.2 \approx 10{,}210 \text{ bits/min}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with practical BCI rates.** (1 mark)
Practical EEG P300 spellers achieve only 20–40 bits/min, a factor of 250× lower, because of (i) classifier errors, (ii) human reaction-time bottleneck, (iii) non-Gaussian non-stationary noise, and (iv) lack of error-correcting codes.

**Step 6 — Conclusion.** (½ mark)
The Shannon bound is *not* the limiting factor for BCIs; **the human and the algorithm** are. This motivates advanced adaptive decoders (RNN, transfer learning).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently omit the **per-minute conversion** and lose 1 mark. The board specifies ITR in bits/min; bits/s is incomplete.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **Population Vector Algorithm** for decoding motor cortical signals. Derive the closed-form expression for the decoded reach vector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR)**? Explain how it enables both **motor control and sensory feedback** in upper-limb amputees. Discuss its advantages over conventional myoelectric prostheses. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Question B (a) — Population Vector Decoder (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Concept.** (1 mark)
Georgopoulos (1986) showed that M1 neurons are *directionally tuned*: each neuron has a **preferred direction** $\mathbf{d}_i$ along which its firing rate is maximum. The *vector sum* of all weighted preferred directions estimates the reach direction.

**Step 2 — Tuning curve.** (1 mark)

$$
f_i(\theta) = b_i + k_i \cos(\theta - \theta_{\text{pref},i})
$$

**Step 3 — Linear system.** (1 mark)

$$
\mathbf{f} - \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{D}\, \mathbf{v}
$$

**Step 4 — Pseudoinverse.** (1 mark)

$$
\hat{\mathbf{v}} = (\mathbf{D}^T\mathbf{D})^{-1}\mathbf{D}^T(\mathbf{f}-\mathbf{b})
$$

**Step 5 — Original sum form.** (1 mark)

$$
\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(f_i - b_i)\,\mathbf{d}_i
$$

**Step 6 — Practical advantages.** (1 mark)
Requires only a few seconds of calibration per session; robust to single-neuron drop-out; works in real time.

**Step 7 — Limitations.** (½ mark)
Assumes cosine tuning; degrades with non-stationarity; needs re-calibration.

#### Solution to Question B (b) — Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition.** (1 mark)
TMR is a **surgical redirection** of residual peripheral nerves (e.g., median, ulnar, radial) onto denervated *target muscles* (e.g., pectoralis major/minor). The reinnervated muscle's surface EMG (sEMG) becomes a high-fidelity, naturally-modulated signal source.

**Step 2 — Motor control mechanism.** (1 mark)
When the amputee *thinks* "close hand", the median nerve fires, the reinnervated muscle contracts, surface electrodes pick up the EMG, and the prosthetic hand closes. Up to 6–8 intuitive DOF are recovered from a single TMR site.

**Step 3 — Sensory feedback mechanism.** (2 marks)
Pressure sensors on the prosthetic fingertips drive a **cutaneous stimulator** placed over the reinnervated skin. When the prosthetic grips an object, the user *feels* the pressure on their chest (reinnervated dermatome). Because the brain's somatotopic map re-maps, the patient eventually localises the sensation to the *phantom hand*, not the chest.

**Step 4 — Advantages over conventional myoelectric prostheses.** (1.5 marks)
- Intuitive control (no mode-switching buttons).
- Higher information transfer (multiple DOF).
- Reduced **phantom limb pain** (50 % reduction reported in Kuiken et al. studies).
- No need for invasive cortical electrodes.

**Step 5 — Limitations.** (½ mark)
- Requires invasive surgery and months of reinnervation.
- EMG crosstalk in multi-site TMR.
- Demands intensive user training.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write TMR as *"a type of electrode"* — it is a **surgical nerve-rerouting procedure**, not an electrode. Losing this distinction costs 2 marks. Also, TMR is distinct from **RPNI (Regenerative Peripheral Nerve Interface)**, which uses a free muscle graft; do not confuse the two.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before the KTU exam — memorise this list:**

- ☐ **BCI = broad** (communication/monitoring); **BMI = motor restoration** subset. Always quote the difference.
- ☐ **EEG** → scalp, 0.5–100 Hz, low spatial res; **ECoG** → subdural, 0.5–500 Hz, high gamma; **Utah Array** → intracortical, 300–7 000 Hz, single units.
- ☐ **Shannon limit:** $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$; report in **bits/min** for BCI.
- ☐ **Safety envelope:** $Q < 0.4$ µC/phase, $D < 30$ µC/cm². Always convert µs→s and µm²→cm².
- ☐ **Population Vector formula:** $\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (f_i - b_i)\mathbf{d}_i$. Must be a *vector*, not a scalar.
- ☐ **Kalman filter** is *recursive* — predictor-corrector; standard in BrainGate trials.
- ☐ **Bidirectional (closed-loop) prosthesis** must include *both* motor and sensory channels; mention feedback modulating M1.
- ☐ **TMR** is *surgical*, not electrical; it routes nerves onto muscle targets for sEMG and sensory re-mapping.
- ☐ **Sensory feedback** is achieved via **peripheral nerve stimulation** *or* **S1 ICMS**; ICMS gives *discrete* percepts (tingle/tap), peripheral gives *graded* touch.
- ☐ **Phantom limb pain** is *reduced* by both mirror therapy, TMR, and graded sensory feedback.
- ☐ **Cosine tuning** is the canonical M1 model; firing rate ∝ $\cos(\theta - \theta_{\text{pref}})$.
- ☐ **Neural amplifier spec:** gain ~ 80 dB, input-referred noise < 5 µV RMS, CMRR > 80 dB.
- ☐ **High-gamma band (75–200 Hz)** in ECoG is the most information-rich for kinematic decoding.
- ☐ **Bidirectional chronic stability** of cortical arrays remains the *grand challenge* — gliosis, micro-motion, BBB breach.
- ☐ For 14-mark questions, always draw a **block diagram** *and* derive an equation — both are required for full marks.
